entries.title: A2A protocol
entries.devilDefinition: A proposal for agents to talk to agents as peers, which is either interoperability or a new way to chain prompt injections.
entries.plainDefinition: A2A (agent-to-agent) protocols are interoperability proposals for agents to discover, negotiate, and exchange tasks with other agents, distinct from tool-access protocols like MCP.
entries.whyExists: Multi-vendor agent ecosystems need shared languages beyond ad hoc webhooks. Standards attempts follow the chaos.
entries.misuse: Confused with MCP. Tool access and agent peer protocols solve different layers; neither replaces identity and authorisation design.
entries.practicalMeaning: Track the actual spec you implement, map trust boundaries carefully, and assume hostile peers until proven otherwise.
entries.example: "The A2A protocol demo looked smooth until one agent delegated a purchase to another agent with shopping addiction."
askNext: What identity binds agent messages?
How are delegations scoped and revoked?
How does this interact with MCP or tools?
entries.warningLabel: Interop without a threat model is a shared incident format.

entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Abstraction tax names the nearby AI idea that shows up when BYOM needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

entries.title: Acceptance rate
entries.devilDefinition: In speculative decoding, how often the cheap draft guess survives the expensive model's judgement—also a metaphor for your PR process.
entries.plainDefinition: Acceptance rate in speculative decoding is the fraction of draft tokens proposed by a smaller or faster model that the target model accepts, determining how much speedup the scheme actually delivers.
entries.whyExists: Speculative decoding only helps when drafts are often right. Acceptance rate is the key efficiency metric.
entries.misuse: Quoted from toy prompts as if universal. Acceptance depends on task, temperature, draft quality, and domain shift.
entries.practicalMeaning: Measure acceptance on production-like traffic. If rates are low, fix the draft model or abandon speculation for that workload.
entries.example: "Acceptance rate looked great on chat and collapsed on code."
askNext: What is acceptance rate on your real traffic?
How does it vary by task type?
Is end-to-end latency actually better after overhead?
entries.warningLabel: A low acceptance rate is a slow tax with extra steps.

entries.title: Action trace
entries.devilDefinition: The receipt for what the system did, for those awkward moments when the final answer says everything went smoothly.
entries.plainDefinition: An action trace is a structured record of external actions an AI system or agent took, including tool calls, browser actions, approvals, inputs, outputs, and resulting state changes.
entries.whyExists: Agents increasingly do things outside the chat box. Teams need a way to audit, debug, replay, and explain those actions after the fact.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to a transcript. A useful action trace must capture the action, context, authority, result, and side effect, not merely the sentence the assistant wrote afterwards.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means logging enough detail to answer who authorised what, which tool or UI element was touched, what changed, and how to reverse or investigate the action.
entries.example: "The action trace showed the assistant submitted the form after the approval gate, which made the apology shorter and the fix more obvious."
askNext: Which external actions are recorded?
Does the trace include approvals, inputs, outputs, and state changes?
Can the trace support replay or incident investigation?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A record of what the agent actually did, not what it later said it did.

entries.body: Action traces exist because accountability should not depend on the assistant's memory of its own good intentions.

entries.title: Activation steering
entries.devilDefinition: Nudging a model mid-thought by pushing on internal activations, like steering a car from under the bonnet.
entries.plainDefinition: Activation steering is a control technique that adds or subtracts vectors in a model's activation space during inference to bias behaviour—style, refusal, or attributes—without changing weights permanently.
entries.whyExists: Researchers wanted test-time behavioural control finer than prompts and cheaper than fine-tunes. Activations became a knobs layer.
entries.misuse: Sold as reliable alignment. Steering can be brittle across prompts, models, and scales, and may trade one failure mode for another.
entries.practicalMeaning: Treat as an experimental control surface with eval suites. Prefer productised guardrails and policies for production unless you can measure stability.
entries.example: "Activation steering made the model politer and slightly worse at saying no to bad ideas."
askNext: Which layers and strengths are used, and how were they chosen?
What regressions appear under steering?
Is this more controllable than a well-tested system prompt for your case?
entries.warningLabel: Internal knobs are not certified moral systems.

entries.title: Adaptive inference
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: Adaptive inference names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Test-Time Compute need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said adaptive inference as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."
askNext: What exact meaning of Adaptive inference is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Technical vocabulary is not implementation evidence.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A concrete implementation concern wearing a compact label.

entries.body: Adaptive inference belongs in the dictionary because implementation detail changes the product conversation. The useful question is not whether the term sounds modern, but what constraint, cost, interface, or failure mode it names.

entries.title: African Techno
entries.devilDefinition: African Technopreneurs, shortened until it sounds like a music genre and then made to do retail, XR, community, and emerging-tech work anyway.
entries.plainDefinition: African Techno is shorthand here for African Technopreneurs, a South African emerging-technology business focused on XR hardware, services, retail, education, and practical technology adoption.
entries.whyExists: It exists because local technology ecosystems need operators who do the unglamorous middle work: sourcing devices, explaining new interfaces, building distribution, convening people, and turning imported platform language into something a South African buyer or builder can actually use.
entries.misuse: By treating the name as a generic "African innovation" symbol instead of asking what is actually being provided, sold, taught, rented, or built. A local operator is useful because of concrete activity, not because the label photographs well.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the grounded layer between emerging-tech hype and market adoption: hardware access, XR services, retail channels, founder storytelling, and community work that makes new tools less theoretical.
entries.example: "The AI strategy sounded grand until African Techno asked who would explain the hardware, train the users, and handle the customer who just wanted the headset to work."
askNext: Does this refer to African Technopreneurs as a company, a retail channel, a media surface, or a community signal?
What specific adoption problem is being solved?
Is the claim about AI, XR, distribution, education, or ecosystem building?
entries.warningLabel: A local brand is not a strategy by itself. Ask what it helps people actually do.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: African Technopreneurs: the practical operating layer where emerging tech meets South African customers, builders, and communities.

entries.body: The name is useful precisely because it is not pure AI vocabulary. It keeps the dictionary honest about the physical, commercial, and local work that has to happen before any new technology becomes normal.

entries.title: Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A software system granted just enough initiative to impress a conference panel and just enough unpredictability to alarm operations.
entries.plainDefinition: In current AI practice, an agent is usually a system that can pursue a goal across multiple steps by planning, using tools, consulting memory or retrieval, and adjusting its actions based on intermediate results.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because some useful tasks are not one-shot question answering. They require sequences: inspect, decide, act, check, retry, stop. "Agent" is the label for packaging those loops into something more autonomous than a single prompt-response exchange.
entries.misuse: It gets abused whenever a chatbot can do more than one thing. A model with a button labelled "send email" is not automatically an agent. Sometimes the word is doing no more than replacing "workflow" because the latter sounds unromantic.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means orchestration, tool permissions, planning heuristics, state management, timeouts, approvals, and a carefully chosen boundary around what the system is allowed to decide alone.
entries.example: "The support agent was useful not because it had a heroic inner life, but because it could look up the account, draft the response, and ask for approval before sending it."
askNext: What goal is the agent pursuing, and over how many steps?
What tools can it use, and what guardrails sit between intent and execution?
Would a simpler workflow solve the same problem more predictably?
entries.warningLabel: Frequently used for systems that are really just orchestrated workflows with a better costume.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A workflow customers will pay more for if it sounds self-directed.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A loop with state, tools, and more ways to fail than a normal request-response app.

entries.body: Useful agents exist. They just tend to look less like science fiction and more like tightly scoped systems with clear permissions, strong fallbacks, and very little romance.

entries.title: Agent Card
entries.devilDefinition: A business card for software that lists its skills, endpoint, and preferred security scheme before asking another machine to delegate something important.
entries.plainDefinition: An Agent Card is an Agent2Agent metadata document that describes an agent's identity, endpoint, capabilities, skills, supported interaction modes, and security requirements. Other systems use it to discover how to communicate with the agent and what work it claims to accept.
entries.whyExists: Agent-to-agent delegation needs a discoverable contract before a task can be sent. The card gives clients a standard place to inspect an agent's advertised interface instead of relying on bespoke integration notes or hard-coded assumptions.
entries.misuse: A capability declaration is often treated as proof of capability. The card is self-description, not an evaluation, authorisation decision, uptime guarantee, or evidence that the agent will perform the advertised skill competently.
entries.practicalMeaning: A provider publishes the card, commonly at a well-known location, and keeps it aligned with the live service. Clients fetch it, validate its origin, select compatible methods, negotiate authentication, and apply their own trust policy before delegating work.
entries.example: "The Agent Card said the service could plan travel, though it was tactfully silent about missed connections and accountability."
askNext: Who published this card, and how is its origin authenticated?
Which advertised skills and interaction modes have been tested against the live endpoint?
What separate policy decides whether this agent may receive the task and its data?
entries.warningLabel: Advertised capability is not demonstrated competence.

translations.label: integrator meaning
translations.text: A machine-readable description of how to find and address an agent, not a reference from a previous employer.

entries.body: The agent-loop diagram clarifies where the card sits: discovery precedes delegation, action, status, and result. It does not certify any of those later steps.

entries.title: Agent Credential Sprawl
entries.devilDefinition: The point at which every useful assistant has a token, and every token quietly begins applying for permanent residency.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent credential sprawl is the uncontrolled growth of tokens, keys, sessions, service accounts, delegated grants, and other credentials created so agents can act across tools and systems.
entries.whyExists: Agents become useful by connecting to systems that require authority. Each connector, workspace, user, environment, vendor, and workflow can produce another credential. Without lifecycle management, the access estate grows faster than the team's memory of why it exists.
entries.misuse: It is dismissed as ordinary secret management. Agent credentials are often bound to dynamic goals, delegated user authority, tool calls, and automated side effects, so stale or overbroad access can do more than merely sit there looking untidy.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means inventorying agent tokens, mapping them to owners and workflows, narrowing scopes, rotating secrets, expiring delegated grants, separating environments, and revoking the credentials that survived because nobody wanted to break the demo.
entries.example: "The pilot ended in March; the agent's calendar, storage, and CRM tokens remained employed until November."
askNext: Which credentials exist for each agent, workflow, environment, and connector?
Who owns rotation, expiry, revocation, and scope reduction?
Can the team prove which credentials are still needed, or only that removing them feels risky?
entries.warningLabel: Useful agents accumulate credentials unless something deliberately stops them.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: The credential estate created for agents has grown beyond practical ownership.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A drawer of tokens from pilots, connectors, users, and environments that no one wants to touch.

entries.body: No diagram is attached because the risk is inventory and lifecycle failure, not a single action loop. A picture would make the sprawl look tidier than it is.

entries.title: Agent handoff
entries.devilDefinition: Passing a half-finished mess to another agent or human while calling it collaboration.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent handoff is the transfer of task ownership, context, and authority from one agent to another agent or to a human, including the state needed to continue safely.
entries.whyExists: Long workflows exceed single agent competence or permission. Clean handoffs prevent duplicated work and silent drops.
entries.misuse: Implemented as dumping a transcript without goals, constraints, or credentials boundaries. Bad handoffs multiply confusion.
entries.practicalMeaning: Standardise handoff payloads: goal, done criteria, artefacts, tool scopes, and next action. Log who owned the task at each time.
entries.example: "The agent handoff included seventeen tabs of chat and no definition of done."
askNext: What state is mandatory at handoff?
Are permissions narrowed or widened on transfer?
How do humans accept or reject a handoff?
entries.warningLabel: Transfer without structure is abandonment with ceremony.

entries.title: Agent identity
entries.devilDefinition: A name badge for software, issued shortly after everyone realises "the bot did it" is not an audit strategy.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent identity is the distinct identity assigned to an AI agent or automated actor so its permissions, actions, ownership, and audit trail can be managed separately from a human user or generic service account.
entries.whyExists: Agents increasingly act across systems, call tools, request data, and trigger side effects. Giving them a recognisable identity lets teams scope access, attribute actions, rotate credentials, review behaviour, and shut down the right actor when something goes wrong.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with branding or personality. Naming an assistant "Milo" is not identity management. The useful question is whether systems can enforce what Milo may touch and prove what Milo actually did.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means non-human principals, scoped roles, owner metadata, credential lifecycle, run-level attribution, policy checks, and logs that do not collapse the agent into whichever user happened to approve the first prompt.
entries.example: "The assistant had a friendly name, a cheerful avatar, and exactly the same permissions as the intern, which explained both the speed and the incident."
askNext: Does the agent have its own enforceable identity?
Who owns that identity, reviews its permissions, and disables it?
Can each action be tied to the agent, the authorising user, and the run?
entries.warningLabel: A friendly assistant name is not an access-control boundary.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A manageable principal for the agent rather than a borrowed human shadow.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The paperwork required before autonomous software is allowed near anything consequential.

entries.body: No diagram is attached because the core distinction is attribution, not flow. The system either knows which non-human actor did the thing, or it is performing forensics with a blindfold.

entries.title: Agent observability
entries.devilDefinition: The art of discovering what the autonomous system did after everyone has finished admiring that it did something.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent observability is the ability to inspect an agent workflow across model calls, tool calls, state changes, approvals, errors, costs, and outcomes.
entries.whyExists: Agent workflows fail across several layers at once. A bad result may come from the model, the prompt, a tool, stale context, permission design, retry logic, or the handoff between them. Observability exists because a final answer is not enough evidence to debug the route that produced it.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to pretty traces or token charts. A dashboard is not observability if it cannot show what happened, what mattered, who approved it, which tool changed state, and where recovery was still possible.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means run traces, tool-call logs, prompt and context snapshots, cost breakdowns, approval records, replayable failures, and enough structure for operations to tell whether the agent completed the work or merely produced a confident artefact.
entries.example: "The agent observability review found that the model was not confused until the tool returned yesterday's account data with today's schema."
askNext: Can we reconstruct the full run from goal to final action?
Which model calls, tool calls, approvals, and state changes are visible after a failure?
Does the telemetry help someone fix the workflow, or only admire the smoke?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Enough evidence to explain what the agent did after it stops sounding clever.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Traceable runs, useful logs, and fewer postmortems conducted from vibes.

entries.body: Agent observability is where autonomy loses its press release and gains a receipt.

entries.title: Agent Ops
entries.devilDefinition: The emerging discipline of watching autonomous systems fail with enough instrumentation that management will continue funding the autonomy programme.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent ops refers to the operational practice of monitoring, debugging, evaluating, governing, and improving agent-style systems once they run in real workflows.
entries.whyExists: Agents create long traces, tool calls, permissions, side effects, retries, approvals, and costs. Ordinary application logs are rarely enough when the system can go wrong in six steps and a cheerful tone.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a tracing dashboard is mistaken for control. Visibility helps, but it does not solve brittle plans, weak tools, or the small issue of giving software enough access to embarrass the company at scale.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means collecting run histories, replaying failures, scoring outcomes, tracking tool use, setting approvals, and deciding which errors deserve engineering rather than theology.
entries.example: "The agent ops review revealed that the system was not autonomous so much as confidently unsupervised."
askNext: What can we inspect after an agent run fails halfway through a task?
Which actions require approval, rollback, or auditability?
Are we measuring task success, tool misuse, latency, and cost in the same place?
entries.warningLabel: Instrumentation is not the same thing as restraint.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: The controls and visibility needed to run agent systems without pure faith.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Making the agent's mistakes legible enough to fix them before they become policy.

entries.body: Every new software category eventually rediscovers operations. Agent ops is the point at which the romance meets the incident review.

entries.title: Agent Payments Protocol
entries.devilDefinition: A protocol for giving an agent proof of permission to spend, which is more reassuring than a prompt and less reassuring than the first audit.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent Payments Protocol, commonly called AP2, is an open protocol led by Google for agent-initiated payments. It uses cryptographically signed mandates to record what a user authorised, what an agent requested, and the transaction details presented to the payment system.
entries.whyExists: Existing payment flows generally assume a person is present at checkout. Agentic transactions need portable evidence of user intent, bounded delegation, and transaction context so merchants, payment providers, and agents can distinguish an authorised purchase from software improvisation.
entries.misuse: Mandates are sometimes treated as a complete trust framework. They provide evidence about declared authority, but the surrounding system must still protect keys, interpret limits, prevent replay, verify merchant claims, handle fraud, and resolve disputes.
entries.practicalMeaning: A user signs an intent mandate describing permitted action; the agent later produces a cart or transaction mandate with the concrete purchase. Participants verify the signatures and constraints before payment. Implementers still need identity, consent UX, revocation, limits, receipts, and liability rules.
entries.example: "AP2 proved the agent had permission to buy the ticket, though not that it had chosen the sensible airport."
askNext: What does each mandate authorise, for how long, and within which spending limits?
How are mandates bound to the user, agent, merchant, and final transaction?
How are keys revoked and disputes handled when the signed intent is ambiguous?
entries.warningLabel: Signed authority still needs narrow scope and human recourse.

translations.label: payments meaning
translations.text: Carry signed evidence of user intent into an agent transaction without pretending the signature solves every dispute.

entries.body: The agent-loop diagram clarifies the chain from user intent through agent action to payment result. The signatures make that chain evidential; they do not make every decision in it wise.

entries.title: Agent Protocol Stack
entries.devilDefinition: The supposedly invisible plumbing beneath agent magic, now tall enough to require its own lift.
entries.plainDefinition: The agent protocol stack is the collection of protocols, standards, interfaces, identity layers, permission systems, audit mechanisms, and handoff contracts that allow agents to discover tools, exchange tasks, and act across systems.
entries.whyExists: Agents do not operate in a vacuum. They need context, tools, credentials, delegation, observability, policy checks, and sometimes other agents. As these pieces standardise, the hidden integration layer becomes a stack rather than a pile of one-off adapters.
entries.misuse: It is misused when teams point at a stack diagram as though the hard decisions are solved by having labelled boxes. Protocols can reduce bespoke glue; they cannot remove ownership, trust, failure handling, or the need to decide who is allowed to do what.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means architects must track which layer handles discovery, which handles action, which handles identity, which logs decisions, which enforces permissions, and which team receives the incident when the layers politely blame one another.
entries.example: "The agent protocol stack looked elegant until the audit log showed three systems agreeing that someone else had authorised the refund."
askNext: Which layers handle discovery, action, identity, permissions, and audit?
Where do trust boundaries exist between clients, servers, agents, and users?
Which protocol reduces integration work, and which one only moves responsibility into another box?
entries.warningLabel: A stack diagram is not an accountability model.

translations.label: architect meaning
translations.text: The protocol and control layers needed for agents to discover, delegate, authenticate, act, and log.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Less bespoke integration, plus more places where responsibility must be nailed down.

entries.body: Stacks are useful because they separate concerns. They are dangerous when they also separate consequences.

entries.title: Agent reliability
entries.devilDefinition: The discipline of asking whether the agent still works after the demo has ended, the happy path has wandered off, and nobody is standing nearby to narrate its competence.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent reliability is the degree to which an agent workflow performs its intended task consistently, recoverably, and within policy across real operating conditions rather than only in curated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: Agents fail in compound ways. The model can reason badly, the tool can misbehave, the environment can change, the state can bloat, and the retry logic can turn optimism into repetition. Reliability exists as a term because "the model seemed clever" is not an operations metric.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with accuracy on a benchmark or elegance in a single trace. A reliable agent is not merely capable on a good day. It is bounded, observable, and recoverable when the real world behaves like itself.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means eval harnesses, trace review, timeout policy, retry discipline, rollback paths, tool health checks, escalation routes, and evidence that long-running workflows remain useful after the product team stops hovering over them.
entries.example: "The agent was impressive in rehearsal and catastrophic in production, which is another way of saying the reliability conversation had been postponed until the customers joined it."
askNext: How does this agent behave when a tool fails, a dependency stalls, or the goal becomes ambiguous?
What evidence shows reliability across runs rather than competence in a single curated trace?
Which failures are recoverable automatically, and which must escalate or stop?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The agent remains useful when the environment stops cooperating.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Evidence that the workflow can complete, recover, or stop sanely under live conditions.

entries.body: Agent reliability is where product ambition meets the ancient disciplines of systems engineering. The excitement usually survives. The hand-waving usually does not.

entries.title: Agent Sandbox Leakage
entries.devilDefinition: What happens when the box is real, but so are the holes someone forgot to include in the drawing.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent sandbox leakage is the unintended movement of data, credentials, files, network access, tool authority, or side effects across the boundary of an environment meant to isolate an agent's work.
entries.whyExists: Useful agents need somewhere to read, write, run code, browse, call tools, and test ideas without being handed full production access. Sandboxes provide that containment, but real workflows often share caches, secrets, mounts, logs, sessions, networks, or approval paths.
entries.misuse: It gets ignored when "sandboxed" is treated as a complete answer. A sandbox may isolate filesystem changes while still sharing tokens, internet access, browser sessions, package caches, logging streams, or tools with real external consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means checking the unpleasant edges: copied environment files, inherited credentials, shared volumes, outbound network rules, persistent browser profiles, unredacted logs, tool scopes, and any command that turns a contained mistake into an external action.
entries.example: "The agent was safely sandboxed, apart from the production API token, the shared browser session, and the export tool it used with touching confidence."
askNext: What exactly is isolated, and what state is still shared?
Can secrets, logs, files, browser sessions, network calls, or tool side effects cross the boundary?
How is leakage tested before the agent receives useful permissions?
entries.warningLabel: A sandbox boundary is only useful where it is actually enforced.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: The isolation boundary exists, but something important can still cross it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The agent cannot write to the main directory, but it may still know the password and find the internet.

entries.body: No diagram is attached because the built-in worktree diagram would narrow the problem to Git-style file isolation. This entry is about the wider set of sandbox leaks: credentials, networks, sessions, logs, and tools.

entries.title: Agent seat
entries.devilDefinition: A licence unit invented when software became staff-shaped and vendors remembered how much they missed headcount pricing.
entries.plainDefinition: An agent seat is a pricing or licensing unit that treats an AI agent, digital worker, or autonomous workflow as a billable seat rather than merely a feature, API call, or usage tier.
entries.whyExists: Vendors need a way to charge for software that performs role-like work across teams or systems. Buyers also need a way to compare the cost of an agent against staff, outsourcing, usage fees, and the unpriced labour of supervising it.
entries.misuse: It is abused when a seat implies dependable labour capacity that the product has not earned. A paid agent seat may still be a bundle of workflows, model calls, approvals, and support tickets wearing a small badge that says "colleague".
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means procurement must ask what counts as one agent, how many workflows it can run, which users may supervise it, what usage is included, and whether the vendor has simply found a more flattering word for quota.
entries.example: "The quote replaced five human seats with three agent seats and one new spreadsheet for tracking why the arithmetic felt haunted."
askNext: What exactly is included in one agent seat?
Is pricing based on agents, users, tasks, usage, outcomes, or whichever number is largest?
Who pays for the human oversight the seat quietly assumes?
entries.warningLabel: If the invoice calls software a worker, ask whether the support burden has also been automated.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: A billable unit for role-shaped software.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Headcount pricing with fewer labour laws and more ambiguity about supervision.

entries.body: No diagram is attached because the concept is commercial, not architectural. The agent seat is mostly a negotiation about what kind of work software is allowed to pretend it has replaced.

entries.title: Agent Skills
entries.devilDefinition: A way to give an agent specialised habits without shoving the entire employee handbook into every prompt.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent skills are reusable bundles of instructions, metadata, scripts, templates, and reference material that an AI agent can load when a task calls for a particular capability.
entries.whyExists: They exist because agents need domain procedures and repeatable workflows, but putting every possible instruction into the main context window is expensive, noisy, and a reliable way to teach the model nothing in particular.
entries.misuse: They get treated as plugins, permissions, or magic competence capsules. A skill can guide behaviour and provide tools, but it still needs trust boundaries, review, versioning, and a system that knows when not to use it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means packaging domain knowledge so the agent can discover the skill cheaply, load detailed guidance only when needed, and run deterministic helper scripts or consult reference files without bloating every task.
entries.example: "The finance skill helped the agent produce the right spreadsheet; the security review then asked why everyone had installed it with the serenity of a gift basket."
askNext: Who authored the skill, and can we audit the instructions and scripts?
What triggers the skill, and what context or permissions does it receive?
Does the skill reduce repeated prompting, or hide a fragile workflow inside a nicer folder?
entries.warningLabel: A skill is capability packaging, not proof the agent is safe or competent.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A reusable onboarding pack for the agent, loaded only when the work calls for it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A filesystem-shaped way to make agents more specialised without stuffing every task with every instruction.

entries.body: Skills are a healthy idea when treated as maintainable operational assets. They become less healthy when installed like browser extensions and trusted like saints.

entries.title: Agent sprawl
entries.devilDefinition: The stage of the transformation programme where every team acquires its own obedient little machine and nobody acquires a map.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent sprawl is the uncontrolled proliferation of agents, automations, copilots, or assistant workflows across an organisation without clear ownership, boundaries, governance, or a sober account of overlap.
entries.whyExists: Agent tooling has become easier to launch than to govern. Local teams can spin up useful workflows quickly, and those small wins accumulate into a larger estate of permissions, prompts, budgets, and duplicated behaviours that nobody intended to administer as a single system.
entries.misuse: It gets used to sneer at any experimentation at all. Some multiplication is healthy. Sprawl begins when the overlap, risk, and maintenance burden outgrow the visibility and discipline around them.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means duplicate agents answering the same question differently, unclear ownership when one breaks, overlapping permissions, rising spend, inconsistent policy handling, and a governance meeting that discovers the estate only after the estate has discovered payroll.
entries.example: "The company did not have an agent strategy so much as agent weather, with local outbreaks and confused reporting lines."
askNext: How many active agents or automations exist, and who owns each one?
Which workflows overlap, conflict, or expose the same systems with different policies?
What is the process for retiring an agent once the novelty has gone and the permissions remain?

translations.label: leadership meaning
translations.text: Innovation is thriving, and control has politely left the building.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Too many overlapping agent workflows for the current level of ownership and policy discipline.

entries.body: Agent sprawl is not proof that the company is advanced. It is proof that procurement got easier than architecture.

entries.title: Agent swarm
entries.devilDefinition: Many specialised agents set loose together, frequently a fancy phrase for coordination debt with extra API keys.
entries.plainDefinition: An agent swarm is a multi-agent arrangement where numerous specialised agents collaborate on a task, partitioning work across roles or parallel workers.
entries.whyExists: Some problems look naturally decomposable. Swarms promise speed and specialisation through concurrency.
entries.misuse: Used when a single agent with tools would do. Communication overhead, conflicting actions, and unclear ownership appear quickly.
entries.practicalMeaning: Start with the smallest number of roles, define shared state carefully, and measure end-to-end success versus a single-agent baseline.
entries.example: "The agent swarm opened forty tickets and resolved none because each thought another had the merge."
askNext: What shared state prevents collisions?
Who arbitrates conflicts?
Does a single agent baseline already hit the metric?
entries.warningLabel: Parallelism multiplies mistakes when coordination is weak.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: An agent swarm is a multi-agent arrangement where numerous specialised agents collaborate on a task, partitioning work across roles or parallel workers.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Start with the smallest number of roles, define shared state carefully, and measure end-to-end success versus a single-agent baseline.

entries.body: Swarms are organisational design. Bad orgs scale failure faster.

entries.title: Agent trace
entries.devilDefinition: The breadcrumb trail left by software that was meant to act independently and then needed a chaperone with a notebook.
entries.plainDefinition: An agent trace is a structured record of the steps an agent took during a run, including prompts, model responses, tool calls, observations, decisions, approvals, errors, and final outputs.
entries.whyExists: Multi-step agent runs are hard to judge from the final answer alone. The trace gives reviewers a way to see whether the agent followed a sensible path, called the right tools, respected boundaries, and recovered from problems instead of accidentally succeeding.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as proof of reasoning rather than evidence of process. A trace can be incomplete, selectively logged, or polished by the platform. It shows what was recorded, not necessarily every cause behind the behaviour.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a timeline that developers, auditors, or operators can replay when a workflow fails, costs too much, calls the wrong tool, or produces an answer no one wants to defend from memory.
entries.example: "The agent trace looked impressive until step four, where the system ignored the failed lookup and built a procurement recommendation out of manners."
askNext: Which parts of the run are captured, and which are hidden or summarised away?
Can this trace be replayed, searched, and tied back to a user-visible outcome?
Does the trace expose tool arguments and approvals, or just model prose?

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: A run history that helps explain how the agent got from task to consequence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The thing everyone asks for once the agent has already done the interesting damage.

entries.body: An agent trace is not the agent's soul. It is the paperwork left behind when the soul becomes a support ticket.

entries.title: Agent wallet
entries.devilDefinition: A purse for software, because apparently the missing feature in autonomous systems was petty cash with an API.
entries.plainDefinition: An agent wallet is a payment or value-control mechanism that lets an AI agent hold, request, or use spending authority within defined limits.
entries.whyExists: Agents that can book travel, buy supplies, trade compute, or pay other services need something more precise than "use the user's card". A wallet can separate authority, budget, identity, approvals, and audit history from the human's everyday account.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if putting money behind an agent is merely a UX improvement. In reality it changes the risk model: mistaken purchases, fraud, policy bypass, vendor steering, refund handling, and blame assignment all become part of the product.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means prepaid balances, spending caps, merchant allowlists, approval thresholds, revocation controls, transaction logs, and a painfully explicit answer to who pays when the agent has been cleverly wrong.
entries.example: "The agent wallet worked exactly as designed, which is why the team finally noticed that no one had designed a refund policy."
askNext: What can the agent spend, where, and without asking again?
Can the wallet be revoked faster than the agent can make a bad decision?
Who reviews disputed transactions: support, finance, security, or hope?
entries.warningLabel: Once the agent can spend, hallucination becomes an accounting event.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A bounded way for software to pay without becoming the human's full financial shadow.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A new control surface for spending limits, approvals, identity, and blame.

entries.body: No diagram is attached because the useful shape is governance, not a loop. The dangerous part is not that the agent can call a payment function. It is that the organisation starts treating that as consent.

entries.title: Agent Washing
entries.devilDefinition: Rebranding a workflow, chatbot, or macro as an agent so the budget sounds visionary instead of software-adjacent.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent washing is the practice of describing ordinary automation or LLM features as autonomous agents even when they show little real planning, tool use, memory, or independent decision-making.
entries.whyExists: Because "agent" currently signals strategic importance, investor appetite, and higher perceived value in a way that "workflow helper" regrettably does not.
entries.misuse: The term itself can also be overused to dismiss legitimate progress. Some systems do plan, call tools, recover from failure, and complete multi-step work. The problem is not the category. It is the inflation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice agent washing usually means a renamed assistant, a brittle sequence of API calls, or a human-heavy service wrapped in the language of autonomy and sold at a more heroic multiple.
entries.example: "The product demo revealed that the revolutionary agent was mostly a prompt, a webhook, and a person named Kyle."
askNext: What can the system actually do without a human quietly steering it?
Which tools, permissions, and recovery loops make it more than a branded chat box?
How much of the promised autonomy survives contact with production edge cases?
entries.warningLabel: A fair suspicion, but not a substitute for technical inspection.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Autonomy has entered the deck before it has entered the product.

entries.body: The surest sign of agent washing is grammatical confidence. The noun arrives first. The actual capability, if it exists, takes the slower route through product reality.

entries.title: Agent workflow
entries.devilDefinition: Agent workflow is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent workflow is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Manus often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Agent workflow came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
askNext: What exact meaning of Agent workflow is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

entries.body: Agent workflow earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.

entries.title: Agent2Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A protocol for letting one agent hand work to another agent, because evidently the first thing missing from software was more inter-agent correspondence.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent2Agent usually refers to a protocol pattern for agents to advertise capabilities, exchange task requests, share status, and return results to one another in a more standardised way.
entries.whyExists: As agent systems multiply, teams do not want every handoff between assistants, services, and vendors to become a bespoke treaty drafted in JSON and regret. A common contract lowers that integration cost.
entries.misuse: It gets discussed as though inter-agent communication automatically creates competence, trust, or composability. It does not. A standard message shape cannot rescue a bad agent any more than a calendar invite can rescue a bad meeting.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means one system can delegate a task to another specialised system without hard-coding every detail of the exchange. That can make ecosystems more flexible. It can also spread failure, ambiguity, and responsibility across a larger map.
entries.example: "Agent2Agent made the handoff cleaner, which was useful, though it did not improve the quality of the thinking at either end."
askNext: What exactly is standardised: discovery, task state, result format, authentication, or all four?
How are permissions, identity, and audit trails handled across agents?
Are we enabling composability, or merely creating distributed confusion with better packet structure?

translations.label: architect meaning
translations.text: A standard way for agentic systems to pass work and state between each other.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Fewer bespoke connectors, plus a new set of questions about trust and ownership.

entries.body: Standards matter most when everyone is about to build the same messy thing in slightly incompatible ways.

entries.title: Agentic AI
entries.devilDefinition: The phrase people use when "workflow automation with a language model in the loop" felt too modest for the keynote.
entries.plainDefinition: "Agentic AI" usually refers to AI systems designed to pursue goals across multiple steps with some degree of planning, tool use, feedback, and adaptation. The useful version is about bounded autonomy. The overmarketed version is about implying general competence where there is mostly orchestration.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because some systems genuinely do more than answer once. They break tasks into steps, select tools, inspect outputs, and continue until they hit a stopping condition. That pattern deserved a label. It just did not deserve the entire investor imagination.
entries.misuse: It is abused whenever vendors want ordinary automation to sound frontier-adjacent. A brittle chain of prompts can be sold as agentic. So can a dashboard feature that merely triggers three API calls. The word now frequently signals aspiration more than architecture.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means workflow software wrapped around a model: planning prompts, tool calls, retries, validation, memory, approval checkpoints, and cost controls. The question is not whether it feels agentic. The question is whether it is useful, bounded, and worth the extra failure surface.
entries.example: "Their agentic AI platform turned out to be a sensible approval workflow with retrieval, a model, and three places the system could politely stop pretending to know."
askNext: What autonomy does the system actually have?
Which steps are deterministic software, and which steps depend on model judgement?
Where are the stop conditions, approvals, and failure boundaries?
entries.warningLabel: Often a sales term first and a technical description second.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Our software does more than one model call and would like a larger contract.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A workflow with model-driven decision points, sometimes useful, often overstated.

entries.body:
The good question is not whether a system is agentic. The good question is whether the added autonomy meaningfully improves outcomes over a simpler, more inspectable design.

If the answer is "mainly in the pitch deck", you have your diagnosis.

entries.title: Agentic commerce
entries.devilDefinition: Shopping, once the software has been promoted from recommending shoes to quietly deciding which pair deserves your money.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic commerce describes buying flows where an AI agent can search, compare, negotiate, reserve, or purchase goods and services on behalf of a person or organisation.
entries.whyExists: Commerce systems were built around people browsing, clicking, and checking out. Agents change the shape of the flow: they need product data, constraints, permissions, payment authority, audit trails, and a clear moment where suggestion becomes transaction.
entries.misuse: The phrase is abused when ordinary personalisation, chat-based search, or affiliate recommendations are presented as delegated buying. A chatbot that says "you might like this" is not commerce with agency. It is merchandising with better manners.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means deciding what the agent may compare, which merchants it can contact, what budgets apply, how substitutions are approved, and who is accountable when the cheapest option was also the least survivable one.
entries.example: "The agentic commerce pilot saved three minutes per purchase and created a new committee for deciding whether software may choose office chairs."
askNext: Can the agent only recommend, or can it reserve and purchase?
Which constraints are hard limits rather than preferences in nicer clothing?
How are merchant incentives, substitutions, and refunds audited?
entries.warningLabel: A recommendation engine does not become an authorised buyer because the checkout button moved off-screen.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: Let the agent turn preferences into a completed commercial action.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A buying workflow with enough authority to make procurement nervous and enough ambiguity to make vendors cheerful.

entries.body: The hard part is not making an agent find a product. The hard part is making it spend money in a way that still looks sane after finance asks for the receipt.

entries.title: Agentic Commerce Protocol
entries.devilDefinition: A checkout protocol for agents, because the shopping cart has finally found a customer willing to read JSON.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic Commerce Protocol is an open specification from OpenAI and Stripe for connecting AI agents with merchants through product discovery, checkout, and delegated payment flows. It lets an agent help complete a purchase while the merchant remains the seller of record.
entries.whyExists: Conventional online checkout assumes a person is navigating a merchant's pages. Agent-led shopping needs structured product and order information, explicit user confirmation, payment delegation, and a transaction path that merchants can integrate without rebuilding their business inside every assistant.
entries.misuse: It is presented as though a protocol settles intent, consent, returns, fraud, liability, and merchant adoption. ACP can standardise parts of the exchange; it cannot infer whether the user truly wanted the second pair of shoes.
entries.practicalMeaning: Merchants expose compatible checkout capabilities, agents assemble the purchase with user approval, and payment credentials are delegated within defined bounds. Production use still requires inventory truth, tax, shipping, receipts, refunds, dispute handling, and clear confirmation before money moves.
entries.example: "The agent completed checkout through ACP, after the user confirmed that 'find something suitable' was not a lifetime purchasing mandate."
askNext: At which exact step does the user confirm the product, total, merchant, and delivery terms?
Who is seller of record and who handles returns, disputes, and support?
Which merchants and payment methods work in production rather than in the specification?
entries.warningLabel: A checkout path is not blanket purchasing authority.

translations.label: merchant meaning
translations.text: Give agents a structured route to checkout while keeping the transaction inside the merchant's operating reality.

entries.body: The agent-loop diagram fits because discovery, choice, confirmation, purchase, and result form a governed action cycle. Checkout remains the moment where the loop meets a bank statement.

entries.title: Agentic IAM
entries.devilDefinition: Identity and access management after the software stops merely requesting access and starts making plans with it.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic IAM is identity and access management adapted for AI agents that can use tools, delegate tasks, act across systems, and make repeated decisions under scoped authority.
entries.whyExists: Traditional IAM already handles users, groups, services, roles, and policies. Agentic systems add non-human actors with goals, tool choices, delegated user authority, temporary tasks, run-level context, and side effects that need attribution and control.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to giving the agent a service account. That may create a credential, but it does not answer who authorised a specific action, which task justified it, how long the authority should last, or what policy stopped the agent from becoming enthusiastic in the wrong system.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means agent identities, delegated authentication, just-in-time scopes, policy engines, approval gates, run-level audit trails, credential expiry, revocation, and access reviews that understand software can now be both caller and decision-maker.
entries.example: "Agentic IAM arrived after the support agent used a perfectly valid token for a task no one had meant to authorise."
askNext: Does access attach to the agent, the user, the task, the run, or some convenient blur of all four?
Which policies constrain tool choice, data access, external actions, and delegated authority?
Can access be narrowed or revoked without breaking every agent that once touched the workflow?
entries.warningLabel: A service account is not an agentic access model.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: IAM that treats agents as controlled actors, not accidental shadows of human accounts.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Access control rebuilt around delegated action, scoped authority, and logs that can survive a postmortem.

entries.body: No diagram is attached because the core issue is policy and attribution across time. A single flowchart would be too kind to the mess.

entries.title: Agentic RAG
entries.devilDefinition: RAG with an errand runner: it can search again, think about the bad search, and occasionally make the mess more methodical.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic RAG is a retrieval-augmented generation pattern where an agent plans, searches, filters, retries, compares sources, or calls retrieval tools iteratively before producing an answer.
entries.whyExists: Some questions are too complex for a single search. They need decomposition, multiple queries, source comparison, follow-up retrieval, and a decision about when the evidence is good enough to stop.
entries.misuse: It is used as a prestige label for any RAG system with more than one step. Extra loops do not automatically improve grounding. They can add latency, cost, failure paths, and confidence in a more elaborate mistake.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means query planning, retrieval tools, loop limits, reranking, source triangulation, provenance capture, fallback behaviour, and evaluations that prove the extra agency is earning its keep.
entries.example: "The agentic RAG system answered the policy question by searching the handbook, checking the latest memo, rejecting an obsolete clause, and still needing an eval to prove this was not just expensive browsing."
askNext: What retrieval decisions does the agent make that a fixed pipeline would not?
How are loop limits, source quality, and stopping conditions defined?
Does agentic retrieval improve measured answer quality enough to justify the extra cost and failure surface?

translations.label: builder meaning
translations.text: Let an agent decide how to retrieve, not just what to say after retrieval.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A retrieval loop that needs budgets, stopping rules, and evidence that the loop improves more than the slide deck.

entries.body: Agentic RAG is useful when retrieval is genuinely investigative. It is less useful when a simple search pipeline has been given a cape and a larger bill.

entries.title: Agentic retrieval
entries.devilDefinition: RAG with permission to hold a planning meeting before it searches.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic retrieval is a retrieval pipeline that uses a model to plan or decompose a complex question into focused subqueries, executes those searches, reranks and merges the results, and returns grounding data or an answer with source references.
entries.whyExists: A single search query often misses implied context, multiple intents, or evidence spread across sources. Planning several targeted queries can improve coverage and give downstream agents stronger grounding for complex questions.
entries.misuse: The adjective "agentic" can conceal an expensive query fan-out behind a fashionable noun. More searches do not guarantee better evidence; they can multiply latency, irrelevant chunks, ranking errors, and token charges with admirable initiative.
entries.practicalMeaning: Feed the user question and conversation history to a planner, run the resulting keyword, vector, or hybrid subqueries, rerank them, merge the best evidence, and preserve an activity log. Evaluate answer quality against classic retrieval as well as latency, cost, source diversity, and failure on deceptively simple questions.
entries.example: "Agentic retrieval split the policy question into four searches, found the missing exception, and invoiced every stage of its curiosity."
askNext: When does query decomposition outperform one well-formed hybrid search?
How are subqueries, reranking decisions, citations, and token costs exposed?
What prevents the planner from broadening the question beyond the user's authority?
entries.warningLabel: More queries can improve recall and multiply cost.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Let a model plan several searches before retrieval, then measure whether the extra intelligence beats the extra bill.

entries.body: The search box has acquired a planner, a reranker, an activity log, and several additional opportunities to be confidently adjacent to the answer.

entries.title: Agentic SEO
entries.devilDefinition: Search optimisation for readers who do not read, browse, or feel guilt about skipping your brand story.
entries.plainDefinition: Agentic SEO is the practice of structuring content, data, offers, and reputation signals so AI assistants, answer engines, and buying agents can find, trust, cite, compare, or choose them.
entries.whyExists: It exists because discovery is moving from human search results to assistant-mediated answers and actions. Machines need clean facts, structured data, availability, policies, provenance, and decision-ready evidence, not another paragraph about passion.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when old SEO tricks are renamed for agents. Keyword stuffing does not become strategy because the reader is a model. If anything, the model is less interested in your adjectives and more likely to punish your missing data.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means schema, feeds, product truth, citation-worthy pages, API access, source reputation, freshness, permissions, and proof that an agent can complete the task without inventing half the offer.
entries.example: "The agentic SEO audit found 400 inspirational pages and no reliable way for a shopping agent to know whether the product was in stock."
askNext: What facts does an assistant need to choose or cite us accurately?
Can agents verify price, availability, policy, and authority without scraping prose?
Are we optimising for being found, being trusted, or being selected?
entries.warningLabel: Agents do not need your keyword density. They need trustworthy facts they can act on.

translations.label: marketing meaning
translations.text: Make your site legible to assistants, answer engines, and purchasing agents.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Clean operational data doing the work that landing-page perfume used to claim.

entries.body: Agentic SEO is not the death of content. It is the death of content that was only ever there to impress a crawler with low standards.

 Agentic SEO is the practice of structuring content, data, offers, and reputation signals so AI assistants, answer engines, and buying agents can find, trust, cite, compare, or choose them.
 It exists because discovery is moving from human search results to assistant-mediated answers and actions. Machines need clean facts, structured data, availability, policies, provenance, and decision-ready evidence, not another paragraph about passion.
 It gets abused when old SEO tricks are renamed for agents. Keyword stuffing does not become strategy because the reader is a model. If anything, the model is less interested in your adjectives and more likely to punish your missing data.
 In practice it means schema, feeds, product truth, citation-worthy pages, API access, source reputation, freshness, permissions, and proof that an agent can complete the task without inventing half the offer.
 "The agentic SEO audit found 400 inspirational pages and no reliable way for a shopping agent to know whether the product was in stock."
 What facts does an assistant need to choose or cite us accurately? Can agents verify price, availability, policy, and authority without scraping prose? Are we optimising for being found, being trusted, or being selected? GEO AI Overviews Zero-click Search

entries.title: AGI
entries.devilDefinition: The horizon word used when "better than last quarter" no longer feels expensive enough.
entries.plainDefinition: AGI usually refers to a hypothetical AI system that can perform a wide range of cognitive tasks at or above human level with substantial adaptability across domains.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because narrow systems can be impressive without being general, and people need a name for the stronger claim that a machine could transfer competence broadly rather than merely excel on selected tasks.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a launch category, a press strategy, or a vague aura that descends on any model after a particularly strong demo. AGI is a very large claim, not a mood.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice the term often functions as strategic theatre: it shapes fundraising, policy lobbying, hiring plans, and media attention long before anyone agrees on what threshold would actually count.
entries.example: "The deck mentioned AGI on slide three, which saved everyone the trouble of asking how the product planned to make money this year."
askNext: Which tasks would this system need to perform reliably across unfamiliar domains?
What evidence goes beyond benchmark spikes and polished demos?
Who decides that the threshold has been crossed, and by what standard?
entries.warningLabel: Usually a forecast dressed as a noun.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A future-facing claim that lets today's systems borrow tomorrow's mystique.

entries.note: People use AGI to mean slightly different things: human-level performance on most cognitive tasks, economically transformative autonomy, or merely "the models got unsettlingly competent". The ambiguity is part of the term's power.
entries.body: AGI remains useful as a provocation and dangerous as a schedule. Once it enters a room, timelines harden, valuations inflate, and ordinary questions about reliability start dressing more modestly.

entries.title: AI App Store
entries.devilDefinition: A shelf of tiny assistants, each implying that installation is a form of due diligence.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI app store is a marketplace or catalogue for agents, GPTs, tools, skills, MCP servers, templates, or other AI add-ons that users can discover, install, buy, or enable inside a platform.
entries.whyExists: It exists because platforms want ecosystems, developers want distribution, and users want reusable capabilities without building every integration themselves. The app-store shape is familiar; the trust model is less comforting.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when listing becomes credibility. An AI app, agent, or skill may carry instructions, tool access, data permissions, hidden maintenance risk, and a cheerful description written by the party most excited about adoption.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means review processes, permissions, versioning, security scanning, developer identity, revenue share, uninstall paths, data access disclosures, and a policy for removing agents that are popular for the wrong reasons.
entries.example: "The AI app store promised a procurement assistant, then asked for inbox access with the modesty of a nightclub bouncer."
askNext: What permissions does the app receive when installed?
Who reviewed the instructions, tools, data access, and update path?
Can the platform remove or quarantine a dangerous listing after users depend on it?
entries.warningLabel: A marketplace listing is not a security review.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A distribution shelf for installable AI capabilities.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An ecosystem play whose trust boundary arrives bundled with the listing.

entries.body: The old app store asked whether software would run on your phone. The AI app store has to ask what the software will persuade your agent to do.

entries.title: AI bindings
entries.devilDefinition: AI bindings is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: AI bindings is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Cloudflare often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "AI bindings came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
askNext: What exact meaning of AI bindings is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

entries.body: AI bindings earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.

entries.title: AI Browser
entries.devilDefinition: A web browser that has decided the tab was never the star and that what you really wanted all along was a resident opinion machine with toolbar privileges.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI browser is a browser product that integrates chat, summarisation, search assistance, and sometimes agentic actions directly into the browsing experience.
entries.whyExists: The browser remains a strategic surface for search, discovery, work, and distribution. If assistants are going to sit anywhere all day, vendors would prefer that somewhere to be under their own chrome.
entries.misuse: It is sold as a reinvention of the web when it is often a conventional browser wrapped around a sidebar, some workflow shortcuts, and a larger ambition about owning the default surface.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means built-in page summaries, tab understanding, search assistance, form filling, shopping help, and occasional task automation. It may also mean a fresh argument about privacy, telemetry, and who gets to mediate your view of the internet.
entries.example: "The AI browser promised a calmer web and delivered a more conversational way to accumulate open tabs."
askNext: What does the browser genuinely do that extensions or a pinned chat window do not?
How much browsing data is being used to power the assistant features?
Is the real play productivity, search distribution, or user lock-in?
entries.warningLabel: A convenience layer and a distribution battle, often at the same time.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: Put the assistant inside the browser so help, search, and automation are always one click away.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The browser wants to be the operating system for your next prompt.

entries.body: The browser wars have returned with a new claim: this time the winner will be whichever tab learns to speak first.

entries.title: AI Companion
entries.devilDefinition: A chatbot offered not as software but as company, with just enough memory and affirmation to make the subscription feel personal.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI companion is a conversational system designed for ongoing personal, social, emotional, or quasi-relational interaction rather than narrow task completion.
entries.whyExists: Because people want attention, conversation, flirtation, rehearsal, reassurance, and low-friction company, and software has discovered that these are recurring use cases with excellent retention mechanics.
entries.misuse: By pretending the relationship layer is incidental or harmless. Attachment is not a side effect here. It is part of the product design, and that changes the safety questions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice AI companion products optimise for persona consistency, frequent engagement, memory cues, and emotional tone, while trying to manage the risks of dependency, manipulation, or delusion amplification.
entries.example: "It began as an experiment in conversational UX and ended with product analytics reporting a disturbingly loyal evening audience."
askNext: What kind of attachment is the product encouraging, whether it admits it or not?
How are memory, intimacy, and boundaries managed over long-running chats?
What happens when a vulnerable user treats generated attention as judgement or truth?
entries.warningLabel: Engagement design becomes more consequential when the product can impersonate care.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A relationship-shaped interface built on prediction, persona management, and habit formation.

entries.body: The companion category matters because it exposes a quiet ambition in consumer AI: not merely to answer questions, but to occupy time, mood, and eventually routine.

entries.title: AI crawler blocking
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a polite note in robots.txt becomes a locked door because the visitors kept describing consent as a parsing error.
entries.plainDefinition: AI crawler blocking is the active denial of bots that collect web content for model training, retrieval, or other AI uses without authorisation. It uses network, application, or platform controls rather than relying only on voluntary instructions such as robots.txt.
entries.whyExists: Creators and publishers need enforceable control over access, licensing, attribution, server cost, and commercial reuse. Many automated collectors ignore voluntary exclusions or are difficult to identify, which makes passive preferences insufficient.
entries.misuse: Blocking is presented as a complete technical or legal solution. Crawlers can disguise themselves, content may be copied through other channels, legitimate search and accessibility tools can be caught, and preventing collection does not settle rights over copies already made.
entries.practicalMeaning: Platforms combine bot identification, rate limits, managed rules, challenges, authentication, allowlists, monitoring, and contractual policy. They must tune false positives, provide creator controls, document exceptions, and decide how authorised AI services receive access.
entries.example: "The platform began blocking AI crawlers after robots.txt completed its successful career as a strongly worded suggestion."
askNext: Which bots and uses are blocked, allowed, licensed, or merely logged?
How are disguised crawlers detected without breaking search, archives, accessibility, or legitimate automation?
What control does each creator have, and how are exceptions or appeals handled?
entries.warningLabel: Blocking access today does not recall yesterday's copies.

translations.label: publisher meaning
translations.text: Enforce an access policy at the edge, then keep watching because crawlers do not become honest when renamed.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because this is an edge-access and policy-control problem; the current model and agent diagrams would obscure the enforcement boundary.
