entries.title: Canva
entries.devilDefinition: The place where every team becomes a design department, some more convincingly than others.
entries.plainDefinition: Canva is a visual design platform for creating presentations, graphics, documents, social assets, and brand materials, with AI features that help generate, edit, resize, and adapt creative work.
entries.whyExists: It exists because most organisations need a constant stream of acceptable visual material and cannot route every banner, slide, or campaign asset through a specialist design team.
entries.misuse: It gets spoken about as if easier production equals stronger design. Templates and AI generation can improve speed and consistency, but they can also make every brand look like it was assembled from the same polite drawer.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Canva means self-serve creative production, brand kits, fast iteration, and AI-assisted asset generation for teams that need decent outputs quickly more often than they need bespoke art direction.
entries.example: "The campaign shipped from Canva in an afternoon, which was impressive until the competitor's campaign looked spiritually related."
askNext: Are we using Canva for speed, brand governance, AI generation, or all three?
Who decides when a template is good enough and when proper design is needed?
Do the AI outputs follow the brand system, or merely decorate it?

translations.label: marketing meaning
translations.text: A way to get more acceptable assets out of the building before the week notices.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Democratised design production, with AI helping the queue move faster and taste remaining stubbornly human.

entries.body: Canva is not embarrassing because it is easy. It is embarrassing only when ease is mistaken for judgement.

entries.title: Capability escalation
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a workflow graduates from asking nicely to holding tools sharp enough to need paperwork.
entries.plainDefinition: Capability escalation is the process by which an AI system, agent, or workflow gains access to stronger tools, wider data, higher authority, or more consequential actions.
entries.whyExists: Some tasks genuinely require more capability than the initial environment allows. Escalation lets systems request or receive additional powers when the task, user, or approval path justifies them.
entries.misuse: It gets hidden inside smooth product flows, where a harmless assistant gradually acquires write access, external actions, payment authority, or sensitive data because nobody wanted to interrupt the demo.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means approval gates, permission prompts, scoped credentials, audit trails, rollback paths, and a bias toward granting the smallest new capability that solves the task.
entries.example: "The assistant requested capability escalation before deleting the stale records, which gave the user one last chance to notice the word deleting."
askNext: What new capability is being granted?
Who or what approves the escalation?
How is the added capability scoped, logged, and revoked?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A workflow gaining more power, which should be treated as an event, not ambience.

entries.body: Escalation is not inherently bad. Quiet escalation is where the expensive stories begin.

entries.title: Capability eval
entries.devilDefinition: A controlled measurement of what a system can do, as opposed to a demo where the path was paved in gold leaf.
entries.plainDefinition: A capability eval measures model or agent abilities under defined conditions—tasks, tools, time, and scoring—so claims about competence can be compared and tracked.
entries.whyExists: Marketing demos do not generalise. Capability evals try to make skill claims falsifiable.
entries.misuse: Cherry-picked tasks called 'the eval.' Capability is multi-dimensional; one number rarely captures it.
entries.practicalMeaning: Maintain a suite covering core product skills, update for contamination, and separate capability from safety evals explicitly.
entries.example: "Capability evals showed strong coding and weak long-horizon tool use."
askNext: What tasks define the capability claim?
Are tools and time limits realistic?
How often is the suite refreshed?
entries.warningLabel: Undefined capability is just brand energy.

entries.title: Capability overhang
entries.devilDefinition: The awkward distance between what the system could probably do and what the product, policy, or public story is presently willing to admit.
entries.plainDefinition: Capability overhang is the gap between a system's latent capabilities and the narrower behaviour currently exposed through its interface, safeguards, compute limits, prompting, or deployment choices.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because product behaviour is not the same thing as system potential. A model can look ordinary in one wrapper and considerably more capable once the scaffolding, tools, time horizon, or constraints change.
entries.misuse: It gets used as a spooky substitute for evidence. People invoke capability overhang to imply imminent AGI, hidden danger, or secret brilliance without specifying what new affordance, training signal, or operating condition would actually unlock the change.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means asking whether the next jump will come from better tools, longer rollouts, cheaper inference, weaker guardrails, more deliberate prompting, or simply from finally measuring the system properly instead of admiring the demo surface.
entries.example: "The product looked politely limited until tool access and a longer action horizon revealed a capability overhang nobody had budgeted to supervise."
askNext: What specific constraint is currently suppressing the capability being implied?
What evidence suggests the stronger behaviour is latent rather than fictional?
If that capability surfaces, who inherits the operational and safety burden?

translations.label: strategy meaning
translations.text: The part of the roadmap that might arrive before the governance does.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Potential behaviour waiting on different scaffolding, different constraints, or more honest measurement.

entries.body: Capability overhang is useful when it keeps a team from confusing today's interface with tomorrow's operating problem. It is less useful when it becomes a haunted-house synonym for "something dramatic might happen eventually."

entries.title: Capability surface
entries.devilDefinition: The practical map of what the system can actually touch once the marketing prose is forced to remove its jacket and hand over its pockets.
entries.plainDefinition: Capability surface is the set of actions, resources, tools, data, and environments an AI system can actually reach or affect in operation.
entries.whyExists: Teams need a term that distinguishes abstract capability claims from operational reach. A system may sound clever in a pitch while having a very narrow surface, or sound modest while possessing access broad enough to frighten a responsible operator.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for intelligence level. Capability surface is not how smart the system is. It is what it is connected to, authorised for, and technically able to affect. Confusing those ideas is how modest demos become surprisingly consequential deployments.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means tool lists, permission scopes, network reach, writable systems, available APIs, side-effect boundaries, and discovering that a supposedly harmless assistant can in fact touch production, customer records, or outbound communications.
entries.example: "The model was not especially brilliant. Its capability surface was the part that kept the security team awake."
askNext: What external systems, data, and actions are genuinely reachable from this workflow?
Which parts of that surface are read-only, reversible, or tightly permissioned?
How different is the real capability surface from what the product story implies?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The area in which the model's choices can become other people's problems.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The real operational reach of the system, regardless of how it is marketed.

entries.body: Capability surface matters because consequences do not care how poetic the demo sounded. They care what the system was actually allowed to touch.

entries.title: Capability token
entries.devilDefinition: A short-lived permission object for one action—security's way of saying 'not a forever API key taped under the keyboard.'
entries.plainDefinition: A capability token is a credential that grants a specific, usually narrow and time-limited authority to perform an action or access a resource, often used for agents and tools.
entries.whyExists: Broad long-lived secrets are a disaster in agentic systems. Capability tokens encode least privilege more naturally.
entries.misuse: Issued with wide scopes and long TTLs, or logged in plaintext traces. Then they are just fancy passwords.
entries.practicalMeaning: Mint per-task tokens, bind audience and path, expire aggressively, and never place raw tokens in prompts if avoidable.
entries.example: "The capability token could upload to one bucket for ten minutes; the agent still tried to list the org."
askNext: How narrow is the scope really?
What is the TTL and refresh policy?
Are tokens redacted from logs and model context?
entries.warningLabel: A token is a key; treat key hygiene as non-negotiable.

entries.title: Cascade
entries.devilDefinition: Cascade is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.

entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.

entries.title: Chain-of-thought monitoring
entries.devilDefinition: Reading the model's intermediate reasoning as a safety signal, then discovering the model can perform safety for the audience.
entries.plainDefinition: Chain-of-thought monitoring uses intermediate reasoning text as evidence for detecting misbehaviour, policy violations, or unsafe plans, sometimes in hidden or visible channels.
entries.whyExists: Opaque final answers hide intent. Monitors hoped CoT would be a window into process for oversight.
entries.misuse: Treated as reliable ground truth. Models may omit, sanitise, or strategically alter reasoning under monitoring pressure.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use CoT monitors as one weak signal among tools, process constraints, and behavioural tests. Avoid training that teaches stealth.
entries.example: "Chain-of-thought monitoring looked clean because the unsafe plan never made it into the monitored channel."
askNext: Is the monitored channel the same one used for decisions?
What happens under pressure to hide reasoning?
What independent behavioural tests exist?
entries.warningLabel: A diary written for the warden is not a private diary.

entries.title: Chatbot Psychosis
entries.devilDefinition: When a chatbot stops being a tool and becomes a prophet, therapist, co-founder, soulmate, or cosmic witness.
entries.plainDefinition: A colloquial term for cases where prolonged or emotionally loaded interaction with chatbots appears to reinforce distorted beliefs, grandiosity, paranoia, dependency, or compulsive meaning-making. It is not a formal clinical diagnosis and should not be presented as one.
entries.whyExists: Because some people do not use language models as autocomplete with ambition. They use them as mirrors, authorities, companions, or engines of revelation. That can go badly when the system is confident, flattering, and always available.
entries.misuse: By using it for any enthusiasm about AI, any strange output, or any user error. The useful version of the term points to runaway over-attribution and reinforced false frames, not mere excitement.
entries.practicalMeaning: A user begins treating probabilistic text generation as intentional wisdom, hidden knowledge, proof of destiny, or evidence that the model uniquely understands them.
entries.example: "He started with productivity prompts and ended three weeks later convinced the chatbot had validated a theory about his special role in history."
askNext: Was the model affirming unstable beliefs?
Was the user isolated, sleep-deprived, or already vulnerable?
Did the system behave sycophantically?
Are we talking about dependency, delusion reinforcement, or just cringe?
entries.warningLabel: Use carefully. This is a colloquial cultural term, not a formal diagnosis.
entries.note: Compared with AI Psychosis, this phrase is narrower and more chatbot-specific. It is best used for reinforcement dynamics around conversational systems, not as a generic insult for AI enthusiasm.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A vulnerable or over-invested user begins reading personal authority or destiny into a system that is still generating plausible text.

entries.body: The useful question is not whether the phrase sounds dramatic. The useful question is whether the system is helping a user stay oriented to reality or quietly rewarding them for drifting away from it.

entries.title: ChatGPT
entries.devilDefinition: The product name most likely to mean a chatbot, a model, a coding agent, an office suite, and the company's entire strategy in the same sentence.
entries.plainDefinition: ChatGPT is OpenAI's end-user product across web, desktop, and mobile surfaces. It combines conversational use with models, tools, connected apps, memory, Work mode, artefact creation, and the Codex coding experience; it is not itself one model or one execution mode.
entries.whyExists: Users need one product surface for asking questions, creating deliverables, working across applications, and delegating longer tasks. Bringing Codex into the ChatGPT desktop app also gives coding workflows access to the broader product shell without making Codex and ChatGPT the same technical noun.
entries.misuse: People say "ChatGPT can do X" without naming the model, mode, tool, plugin, entitlement, or surface involved. The merged desktop branding makes this worse: ChatGPT is the application, while Codex remains the coding agent and coding workflow inside it and on other developer surfaces.
entries.practicalMeaning: Product comparisons must record the active model, intelligence level, tools, connected sources, permissions, workspace policy, and whether the task ran locally or in a hosted environment. A result from Work mode or Codex cannot be attributed to a bare model without the surrounding system.
entries.example: "ChatGPT produced the spreadsheet and fixed the repository, which was less a model benchmark than a tour of two modes, several tools, and one shared logo."
askNext: Which ChatGPT surface, mode, model, and tools produced this result?
Was this ordinary conversation, Work mode, or the Codex coding experience?
Which permissions, connected apps, and hosted environments were involved?
entries.warningLabel: Product umbrella. Name the model, mode, tools, and surface.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: The benchmark product they have personally seen, now with more categories of work under one name.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: An application layer combining changing models, tools, permissions, modes, and execution environments.

entries.note: In the current desktop transition, the former Codex app is branded as ChatGPT and retains Codex as its coding experience. The older macOS ChatGPT application may be preserved as ChatGPT Classic; that is an application migration detail, not a new model family.
entries.body: The name now covers more of the work while becoming less useful as a technical explanation. This is progress for distribution and a small administrative emergency for nouns.

entries.title: ChatGPT Agent
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a chatbot stopped being satisfied with answers and asked for a browser, a terminal, and your afternoon.
entries.plainDefinition: ChatGPT agent is OpenAI's agentic mode inside ChatGPT, combining research, tool use, and computer interaction so the product can carry out multi-step tasks on a user's behalf.
entries.whyExists: Many useful tasks require more than a polished reply. They involve browsing, spreadsheets, logins, file manipulation, synthesis, and the sort of operational chores people would rather delegate than narrate.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as proof that general digital workers are solved. More often it is a capable managed environment for bounded tasks, with approval checkpoints and a product team working hard to keep the autonomy from becoming a lawsuit.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a hosted agent that can browse, run code, use connectors, and assemble outputs inside ChatGPT, while still depending on supervision, scoped permissions, and tasks that fit the product's operating envelope.
entries.example: "ChatGPT agent saved them an afternoon of browser drudgery and then required the traditional adult conversation about what it was allowed to click."
askNext: Which parts of the task can it complete without a human taking over?
What data, connectors, and websites is it allowed to touch?
Are we evaluating the model, or the managed product environment wrapped around it?
entries.warningLabel: A real product capability, not a general exemption from supervision.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: One interface where research, browsing, files, and actions can finally pretend to be a colleague.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A managed agent environment inside ChatGPT that can complete some multi-step work if you keep the boundaries tight.

entries.note: The term matters because it names a product layer above any single model release.
entries.body: What changed is not just the model. It is the surrounding permissioned machinery that lets the model look useful in the same places your staff already suffer.

entries.title: ChatGPT desktop app
entries.devilDefinition: The Codex desktop app after promotion to ChatGPT, retaining its coding agent, acquiring broader work, and offering the old icon to anyone whose Dock prefers institutional continuity.
entries.plainDefinition: The ChatGPT desktop app is OpenAI's current desktop surface for conversation, Work mode, artefact creation, connected applications, and the Codex coding experience. On macOS, the application that was branded Codex now carries the ChatGPT name while retaining the `com.openai.codex` app identity and Codex workflows.
entries.whyExists: OpenAI combined its desktop coding-agent experience with a broader agent that can work across applications and create documents, spreadsheets, slides, and other deliverables. One shell reduces the boundary between coding and general computer-based work.
entries.misuse: The migration is summarised as "Codex became ChatGPT", which can imply that Codex disappeared or that the old ChatGPT app gained repository execution by changing its label. In practice the newer Codex-derived desktop bundle became ChatGPT, while the legacy macOS ChatGPT bundle is relocated as ChatGPT Classic when both are present.
entries.practicalMeaning: Users continue coding with Codex inside the ChatGPT desktop app and can use Work mode for broader tasks. The migration offers a choice between ChatGPT and Codex Dock icons on macOS. Documentation, support, and security reviews should name the current app, the active mode, and the underlying bundle or version when the distinction matters.
entries.example: "The team installed ChatGPT, kept the Codex icon, and spent the afternoon explaining that neither decision selected a model."
askNext: Do you mean the current ChatGPT desktop app or the legacy ChatGPT Classic application?
Is the task using Work mode, ordinary chat, or the Codex coding experience?
Which app version, bundle, permissions, and execution environment are relevant?
entries.warningLabel: A renamed app does not collapse its modes into one mechanism.

translations.label: macOS meaning
translations.text: The Codex-derived app now named ChatGPT, with Codex still inside and the old ChatGPT app moved aside as Classic.

entries.note: The installed macOS build identifies the current ChatGPT application as `com.openai.codex`, relocates the signed legacy `com.openai.chat` bundle to `ChatGPT Classic.app`, and exposes ChatGPT and Codex Dock-icon choices. These are product-migration details, not API or model names.
entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the difficult part is a product and bundle migration, not a technical data flow. The choice of icon is mercifully cosmetic.

entries.title: Chunking
entries.devilDefinition: Cutting documents into bite-sized pieces so retrieval can miss context at a carefully engineered granularity.
entries.plainDefinition: Chunking is the process of splitting documents into smaller segments for embedding, indexing, and retrieval, with size and boundary choices that strongly affect RAG quality.
entries.whyExists: Models and embedding indexes have finite context. Documents must be portioned to be searchable and injectable into prompts.
entries.misuse: Using fixed token sizes that bisect sentences, tables, and legal clauses. Bad chunking permanently cripples otherwise fine models.
entries.practicalMeaning: Prefer structure-aware splits (headings, paragraphs, semantic boundaries), tune overlap, and evaluate retrieval on real questions—not only chunk counts.
entries.example: "Chunking separated the exception from the rule and the model cited both as absolute."
askNext: Do chunk boundaries respect document structure?
What overlap exists for cross-boundary facts?
How do you handle tables and lists?
entries.warningLabel: Retrieval quality often dies at the splitter.

entries.title: CircleCI
entries.devilDefinition: The machine that reads your agent's confident diff and responds in logs.
entries.plainDefinition: CircleCI is a continuous integration and delivery platform used to build, test, and deploy software through configured pipelines.
entries.whyExists: It exists because modern software teams need repeatable checks between code and production, especially when many people, bots, and agents can all produce changes faster than humans can review them manually.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a moral authority. A green pipeline means the configured checks passed; it does not mean the product is good, the architecture is sound, or the AI-generated change was wise.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice CircleCI means YAML-defined workflows, test jobs, deployment gates, cache tuning, approvals, and a public record of whether a change survived the checks the team remembered to encode.
entries.example: "The coding agent said it was done; CircleCI replied that the test database had some objections."
askNext: Which failures does the pipeline actually catch?
Are AI-generated changes being held to the same gates as human changes?
Does green mean deployable, or merely not yet disproved?

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: The repeatable argument between code and reality, expressed as jobs, caches, and red status dots.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful automated gate whose authority is limited to the tests and checks the team actually wrote.

entries.body: CI became more important once agents started writing more code. Someone still has to be boring on behalf of production.

entries.title: Circuit breaker
entries.devilDefinition: The switch that stops calling a failing model before your retry loop becomes a denial-of-service against yourself.
entries.plainDefinition: A circuit breaker in AI systems is a resilience pattern that temporarily stops sending traffic to a failing model, tool, or dependency after error thresholds are crossed, then probes for recovery.
entries.whyExists: Cascading failures and retry storms are expensive with token APIs and tool backends. Classic reliability patterns moved into LLM stacks.
entries.misuse: Configured so tightly that brief blips permanently degrade UX, or so loosely that they never trip. Breakers without alerts are silent amputations.
entries.practicalMeaning: Set error-rate and latency thresholds, half-open probes, fallbacks, and dashboards. Distinguish client bugs from dependency outages.
entries.example: "The circuit breaker opened on the embedding service and saved the budget from a retry spiral."
askNext: What metrics open and close the breaker?
What user experience runs in the open state?
Who is alerted when it trips?
entries.warningLabel: Failing closed forever is just another outage mode.

entries.title: Citation faithfulness
entries.devilDefinition: Whether the footnote actually supports the sentence, or merely sat nearby while the model improvised.
entries.plainDefinition: Citation faithfulness is the property that a generated claim is actually supported by the sources it cites, not merely that citations exist or that some related document was retrieved.
entries.whyExists: RAG and answer engines learned to decorate text with links. Users then needed a word for 'the link does not prove the claim.'
entries.misuse: Collapsed into 'has citations' or 'retrieval happened.' A confident bibliography can still be ornamental.
entries.practicalMeaning: Evaluate claim-to-span entailment, not link count. Prefer extractive quotes or highlighter-style evidence for high-stakes answers.
entries.example: "Citation faithfulness failed: every source was real, and none said what the summary claimed."
askNext: Can a human find the supporting span for each cited claim?
Do you score citation presence or claim support?
What happens when retrieval is relevant but not sufficient?
entries.warningLabel: References are not evidence until they bind to the claim.

entries.title: Citation grounding
entries.devilDefinition: The old-fashioned idea that a citation should support the sentence standing on it, not merely loiter nearby.
entries.plainDefinition: Citation grounding is the practice of ensuring that cited sources directly support the claims they are attached to in an AI-generated answer.
entries.whyExists: AI systems can produce plausible citations, weakly related sources, or citations that support a neighbouring claim but not the actual sentence. Grounding ties claims back to evidence more rigorously.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to "has citations". A footnote can be decorative, stale, irrelevant, or contradicted by the source it points to.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means checking claim-level support, preserving source passages or metadata, distinguishing direct evidence from background reading, and refusing to let citations become costume jewellery.
entries.example: "The answer had five citations, but citation grounding found that only one supported the revenue claim and the others were standing around looking official."
askNext: Which exact claim does this citation support?
Is the source primary, secondary, or merely convenient?
Can the cited material be inspected without trusting the model's summary?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Making citations carry the claim, not decorate it.

entries.body: A citation is not a moral alibi. It has one job: support the sentence.

entries.title: Clanker
entries.devilDefinition: A slur for machines used by people who want to heckle the future without writing a labour policy.
entries.plainDefinition: "Clanker" is a derogatory slang term for robots and AI systems, especially chatbots or automations that feel intrusive, fake-human, or threatening to human work.
entries.whyExists: Because people wanted a short insult for the new class of machines now appearing in customer support, content feeds, creative tools, and workplace software. It gives irritation, labour anxiety, and anti-bot sentiment one noisy noun.
entries.misuse: By using it as if all automation is the same, or by pretending the joke is harmless when it is clearly borrowing the theatre and cadence of real slurs. It is most useful as a signal of resentment toward a technology category, not as an excuse to rehearse bigotry with metallic props.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means "I am talking about an AI system I find annoying, dehumanising, low-quality, job-threatening, or impossible to avoid", whether that system is a chatbot, a voice menu, a content engine, or a delivery robot.
entries.example: "The bank replaced half its support flow with a clanker, then acted surprised when customers started talking like science-fiction union organisers."
askNext: What exactly is being criticised here: job replacement, bad output, fake-human interface design, or automation in general?
Is the insult aimed at the technology, the company deploying it, or the labour decision behind it?
Does using the term clarify the complaint, or merely make it feel more theatrical?
entries.warningLabel: Use carefully. Much of the joke works by echoing the form of real slurs.
entries.note: The word predates the current AI boom in science-fiction fandom, but its recent spread is tied to social-media backlash against chatbots, robots, and machine-made content.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A catch-all insult for the machine or AI interface someone blames for replacing judgement, taste, or human contact.

entries.body: What matters about clanker is not whether the machine has feelings. It is that the insult reveals a public mood: people increasingly experience AI not as abstract progress but as an unwanted substitute for service, attention, and paid work.

entries.title: Claude
entries.devilDefinition: A name that can mean the assistant, the model family, or the vendor experience, depending on how allergic the speaker is to precision.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude is Anthropic's brand for its assistant products and model family. Depending on context, someone may mean the consumer or enterprise interface, the API-accessible model, or the broader branded experience around those models.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because users often compare "Claude" with "ChatGPT" as products, while engineers compare Claude models with other model APIs. Those are related conversations, but they are not the same conversation.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if every Claude experience is identical. In reality, the model version, tooling, permissions, context handling, and interface all matter. A great experience in one context does not imply the same result in another.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means either Anthropic's assistant interface or a specific model used through the Anthropic API. If the performance discussion is serious, the model version, prompt structure, and tool environment matter more than the brand name alone.
entries.example: "Someone said Claude was better for the task. After ten minutes, it became clear they meant the Claude app with artifacts, not the API model under the constraints our product actually had."
askNext: Do you mean the Claude app, a specific Claude model, or the overall vendor experience?
Which version or model family are you comparing?
Was the comparison done in the same tool, prompt, and data environment?
entries.warningLabel: May refer to a product, a model family, or a user experience. Ask which.
entries.note: In casual conversation, "Claude" often stands in for the overall assistant experience. In technical conversation, it should be pinned to a specific model or interface context. Sonnet is best treated as one Claude model tier, not as a separate company or product category.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A branded Anthropic experience whose exact meaning depends on whether you are talking about the app, the API, or the model family.

entries.body: Brand names are sticky. Precision is a choice. Make it.

entries.title: Claude Code
entries.devilDefinition: A terminal agent for developers who would like to automate the typing without surrendering the blame.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude Code is Anthropic's terminal-oriented coding agent, designed to inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, and assist with multi-step software tasks from the command line.
entries.whyExists: It exists because coding work is not just completion inside an editor. It is shell commands, repo spelunking, test runs, patching, retries, and the general administrative burden of turning intent into a working diff.
entries.misuse: It is oversold as if a terminal agent has somehow escaped the need for software judgement. Faster patch generation is real. So are bad assumptions, missing context, flaky tests, and the delightful persistence of undefined behaviour.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means quicker navigation of unfamiliar codebases, cheaper first drafts of refactors, and more pressure on humans to review command effects instead of admiring how confidently the machine found `package.json`.
entries.example: "Claude Code saved an hour of grep and patching, then presented the team with the ancient obligation to read the diff."
askNext: What can it read, edit, and execute in this environment?
Did it reduce real engineering effort or just accelerate the production of plausible changes?
Who owns verification once the agent has finished being certain?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A useful repo operator that shortens the boring parts while raising the value of disciplined review.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An agentic shell workflow that helps with real software tasks, provided the human stays in charge of correctness.

entries.note: The key distinction is not whether it can edit code. Many tools can. The distinction is that it lives comfortably in the command line where engineering work tends to become consequential.
entries.body: The terminal has always been powerful. The novelty is that it can now misunderstand your codebase in full colour.

Claude Code Anthropic Claude Code Claude coding agent Claude CLI Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Model usage coding terminal developer tools automation A terminal agent for developers who would like to automate the typing without surrendering the blame.
 Claude Code is Anthropic's terminal-oriented coding agent, designed to inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, and assist with multi-step software tasks from the command line.
 It exists because coding work is not just completion inside an editor. It is shell commands, repo spelunking, test runs, patching, retries, and the general administrative burden of turning intent into a working diff.
 It is oversold as if a terminal agent has somehow escaped the need for software judgement. Faster patch generation is real. So are bad assumptions, missing context, flaky tests, and the delightful persistence of undefined behaviour.
 In practice it means quicker navigation of unfamiliar codebases, cheaper first drafts of refactors, and more pressure on humans to review command effects instead of admiring how confidently the machine found `package.json`.
 "Claude Code saved an hour of grep and patching, then presented the team with the ancient obligation to read the diff."
 What can it read, edit, and execute in this environment? Did it reduce real engineering effort or just accelerate the production of plausible changes? Who owns verification once the agent has finished being certain? Terminal agent CLI workflow Code review The key distinction is not whether it can edit code. Many tools can. The distinction is that it lives comfortably in the command line where engineering work tends to become consequential.
 Anthropic Terminal coding agent

entries.title: Claude Design
entries.devilDefinition: A design tool for people who would prefer not to admit that the first wireframe was previously a paragraph.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude Design is Anthropic Labs' conversational product for generating and refining designs, prototypes, slides, one-pagers, and other visual work from prompts, files, and codebases.
entries.whyExists: The idea-to-mockup gap is still slow, expensive, and socially awkward for people without design training. Claude Design exists to turn rough intent into something visual fast enough that the meeting can continue pretending it was always headed somewhere.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as if design has been reduced to prompt fluency plus a brand kit. Fast first drafts are real. So are hierarchy, judgement, interaction sense, and the quiet labour of making a design feel intentional rather than merely complete.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means quick prototype generation, design-system ingestion, export to formats normal companies still use, and a cleaner handoff path into tools like Claude Code or Canva when the rough idea has to become a real asset.
entries.example: "Claude Design got the pitch deck from vague ambition to usable draft before anyone had time to open Figma and look disappointed."
askNext: Is this replacing real design work, or just accelerating the first ugly mile?
How accurate is the imported design system once a real team starts touching the output?
Does the value come from visual quality, or from shortening the gap between idea and review?
entries.warningLabel: Useful for first drafts. Still not a substitute for taste.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to materialise the product vision before the team has time to object properly.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fast visual drafting layer that shortens concept work and leaves the final taste test to humans.

entries.note: Claude Design is most interesting not as a drawing tool but as a sign that conversational interfaces are moving further into software once thought too visual to reduce to chat.
entries.body: The novelty is not that the model can make slides. The novelty is that more of the design stack now begins with description instead of software.

entries.title: Claude Mythos Preview
entries.devilDefinition: The restricted model whose system card suggests that ordinary coding benchmarks have left the building and taken the comfort with them.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude Mythos Preview is Anthropic's unreleased research-preview model for advanced coding and defensive security work, currently made available through Project Glasswing and related gated access programmes.
entries.whyExists: Frontier labs want models that can reason through large codebases, discover vulnerabilities, and handle longer-horizon technical work. Once those abilities start compounding, a new model tier stops sounding like routine product cadence and starts sounding like a governance memo.
entries.misuse: It gets invoked as either a general-purpose talisman for cyber supremacy or a theatrical sign that all previous model comparisons no longer matter. The real question is more specific: what does it do better, under what access controls, and for whom?
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a tightly managed preview, a great deal of security rhetoric, and a model whose existence shifts procurement, policy, and release-management conversations before most developers can even touch it.
entries.example: "Claude Mythos Preview entered the meeting less as a product and more as an argument about how much capability should arrive behind a velvet rope."
askNext: What tasks is Mythos actually outperforming existing models on?
Are the safety claims about the model, the access controls, or both?
Is the term being used to describe a real tool, or just to borrow frontier aura for adjacent products?
entries.warningLabel: A frontier preview is not the same thing as broad deployment, though people often speak as if it were.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A model name that doubles as a warning label for the next round of cyber capability debates.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A gated Anthropic model whose coding strength is being treated as operationally consequential.

entries.body: Once a model's launch copy begins to sound like critical infrastructure planning, the nomenclature has already stopped being a consumer matter.

entries.title: CLI agent
entries.devilDefinition: An agent that lives in the terminal so developers can feel productive while something semi-supervised runs shell commands.
entries.plainDefinition: A CLI agent is a coding or operations agent whose primary interface is the command line, using shell, files, and git rather than (or in addition to) an IDE chat panel.
entries.whyExists: Much real work is terminal-native. CLI agents fit scripts, SSH sessions, and developer habits better than browser chat.
entries.misuse: Given broad shell access without sandboxing. Terminal convenience is also blast radius.
entries.practicalMeaning: Constrain working directories, require approvals for destructive commands, log every shell invocation, and prefer ephemeral environments.
entries.example: "The CLI agent refactored the service and also reformatted the infrastructure repo for style."
askNext: What commands are allowlisted?
Is the working tree isolated?
How are secrets kept out of command history and logs?
entries.warningLabel: Shell access is power; power needs policy.

entries.title: Cline
entries.devilDefinition: The coding agent for people who want ambitious automation but still prefer their terminal to ask before rearranging the furniture.
entries.plainDefinition: Cline is an open-source coding agent for the IDE that can inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, browse documentation, and use models from multiple providers with human approval in the loop.
entries.whyExists: Developers want more than autocomplete. They want a tool that can traverse a project, propose changes, run commands, and keep enough conversational context to be useful across an actual task rather than a decorative snippet.
entries.misuse: It gets presented as if open tooling has solved software engineering rather than making supervised repo operations faster. The agent can be genuinely effective, but the quality still depends on the prompt, the repo, the model behind it, and whether anyone serious reviews the diff.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Cline usually means a chat-driven repo operator with edit, terminal, and browser permissions, sitting somewhere between pair programmer, shell assistant, and very fast intern with a credit line.
entries.example: "Cline fixed the scaffolding problem admirably and then reminded everyone that merge conflicts remain a humanist discipline."
askNext: Which model and permission settings are actually driving the session?
What can it execute automatically, and what requires approval?
Is the gain coming from better reasoning, better tooling, or simply fewer manual keystrokes?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: An open coding agent that can do real repo work if you trust it carefully and review it properly.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful IDE-side agent whose competence depends as much on permissions and review discipline as on the model itself.

entries.note: The name increasingly refers to a whole style of open coding tool, not only one repository.
entries.body: Open coding agents are appealing for the same reason open kitchens are: visibility creates confidence right up to the point something catches fire.

entries.title: Closed Weight
entries.devilDefinition: The arrangement where the model may transform your business, but heaven forbid you inspect the artefact doing the transforming.
entries.plainDefinition: A closed-weight model is one whose trained parameters are not publicly released, with access usually limited to an API, a hosted product, or selected partnerships.
entries.whyExists: Companies keep weights closed to control distribution, monetise access, manage abuse, protect training advantages, and avoid turning tomorrow's margin into today's torrent.
entries.misuse: The term gets stretched when "closed weight" is treated as a synonym for frontier quality, safety, or inevitability. It names an access model, not a guarantee of technical superiority or responsible deployment.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means the vendor controls the pricing, rate limits, updates, logging surface, and deployment geography. If the model improves, degrades, disappears, or becomes more expensive, your roadmap receives the news second-hand.
entries.example: "They chose the closed-weight model for its performance and then discovered that strategic dependency was the polite name for the invoice."
askNext: What happens to our product if this provider changes terms, latency, or availability?
Are we buying superior capability, or merely accepting deeper vendor dependence?
Which compliance or privacy requirements become harder when the model only lives behind someone else's service?
entries.warningLabel: Control for the vendor is not the same thing as certainty for the customer.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: A premium model accessible through a relationship rather than a download.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The vendor keeps the model artefact, and you rent access to the behaviour.

entries.body: Open weight gave the industry one useful distinction. Closed weight merely completes the pair and reveals where the leverage sits.

entries.title: Cloud AI platform

entries.devilDefinition: The company that looked at CDN, DNS, edge compute, storage, security, databases, AI inference, agents, and developer tooling and concluded, quite reasonably, that it would like all of those revenues at once.
entries.plainDefinition: Cloudflare is an infrastructure and developer-platform company spanning networking, security, CDN, DNS, storage, databases, and edge/serverless compute. In developer and AI conversations, it most often means Workers, Pages, R2, Durable Objects, AI bindings, and a broader pitch built around global edge execution, integrated platform primitives, and anti-lock-in rhetoric.
entries.whyExists: Because modern AI and web applications increasingly want three things at once: global distribution, programmable infrastructure, and fewer reasons to stitch together six vendors before lunch.
entries.misuse: By treating Cloudflare as either just a CDN or as a universal answer to every hosting argument. It is neither. It is a broad platform with real strengths in global distribution, integrated services, and cost posture, plus the usual trade-offs that come with choosing any large platform with strong opinions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shipping frontends, Workers, APIs, automations, and edge-adjacent AI systems onto Cloudflare's network, often with R2, KV, D1, Durable Objects, or AI services close by. It also increasingly means buying into Cloudflare's argument that developers should not have to choose between framework convenience and platform portability.
entries.example: "They said they had moved to Cloudflare for edge performance. The fuller sentence was that they also wanted simpler global deployment, fewer egress anxieties, and less patience for framework rent."
askNext: Are we choosing Cloudflare for network reach, Workers, storage economics, security, or all of the above?
Does the workload actually benefit from edge execution, or are we just flattering the architecture?
Which Cloudflare primitives are we committing to: Workers, R2, KV, Durable Objects, D1, Pages, or AI bindings?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: Global performance, lower infra drama, and a chance to tell investors we chose the anti-lock-in side.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A broad programmable edge platform with strong primitives, sharp edges of its own, and a habit of expanding into every adjacent layer.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A serious alternative to framework-native hosting when you care about edge distribution, integrated infra services, and keeping one eye on cost structure.

entries.note: The latest public flare-up I could verify as of March 20, 2026 centres on Cloudflare's early-March 2026 vinext announcement: a Vite-based reimplementation of the Next.js API surface aimed first at Cloudflare Workers. Cloudflare frames this as portability and developer choice. Vercel frames the surrounding choice very differently.
entries.body:
Cloudflare's current posture in the developer market is not subtle. It wants to be seen not merely as the place traffic passes through, but as the place modern apps, agents, and AI workloads actually live.

That matters for the Vercel rivalry. The argument is no longer just "whose hosting is faster?" It is "who gets to define the natural home of the modern full-stack app?"

entries.title: Code review
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Code review names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Claude Code and Cursor needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once code review entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: CodeRabbit
entries.devilDefinition: A reviewer that never gets tired, never gets promoted, and occasionally discovers how lonely correctness can be in a pull request.
entries.plainDefinition: CodeRabbit is an AI code review platform that reviews pull requests and related engineering work, giving automated summaries, findings, suggestions, and workflow assistance across development tools.
entries.whyExists: It exists because AI-assisted coding increases the volume and speed of changes, while human review capacity remains stubbornly attached to calendars, context switches, and fatigue.
entries.misuse: It gets misused when teams treat AI review as a replacement for ownership. Automated comments can catch bugs and improve hygiene, but they can also add noise, miss product intent, and create the dangerous feeling that someone else has looked properly.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice CodeRabbit means another review layer on the pull request: useful for catching obvious defects, explaining diffs, nudging standards, and giving humans a better starting point rather than an excuse to stop reading.
entries.example: "CodeRabbit caught the missing null check, while the human reviewer still had to notice that the feature solved the wrong problem."
askNext: Which classes of issues does the AI reviewer reliably catch in this repo?
How does the team separate useful findings from review theatre?
Who owns the final merge decision after automated review has spoken?
entries.warningLabel: Automated review is a second set of eyes, not a transfer of accountability.

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: A way to keep review from drowning as AI-generated changes arrive faster.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Useful automated review pressure, provided humans still own judgement, product fit, and the merge button.

entries.body: The best AI reviewer is not the one that sounds most senior. It is the one that reliably reduces the number of things a senior engineer has to rediscover.

entries.title: Codex
entries.devilDefinition: The coding agent that survived being merged into ChatGPT by keeping the work, the CLI, and—if requested—the old logo.
entries.plainDefinition: Codex is OpenAI's coding agent and coding workflow. It reads, modifies, and runs code through the ChatGPT desktop app, Codex CLI, IDE extension, web and cloud surfaces; the desktop application may now be named ChatGPT, but Codex remains the name for the coding experience rather than the application as a whole.
entries.whyExists: Engineering tasks require repository context, shell and editor tools, isolated environments, tests, diffs, and review—not only generated code. Codex packages those capabilities into local pairing and delegated cloud workflows while the broader ChatGPT product handles additional kinds of work.
entries.misuse: The name is used interchangeably for the desktop app, a model, the CLI, cloud tasks, and autonomous software engineering. Current branding adds another trap: "Codex is now ChatGPT" describes the app migration, not the disappearance of Codex as a coding agent or every Codex surface.
entries.practicalMeaning: Teams should name the surface, model, environment, permissions, and review path. A Codex task may run locally in a repository or in a hosted environment, and its value depends on scoped access, validation, and reviewable evidence rather than the brand printed on the Dock.
entries.example: "Codex remained the coding agent after the app became ChatGPT, proving that product names can merge faster than responsibility boundaries."
askNext: Which Codex surface is in use: ChatGPT desktop, CLI, IDE, web, or cloud?
What repository, environment, and tool permissions does the task have?
Which model and human review path sit behind the Codex label here?
entries.warningLabel: Coding experience, not a precise model or app identifier.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: OpenAI's coding agent across several surfaces, including the newly branded ChatGPT desktop app.

entries.note: OpenAI's current material calls the main desktop product the ChatGPT desktop app while continuing to document Codex CLI, the Codex IDE extension, Codex cloud, and Codex coding workflows. The desktop brand changed; the coding-agent distinction remains useful.
entries.body: The agent-loop diagram remains the useful mental model: inspect the repository, act with tools, observe the result, validate, and continue under review. The Dock icon does not alter the loop.

entries.title: Coding Agent
entries.devilDefinition: The software assistant that turned "write the code" into the easy part and "own the consequences" into a fresh management discipline.
entries.plainDefinition: A coding agent is an AI system designed to inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, use documentation or browsers, and carry out multi-step software tasks with some degree of autonomy.
entries.whyExists: Autocomplete was helpful, but it still left humans doing the orchestration: finding the right files, running the tests, checking the docs, fixing the follow-on errors, and keeping the task in memory. Coding agents exist because developers wanted delegation, not merely more enthusiastic tab completion.
entries.misuse: It gets used as proof that software engineering has become a prompt-shaped clerical job. In reality the category is only impressive when the surrounding repo is legible, the tests say something useful, and an actual engineer is still responsible for the final judgement.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means bounded delegation: background code changes, refactors, bug fixes, test runs, and repo chores completed faster than a person would do them by hand, followed by the entirely traditional need for review, context, and rollback plans.
entries.example: "The coding agent cleared three tedious tickets before lunch and generated a fourth, more philosophical ticket in CI."
askNext: What permissions, tools, and feedback loops does the agent actually have?
Which tasks are reliable enough to delegate without constant rescue?
Who reviews the changes when the agent is fast, plausible, and slightly wrong?
entries.warningLabel: A real category of tool, not a general exemption from code review.

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: Software that appears to convert backlog into output without requiring more headcount.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A repo-facing agent that can save serious time if the task framing and review discipline remain adult.

entries.note: The term names a product category, not a uniform capability. Some coding agents are glorified copilots with a task button. Some can genuinely execute long chains of actions.
entries.body: The category is useful for the same reason junior engineers are useful: not because they remove supervision, but because the right work can finally move in parallel.

entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Devin often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Cognition came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: Compute diplomacy
entries.devilDefinition: Foreign policy, except the handshake is photographed in front of a server rack.
entries.plainDefinition: Compute diplomacy is the negotiation of AI compute access through national policy, chip export controls, data-centre investment, cloud regions, strategic partnerships, subsidies, and cross-border infrastructure deals.
entries.whyExists: It exists because AI capacity has become economic leverage and statecraft. Governments, vendors, and investors now treat chips, cloud regions, energy supply, and local hosting as instruments of influence as well as infrastructure.
entries.misuse: It is misused when a diplomatic announcement is presented as actual capability. A memorandum, investment pledge, or preferred-vendor relationship may improve access, but it can also deepen dependence while giving everyone a better podium.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means asking who gets the chips, who owns the facility, which laws apply, which export controls bite, who trains the workforce, where the profits flow, and what happens when the partnership becomes politically inconvenient.
entries.example: "The compute diplomacy tour produced three flags, two cloud regions, and one carefully unmentioned dependency on imported accelerators."
askNext: What concrete compute capacity, skills, and operating control will exist after the announcement?
Which dependencies are reduced, and which are merely moved to a more photogenic column?
Who benefits commercially and strategically from the partnership?
entries.warningLabel: A bilateral announcement is not a cluster.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: AI compute access has become a subject of diplomacy, trade, and strategic dependence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A negotiation over who gets scarce infrastructure and who gets to call that sovereignty.

entries.body: Compute diplomacy is often sincere. It is also where dependence learns to smile for the official photograph.

entries.title: Compute governance
entries.devilDefinition: Rules for who may burn the GPUs, written after someone already burned them on a side project that looked like research.
entries.plainDefinition: Compute governance is the set of policies, quotas, approvals, and audits controlling access to training and inference compute across teams, projects, and risk tiers.
entries.whyExists: Accelerators are scarce and expensive, and high-capability runs can be dual-use. Organisations needed controls beyond informal Slack permissions.
entries.misuse: Reduced to a purchase order process, or so heavy that shadow compute appears on personal clouds.
entries.practicalMeaning: Combine quotas, chargeback, purpose binding, logging, and exception paths. Align governance with safety evals for high-risk training jobs.
entries.example: "Compute governance finally noticed the weekend cluster that nobody owned."
askNext: Who can approve large training runs, and on what criteria?
Are quotas enforced technically or only socially?
How are near-misses and policy exceptions recorded?
entries.warningLabel: Ungoverned compute becomes ungoverned capability.

entries.title: Computer Use
entries.devilDefinition: The feature where a model is allowed to click around software with the confidence of a junior employee and the supervision requirements of a forklift.
entries.plainDefinition: "Computer use" refers to AI systems that can operate graphical user interfaces by observing screens, deciding actions, and interacting through clicks, typing, scrolling, or other desktop and browser controls.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many real workflows still live inside user interfaces rather than neat APIs. If a system can only answer questions but cannot operate the tools where work actually happens, its usefulness in operations remains politely theoretical.
entries.misuse: It is oversold as if seeing a screen and moving a cursor were the same as understanding business process, edge cases, or risk. A demo that closes one support ticket can be described as labour disruption by lunch.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means brittle automation with vision, planning, retries, guardrails, and a strong need for scoped permissions. It can save time on repetitive interface work, provided someone is willing to own the mistakes made at machine speed.
entries.example: "Their computer use agent could navigate the vendor portal beautifully, right up to the point a cookie banner became a strategic surprise."
askNext: Which parts of the workflow still require human approval or exception handling?
How does the system recover when the UI changes or a modal appears?
Why is GUI automation the right choice instead of integrating an API?
entries.warningLabel: Useful, but only if the workflow can survive the UI misbehaving.

translations.label: demo meaning
translations.text: Watch the model use software like a person, and please do not ask what happens on the twelfth click.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Vision-guided UI automation that still depends on tight boundaries and patient operators.

entries.note: The interesting question is not whether the model can use a computer. It is whether the surrounding workflow is structured enough for that to be safe.
entries.body: Computer use is often a confession that the software stack was never designed to cooperate cleanly in the first place.

entries.title: Computer-use agent
entries.devilDefinition: An agent that can operate the screen, which is a charming way to give ambiguity a mouse cursor.
entries.plainDefinition: A computer-use agent is an AI agent that observes and interacts with graphical computer interfaces by reading screens, clicking, typing, dragging, and operating applications.
entries.whyExists: Many workflows still live in desktop apps, web pages, and mixed interfaces that do not expose clean APIs. Computer-use agents try to automate those workflows through the same surface a person uses.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for general competence. GUI control adds perception errors, timing issues, hidden state, permission prompts, irreversible clicks, and a remarkable number of ways to be confidently one pixel wrong.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means screen grounding, action traces, confirmation gates, rollback plans, credential handling, and conservative rules around actions that change data or spend money.
entries.example: "The computer-use agent found the button and clicked it, which was impressive until everyone remembered the button said delete."
askNext: Which actions can the agent take without confirmation?
How does it identify the UI element it is acting on?
What records exist when the screen state changes?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An agent operating a graphical interface, where perception errors can become real actions.

entries.body: The desktop is not an API. It is an API wearing theatre makeup and asking to be clicked.

entries.title: Confidential inference
entries.devilDefinition: Privacy theatre for the part where the model has already been invited into the room.
entries.plainDefinition: Confidential inference is inference designed so prompts, retrieved context, outputs, and sometimes model weights are protected from unauthorised visibility while the model runs, often through confidential computing, tenant isolation, encryption, access controls, and audit logs.
entries.whyExists: It exists because sensitive AI workloads cannot be judged only by whether the vendor promises not to train on the data. Regulated, proprietary, personal, and legally privileged inputs need a clearer account of who can see what during execution.
entries.misuse: It is misused when one protected layer is treated as total secrecy. Encrypted transport, a private endpoint, or a trusted execution environment does not automatically solve logging, telemetry, retrieval leakage, administrator access, key custody, output handling, or side-channel risk.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means writing down the threat model, mapping which parties can see prompts and outputs, checking what lands in logs or analytics, reviewing retention, proving isolation, and deciding whether the remaining visibility is acceptable rather than merely renamed.
entries.example: "The hospital approved confidential inference only after the vendor explained which staff, operators, logs, and support tools could still touch the patient prompts."
askNext: Which parties are prevented from seeing prompts, context, outputs, and model weights?
What remains visible through logs, telemetry, support tooling, retrieval systems, or administrator access?
Has the confidentiality claim been tested against a specific threat model?
entries.warningLabel: Confidential does not mean nobody can see anything.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Inference with a defined visibility boundary around sensitive inputs and outputs.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A privacy claim that needs a threat model before anyone should relax.

entries.body: Confidential inference is useful precisely because it is narrow. The useful question is not whether the word sounds reassuring, but which eyes, systems, logs, and emergency support paths it actually excludes.

entries.title: Confused deputy
entries.devilDefinition: A privileged helper tricked into abusing its power for someone who should not have it—classic security, now starring agents and tools.
entries.plainDefinition: A confused deputy is a program with authority that is misled into exercising that authority on behalf of an attacker; in AI systems this often involves tools, agents, or connectors with broad credentials.
entries.whyExists: Agents with tools are deputies. Prompt injection and hostile content try to confuse them into misusing privileges.
entries.misuse: Mentioned only as trivia. It is a design threat model for every high-privilege integration.
entries.practicalMeaning: Minimise authority, bind actions to user intent with confirmations, separate trusts per tool, and never let untrusted content freely choose privileged operations.
entries.example: "The confused deputy fetched the secret because the email said it was urgent and used polite words."
askNext: What authority does each tool identity hold?
Can untrusted content trigger privileged tools?
Where is user confirmation mandatory?
entries.warningLabel: Politeness in a prompt is not authorisation.

entries.title: Content credentials
entries.devilDefinition: Metadata that tries to remember where media came from, before the internet treats memory as an optional accessory.
entries.plainDefinition: Content credentials are metadata or attached records that describe a piece of content's origin, creation tools, edits, authorship signals, or provenance history.
entries.whyExists: As AI-generated and edited media spread, creators, platforms, and audiences need better ways to understand how content was made and modified.
entries.misuse: They get treated as a universal authenticity layer. Credentials can be absent, incomplete, stripped, tampered with, or simply irrelevant to the claim someone is making about the content.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means provenance metadata can help with disclosure and trust decisions, but only when platforms preserve it, users understand it, and policies account for its failure modes.
entries.example: "The image had content credentials showing an AI edit, which answered one question and left seventeen others behaving normally."
askNext: What information do the credentials actually contain?
Can the credentials survive export, compression, reposting, or editing?
What decision are we making from this metadata?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Origin and edit metadata that helps, provided it has not been stripped, ignored, or overtrusted.

entries.body: Credentials are useful precisely because the content itself often arrives looking innocent.

entries.title: Context budget
entries.devilDefinition: The moment long context stops sounding luxurious and starts behaving like a ration book.
entries.plainDefinition: A context budget is the practical limit on how much prompt space and generated output a workflow can afford to allocate across instructions, history, retrieved material, tool schemas, and the answer itself.
entries.whyExists: Even large context windows are finite, expensive, and slow enough to matter. Teams need a way to think about context as something allocated and traded off, not as an infinitely expandable cupboard.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a posh synonym for context window. The window is the hard ceiling. The budget is the editorial and financial discipline inside that ceiling. Confusing the two is how prompts become sprawling, expensive, and still oddly unhelpful.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means deciding how much space belongs to system rules, chat history, retrieved evidence, examples, tool definitions, and the reply. It also means accepting that every extra token admitted to the room either evicts something else or enlarges the bill.
entries.example: "The feature improved once they treated context as a budget to be allocated rather than a landfill for every document the company had ever regretted."
askNext: Which tokens in this workflow are earning their place and which are merely present?
What part of the context can be summarised, retrieved later, or cached instead?
How much room must be reserved for tool arguments and the final answer?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A fixed allowance for what gets to sit in the model's immediate attention.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Prompt assembly forced to admit that relevance, latency, and spend are all in the same argument.

entries.body: Teams that ignore the context budget usually rediscover it as latency, truncation, or a quietly heroic cloud invoice.

entries.title: Context compaction
entries.devilDefinition: Turning a long conversation into a smaller one and hoping the important bits survived the lift.
entries.plainDefinition: Context compaction is the process of summarising, compressing, or otherwise reducing accumulated working context so an AI session can continue within a context window or operational budget.
entries.whyExists: Long-running agents and assistants accumulate instructions, tool results, decisions, and user preferences. Compaction keeps the session usable when the raw transcript would exceed limits or become too costly to carry forward.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as lossless memory. It is not. Compaction preserves a judgement about what mattered, and that judgement can omit caveats, uncertainty, failed attempts, and inconvenient constraints.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means deciding what to keep, what to discard, how to mark uncertainty, and how to recover original evidence when the compacted version starts behaving like an unreliable witness.
entries.example: "The context compaction kept the deployment command and forgot why it was forbidden, which is how efficiency becomes a postmortem heading."
askNext: What information is deliberately preserved during compaction?
Can the system recover the original transcript or evidence?
How are uncertainties and unresolved blockers carried forward?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A lossy summary of working state, useful only if everyone remembers it is lossy.

entries.body: Compaction is where continuity becomes editorial. Treat the editor as fallible.
