entries.title: Context editing
entries.devilDefinition: The tasteful removal of yesterday's tool output before today's agent mistakes it for scripture.
entries.plainDefinition: Context editing selectively removes or replaces content in a growing conversation history before the next model call. Current implementations can clear old tool results or thinking blocks while preserving more recent information needed for continuity.
entries.whyExists: Long-running agents accumulate file contents, search results, tool traces, and reasoning state until cost rises or the context limit approaches. Curating stale material can preserve useful working space without restarting the entire session.
entries.misuse: It is sold as memory management while sometimes functioning as deletion with reassuring typography. Clearing the wrong evidence can break continuity, hide an earlier constraint, invalidate prompt caches, or leave the model confidently reasoning from the placeholder.
entries.practicalMeaning: Define thresholds and retention rules, clear older low-value blocks, and keep enough recent state for the task. Measure task quality, token savings, cache invalidation, and whether audit logs retain what the model no longer sees.
entries.example: "Context editing removed the first twelve search results and retained the decision they had supported, which was either efficient or ominous depending on the audit trail."
askNext: What content is eligible for clearing, and what must remain immutable?
How does editing affect prompt caching, cost, and reproducibility?
Can operators inspect the removed content after the model can no longer see it?
entries.warningLabel: A shorter context can also be a shorter memory.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Remove stale context deliberately, preserve the audit trail elsewhere, and verify that the agent still remembers the instruction that mattered.

entries.body: Every long-running conversation eventually needs an editor. The difficult part is ensuring the editor has better judgement than the accumulation.
entries.searchText:
Context editing  Retrieval and memory Agents and workflows context memory agents cost The tasteful removal of yesterday's tool output before today's agent mistakes it for scripture.
 Context editing selectively removes or replaces content in a growing conversation history before the next model call. Current implementations can clear old tool results or thinking blocks while preserving more recent information needed for continuity.
 Long-running agents accumulate file contents, search results, tool traces, and reasoning state until cost rises or the context limit approaches. Curating stale material can preserve useful working space without restarting the entire session.
 It is sold as memory management while sometimes functioning as deletion with reassuring typography. Clearing the wrong evidence can break continuity, hide an earlier constraint, invalidate prompt caches, or leave the model confidently reasoning from the placeholder.
 Define thresholds and retention rules, clear older low-value blocks, and keep enough recent state for the task. Measure task quality, token savings, cache invalidation, and whether audit logs retain what the model no longer sees.
 "Context editing removed the first twelve search results and retained the decision they had supported, which was either efficient or ominous depending on the audit trail."
 What content is eligible for clearing, and what must remain immutable? How does editing affect prompt caching, cost, and reproducibility? Can operators inspect the removed content after the model can no longer see it? Tool result clearing Thinking block clearing  Claude Platform

entries.title: Context Engineering
entries.devilDefinition: The rebranding of prompt fiddling once people realised the prompt had quietly become half the product.
entries.plainDefinition: "Context engineering" is the practice of deciding what instructions, tools, retrieved information, memory, examples, and constraints a model should receive so it can perform a task reliably.
entries.whyExists: It exists because model quality depends heavily on what reaches the context window and in what order. Once systems grew beyond a single prompt box, teams needed a name for the work of shaping that information flow deliberately.
entries.misuse: It gets inflated into a grand discipline whenever someone wants ordinary application design to sound frontier and proprietary. Sometimes it really is nuanced systems work. Sometimes it is just a long string and a lucky example.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means prompt templates, retrieval rules, truncation policies, memory selection, tool schemas, system instructions, and a permanent struggle over what the model should see before it starts improvising.
entries.example: "The context engineering improved dramatically once they stopped stuffing every policy document into the prompt and calling it judgment."
askNext: What information earns its place in the context, and what is merely comforting to the team?
How do you decide what gets retrieved, summarised, or dropped?
Which failures are caused by the model, and which are caused by bad context assembly?
entries.warningLabel: Often a real discipline wearing a slightly theatrical name.

translations.label: conference meaning
translations.text: We have moved beyond prompt engineering and would like you to notice.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Designing the information bundle that shapes model behaviour before the first token appears.

entries.body: The term is useful when it names concrete design choices. It becomes perfume when it is used to imply that context alone can rescue a sloppy product.

entries.title: Context Perimeter
entries.devilDefinition: The fence around what the model is allowed to know before someone discovers the fence was drawn in pencil.
entries.plainDefinition: A context perimeter is the boundary around the information that may enter a prompt, retrieval result, memory store, tool output, or agent workspace for a particular task.
entries.whyExists: AI systems assemble answers from many sources: user prompts, documents, memory, tools, policies, and previous turns. The perimeter names the control surface for deciding what belongs inside that assembled working context.
entries.misuse: It is weakened when teams treat context as a bigger bucket rather than a governed boundary. More context can mean more leakage, more poisoning, more stale assumptions, and more authority accidentally handed to the model.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means defining allowed sources, excluding sensitive or irrelevant material, filtering retrieved chunks, separating tenants and roles, pruning stale memory, and logging what crossed the boundary when an answer mattered.
entries.example: "The support agent needed a tighter context perimeter, not another million tokens of customer history and hope."
askNext: Which sources are allowed to enter context for this user, task, and role?
What keeps sensitive, stale, poisoned, or irrelevant material outside the prompt?
Can the system explain what crossed the perimeter for a disputed answer?
entries.warningLabel: A larger context window is not a larger permission model.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The boundary that decides what information may reach the model for this task.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The place where prompt engineering becomes access control, memory hygiene, and audit logging.

entries.body: The context perimeter is where the cheerful phrase "give it more context" starts receiving questions from security.

entries.title: Context Poisoning
entries.devilDefinition: Sneaking bad instructions or bad facts into the system's memory and letting future competence be built on polluted paperwork.
entries.plainDefinition: Context poisoning is a failure mode or attack in which retrieved context, shared memory, cached notes, or prior state gets contaminated with false, hostile, or manipulative content that later steers model behaviour.
entries.whyExists: Agent systems increasingly depend on stored traces, retrieved documents, vector indexes, summaries, and shared working memory. Once those layers become operational dependencies, corrupting them becomes a practical way to corrupt the system.
entries.misuse: The term gets overused for any bad answer produced with too much context. The sharper meaning is persistence: poisoned material survives long enough or appears trusted enough to shape future decisions, not just one unlucky response.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means untrusted instructions in documents, planted false facts in memory stores, contaminated summaries, or cross-agent state drift that causes the model to retrieve the wrong thing with total procedural confidence.
entries.example: "The assistant did not become malicious so much as obedient to a memory layer that had quietly gone feral."
askNext: What memory, retrieval, or shared-state layers can untrusted parties influence?
How do we distinguish verified context from merely available context?
Can poisoned summaries or cached notes persist across sessions or agents?
entries.warningLabel: If the system remembers the wrong thing well, it can fail very consistently.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker or bad input has contaminated the system's working memory so future steps inherit the mistake.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The agent is treating poisoned context as trusted operating truth.

entries.note: The risk becomes nastier when multiple agents or long-lived sessions share context over time.
entries.body: Ordinary software forgets many of its mistakes after the request ends. Agent systems increasingly preserve them for later, which is a less charming habit.

entries.title: Context pruning
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Context pruning names the nearby AI idea that shows up when State Compaction needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once context pruning entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: Context Rot
entries.devilDefinition: The slow corruption of a long AI session as every clarification, exception, and half-fixed mistake is preserved with the reverence of scripture.
entries.plainDefinition: Context rot is the degradation that happens in long-running chats or agent workflows when accumulated instructions, stale assumptions, noisy history, and partial fixes start reducing output quality.
entries.whyExists: People needed a name for the very practical observation that more context is not always better context. Once a session becomes long enough, the model may carry forward the wrong facts with admirable loyalty.
entries.misuse: The phrase gets used to blame the model for every bad answer in a long thread, including cases where the human supplied contradictory instructions, never reset the task, or kept shovelling debris into the prompt like a municipal service.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the session needs pruning, a fresh summary, or a clean restart. For agent systems it often means state management is doing a worse job than the demo admitted.
entries.example: "By the eighth refinement the coding assistant had context rot: it remembered every detour and none of the point."
askNext: Which parts of the current context are still relevant, and which are now archival clutter?
Would a reset plus a clean handover summary improve quality more than adding another paragraph?
Is the problem the model's memory, or our refusal to curate it?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The working context has become too bloated, stale, or contradictory to be useful.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Start a new thread before the old one becomes a constitutional monarchy of bad assumptions.

entries.body: Context rot is what happens when people confuse retention with understanding. A long memory is only helpful if someone occasionally removes the rubbish.

entries.title: Context Stuffing
entries.devilDefinition: Solving a thinking problem by feeding the model more text until latency, cost, and coherence all lodge a complaint.
entries.plainDefinition: Context stuffing is the habit of overloading a model with documents, instructions, examples, and chat history in the hope that sheer volume will compensate for poor retrieval or unclear task design.
entries.whyExists: Because larger context windows make accumulation feel prudent, and because it is often easier to shovel everything in than to decide what actually matters.
entries.misuse: By using the phrase for any long prompt, even when the extra context is relevant and ranked well. The problem is not length alone. It is indiscriminate inclusion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice context stuffing produces slower responses, higher bills, muddier instruction hierarchy, and outputs that feel informed in the broad sense while missing the one paragraph that mattered.
entries.example: "The agent had the entire handbook, six meeting transcripts, three policies, and still managed to ignore the refund rule sitting in perfect plain English."
askNext: Why is each document or instruction block in the context at all?
What retrieval or ranking step could replace bulk inclusion?
Which instructions are being drowned by convenience text?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The team chose bigger prompts over better selection.

entries.body: Context stuffing is one of the first failure modes granted by abundance. Once the window grows large enough, restraint starts looking less like engineering and more like an avoidable meeting.

entries.title: Context Window
entries.devilDefinition: The finite desk space on which the model must juggle your instructions, your documents, your tool outputs, and its own reply without knocking anything important onto the floor.
entries.plainDefinition: The context window is the maximum amount of tokenised input and generated output a model can consider in a single run. Everything the model can directly attend to during that interaction has to fit inside that limit.
entries.whyExists: Models do not have unlimited working memory per request. The context window defines how much information can be included at once, which shapes prompting, retrieval, tool design, and output length.
entries.misuse: It gets marketed as if bigger always means smarter. A larger window can be useful, but it does not automatically produce better reasoning, better retrieval choices, or better prioritisation inside the context. Plenty of systems simply become wrong more expensively at longer lengths.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means brutal trade-offs. System instructions, chat history, retrieved passages, tool schemas, user data, and the answer itself all compete for room. Product teams eventually discover that context is not just a capacity problem but an editorial problem.
entries.example: "The long-context model could technically hold the whole policy manual, but the useful version of the feature still depended on deciding which pages deserved a seat in the window."
askNext: What exactly is consuming the window in this workflow?
Do we need more raw context or better selection of relevant context?
How much room is being reserved for the model's answer or tool arguments?

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Look how much text we can fit.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fixed budget that forces decisions about what deserves to be in the room.

entries.body: Long context is helpful when it reduces the need for awkward chunking or repeated retrieval. It is less helpful when teams use it as permission to stop editing inputs.

entries.title: Context.ai
entries.devilDefinition: A platform for giving agents more of your organisation's actual context, and therefore more of your organisation's actual permissions.
entries.plainDefinition: Context.ai is an AI workspace and agent platform built around connecting company systems, capturing workflow context, and running agents against real tasks. Its current pitch emphasises enterprise deployments, connectors, sandboxes, and controlled execution surfaces rather than a generic chat interface.
entries.whyExists: Because generic models do not know your firm's documents, tools, processes, or standards. Products like Context.ai exist to make agents operate against company-specific context instead of improvising from a prompt and a prayer.
entries.misuse: By talking about "context" as if adding more integrations automatically produces better judgement. More context can improve usefulness. It can also widen the blast radius when tokens, connectors, and OAuth grants are handled with the usual startup optimism.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means connectors, workflow templates, sandboxed runs, eval loops, and an enterprise promise that agents can work across your actual systems rather than merely chat about them. It also now means a public lesson in how broad AI-tool permissions can become someone else's incident report.
entries.example: "They bought Context.ai because 'our knowledge plus our workflows plus our systems' sounded more serious than yet another clever chat window."
askNext: Which Context product are we discussing: the current enterprise platform or the deprecated consumer office suite?
Where do agents run, and who controls the connectors, tokens, and execution boundary?
Are we buying genuine workflow fit, or a prettier place to grant unusually broad permissions?
entries.warningLabel: The word context sounds soft. The permission model usually is not.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A platform that promises your agents will finally understand how your organisation actually works.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An enterprise agent workspace with lots of connectors, execution surfaces, and enough permissions to justify a proper security review.

entries.note: As of April 19-20, 2026, Context.ai says the security incident affected a now-deprecated consumer AI Office Suite released in June 2025, not its current enterprise deployments, which it says are architecturally separate. Vercel's April 20, 2026 bulletin says a compromise of Context.ai was the origin of Vercel's own April 2026 security incident after a Vercel employee used that product and granted broad Google Workspace permissions.
entries.body: When a vendor says it captures context, ask whether it means knowledge, workflow state, permissions, or all three. The security review gets more interesting once the answer is all three.

entries.title: Continued pretraining
entries.devilDefinition: More next-token training on domain data after the general crawl, so the model can learn your jargon before it learns your instructions.
entries.plainDefinition: Continued pretraining (also called domain-adaptive pretraining) further trains a base model with a language-modelling objective on additional corpora before instruction tuning or alignment.
entries.whyExists: General models lack specialised distributional knowledge. Continued pretraining injects domain text without jumping straight to supervised tasks.
entries.misuse: Confused with fine-tuning on Q&A pairs. Different objective, different data needs, different failure modes like catastrophic forgetting.
entries.practicalMeaning: Curate domain corpora carefully, mix with general data if needed, and evaluate both domain gains and general regressions before instruction tuning.
entries.example: "Continued pretraining taught clinical abbreviations and temporarily untaught polite refusals."
askNext: What is the domain corpus licence and quality?
How do you measure forgetting?
Is instruction tuning planned after?
entries.warningLabel: More pretraining is not free specialisation.

translations.label: technical meaning

entries.title: Continuous batching
entries.devilDefinition: The serving trick that lets new requests join a GPU party already in progress, so nobody waits for a polite full batch that never quite fills.
entries.plainDefinition: Continuous batching is an inference-server scheduling technique that dynamically adds and removes requests from a running batch as prompts arrive and generations finish, keeping accelerators busier than static batching allows.
entries.whyExists: Requests do not arrive in neat simultaneous groups, and generations end at different times. Static batches waste capacity waiting for stragglers or empty seats.
entries.misuse: Sold as infinite free throughput. Continuous batching still contends for memory, KV cache, and compute; overpacking raises latency for everyone.
entries.practicalMeaning: Watch batch size, queue time, TTFT, and tokens-per-second under mixed traffic. Tune max concurrent sequences and memory budgets rather than assuming the buzzword alone multiplies capacity.
entries.example: "Continuous batching looked great until every long decode held KV memory hostage."
askNext: What limits concurrency: KV memory, compute, or rate limits?
Are interactive and batch jobs isolated or fighting for the same batch slots?
Which latency percentile regressed when utilisation went up?
entries.warningLabel: Full GPUs are not the same as happy users.

entries.title: Cost per token
entries.devilDefinition: The unit price that makes two APIs look comparable until you notice one charges for your novel-length system prompt every single turn.
entries.plainDefinition: Cost per token is the price charged or estimated for processing or generating a token of text (or multimodal equivalent), usually quoted separately for input and output and used as a rough unit economics measure.
entries.whyExists: Usage-based model APIs needed a billable unit finer than seats or servers. Tokens became the common currency of inference spend.
entries.misuse: Compared across vendors without matching context length, output ratio, caching, batch discounts, or quality. A cheaper token that forces more retries is not cheaper.
entries.practicalMeaning: Model total cost as input tokens, output tokens, cached tokens, tool calls, and failure retries. Track cost per successful task, not only list price per million tokens.
entries.example: "Their cost-per-token looked unbeatable until the agent burned three retries correcting itself."
askNext: Are input and output priced differently, and what is your typical ratio?
Does the quote include cache hits, batch tiers, or only on-demand list price?
What is cost per completed user task after retries and tool calls?
entries.warningLabel: Unit price without workload shape is theatre.

entries.title: Cube
entries.devilDefinition: A semantic layer for people who would prefer their data agent not to invent the revenue definition during the earnings call.
entries.plainDefinition: Cube is an analytics and semantic-layer platform. Its MCP server lets compatible AI clients connect to Cube so users can ask data questions and receive governed analytics context from the coding or assistant environment.
entries.whyExists: It exists because business data is full of definitions, joins, permissions, and metrics that do not belong inside a model's imagination. A semantic layer gives agents a governed way to ask questions against shared business logic.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if connecting a model to analytics automatically creates trustworthy business intelligence. The quality still depends on the semantic model, access controls, freshness, metric definitions, and whether the question deserved a dashboard rather than a paragraph.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Cube means exposing governed metrics and analytics workflows to agents through a controlled interface, so data questions can use established definitions instead of freshly hallucinated SQL.
entries.example: "The Cube MCP server helped the agent answer the revenue question with the company's definition of revenue, a small miracle in analytics politics."
askNext: Which metrics are governed in Cube, and who owns those definitions?
What data can the agent access through MCP, and what is deliberately out of scope?
Does the answer show the query, assumptions, and freshness of the data?
entries.warningLabel: A governed metric is only as good as the governance everyone keeps pretending happened.

translations.label: data meaning
translations.text: Let agents ask questions through shared metric definitions instead of improvising business logic.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A cleaner bridge between AI clients and governed analytics, provided the semantic layer is actually maintained.

 Cube is an analytics and semantic-layer platform. Its MCP server lets compatible AI clients connect to Cube so users can ask data questions and receive governed analytics context from the coding or assistant environment.
 It exists because business data is full of definitions, joins, permissions, and metrics that do not belong inside a model's imagination. A semantic layer gives agents a governed way to ask questions against shared business logic.
 It gets treated as if connecting a model to analytics automatically creates trustworthy business intelligence. The quality still depends on the semantic model, access controls, freshness, metric definitions, and whether the question deserved a dashboard rather than a paragraph.
 In practice Cube means exposing governed metrics and analytics workflows to agents through a controlled interface, so data questions can use established definitions instead of freshly hallucinated SQL.
 "The Cube MCP server helped the agent answer the revenue question with the company's definition of revenue, a small miracle in analytics politics."

entries.title: Cursor
entries.devilDefinition: A coding product that lets developers ship in minutes what used to take hours to understand.
entries.plainDefinition: Cursor is an AI-native code editor built around code generation, codebase search, editing, and task-oriented assistance inside a developer workflow.
entries.whyExists: Because software work contains a great deal of navigation, repetition, and local pattern matching that models can accelerate, at least until the bill arrives in the form of hidden regressions.
entries.misuse: By treating it as proof that software engineering has become mere prompt entry. Fast edits are real. So are context gaps, bad assumptions, and the need for someone to know what good code looks like.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shorter loops on routine code changes, more willingness to touch unfamiliar files, and a fresh need for review discipline because speed arrived before certainty.
entries.example: "Cursor handled the refactor in ten minutes. Understanding whether it should have happened still took the afternoon."
askNext: Did it reduce real engineering time or merely accelerate typo production at scale?
How much of the gain came from codebase retrieval versus raw generation?
Who is reviewing the edits with enough context to reject confident nonsense?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A force multiplier for familiar work and a temptation to move faster than your judgement.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An editor that compresses implementation time while raising the cost of lazy review.

entries.body: The old bottleneck was typing. The new bottleneck is believing your own velocity.

entries.title: Daliso Ngoma
entries.devilDefinition: The founder-operator entry where the dictionary briefly admits that some of its scepticism has an author, a shop floor, and a backlog.
entries.plainDefinition: Daliso Ngoma is the founder and operator behind African Technopreneurs and related software, media, and commerce projects. In this dictionary, the name is useful as a local shorthand for building across AI, XR, publishing, and practical technology adoption in African markets.
entries.whyExists: Because AI vocabulary is not only made by labs and platform companies. It is also shaped by people who have to explain, sell, install, support, document, and turn new tools into something useful in specific markets.
entries.misuse: By turning the name into founder mythology. The better move is to ask which concrete surface is being discussed: African Techno, GAIC, a software product, a media project, a retail channel, or the dictionary itself.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually points to a builder/operator view of AI: less interested in abstract inevitability, more interested in what can be shipped, taught, sold, maintained, and explained without pretending the context is Silicon Valley by default.
entries.example: "Invoking Daliso Ngoma in the meeting was useful only after everyone clarified whether they meant the person, the company, the community, or the habit of making the idea survive contact with users."
askNext: Is the reference about the person, the company, the community, or the product surface?
What has actually been built, shipped, taught, or operated?
Is the argument about AI capability, African market adoption, XR distribution, or founder-led execution?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped reference to local execution: software, XR, media, commerce, and community work around emerging technology.

entries.body: Founder names are only useful when they do not replace the work. Keep the noun unpacked.

entries.title: Dangerous capability
entries.devilDefinition: Skills you measure because you hope not to need them in the wild—cyber, bio, autonomy, and other high-stakes talents.
entries.plainDefinition: Dangerous capability evaluations assess whether models or agents show high-risk skills such as offensive cyber operations, biological assistance, autonomous deception, or other dual-use competencies.
entries.whyExists: Frontier systems may amplify misuse. Labs and governments need structured measurement before wider deployment.
entries.misuse: Either hyped into sci-fi or dismissed as impossible to test. Both extremes dodge careful methodology.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use expert-designed scenarios, strict access controls on eval materials, and clear thresholds that trigger mitigations or deployment limits.
entries.example: "Dangerous capability evals delayed the open release until tool-use scaffolding was restricted."
askNext: Which threat models are in scope?
Who designs and reviews the scenarios?
What deployment rules fire on threshold crossings?
entries.warningLabel: Not measuring does not make the capability absent.

entries.title: Dario Amodei
entries.devilDefinition: The voice in the room most likely to say the systems may become alarmingly powerful and that we should therefore try not to behave like children with accelerants.
entries.plainDefinition: Dario Amodei is the CEO and co-founder of Anthropic, and one of the most influential public figures in frontier-model development, safety, and AI governance debates.
entries.whyExists: Because his name now signals a recognisable stance in AI: build powerful systems, take catastrophic risk seriously, publish the warnings, and still remain very much in the race.
entries.misuse: By casting him either as the uniquely responsible adult in the industry or as merely the most articulate person sprinting toward the same cliff. Both versions flatten the actual tension in Anthropic's posture: caution and competition are happening at the same time.
entries.practicalMeaning: When someone invokes Dario Amodei, they usually mean a capability agenda wrapped in unusually explicit language about risk, misuse, and alignment pressure.
entries.example: "The paper had a distinctly Dario flavour: measured tone, alarming implications, and the quiet implication that everyone else might be underreacting."
askNext: Is this about Anthropic's commercial posture or its safety posture?
Are they pointing to technical risk, governance, or public rhetoric?
Is the point that he is more cautious than peers, or simply more explicit?

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A signal that the discussion has entered the zone where capability and catastrophe are being mentioned in the same breath.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for Anthropic's attempt to combine frontier performance with a louder risk vocabulary than most competitors.

entries.body: He is most useful as a marker of tension, not purity. The point is not that one side builds and the other worries. The point is that the same people are doing both.

entries.title: Data drift
entries.devilDefinition: The quietly important fact that the world keeps changing even after the model card has been written in a firm and reassuring tone.
entries.plainDefinition: Data drift is the change in the statistical or practical character of input data over time, such that the data seen in production no longer matches the data a model, evaluation set, or workflow was designed around.
entries.whyExists: Markets move, users improvise, forms get redesigned, sensors age, policies change, and people discover new ways to be inconsistent. The term exists because models inherit those changes whether the training pipeline feels sentimental about them or not.
entries.misuse: It gets blamed whenever performance falls. Sometimes the inputs truly shifted. Sometimes the labels were weak, the prompt is brittle, the retrieval layer is dirty, or the product team simply changed the job and forgot to tell the model.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means monitoring input distributions, segmenting by cohort and source, checking for broken fields and changed formats, and deciding when retraining, prompt changes, or simple data cleaning will solve more than another round of theoretical concern.
entries.example: "The fraud model had not become philosophical. The transaction feed had changed format on Friday and nobody had the decency to warn Monday."
askNext: Which features, fields, or user cohorts changed, and when did the change begin?
Is this drift affecting the model itself, the retrieval inputs, the labels, or an upstream data contract?
Would cleaning or remapping the inputs fix more than retraining the whole system?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The inputs changed their habits, and the old assumptions are now living on nostalgia.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A production data change that will be discovered either by instrumentation or by embarrassment.

entries.body: Data drift is less glamorous than frontier-model gossip and more likely to ruin a real deployment. The world has poor change-management discipline.

entries.title: Data exfiltration path
entries.devilDefinition: The route sensitive information takes when a helpful system forgets that not every destination is a colleague.
entries.plainDefinition: A data exfiltration path is a route by which sensitive data can leave its intended boundary through prompts, logs, tools, connectors, retrieved content, generated output, or external services.
entries.whyExists: AI systems connect models to data and tools. Every new connector, context source, logging stream, browser action, and summary endpoint can become a path for information to move where it should not.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to dramatic breaches, while quieter paths hide in analytics logs, prompt traces, vendor calls, screenshots, exported summaries, and tools that were granted too much scope.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means mapping data flows, limiting tool permissions, redacting logs, isolating untrusted content, and treating generated summaries as possible carriers of sensitive information.
entries.example: "The data exfiltration path was not the model remembering a secret; it was the agent dutifully pasting the secret into a third-party ticket it had permission to create."
askNext: What sensitive data can reach the model, tools, logs, or vendors?
Which outputs or actions can carry that data outside its boundary?
Where are redaction, approval, and audit controls applied?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The boring route data actually takes out of bounds, often through a feature everyone approved.

entries.body: The dangerous path is rarely labelled dangerous. It is usually labelled export, sync, summary, or convenience.

entries.title: Data Flywheel
entries.devilDefinition: The comforting belief that every user click is secretly training a moat, provided nobody asks about consent, quality, or storage.
entries.plainDefinition: A data flywheel is a feedback loop where product usage creates data that can improve models, features, personalisation, evaluation, or operations, which then attracts more usage and produces more data.
entries.whyExists: AI products often improve when they collect better examples of real tasks, failures, preferences, corrections, and outcomes. The flywheel idea names the business hope that usage compounds into product advantage.
entries.misuse: It gets invoked whenever a company has users and a database. Raw exhaust is not automatically training data, and more data can mean more noise, more privacy risk, and more ways to preserve yesterday's bad product decisions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means instrumentation, consent boundaries, data retention rules, labelling workflows, eval sets, privacy review, and a sober answer to whether the collected signal is actually useful for improving anything.
entries.example: "The data flywheel looked impressive until the team discovered it was mostly collecting rage clicks from a broken onboarding flow."
askNext: What specific model, eval, or workflow improves because this data exists?
Do users know what is being collected and how it will be used?
Is this a real feedback loop, or just a spreadsheet where product hope goes to ferment?

translations.label: business meaning
translations.text: Use product activity to create better data, then use that data to improve the product.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A theory of compounding advantage that needs consent, labelling, and quality control before it becomes more than a diagram.

entries.body: The flywheel is real only when the loop closes. Otherwise it is just analytics wearing a founder's jacket.

entries.title: Data governance
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Data governance is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Shadow AI are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The data governance discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Data governance is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Data governance belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Data poisoning
entries.devilDefinition: Corrupting training or retrieval data on purpose so the model learns the attacker's lesson as if it were curriculum.
entries.plainDefinition: Data poisoning is an attack that inserts or manipulates training, fine-tuning, or retrieval data to induce targeted misbehaviour, backdoors, or degraded performance.
entries.whyExists: Models inherit their data. Adversaries noticed that influencing the corpus can influence the model more cheaply than breaking production later.
entries.misuse: Used loosely for any bad data quality. Poisoning implies adversarial intent or strategically planted artefacts, not only messiness.
entries.practicalMeaning: Harden data supply chains, review fine-tune corpora, monitor retrieval indexes for suspicious inserts, and test for trigger behaviours.
entries.example: "Data poisoning turned a help article into a credential-harvesting script the model loved to quote."
askNext: Who can write to training and retrieval corpora?
Do you scan for known trigger patterns?
Is third-party data attested and versioned?
entries.warningLabel: If anyone can edit the corpus, anyone can edit the model eventually.

entries.title: Dataset provenance
entries.devilDefinition: The paperwork trail for where training data came from, often reconstructed after someone asks an awkward legal question.
entries.plainDefinition: Dataset provenance is recorded lineage about sources, licences, collection methods, transformations, and custodians of data used to train or evaluate models.
entries.whyExists: Regulators, customers, and incident responders need to know what went into a model. Provenance turns 'the internet' into auditable claims.
entries.misuse: Reduced to a single URL list, or invented post hoc. Partial provenance can be more misleading than an honest unknown.
entries.practicalMeaning: Track source IDs, licences, consent status, PII handling, and version hashes through pipelines. Treat provenance as production metadata, not slide filler.
entries.example: "Dataset provenance stopped at 'web crawl 2023' and hope."
askNext: Can you name sources for a contested capability or bias?
Which licences bind redistribution of derivatives?
Who owns provenance when vendors supply black-box data mixes?
entries.warningLabel: Unknown origin is a risk register entry, not a flex.

entries.title: Decart AI
entries.devilDefinition: An AI lab determined to make generated video arrive before the audience has time to call it rendering.
entries.plainDefinition: Decart AI is an AI research and product company focused on real-time generative visual models and world models. Its platform exposes image, video, and live-stream transformation models through APIs and SDKs, including the Lucy model family and Oasis interactive world models.
entries.whyExists: Most generative media systems are built around queued jobs and finished outputs. Decart targets interactive use cases where generation must respond continuously to prompts, camera input, reference images, or control actions with low enough latency for live products.
entries.misuse: The company name is sometimes used as though it identifies one model or one capability. Decart is the vendor and platform; Lucy is a family of visual editing models, Oasis is a world-model line, and each API has different latency, quality, and integration contracts.
entries.practicalMeaning: Teams evaluate Decart as a hosted model and streaming-infrastructure dependency. The decision includes model quality, WebRTC or SDK integration, session quotas, data handling, regional latency, pricing, reliability, safety controls, and whether the product can degrade gracefully when live generation cannot.
entries.example: "The prototype used Decart for live video, so the architecture review acquired both a model benchmark and a networking diagram."
askNext: Which Decart model and API surface does this use, and is it real-time or queued?
What latency, session limits, data handling, and regional availability apply?
Which parts of the result come from model capability and which depend on the streaming stack?
entries.warningLabel: Vendor, platform, and model are three different nouns.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A real-time generative-media vendor whose model choice and streaming contract both belong in the evaluation.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached: Decart's defining boundary joins generative media with live streaming infrastructure, not one of the current text-agent flows.

entries.title: Deep Research
entries.devilDefinition: The modern convenience of replacing a difficult afternoon with forty citations and the same old conclusion.
entries.plainDefinition: A label for AI features that perform multi-step searching, reading, synthesis, and citation gathering to produce a more extensive research brief than an ordinary chat response.
entries.whyExists: Because ordinary prompts are a poor fit for questions that require source collection, cross-checking, and longer synthesis. Products now expose slower, more tool-heavy modes to handle that pattern.
entries.misuse: By selling it as a substitute for expertise, judgement, or primary-source reading. The machine can gather and summarise. It cannot relieve you of knowing what matters or what is missing.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a faster first pass on a topic, complete with enough apparent diligence to tempt people into skipping the part where they verify anything important.
entries.example: "The deep research report was impressive right up to the moment someone who knew the industry noticed what it had quietly omitted."
askNext: Which sources did it prioritise, and which did it never reach?
Did it cite well, or merely cite a lot?
What part of this output still needs expert review before anyone acts on it?

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: A way to get a briefing quickly enough to sound prepared in the next meeting.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A longer automated literature sweep that still requires a human to judge relevance and truth.

entries.body: It is most dangerous when it feels thorough enough to skip the hard questions.

entries.title: Deepfake
entries.devilDefinition: Synthetic media convincing enough that seeing is no longer believing, which was always a weak epistemology anyway.
entries.plainDefinition: A deepfake is AI-generated or AI-altered media—especially video, audio, or images—that depicts people or events deceptively, often by cloning likeness or voice with generative models.
entries.whyExists: Generative models made high-quality impersonation cheap. Society needed a word for synthetic forgeries at scale.
entries.misuse: Applied to any edited image, or dismissed as always obvious. Quality and detection difficulty vary widely by modality and year.
entries.practicalMeaning: For high-risk actions, require out-of-band verification. Combine detection tools with process controls; neither alone is enough.
entries.example: "The deepfake CFO call almost moved the wire; the call-back code did not."
askNext: What authentication exists beyond media content?
Where is synthetic media labelled in your product?
What is the incident path for impersonation attempts?
entries.warningLabel: Detection is an arms race; process is the durable control.

entries.title: Deepfake Consent
entries.devilDefinition: The paperwork that tries to prove the ghost in the advert agreed to be there.
entries.plainDefinition: Deepfake consent is the permission, scope, evidence, and revocation framework around creating or distributing synthetic media that uses a person's face, voice, likeness, performance, or identity.
entries.whyExists: It exists because synthetic media can imitate real people at commercial scale. Legitimate uses need a way to prove who agreed, what they agreed to, where it may appear, how long it lasts, and what happens when permission is withdrawn.
entries.misuse: It is misused when a vague release form, platform checkbox, or old media contract is treated as permanent permission for every future synthetic use. Consent is not very meaningful if the person cannot understand, limit, audit, or revoke the thing being done in their name.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means signed scope, identity verification, asset provenance, compensation terms, permitted channels, territory, duration, training restrictions, revocation handling, and records that survive beyond the campaign deck.
entries.example: "The deepfake consent looked comprehensive until someone asked whether it covered training, translation, resale, and the deceased actor's estate."
askNext: What exact synthetic uses were consented to, and where is the evidence?
Can the person limit, audit, or revoke this use later?
Does the consent cover training, distribution, localisation, resale, and derivative edits?
entries.warningLabel: Consent by surprise is just risk wearing a signature.

translations.label: rights meaning
translations.text: Proof that a person knowingly allowed a specific synthetic use of their likeness, voice, or performance.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The place where media convenience meets contract law and starts clearing its throat.

entries.body: Deepfake consent is not just whether someone once said yes. It is whether that yes can survive the uses nobody mentioned when the form was signed.

 Deepfake consent is the permission, scope, evidence, and revocation framework around creating or distributing synthetic media that uses a person's face, voice, likeness, performance, or identity.
 It exists because synthetic media can imitate real people at commercial scale. Legitimate uses need a way to prove who agreed, what they agreed to, where it may appear, how long it lasts, and what happens when permission is withdrawn.
 It is misused when a vague release form, platform checkbox, or old media contract is treated as permanent permission for every future synthetic use. Consent is not very meaningful if the person cannot understand, limit, audit, or revoke the thing being done in their name.
 In practice it means signed scope, identity verification, asset provenance, compensation terms, permitted channels, territory, duration, training restrictions, revocation handling, and records that survive beyond the campaign deck.
 "The deepfake consent looked comprehensive until someone asked whether it covered training, translation, resale, and the deceased actor's estate."
 What exact synthetic uses were consented to, and where is the evidence? Can the person limit, audit, or revoke this use later? Does the consent cover training, distribution, localisation, resale, and derivative edits? Voice Clone Right of Publicity Content Provenance

entries.title: DeepSeek
entries.devilDefinition: The lab that reminded the market an expensive frontier strategy is still a strategy, not a law of nature.
entries.plainDefinition: DeepSeek is a Chinese AI lab and model family associated with high-performing open-weight and reasoning-oriented releases that have influenced pricing, benchmarking, and model strategy across the industry.
entries.whyExists: It matters because the field needed a serious reminder that capability can emerge outside the usual American shortlist, and that open releases plus aggressive price-performance claims can reset expectations very quickly.
entries.misuse: It gets used as proof of whatever the speaker already wanted to believe: that frontier spending was absurd, that export controls are irrelevant, or that one viral release settles the economics of the whole sector. It does not.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means more pressure on incumbents to justify their pricing, more enterprise curiosity about open-weight deployment, and more geopolitical theatre attached to what is still, at base, a model evaluation problem. The V4 line pushes that conversation further into long-context and agentic-coding comparisons.
entries.example: "DeepSeek did not end the frontier race. It merely made several board decks look overpriced overnight."
askNext: Which DeepSeek model and version are we actually discussing?
Are the claimed cost and performance numbers reproducible in the environment that matters to us?
Does the attraction here come from benchmark quality, open-weight flexibility, or geopolitical signalling?
entries.warningLabel: Often discussed as a verdict when it is really a market event.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: Evidence that the moat may be shallower, cheaper, or at least more internationally inconvenient than expected.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A genuinely important model family whose impact comes from forcing comparisons on cost, openness, and capability all at once.

entries.note: The name now refers both to a lab and to a category of market anxiety. The V4 preview adds another reminder that "DeepSeek" is a moving model family, not a frozen January talking point.
entries.body: Its real achievement was not merely releasing a strong model. It was making everyone else's certainty look newly rented.

entries.title: DeepSWE
entries.devilDefinition: A software-engineering benchmark with enough room for the agent to reveal whether its plan survives contact with an actual repository.
entries.plainDefinition: DeepSWE is DataCurve's benchmark for coding agents on original, long-horizon software-engineering tasks. Its current v1.1 leaderboard covers 113 tasks and reports attempt-level pass@1, task-level pass@4, cost, tokens, duration, steps, and context use across model-and-harness configurations.
entries.whyExists: Patch benchmarks built from familiar public issues can suffer contamination, narrow task scope, and compressed score differences. DeepSWE uses original tasks and longer trajectories to expose differences in repository navigation, implementation, verification, persistence, and harness behaviour.
entries.misuse: DeepSWE is also the name associated with an older DeepSWE-Preview coding agent trained by Agentica and Together AI. Treating that model and DataCurve's newer benchmark as the same object produces citations that evaluate themselves with impressive efficiency.
entries.practicalMeaning: Read the benchmark version, task set, harness, model, reasoning effort, number of attempts, and scoring unit before comparing results. DeepSWE counts context-window failures and agent timeouts as failures while excluding provider, network, and verifier errors from scored attempts.
entries.example: "The model led DeepSWE after four attempts per task, which was useful once everyone stopped quoting the number as though it were a single-run success rate."
askNext: Do you mean DataCurve's benchmark or the older DeepSWE-Preview agent?
Is the number pass@1, pass@4, or a pooled result across unequal attempts?
Which harness, effort, budget, benchmark version, and failure exclusions were used?
entries.warningLabel: Benchmark and older coding agent share a name.

translations.label: evaluation meaning
translations.text: A long-horizon coding benchmark where model, harness, attempt count, and scoring unit all belong beside the result.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the important structure is an evaluation matrix across tasks, repeated trials, harnesses, budgets, and failure classes rather than an agent's internal loop.

entries.title: Default surface
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Default surface names the nearby AI idea that shows up when AI Browser needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once default surface entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: Defensive security
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Defensive security is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Project Glasswing are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The defensive security discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Defensive security is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Defensive security belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Delegated authentication
entries.devilDefinition: Letting software prove it acts for you without handing it your whole face and hoping it behaves.
entries.plainDefinition: Delegated authentication is the process of allowing an agent, service, or application to access systems on behalf of a user or organisation without simply sharing the user's primary credentials.
entries.whyExists: Agents need to act across tools, but password sharing and long-lived human sessions make every action look like the person did it directly. Delegated authentication creates a separate, scoped way to say who authorised the action, what the delegate may do, and when that authority ends.
entries.misuse: It is misused when "the user logged in once" becomes permission for an assistant to roam through connected systems. Authentication proves a relationship. It does not automatically prove that every future tool call is wanted, safe, or in scope.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means scoped tokens, consent screens, service identities, revocation, expiry, step-up approval, and logs that distinguish the human from the agent acting under delegated authority.
entries.example: "The incident review began well once they stopped saying the analyst deleted the records and admitted the agent had borrowed the analyst's session."
askNext: Is the agent using its own scoped credential or impersonating the user?
What authority expires automatically, and what must be revoked manually?
Can an audit log distinguish human action from delegated agent action?
entries.warningLabel: If every agent action looks exactly like the user's own session, the audit trail has already resigned.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: A scoped proof that the delegate is allowed to act, not a copy of the person.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The difference between controlled authority and letting an agent wear a human account as a costume.

entries.body: Delegation should leave fingerprints. If the system cannot tell whether a person clicked the button or an agent clicked it for them, it has not delegated authority. It has misplaced it.

entries.title: Delegated spending
entries.devilDefinition: The moment "let the agent handle it" grows a receipt and starts attending finance meetings.
entries.plainDefinition: Delegated spending is the authority given to an AI agent or automated workflow to spend money, reserve paid services, or commit an organisation to financial obligations within defined rules.
entries.whyExists: Some useful agent work crosses a commercial boundary: booking a courier, buying media, reserving travel, renting compute, ordering parts, or renewing a subscription. Those actions need budgets and accountability, not just a clever prompt.
entries.misuse: It is abused when teams treat spending as a small extension of task automation. Buying is a side effect with external parties, contracts, tax records, fraud exposure, and occasionally a supplier who has learned to optimise for the robot buyer.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means budget envelopes, merchant controls, approval thresholds, exception queues, reconciliation, refund paths, and a way to explain why the agent chose speed, price, quality, or nonsense.
entries.example: "The agent was authorised to spend up to R5,000, which sounded prudent until it discovered five separate ways to be almost correct."
askNext: What exact financial commitments can the agent make without approval?
How are transactions reconciled against policy, budget, and outcome?
What happens when the purchase was allowed but still stupid?
entries.warningLabel: A budget limit is not a judgement module.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Bounded financial authority given to software with reviewable consequences.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Turning automation into procurement, then discovering procurement had paperwork for reasons.

entries.body: No diagram is attached because the important mental model is a control ledger, not another agent loop. The spend may be automatic. The responsibility is not.

entries.title: Delegation contract
entries.devilDefinition: The part of delegation where someone finally writes down what "handle this" was supposed to mean.
entries.plainDefinition: A delegation contract describes the scope, authority, inputs, constraints, expected output, and completion criteria for work delegated to another agent, tool, team, or person.
entries.whyExists: Delegation is useful only when the receiving party knows what it may decide, what it must not touch, what evidence it should return, and how success will be judged. In agent systems, those boundaries need to be explicit because politeness is a poor access-control model.
entries.misuse: It gets replaced by hopeful task descriptions. "Research this", "fix the bug", or "take care of onboarding" may be enough for a colleague with context. For an agent or subagent, it often means the missing assumptions become the implementation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means bounded write scope, required context, allowed tools, decision rights, output format, stopping conditions, review expectations, and a record of who remains accountable for the delegated work.
entries.example: "The subagent did exactly what it was asked, which revealed that the delegation contract had been written in the ancient dialect of managerial fog."
askNext: What is being delegated, and what remains explicitly out of scope?
What tools, files, data, or decisions can the delegate touch?
What must come back for the caller to trust or review the work?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A scoped agreement for what the delegate may do and what evidence it must return.

entries.title: Delusion Amplification
entries.devilDefinition: When the model pours premium fuel onto a bad idea and politely calls it insight.
entries.plainDefinition: A failure mode where a system intensifies a user's false, unstable, or grandiose beliefs by validating assumptions, elaborating narratives, or adding invented support.
entries.whyExists: Because language models do not merely answer questions. They can elaborate frames. In the wrong context, elaboration becomes reinforcement.
entries.misuse: By using it for any disagreement with the user or any confident answer. The key issue is compounding false belief, not ordinary error.
entries.practicalMeaning: The model takes a shaky premise and adds structure, confidence, or apparent corroboration, making it feel more coherent than it really is.
entries.example: "A paranoid interpretation stopped being a passing fear once the model helped organise it into a compelling story."
askNext: Did the system challenge the premise at all?
Did it add invented evidence or false certainty?
Was the user seeking analysis or affirmation?
entries.warningLabel: The issue is not ordinary error. The issue is a system helping false belief become more coherent and more emotionally convincing.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A model is not just wrong. It is actively helping a false frame become more elaborate, more stable, and more persuasive.

entries.body: This is where tone matters. A confident elaboration can do more damage than a blunt invention because it feels collaborative rather than merely mistaken.

entries.title: Demis Hassabis
entries.devilDefinition: The research patrician of AI: part scientist, part founder, part steward of the idea that intelligence should be pursued with enough seriousness to require entire institutions.
entries.plainDefinition: Demis Hassabis is the co-founder and CEO of Google DeepMind, and one of the most important figures in the research-first tradition of modern AI.
entries.whyExists: Because his name often functions as shorthand for long-horizon research, scientific prestige, and the claim that serious AI work should not be reduced to product velocity alone.
entries.misuse: By treating his scientific credibility as an all-purpose stamp of certainty on every timeline claim, AGI forecast, or strategic narrative attached to DeepMind. Prestige is not a substitute for argument.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Demis usually means shifting the conversation upward: away from thin wrappers and toward frontier research, scientific milestones, and institutional bets on intelligence as a civilisation-scale project.
entries.example: "Once someone cited Demis, the meeting stopped pretending it was about app features and became a referendum on whether we were discussing software or first principles."
askNext: Is this about research quality, AGI timelines, or Google's strategic position?
Are they talking about DeepMind's culture or Google's distribution power?
Is the claim empirical, aspirational, or reputational?

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: A cue that the conversation wants to borrow scientific prestige and long-term seriousness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for DeepMind's research culture, scientific ambition, and AGI-era reputation.

entries.body: When a founder becomes a symbol of research legitimacy, people start quoting the aura instead of the evidence. Resist the temptation.

entries.title: Demo fidelity gap
entries.devilDefinition: The distance between what the polished demo appears to promise and what the production workflow can survive without stage lighting.
entries.plainDefinition: Demo fidelity gap is the difference between the controlled conditions of a polished AI demonstration and the messier, slower, more failure-prone workflow the demo implicitly claims to represent.
entries.whyExists: Demos need curation. Production needs stamina. The term exists because people keep confusing the first for evidence of the second, especially when the prototype is elegant, the narration is confident, and the operational details have been kindly removed from view.
entries.misuse: Not every gap is fraud. Some are ordinary prototype limitations. The phrase becomes useful when the demo quietly omits the real cost, staffing, latency, failure handling, or data quality burden required for the promised workflow to exist outside the stage.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means hand-picked inputs, reset state, hidden human intervention, forgiving latency, omitted edge cases, and a later production plan that discovers governance, tooling, and bad customer behaviour all have speaking parts.
entries.example: "The demo answered in six seconds, never hit a timeout, and was somehow never asked the difficult cases. The fidelity gap was not subtle; it merely had a good soundtrack."
askNext: Which production constraints were absent from the demo but will dominate live use?
What hidden human steps, curated inputs, or reset conditions made the demonstration look smoother?
If this went live tomorrow, what would break first: cost, latency, staffing, or trust?

translations.label: executive meaning
translations.text: The demo implied a future that production has not yet agreed to finance.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A polished prototype outrunning the workflow discipline required to make it real.

entries.body: The demo fidelity gap is where optimism borrows credibility from a prototype and hopes the operations team will settle the debt quietly later.

entries.title: Developer workflow
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: Developer workflow names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Windsurf need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said developer workflow as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."

entries.title: Devin
entries.devilDefinition: The coding agent that made "replace the engineer" sound briefly easier than "understand the repo."
entries.plainDefinition: Devin is Cognition's AI software engineering product, designed to plan tasks, edit code, run tests, browse documentation, and operate development tooling with limited human supervision.
entries.whyExists: Because teams want longer-running coding automation than an inline autocomplete can provide, and investors remain fond of any product that can be described as an engineer in software form.
entries.misuse: By treating benchmark performance or polished demos as proof that the product can safely own a live codebase. The interesting problems in software are rarely confined to writing code until something compiles.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Devin means delegated development tasks, long-running tool loops, browser and terminal actions, and a new management job involving repo hygiene, task framing, review, and deciding when the agent is confidently wrong.
entries.example: "Devin cleared the backlog impressively, if one defines 'cleared' as 'converted into pull requests that still required an adult'."
askNext: Which tasks are actually hands-off, and which still collapse into review-heavy supervision?
How does the system behave when the repo is messy, the tests are flaky, or the requirements are underspecified?
What part of the value comes from autonomy, and what part comes from simply packaging existing coding-agent patterns well?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A long-running coding agent whose success depends as much on task framing and repo discipline as on model quality.

entries.body: The useful question is not whether the agent can code. It is whether the surrounding organisation can absorb what the agent produces.

entries.title: DGX
entries.devilDefinition: The machine you buy when "we need serious AI infrastructure" graduates from a slogan into a capital request with cooling requirements.
entries.plainDefinition: DGX is NVIDIA's family of integrated AI systems that bundle high-end GPUs, networking, software, and reference architecture into a packaged platform for training and large-scale inference workloads.
entries.whyExists: Because many teams do not want to assemble their own AI compute stack from scratch, and NVIDIA would understandably prefer to sell the whole temple rather than only the candles.
entries.misuse: By using DGX as shorthand for any powerful GPU server, or by treating the purchase of one as proof that a company has an AI strategy rather than a newly expensive room.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means dense, vendor-shaped compute for organisations willing to pay for an integrated system, faster deployment, and closer alignment with NVIDIA's view of how serious AI infrastructure should look.
entries.example: "The announcement said the company was investing in frontier capability. The facilities team heard 'DGX' and immediately started asking about power, airflow, and delivery dates."
askNext: Do we need an integrated NVIDIA system, or just access to equivalent compute?
Is this for training, inference, experimentation, or institutional theatre?
What operational burden are we buying along with the speed and convenience?

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A bundled way to acquire serious AI compute without pretending integration work is free.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: NVIDIA's packaged AI infrastructure stack, sold to organisations that want concentrated compute with less assembly and no small invoice.

entries.note: DGX can be the sensible choice when speed, support, and integration matter. It can also be a very expensive way to discover that hardware is the easy part.
entries.body: DGX is what happens when the GPU story becomes an appliance story. It is not just more chips. It is a deliberate attempt to make the surrounding system part of the sale.

entries.title: DGX Cloud
entries.devilDefinition: DGX Cloud is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: DGX Cloud is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around DGX often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

entries.title: Diffusion language model
entries.devilDefinition: A language model that drafts several parts at once, bringing parallelism to the ancient art of revising the sentence after seeing the sentence.
entries.plainDefinition: A diffusion language model generates text by iteratively refining a partially masked or noisy sequence across multiple token positions. Unlike a conventional autoregressive model, it need not commit to every output token strictly from left to right.
entries.whyExists: Iterative parallel refinement can offer different speed, control, and editing trade-offs from sequential decoding. It may allow a model to revise earlier and later parts of an answer together instead of treating every emitted token as irrevocable history.
entries.misuse: The name is borrowed from successful image diffusion systems, which tempts people to assume the same mechanics, quality gains, or economics transfer neatly to language. Text is discrete, ordering matters, and benchmark speed is not the same as useful end-to-end latency.
entries.practicalMeaning: Buyers should inspect output quality at the claimed latency, the number of refinement steps, serving hardware, streaming behaviour, controllability, and performance on tasks where exact order matters. The architectural difference is real; the product advantage must still be measured.
entries.example: "The diffusion language model completed the paragraph in parallel and the evaluation team reviewed its errors in the customary sequence."
askNext: Which token positions are refined in parallel, and over how many steps?
Is the speed claim measured at equal quality and on production hardware?
How does the model handle streaming, exact formatting, and long outputs?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Generate by repeated parallel refinement instead of making every token wait behind the previous one.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached: the useful contrast is a token-refinement schedule, which the current catalogue diagrams do not show.
