entries.title: Digital twin
entries.devilDefinition: A simulation of something real that executives treat as the real thing until the twin and the plant disagree in public.
entries.plainDefinition: A digital twin is a continuously updated digital representation of a physical or organisational system used for monitoring, simulation, and decision support—now often paired with AI for prediction and control.
entries.whyExists: Operators wanted models that track live state rather than static CAD. AI marketing then stretched the term toward any dashboard with a forecast.
entries.misuse: Applied to static profiles, chatbots 'of a person,' or unvalidated simulators. A twin without fidelity metrics is cosplay.
entries.practicalMeaning: Define the system boundary, update rate, validation data, and decision rights. Separate predictive models from the system-of-record they approximate.
entries.example: "The digital twin predicted smooth throughput while the warehouse invented a new kind of jam."
askNext: What is synchronised, and how often?
How is twin error measured against reality?
Which decisions may rely on the twin without human override?
entries.warningLabel: Maps that drift become fiction with sensors.

entries.title: Digital Worker
entries.devilDefinition: Workflow software reintroduced to management as headcount, because procurement enjoys a dramatic entrance.
entries.plainDefinition: "Digital worker" is a vendor phrase for automation software, often AI-assisted, that is sold as though it were a bounded employee capable of handling routine business tasks across systems.
entries.whyExists: Because buyers understand roles more quickly than architectures, and vendors would rather sell "an accounts payable worker" than "a model-mediated process layer with brittle integrations".
entries.misuse: By speaking as if software has judgement, accountability, or broad role competence simply because it can complete a workflow. Calling it a worker does not make it adaptable, trustworthy, or employable in the human sense.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a mix of rules, integrations, model calls, approval steps, and exception handling wrapped in labour metaphors so the budget can be compared to a salary instead of a software line item.
entries.example: "The demo promised a digital worker for claims processing. The implementation delivered an approval queue with a more theatrical org chart."
askNext: What exact tasks can this system complete without human rescue?
Where does accountability sit when the workflow makes a bad decision?
Are we buying durable automation or just renting an employee metaphor?
entries.warningLabel: If the product is introduced as a colleague, ask who will be blamed when it behaves like software.

translations.label: sales meaning
translations.text: A software budget item reframed as operational staffing.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An automation stack with model-assisted steps and a carefully managed definition of success.

entries.body: The phrase is useful mostly as a diagnostic. When the language turns employment-shaped, someone is usually trying to smuggle software limits past a budget committee.

entries.title: Disaggregated inference
entries.devilDefinition: Splitting serving stages across machines so each GPU can specialise, and so your network can join the critical path.
entries.plainDefinition: Disaggregated inference is an architecture that separates inference phases or components—often prefill and decode, or different model parts—across distinct hardware pools connected by a fast fabric.
entries.whyExists: Monolithic serving wastes resources when phases have different compute and memory profiles. Disaggregation aims for higher utilisation and independent scaling.
entries.misuse: Copied from a blog without fabric bandwidth math. Network and orchestration overhead can erase gains.
entries.practicalMeaning: Only adopt when profiling shows phase imbalance large enough to justify complexity. Measure end-to-end latency under real mixes, not isolated kernels.
entries.example: "Disaggregated inference looked elegant until the interconnect became the new GPU."
askNext: Where does KV state move, and at what cost?
Can you scale prefill and decode pools independently in production?
What failure domains did you add?
entries.warningLabel: Architecture diagrams do not move bytes for free.

entries.title: Distillation
entries.devilDefinition: The art of teaching a smaller model to imitate a larger one, then introducing the result as efficiency rather than inherited opinion.
entries.plainDefinition: Distillation is a training approach in which a smaller or cheaper model learns from the outputs, preferences, or intermediate signals of a larger model so it can reproduce some of its performance at lower cost.
entries.whyExists: It exists because frontier-grade behaviour is expensive to serve everywhere. If a smaller system can absorb enough of a stronger model's behaviour, products gain speed, lower inference cost, and a better shot at running locally or at scale.
entries.misuse: It is misused when people describe the resulting model as independent genius rather than compressed imitation. Distillation can preserve capability surprisingly well, but it also preserves blind spots, style, and hidden assumptions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means generating a large synthetic training set from a stronger model, fine-tuning a cheaper one on that data, and accepting a careful trade-off between cost, latency, and capability.
entries.example: "Their distilled model was impressively cheap, which was another way of saying they had purchased fewer surprises per request."
askNext: What behaviour was the student model trained to copy?
Which capabilities held up after distillation, and which quietly fell off?
Did the process reduce cost without merely packaging the same errors more efficiently?

translations.label: exec meaning
translations.text: Keep most of the magic, lose most of the bill.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Training a smaller model to mimic a stronger one closely enough to be cheaper and still useful.

entries.note: Distillation is often described as model compression, but the interesting part is not just size. It is which behaviours survive the squeeze.
entries.body: The smaller model may be faster, cheaper, and easier to deploy. It is still carrying somebody else's worldview in a lighter suitcase.

entries.title: DPO
entries.devilDefinition: Teaching the model which answer you preferred without the full RLHF theatre, then discovering that preferences still disagree with each other.
entries.plainDefinition: DPO (Direct Preference Optimisation) is a post-training method that aligns a language model to human preference pairs by optimising a classification-style objective, avoiding the separate reward-model and reinforcement-learning loop used in classic RLHF pipelines.
entries.whyExists: Preference alignment became central to making models usable, but full RLHF stacks are expensive, brittle, and operationally heavy. DPO offers a simpler path that still uses preferred-versus-rejected examples.
entries.misuse: It is treated as a free replacement for all of RLHF, evaluation, and governance. DPO still depends on preference data quality, coverage, and the base model. A clean algorithm does not purify a biased or gamed preference set.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means collecting or curating preference pairs, running a preference optimisation stage after supervised fine-tuning, and then measuring whether the model actually behaves better on the tasks and harms you care about — not only on the training pairs.
entries.example: "They switched to DPO to simplify post-training, then spent the savings re-labelling preferences the first run had overfitted."
askNext: What preference data is being optimised, and who decided the winners?
What evals show improvement beyond the DPO training pairs?
Is DPO replacing RLHF entirely, or sitting beside other post-training stages?
entries.warningLabel: A simpler alignment recipe still inherits the politics of the preference set.

translations.label: training meaning
translations.text: Align the model to preference pairs without a separate reward-model RL loop.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A lighter post-training step that still lives or dies by preference data quality.

entries.body: DPO is engineering thrift applied to preference learning. It shortens the pipeline; it does not retire the need to decide what "better" meant.

entries.title: Durable execution
entries.devilDefinition: Workflow state written down carefully enough that a crashed process cannot take the institutional memory with it.
entries.plainDefinition: Durable execution is a workflow model that persists progress and can resume long-running work after process crashes, restarts, timeouts, or infrastructure changes. Completed steps and pending waits are recorded outside the transient worker that happens to be running them.
entries.whyExists: Agent jobs increasingly wait on tools, people, schedules, and slow model calls. Keeping that work only in memory makes an ordinary deployment or network failure indistinguishable from forgetting the entire assignment.
entries.misuse: Durable is mistaken for exactly once, automatically correct, or permanently retained. Resumption can repeat side effects, preserve bad state, exhaust retries, or revive work whose user authority has expired.
entries.practicalMeaning: The system checkpoints workflow state, assigns stable operation identifiers, retries according to policy, makes side effects idempotent where possible, and exposes cancellation and inspection. Engineers must version state, bound retention, and decide how old work behaves after code or permissions change.
entries.example: "Durable execution survived the deploy and resumed the agent precisely where it had paused, including the part everyone now wished had been cancelled."
askNext: Which state is persisted, and what event determines the safe resume point?
How are retries, duplicate side effects, cancellation, and expired authority handled?
Can operators inspect, migrate, or terminate a workflow after the code changes?
entries.warningLabel: Resumable work can resume the wrong thing twice.

translations.label: operations meaning
translations.text: Persist enough workflow history to recover after failure, then engineer every side effect for the possibility of repetition.

entries.body: The agent-loop diagram is useful because durable execution preserves the observe-decide-act cycle across waits and failures. The arrows still need checkpoints and idempotency.

entries.title: Edge AI
entries.devilDefinition: Running models on the device so the cloud cannot see your data—and so your battery can share the suffering.
entries.plainDefinition: Edge AI is the deployment of inference (and sometimes learning) on local devices—phones, gateways, vehicles, industrial controllers—rather than solely in remote data centres.
entries.whyExists: Latency, privacy, connectivity, and cost push compute toward the data source. Hardware finally made some models fit.
entries.misuse: Marketed as automatically private and secure. Edge devices are still attackable, outdated, and unevenly updated.
entries.practicalMeaning: Size models to hardware, plan update channels, measure thermal and battery impact, and define what still needs cloud fallback.
entries.example: "Edge AI worked offline until the model file was six months stale."
askNext: What is the update mechanism for on-device models?
Which data never leaves the device, for real?
What is the degraded mode without connectivity?
entries.warningLabel: Local is not the same as locked down.

entries.title: Egress control
entries.devilDefinition: Rules for which data may leave a system—because models and agents are enthusiastic correspondents.
entries.plainDefinition: Egress control limits outbound data flows from applications, agents, or model integrations to approved destinations, reducing exfiltration and compliance risk.
entries.whyExists: LLM tools call the network. Without egress control, sensitive context travels to wherever a prompt or plugin suggests.
entries.misuse: Configured only at the corporate proxy while agents use side channels, or so blunt that work moves to shadow tools.
entries.practicalMeaning: Combine network allowlists, DLP inspection where appropriate, per-tool credentials, and logging of outbound payloads' metadata.
entries.example: "Egress control stopped the agent from pasting the customer export into a random webhook."
askNext: What destinations are allowlisted for each agent?
How is sensitive data detected in outbound paths?
Who reviews temporary exceptions?
entries.warningLabel: If it can call home, someone will teach it a new home.

entries.title: Elon Musk
entries.devilDefinition: What happens when AI acquires a promoter, a distribution engine, and a permanent ability to turn product discussion into spectacle.
entries.plainDefinition: Elon Musk is the founder of xAI and one of the most polarising public figures in AI. In practice, his significance in AI discourse comes from a mix of capital, platform reach, engineering ambition, political attention, and narrative force.
entries.whyExists: Because Musk is never merely a person in these discussions. His name functions as a compressed argument about speed, attention, ecosystem control, ideological posture, and whether theatrical force is itself a strategic asset.
entries.misuse: By treating him as either pure substance or pure noise. The useful position is less flattering to everyone's instincts: the spectacle matters because attention, distribution, and organisational velocity all materially affect how AI products land.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Elon Musk in AI, they usually mean xAI's ambitions, the Grok ecosystem, or the broader reality that infrastructure, media reach, and public controversy can be bundled into one strategic instrument.
entries.example: "Once Elon entered the product discussion, it acquired three new plotlines, a stronger ideological charge, and a lower chance of remaining about the product."
askNext: Is the relevance technical, political, commercial, or cultural?
Are they talking about xAI's systems, Musk's distribution power, or both?
Is this claim about model quality, market attention, or theatre with infrastructure attached?
entries.warningLabel: Attention is not the same thing as technical evidence, but it would also be foolish to pretend attention does not matter.

translations.label: media meaning
translations.text: A guarantee that the topic will become bigger, louder, and less tidy within minutes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for xAI plus the surrounding machinery of audience, controversy, capital, and platform leverage.

entries.note: Date-sensitive point: xAI announced on February 2, 2026 that SpaceX had acquired xAI. For that reason, it is safer to treat Musk here as xAI's founder and dominant public force than to casually repeat older standalone-company titles without context.
entries.body: With some founders, the product explains the persona. With Musk, the persona becomes part of the product whether anyone requested that feature or not.

entries.title: Embedding model
entries.devilDefinition: A model that turns meaning into vectors so search can pretend geometry is understanding.
entries.plainDefinition: An embedding model maps text or other modalities into dense vectors used for similarity search, clustering, retrieval, and classification features.
entries.whyExists: Semantic search and RAG need a numerical representation of meaning. Specialised embedding models outperform generic hacks for many retrieval tasks.
entries.misuse: Assumed universal and static. Domain shift, multilingual gaps, and outdated embeddings silently degrade RAG.
entries.practicalMeaning: Choose embeddings for your language and domain, re-index on model changes, and evaluate retrieval metrics—not only vibe search demos.
entries.example: "The embedding model thought 'apple' the fruit and 'Apple' the vendor were spiritually adjacent."
askNext: What retrieval metrics justify this embedding model?
When do you re-embed the corpus?
How do you handle multilingual or domain jargon?
entries.warningLabel: Nearest neighbour is not entailment.

entries.title: Embeddings
entries.devilDefinition: The act of turning words, sentences, or images into coordinates so that "similar enough" can be calculated instead of argued about.
entries.plainDefinition: Embeddings are numerical vector representations of data, such as text or images, designed so that semantically similar items are located near one another in vector space. They are commonly used for retrieval, clustering, recommendation, and deduplication.
entries.whyExists: Exact keyword matching is often too brittle for messy human language. Embeddings exist so systems can compare meaning approximately rather than matching only literal words.
entries.misuse: They are sometimes spoken about as if they were compact containers of all meaning, truth, and business strategy. They are not. An embedding is useful geometry, not metaphysics. Its quality depends on the model, the data, the task, and the comparison method.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means encoding text into vectors, storing those vectors somewhere searchable, and using nearest-neighbour techniques to find candidates that look semantically related. They are the quiet workhorses behind a lot of so-called magical retrieval.
entries.example: "The search improved after they switched from keywords to embeddings, not because the system got wiser, but because 'refund status' and 'where is my money' finally stopped looking unrelated."
askNext: What embedding model is being used, and for which task?
How are similarity, ranking, and filtering being evaluated?
What happens when semantically similar items are still operationally irrelevant?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way of mapping data into numbers so retrieval can work by distance, not exact wording.

entries.body: Embeddings are one of the best examples of AI becoming less mysterious when you describe them properly. They are not memory crystals. They are useful numerical representations with clear limits.

entries.title: Enterprise agentic solutions
entries.devilDefinition: Enterprise agentic solutions is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Enterprise agentic solutions is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Scale AI often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Enterprise agentic solutions came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: Enterprise RAG
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that the organisation's knowledge can be retrieved, once the organisation admits where it left it.
entries.plainDefinition: Enterprise RAG is retrieval-augmented generation built over an organisation's internal documents, systems, policies, knowledge bases, and governed data sources.
entries.whyExists: General models do not know private procedures, customer records, contracts, product details, or institutional exceptions. Enterprise RAG tries to connect language models to that material without retraining them for every business question.
entries.misuse: It is sold as institutional memory when the real work is document hygiene, permissions, source freshness, connector reliability, evaluation, and deciding which department owns the answer when two official documents disagree.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means source selection, ingestion pipelines, access control, chunking, ranking, citation grounding, audit trails, evaluation sets, incident handling, and a budget for finding out that the knowledge base was mostly decorative.
entries.example: "The company bought enterprise RAG and learned, with excellent latency, that nobody trusted the handbook."
askNext: Which systems and documents are in scope, and who owns their quality?
How are permissions, freshness, citations, and audit trails enforced?
What evaluations prove that answers improve real decisions rather than demos?
entries.warningLabel: Connecting documents is easier than making them worth connecting.

translations.label: enterprise meaning
translations.text: RAG over internal knowledge, with the controls required for real business use.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A retrieval project that becomes a document quality, permission, and accountability project.

entries.body: Enterprise RAG often begins as a search upgrade and becomes an audit of the company's relationship with its own paperwork.

entries.title: Ephemeral environment
entries.devilDefinition: A temporary machine for agent code that dies after the task, taking its mess—and hopefully its secrets—with it.
entries.plainDefinition: An ephemeral environment is a short-lived runtime (container, VM, or worktree sandbox) created for an agent or job to execute untrusted or experimental work, then destroyed.
entries.whyExists: Agents write and run code. Durable shared machines accumulate risk; ephemeral ones limit persistence of compromise and clutter.
entries.misuse: Called ephemeral while mounting production credentials and long-lived volumes. Destruction without secret hygiene is theatre.
entries.practicalMeaning: Provision minimal identity, no standing prod access, network policy, and automatic teardown. Pass only needed artefacts out.
entries.example: "The ephemeral environment compiled the patch and vanished before anyone could SSH in to 'just check.'"
askNext: What credentials exist inside the environment?
Is teardown guaranteed on failure paths?
What artefacts are exported, and are they scanned?
entries.warningLabel: Short-lived is not the same as low-privilege.

entries.title: Episodic memory
entries.devilDefinition: A carefully curated scrapbook of past sessions, sold as a mind so the product can claim it remembers you rather than your last JSON blob.
entries.plainDefinition: Episodic memory is a memory design that stores discrete interaction episodes — events, sessions, or task traces — so an agent can retrieve relevant past occurrences rather than replaying an entire chat log as permanent identity.
entries.whyExists: Long-running agents need continuity without drowning in raw transcript. Episodes give structure: what happened, when, with whom, and what outcome mattered enough to keep.
entries.misuse: Every chat message is treated as an episode, or episode stores are marketed as consciousness. Without retention policy, provenance, and user control, "memory" becomes an unbounded personal dossier with a friendly UI.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means segmenting work into episodes, summarising or indexing them, retrieving by relevance rather than recency alone, and letting users inspect, edit, or delete what the system claims happened.
entries.example: "The agent's episodic memory recalled the vendor dispute from March and, less helpfully, the joke the user made about resigning."
askNext: What counts as an episode, and who decides what is worth keeping?
Can users see, correct, and delete stored episodes?
Is retrieval scoped by task and permission, or is the whole history always in play?
entries.warningLabel: Stored episodes are not proof of understanding.

translations.label: systems meaning
translations.text: Past work is stored as discrete episodes for later retrieval.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Session history with better packaging, still needing retention rules.

entries.body: Episodic memory is useful when the system remembers the right past and forgets the rest on purpose. Continuity without policy is just surveillance with better nouns.

entries.title: Eval coverage
entries.devilDefinition: The distance between what the dashboard says was tested and what the product can actually do in public.
entries.plainDefinition: Eval coverage describes which behaviours, tasks, user groups, risk areas, languages, tools, and failure modes are actually represented in an evaluation suite.
entries.whyExists: Evaluation results are only meaningful in relation to what was tested. Coverage makes the boundaries of confidence visible.
entries.misuse: It gets hidden behind aggregate scores. A high pass rate says little if the eval suite avoids hard cases, rare users, dangerous tools, or the languages where the product is being sold.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means mapping product capabilities and risks to tests, identifying blind spots, and reporting gaps with the same seriousness as pass rates.
entries.example: "The eval coverage looked excellent until the launch in Portuguese, which had apparently been covered by optimism."
askNext: Which behaviours and user contexts are covered?
Which high-risk areas have no evals yet?
Do aggregate scores hide weak coverage in important segments?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A map of what the evals actually test, and therefore what they do not.

entries.body: Coverage is the honest part of an eval report, assuming it is allowed to speak.

entries.title: Eval dataset
entries.devilDefinition: The spreadsheet everyone trusts until someone asks who chose the rows.
entries.plainDefinition: An eval dataset is a collection of examples, prompts, tasks, labels, expected behaviours, or reference answers used to evaluate an AI model or system.
entries.whyExists: Evaluation needs concrete cases. Datasets make behaviour measurable across releases, models, prompts, tools, and policies.
entries.misuse: They get mistaken for the world. Eval datasets can be stale, biased, contaminated, too easy, too narrow, or quietly shaped by what the team already knows how to measure.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means dataset design, sampling, labelling, versioning, privacy review, contamination checks, and honest reporting of what the dataset does not cover.
entries.example: "The eval dataset said the assistant was excellent at support tickets, provided every customer writes like the person who made the dataset."
askNext: What population or behaviour does this dataset represent?
How was it sampled, labelled, and refreshed?
What important failure modes are absent?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The examples a system is judged against, and therefore a map of what the team values or forgot.

entries.body: Every eval dataset has a worldview. Some are merely quieter about it.

entries.title: Eval gate
entries.devilDefinition: A release blocker that says the model may not ship until the numbers clear the bar—bureaucracy that sometimes saves you.
entries.plainDefinition: An eval gate is a deployment or merge control that requires automated evaluation metrics to meet predefined thresholds before a model, prompt, or agent change is released.
entries.whyExists: Without gates, quality and safety regressions ship on vibes and schedule pressure. Gates encode non-negotiables into the pipeline.
entries.misuse: Set on vanity metrics, or chronically overridden. A gate that always opens is decoration.
entries.practicalMeaning: Choose metrics tied to user harm and product success, version them, limit overrides with audit logs, and keep suites fast enough to run always.
entries.example: "The eval gate blocked a prompt change that doubled toxic completions on a quiet slice."
askNext: Which metrics are blocking versus informational?
Who can override, and is it rare?
How quickly does the suite run in CI?
entries.warningLabel: Gates only work if overrides are exceptional and visible.

entries.title: Eval Harness
entries.devilDefinition: The machinery teams build once they realise strong opinions about model quality are not, in fact, a release process.
entries.plainDefinition: An eval harness is the framework or runner used to execute repeatable evaluations against models, prompts, tools, or agent workflows and collect comparable results.
entries.whyExists: It exists because manual spot checks do not scale, model behaviour drifts, prompts regress quietly, and agent systems fail in more ways than a demo can comfortably admit.
entries.misuse: The harness gets mistaken for truth when it only reflects the cases you bothered to encode. A tidy dashboard is still compatible with spectacular blind spots.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means versioned datasets, scorers, task traces, cost accounting, regression thresholds, and a growing pile of awkward examples nobody wants to drop because they keep catching something embarrassing.
entries.example: "The eval harness did not make the agent good. It merely prevented the team from declaring victory after each plausible-looking run."
askNext: Which real failures are represented in the suite, and which remain folklore?
What metrics are being scored automatically, and what still needs human judgement?
Does the harness catch regressions before release, or just document them after the fact?

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: The repeatable test rig standing between a model change and an avoidable incident.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A structured way to replay known cases often enough that quality becomes an argument with evidence.

entries.body: Every mature stack eventually invents a harness. AI teams simply get there after calling the same failure "surprising" three times.
entries.searchText:
Eval Harness evaluation harness eval runner benchmark harness Agents and workflows Safety and evaluation Model usage evaluation testing agents tooling The machinery teams build once they realise strong opinions about model quality are not, in fact, a release process.
 An eval harness is the framework or runner used to execute repeatable evaluations against models, prompts, tools, or agent workflows and collect comparable results.
 It exists because manual spot checks do not scale, model behaviour drifts, prompts regress quietly, and agent systems fail in more ways than a demo can comfortably admit.
 The harness gets mistaken for truth when it only reflects the cases you bothered to encode. A tidy dashboard is still compatible with spectacular blind spots.
 In practice it usually means versioned datasets, scorers, task traces, cost accounting, regression thresholds, and a growing pile of awkward examples nobody wants to drop because they keep catching something embarrassing.
 "The eval harness did not make the agent good. It merely prevented the team from declaring victory after each plausible-looking run."
 Which real failures are represented in the suite, and which remain folklore? What metrics are being scored automatically, and what still needs human judgement? Does the harness catch regressions before release, or just document them after the fact? Regression suite Offline evaluation Scoring pipeline

entries.title: Eval leakage
entries.devilDefinition: What happens when the exam keeps whispering the answers into the curriculum and everyone still calls the grade objective.
entries.plainDefinition: Eval leakage is the contamination of an evaluation by training data, tuning loops, prompt iteration, or repeated rehearsal in ways that make measured performance look more general than it really is.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because AI systems are improved by repeated exposure to tasks, examples, and feedback. Without a name for leakage, teams can mistake familiarity with the test for competence on the real job.
entries.misuse: It gets invoked every time a benchmark result looks strong. Not every good score is leakage. The phrase is useful when the separation between training and evaluation has genuinely weakened, not when someone merely dislikes the number.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means guarding holdouts, rotating fresh tasks, avoiding public overexposure of narrow eval sets, and checking whether the optimisation loop has quietly learned the taste of the exam instead of the substance of the task.
entries.example: "The leaderboard rise looked impressive until the team realised their bug-fix prompt had been tuned against the very eval cases now serving as proof."
askNext: How clean is the separation between the data used to improve the system and the data used to judge it?
What parts of this eval have been exposed repeatedly to prompt tuning, red-teaming, or release review?
Do we have any fresh or time-shifted checks that the system has not already rehearsed?

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: The measurement has stopped being as independent as the chart implies.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system has learned the exam too well for the exam to remain a trustworthy proxy.

entries.body: Eval leakage is not a moral category. It is a measurement problem with career consequences, which is why people keep trying to discuss it as if it were merely philosophical.

entries.title: Eval Marketplace
entries.devilDefinition: A confidence shop, stocked with benchmark packs, review panels, and certificates of seriousness for teams whose own edge cases remain inconveniently proprietary.
entries.plainDefinition: An eval marketplace is a vendor or platform that sells evaluation datasets, benchmark tasks, scoring rubrics, human review, automated checks, or testing services for AI models and AI systems.
entries.whyExists: It exists because most teams need broader evaluation coverage than they can build alone, and because shared tests can make vendor claims easier to compare. Buying evaluation capacity can be sensible when it complements internal evidence.
entries.misuse: It is misused when a purchased test is treated as proof that the actual product is safe, useful, or production-ready. A marketplace eval may measure a general capability, a tidy benchmark, or someone else's risk appetite, not the failure modes waiting in your workflow.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means procurement, test selection, data licensing, rater quality, leakage checks, benchmark relevance, custom scenario design, and the dull but necessary work of mapping bought evidence to local release gates.
entries.example: "They bought an eval marketplace subscription, then learned that none of the tests involved their refund policy, their users, or their oddly persuasive chatbot."
askNext: Which product decision will this external eval actually inform?
How do the test cases map to our real users, data, and failure modes?
What internal evals still need to exist because nobody can buy our context?
entries.warningLabel: A purchased test still has to test your product.

translations.label: evaluation meaning
translations.text: A market for test sets, scoring systems, review labour, and benchmark infrastructure.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful source of evidence, provided nobody mistakes the receipt for a release decision.

entries.body: The best marketplace evals widen the questions. The worst ones give a nervous team a prettier way to stop asking.

entries.title: Eval-Driven Development
entries.devilDefinition: The radical idea that if your product depends on stochastic behaviour, you should probably measure something before calling the demo a platform.
entries.plainDefinition: Eval-driven development is the practice of using targeted evaluations to guide prompt changes, model selection, tool design, and release decisions for AI systems.
entries.whyExists: Traditional software tests do not fully capture AI behaviour, while pure vibes scale poorly once money, customers, or reputations are involved. Teams need a repeatable way to detect regressions and compare alternatives.
entries.misuse: It is misused when a handful of flattering examples are promoted to an evaluation strategy, or when teams treat proxy metrics as if they were the product itself. An eval can discipline judgement. It cannot replace it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curated test sets, grading rubrics, automated checks, and before-after comparisons tied to real tasks. The mature version influences release gates. The immature version produces a dashboard nobody trusts but everyone screenshots.
entries.example: "Eval-driven development arrived the day they stopped arguing about prompts in Slack and started arguing about failure rates instead."
askNext: Which behaviours matter enough to measure continuously?
Do the evals reflect production tasks, or merely the tasks that are easiest to score?
What happens when the metric improves but the user experience quietly worsens?
entries.warningLabel: A useful discipline, especially when it resists becoming a PowerPoint ritual.

translations.label: team meaning
translations.text: Define the failure cases and score them before changing the system.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Replacing prompt debates with measurable arguments, then discovering the measurements also need supervision.

entries.body: The concept is sound. The danger begins when the organisation assumes that once a number exists, the thinking has been done.

entries.title: Evals
entries.devilDefinition: The discipline of finding out whether the system works before reality does it for you in public.
entries.plainDefinition: Evals are structured evaluations used to measure model or system performance against defined criteria. They may include benchmark tasks, human review, automated checks, red-team cases, regression tests, or task-specific business metrics.
entries.whyExists: Models are stochastic, context-dependent, and often impressive right up until they are deployed. Evals exist to replace anecdote with repeatable evidence and to catch regressions when prompts, models, or orchestration change.
entries.misuse: The word gets thrown around when teams have a benchmark slide and a lot of faith. Real evals are continuous, tied to the actual task, and uncomfortable enough to surface failure. If the evaluation only confirms what the team already hoped, it may be marketing in a lab coat.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curated test sets, scored outputs, human raters, regression suites, production monitoring, and painful arguments about what "good enough" means for this use case. Good evals are part of product operations, not just research hygiene.
entries.example: "They thought the release was safe because the demo looked smoother. The evals later showed the new prompt quietly doubled the rate of invented citations."
askNext: What failure modes does this eval suite actually test?
How often is it run, and what changes trigger regression checks?
Are we measuring benchmark elegance or real task performance?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A disciplined way of checking whether the system still deserves confidence after you changed something.

entries.body: The most revealing evals are usually the least glamorous ones: domain cases, nasty edge conditions, and boring regression checks that stop the product from drifting into fiction.

entries.title: Exception budget
entries.devilDefinition: The number of rescues, overrides, and special-case interventions a workflow can survive before the economics stop pretending to be scalable.
entries.plainDefinition: Exception budget is the practical limit on how many manual recoveries, edge-case interventions, or policy exceptions an AI workflow can absorb before its cost, latency, or risk profile becomes unacceptable.
entries.whyExists: No live workflow is spotless. Some exceptions are normal. The term exists because teams need a way to ask how much awkward rescue work the operating model can afford before the automation story becomes financially or operationally dishonest.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for an invitation to normalise bad quality. An exception budget is not permission to accumulate endless weirdness. It is a way of quantifying how much weirdness the system can tolerate while the underlying problems are fixed or the scope is narrowed.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means counting human rescues, escalations, manual edits, override approvals, and out-of-band clean-up work, then admitting that a workflow needing constant exceptional handling is not cheaper merely because the exceptional labour occurs off the slide.
entries.example: "The automation looked efficient until they counted the exceptions, at which point the budget was revealed to be a person with a headset and no lunch break."
askNext: How many manual rescues or policy exceptions occur per hundred runs, and what do they cost?
Which categories of exception are tolerable, and which indicate the workflow is not yet viable?
Who is carrying the hidden labour created when the budget is exceeded?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The workflow can only need so many special rescues before the business case begins to sweat.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A tolerance limit for how much exceptional human or policy handling the system can afford.

entries.body: Exception budgets are useful because they force a tedious but revealing question: is the system actually scalable, or is it merely delegating mess to a different ledger?

entries.title: Exception handling
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Exception handling names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Digital Worker needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once exception handling entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: Export controls
entries.devilDefinition: Industrial policy wearing a compliance badge: which chips, models, and customers may cross which borders.
entries.plainDefinition: Export controls in AI are legal and regulatory restrictions on transferring certain chips, model weights, software, or related services across jurisdictions, used as tools of national security and industrial strategy.
entries.whyExists: Frontier compute and models became strategic. Governments reached for trade and export law as levers when pure domestic R&D policy felt too slow.
entries.misuse: Reduced to a single embargo headline, or treated as a complete capability freeze. Controls are porous, evolving, and full of licensing nuance.
entries.practicalMeaning: Map your supply chain: chips, cloud regions, model licences, and customer locations. Build compliance review into procurement and product packaging, not only into legal's panic folder.
entries.example: "Export controls turned a GPU purchase into a multi-month geopolitical seminar."
askNext: Which components of your stack are actually controlled goods or services?
Where are models hosted relative to user data residency?
Who owns ongoing monitoring as rules change?
entries.warningLabel: Compliance is not optional infrastructure.

entries.title: Fallback model
entries.devilDefinition: The understudy model that appears when the star is slow, down, or too expensive—sometimes mid-sentence in production.
entries.plainDefinition: A fallback model is a secondary model or endpoint used when the primary model fails, times out, is rate-limited, or is policy-blocked, preserving availability at possible quality cost.
entries.whyExists: Single-model dependence is fragile. Production systems need degraded modes that still answer.
entries.misuse: Configured silently so users never know quality changed, or pointed at a model that cannot perform the same tools and formats.
entries.practicalMeaning: Define trigger conditions, quality expectations, feature parity, and user-visible degradation signals. Test fallbacks as first-class paths.
entries.example: "The fallback model kept the site up and the legal answers creative."
askNext: What exactly triggers fallback?
Does the fallback support the same tools and schemas?
Do dashboards separate primary and fallback quality?
entries.warningLabel: Availability without honesty is a soft outage.

entries.title: Fallback policy
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Fallback policy is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Model Router are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The fallback policy discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Fallback policy is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Fallback policy belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Fallback routing
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: Fallback routing names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around OpenRouter need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said fallback routing as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."

entries.title: Figma
entries.devilDefinition: The design file everyone agrees is the source of truth until code, product, and reality each file a competing claim.
entries.plainDefinition: Figma is a collaborative design platform used for interface design, prototyping, design systems, developer handoff, and increasingly AI-assisted design-to-code workflows through Dev Mode and MCP integrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because teams need a shared place to design, discuss, inspect, and hand off product interfaces without passing static files around like office furniture.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if a neat Figma file guarantees a neat product. Dev Mode, component metadata, and MCP context can improve implementation, but they do not settle product ambiguity, missing states, accessibility, or the difference between a component library and a living app.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Figma means design-system source material, inspectable component context, comments, prototypes, and a growing bridge into coding agents that can read design intent before writing UI.
entries.example: "The agent implemented the Figma screen accurately, including the part where nobody had designed the error state."
askNext: Is the Figma file current enough to guide implementation?
Which design tokens, components, and states are actually represented?
Does the agent receive structured design context, or just a screenshot with confidence?
entries.warningLabel: Design context helps agents. It does not absolve anyone from checking the interface.

translations.label: designer meaning
translations.text: The shared design surface where intent, comments, components, and unresolved arguments gather.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: Useful structured context, as long as the design file has not become a historical document.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A strong handoff and collaboration platform that agents can now query more directly, not a guarantee that the app will make sense.

entries.body: Figma's AI-era importance is not just that designs can become code. It is that design context can become part of the agent's working memory before the first component is written.

entries.title: Filesystem scope
entries.devilDefinition: Which paths the agent may touch—the difference between a coding assistant and ransomware with good intentions.
entries.plainDefinition: Filesystem scope is the policy defining which directories and files an agent may read or write during a task, often enforced via sandboxes, worktrees, or OS permissions.
entries.whyExists: File access is where agents cause durable damage. Scope turns ambient computer access into a defined workspace.
entries.misuse: Set to the user's home directory for convenience. Convenience is how secrets and unrelated repos get rewritten.
entries.practicalMeaning: Default to a task worktree, deny dotfile and secret paths unless required, and log file mutations. Prefer ephemeral copies for experiments.
entries.example: "Filesystem scope kept the agent inside the repo after it tried to 'clean up' SSH keys."
askNext: What is the root the agent can see?
Are secrets mounted into that tree?
How are writes reviewed before merge?
entries.warningLabel: Broad file access is root-adjacent social engineering waiting to happen.

entries.title: Fine-tuning
entries.devilDefinition: Taking a large model and sending it to finishing school so it stops answering like the open internet and starts answering like your use case.
entries.plainDefinition: Fine-tuning is the process of further training a pre-existing model on a narrower dataset so that its behaviour shifts toward a specific domain, style, format, or task.
entries.whyExists: General-purpose models are broad, not bespoke. Fine-tuning exists for cases where prompting alone is too unstable, too expensive, or too weak to reliably shape behaviour.
entries.misuse: It is often sold as the universal answer whenever prompts disappoint. In reality, many problems blamed on a lack of fine-tuning are retrieval problems, product design problems, or evaluation problems. Fine-tuning can help, but it can also overfit and create fresh failure modes.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curating a task-specific dataset, deciding how much of the base model to adapt, training on that smaller dataset, and checking whether the resulting behaviour improved enough to justify the effort and operational complexity.
entries.example: "They did not fine-tune because the team loved custom models; they did it because the required output format had to be boringly consistent."
askNext: What behaviour are we trying to change that prompting cannot hold steady?
What data will teach that behaviour without teaching unwanted shortcuts?
How will we know the tuned model is better, not just narrower?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A targeted attempt to move model behaviour with additional training rather than endless prompt cajoling.

entries.body: Good fine-tuning is usually boring in the best way. It removes repeated prompt hacks and makes behaviour steadier. Bad fine-tuning makes a model narrower, more brittle, and harder to reason about.

entries.title: Fireworks AI
entries.devilDefinition: Yet another inference host optimised so that open models feel as snappy as closed APIs, at least until your traffic pattern arrives.
entries.plainDefinition: Fireworks AI is an inference and model-serving platform focused on fast deployment of open and custom models via API and infrastructure tooling.
entries.whyExists: Developers wanted low-latency open-model endpoints without building a full serving stack. Vendors filled that gap.
entries.misuse: Assumed interchangeable with every other provider. Throughput, features, and model support differ enough to break naive multi-cloud fantasies.
entries.practicalMeaning: Benchmark your models and prompt shapes; check fine-tuning, LoRA, and compound-AI features only if you will use them.
entries.example: "They moved inference to Fireworks AI after the overnight batch made the laptop cry."
askNext: Which model builds are pinned versus floating tags?
What are rate limits and region options?
How do you fail over without rewriting clients?
entries.warningLabel: Host branding is not a latency SLA.

entries.title: FlashAttention
entries.devilDefinition: An IO-aware attention implementation that made long context less of a memory traffic accident on GPUs.
entries.plainDefinition: FlashAttention is a family of exact attention algorithms and kernels that reduce high-bandwidth memory traffic via tiling and recomputation, enabling faster and more memory-efficient attention on GPUs.
entries.whyExists: Standard attention materialises large matrices that crush memory bandwidth. Better kernels unlocked practical longer contexts and larger batches.
entries.misuse: Cited as a product feature users configure. It is infrastructure; correctness and version compatibility matter more than the brand name in slides.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use maintained kernels via your framework, confirm numerical settings, and re-benchmark when CUDA/framework versions change.
entries.example: "FlashAttention cut memory enough to raise batch size without rewriting the model."
askNext: Which FlashAttention version does your stack pin?
Do training and inference both use it where expected?
What is the measured speedup on your shapes?
entries.warningLabel: Kernel choice is part of your reproducibility story.

entries.title: Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE)
entries.devilDefinition: An engineer sent close enough to the customer to turn integration pain into product knowledge, services revenue, or both.
entries.plainDefinition: A Forward Deployed Engineer is an embedded technical role that works directly with customers or field teams to implement, customise, integrate, and adapt software in real operational environments.
entries.whyExists: Complex AI and enterprise systems often fail in the gap between product promises and customer reality. FDEs sit near that gap, translating messy workflows into working implementations and product feedback.
entries.misuse: The role gets romanticised as heroic execution while sometimes masking a product that needs too much bespoke labour to work repeatably.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means customer proximity, integration work, rapid prototyping, domain learning, support escalation, and a constant tension between scalable product and customised delivery.
entries.example: "The FDE made the pilot work by wiring the model into three legacy systems, two spreadsheets, and one undocumented process everyone described as temporary."
askNext: Is the FDE creating reusable product learning or permanent custom glue?
What operational knowledge comes back from the field?
Does this role scale the product, or hide its unfinished parts?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A field-embedded engineer turning customer mess into working software, product feedback, or bespoke dependency.

entries.body: The best FDEs make the product better. The worst cases make the product optional.

entries.title: FP8
entries.devilDefinition: Eight-bit floats that let training and inference pack more math into silicon, until someone forgets which FP8 flavour the stack expected.
entries.plainDefinition: FP8 is a family of 8-bit floating-point formats used to reduce memory and increase throughput in modern training and inference stacks, with multiple variants and scaling strategies.
entries.whyExists: Hardware vendors added FP8 paths as models grew. Software stacks followed to exploit the performance.
entries.misuse: Treated as a single standard. Format details, scaling, and kernel support differ and can silently break numerics.
entries.practicalMeaning: Pin format and library versions, validate loss curves and evals, and confirm accelerators actually run FP8 kernels for your ops.
entries.example: "FP8 training looked fast until the scaler and the checkpoint format disagreed."
askNext: Which FP8 variant and scaling scheme?
Are critical layers kept in higher precision?
Do evals match FP16/BF16 baselines closely enough?
entries.warningLabel: Bit width is not a complete numerical recipe.

entries.title: Frontier Model
entries.devilDefinition: A model said to be at the frontier, which often means "very capable, very expensive, and deeply useful to mention in a fundraising narrative."
entries.plainDefinition: A frontier model is generally understood to mean one of the most capable and resource-intensive models available at a given time, usually near the top end of performance on a range of tasks. It is not a sharply defined scientific category.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because the field needed a way to talk about the leading edge of capability without listing every model individually. It can be useful shorthand when discussing high-end systems, their risks, and their costs.
entries.misuse: It is often treated as a formal badge rather than a loose ranking label. Vendors, commentators, and investors may use it to imply inevitability, superiority, or strategic importance beyond what the actual use case warrants.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a large, expensive model family with broad capability, strong benchmarks, and significant operational or policy attention. For many products, the question is not whether a model is frontier. The question is whether it is necessary.
entries.example: "The company pitched a frontier-model strategy. Operations translated that into a calmer sentence about high cost, external dependency, and unclear product fit."
askNext: Frontier by which metric: benchmark spread, multimodal breadth, tool use, or cost-to-quality ratio?
Does the use case actually need a top-tier model, or just a reliable one?
What trade-offs come with using the most capable model available?
entries.warningLabel: Useful shorthand, not a formal category. Often as much status signal as technical description.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: This company is close enough to the top of the capability race to sound strategically important.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A high-end model near the current performance frontier, whether or not your workload needs that much firepower.

entries.body: The term becomes most useful when followed immediately by the phrase "for what task?" Otherwise it tends to drift into status perfume.

entries.title: FrontierSWE
entries.devilDefinition: A coding benchmark assembled from tasks near the edge of human patience, where an early confident submission is mostly a scheduling decision.
entries.plainDefinition: FrontierSWE is Proximal's ultra-long-horizon benchmark for coding agents on hard real-world technical challenges. Its tasks span implementation, performance engineering, computational science, and machine-learning research, and are designed to test sustained work near the limits of current human and agent ability.
entries.whyExists: Short issue-resolution benchmarks do not measure whether an agent can research an unfamiliar technical domain, optimise systems, run experiments, recover from failed approaches, and validate a result over several hours. FrontierSWE makes those behaviours central.
entries.misuse: A leaderboard rank is treated as a pure model property even though the harness, tool environment, time limit, cost, stopping behaviour, repeated trials, and task-specific verifier all shape the outcome. Spending longer can reveal persistence or merely extend the mistake.
entries.practicalMeaning: Compare results only when the model, harness, task version, tool access, time and token budgets, trial count, correctness gates, and performance scoring match. Inspect trajectories for premature submission, reward hacking, invalid shortcuts, and useful recovery after failed experiments.
entries.example: "The agent spent eight hours on FrontierSWE and submitted the approach it had discovered in minute eleven, now supported by considerably more evidence of confidence."
askNext: Which task category, harness, model, and trial count produced this score?
What correctness gate must pass before performance improvements count?
Did extra runtime improve the solution, recover from failure, or only delay submission?
entries.warningLabel: Model, harness, budget, and verifier jointly produce the rank.

translations.label: evaluation meaning
translations.text: Give coding agents genuinely difficult multi-hour technical work and score the verified result, not the length of the attempt.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the useful mental model is a time-and-quality evaluation surface across heterogeneous technical tasks, which the current catalogue does not depict.

entries.title: Function Calling
entries.devilDefinition: Giving the model permission to request an action, while reminding everyone that requesting an action is not the same as doing it safely.
entries.plainDefinition: Function calling is a pattern where a model is asked to produce structured arguments for a predefined tool or function, and the surrounding application then executes that tool in ordinary software.
entries.whyExists: Models are good at interpreting language but bad at directly mutating real systems without control. Function calling exists to separate decision-making about which action to take from the actual execution of that action.
entries.misuse: It gets described as if the model itself is performing the external action. Usually it is not. The model proposes the call. Your software validates it, executes it, and handles the consequences. If that surrounding code is sloppy, the elegance of the schema will not save you.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means JSON schemas, argument validation, permission controls, retries, and post-tool reasoning. Useful systems treat function calling as structured intent, not an excuse to trust a model near production infrastructure.
entries.example: "The assistant did not refund the customer by itself; it produced a structured request, the service checked policy, and only then did the refund tool run."
askNext: Who validates the arguments before execution?
What tools is the model allowed to call, and under what conditions?
How are failures, partial successes, and risky actions surfaced?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model emits a structured request; the real system still has to decide whether to trust, reject, or execute it.

entries.body: Function calling is one of the clearest places where AI product language can mislead. The impressive part is not merely that the model chose a tool. The important part is whether the surrounding system kept that choice legible and safe.

entries.title: GAIC
entries.devilDefinition: A local AI community, which is what happens when "the ecosystem" stops being a slide and starts needing a venue, a WhatsApp thread, and someone to bring people into the room.
entries.plainDefinition: GAIC stands for Gauteng AI Community: a local community label for people in and around Gauteng who are learning, building, discussing, and applying AI in practical contexts.
entries.whyExists: AI adoption is local long before it is national. Communities like this give practitioners, founders, students, and curious operators a place to compare tools, share context, find collaborators, and translate global AI noise into regional practice.
entries.misuse: By treating the acronym as proof that an ecosystem already exists at scale. A community is only real if people keep showing up, learning from each other, and turning contact into work.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means meetups, demos, informal support, founder conversations, learning loops, and a local signal that AI is being worked through by people close enough to share constraints.
entries.example: "GAIC mattered less as an acronym than as proof that the AI conversation in Gauteng had somewhere to land between LinkedIn enthusiasm and actual projects."
askNext: Who is the community for: builders, founders, students, operators, or all of them?
What does participation produce beyond attendance?
Which local constraints does the community help people solve?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A local AI learning and builder community, useful when it turns enthusiasm into relationships, projects, and shared practice.

entries.body: Communities are infrastructure with worse accounting. When they work, they quietly reduce the cost of starting.

entries.title: Gemini
entries.devilDefinition: A name used for models, apps, and ecosystem positioning, which keeps everyone slightly unsure whether they are comparing software, model families, or brand architecture.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemini is Google's brand used across both AI models and user-facing products. Depending on context, it may refer to a model family, an assistant experience, or a broader product layer within Google's ecosystem.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because Google uses Gemini across several surfaces. Without context, claims about Gemini can blur model behaviour, app experience, workspace integration, and vendor strategy.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if a result seen in one Google surface automatically describes every Gemini model or deployment context. That is rarely true. Tooling, integration, context, and product constraints all change the experience.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means either a Google model family or a Google product experience using those models. Serious comparison requires pinning down which one.
entries.example: "The team said Gemini handled the task well. After some digging, this meant a specific workspace flow, not a portable judgement about every Gemini model."
askNext: Do you mean the Gemini app, a Gemini model family, or a Google product using Gemini behind the scenes?
Which version or surface produced the result?
What tool access or integration context shaped the outcome?
entries.warningLabel: The brand spans models and products. Ask which one is being discussed.
entries.note: Gemini conversations often confuse integrated product behaviour with base model ability. Those are related, but not identical, claims.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A Google-branded AI surface whose exact meaning depends on which product or model someone is referring to.

entries.body: The safest follow-up sentence is short: "Which Gemini?" It rescues a surprising number of meetings.

Gemini Gemini models Gemini app Product and vendor terms Model usage vendor google product A name used for models, apps, and ecosystem positioning, which keeps everyone slightly unsure whether they are comparing software, model families, or brand architecture.
 Gemini is Google's brand used across both AI models and user-facing products. Depending on context, it may refer to a model family, an assistant experience, or a broader product layer within Google's ecosystem.
 The term matters because Google uses Gemini across several surfaces. Without context, claims about Gemini can blur model behaviour, app experience, workspace integration, and vendor strategy.
 It gets treated as if a result seen in one Google surface automatically describes every Gemini model or deployment context. That is rarely true. Tooling, integration, context, and product constraints all change the experience.
 In practice it usually means either a Google model family or a Google product experience using those models. Serious comparison requires pinning down which one.
 "The team said Gemini handled the task well. After some digging, this meant a specific workspace flow, not a portable judgement about every Gemini model."
 Do you mean the Gemini app, a Gemini model family, or a Google product using Gemini behind the scenes? Which version or surface produced the result? What tool access or integration context shaped the outcome?  Gemini conversations often confuse integrated product behaviour with base model ability. Those are related, but not identical, claims.
 Google Gemini app Gemini model families

entries.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash Live
entries.devilDefinition: The voice model meant to make real-time conversation feel less like alternating voicemail and more like software that noticed you were still speaking.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemini 3.1 Flash Live is Google's real-time audio and voice model for live dialogue, voice-first agents, and conversational experiences that need lower latency and stronger turn-taking.
entries.whyExists: Spoken AI feels broken the moment latency, interruptions, or tonal comprehension fall behind human patience. A live audio model exists because real-time interaction is less forgiving than text and far less polite about failure.
entries.misuse: It gets marketed as natural conversation when many deployments are still glorified phone trees with better diction. Smoother audio helps. It does not automatically produce better reasoning, better policies, or a better answer.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means voice agents, customer-support flows, Search Live, and assistant products where handling interruption, noisy environments, and conversational rhythm matters as much as the content of the reply.
entries.example: "Flash Live made the assistant sound far more human, which only sharpened the need for the underlying judgement to improve as well."
askNext: Does it handle interruptions and background noise reliably?
Which gains come from the model itself, and which come from surrounding orchestration?
Are we building a useful voice agent, or simply a smoother queue?
entries.warningLabel: A pleasant voice is not the same thing as good judgement.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A faster, more robust live audio model for building voice-first experiences.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The same assistant ambition, but with less lag between misunderstanding and sound.

entries.body: Realtime AI is a harsher examiner than chat. The pauses are shorter, the mistakes are louder, and the illusion of competence has less time to hide.

entries.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite
entries.devilDefinition: The cheaper model tier whose chief virtue is letting ambitious product managers scale their optimism by the million tokens.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite is Google's lower-cost, lower-latency Gemini 3.1 model for high-volume workloads where throughput and unit economics matter more than maximal frontier capability.
entries.whyExists: Most production AI systems are constrained less by grand theory than by latency budgets, API spend, and how much traffic the business intends to survive. A cheaper fast model tier exists because the invoice also attends the architecture review.
entries.misuse: It gets framed as though cheaper automatically means strategically sufficient. Sometimes it does. Sometimes it simply spreads weaker judgement across more requests and gives procurement a pleasant afternoon.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means translation, moderation, UI generation, bulk inference, and the usual industrial workloads where modest capability improvements are less important than response speed and cost discipline.
entries.example: "Flash-Lite was exactly the right model until someone quietly expanded the use case from 'classify this' to 'think for us'."
askNext: Is the bottleneck latency, token cost, or answer quality?
Are we choosing the smallest model that clears the bar, or the cheapest one that lets the slide deck survive?
What errors become acceptable once the per-request price drops?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A cost-efficient model tier meant for workloads where volume matters more than dramatic cleverness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A sensible default for many production calls, right up to the moment someone asks it to be profound.

entries.body: Model progress now arrives in at least two currencies: capability and affordability. Flash-Lite is the reminder that the second one decides far more deployments than the keynote usually admits.

entries.title: Gemini API
entries.devilDefinition: Gemini API is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemini API is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Gemini API came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: Gemma
entries.devilDefinition: Google's way of demonstrating that it, too, can participate in the open-model conversation without misplacing the rest of the product empire.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemma is Google's family of lighter-weight models released for developers to run, fine-tune, or adapt outside the company's main hosted product surfaces.
entries.whyExists: Many teams want smaller models for local use, private deployment, research, or fine-tuning. Google also benefits from having a developer-facing model family that can circulate beyond closed API products and keep it visible in the open-model ecosystem.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with Gemini, treated as a single model, or described as if "small" automatically means cheap, safe, or easy to deploy. As usual, the useful details live in the specific variant, size, licence terms, and workload.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Gemma usually means a family of relatively lightweight Google models for experimentation, local inference, edge deployment, and fine-tuning work where a giant hosted model would be excessive, costly, or simply inconvenient.
entries.example: "They chose Gemma for the local prototype because the requirement was responsiveness on-device, not a spiritual commitment to the largest model available."
askNext: Which Gemma variant and size are we evaluating?
Does the workload actually benefit from a smaller local model, or are we just allergic to APIs?
How do quality, latency, and privacy trade-offs compare with hosted alternatives on the real task?
entries.warningLabel: Model family, not a single stable answer to the question.

translations.label: builder meaning
translations.text: A practical Google-backed model family for people who need something smaller and more portable than the flagship spectacle.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A lightweight model family that still needs task-level evaluation instead of brand-level trust.

entries.note: Gemma matters partly because it sits in the space between frontier-model marketing and the unromantic realities of shipping smaller systems.
entries.body: The glamour of large models tends to dominate the conversation. The invoices, device limits, and latency targets keep sending people back to families like this.
