entries.title: General agent
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: General agent names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Manus needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once general agent entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: Generative UI
entries.devilDefinition: An interface assembled at the moment of need, sparing the user a wall of text and giving the design system a more immediate crisis.
entries.plainDefinition: Generative UI is an interface pattern in which an AI system chooses, assembles, or produces interactive components in response to a user's request and current context. The result may be selected from trusted components, filled from tool data, or generated more freely at runtime.
entries.whyExists: Conversational answers are poor containers for forms, comparisons, maps, charts, approvals, and multi-step work. A generated interface can present the relevant controls and information for the task instead of forcing every interaction through prose.
entries.misuse: The phrase covers everything from selecting a predefined card to inventing arbitrary front-end code. Treating those as equivalent conceals large differences in accessibility, determinism, security, testing, brand control, and failure behaviour.
entries.practicalMeaning: Sensible systems constrain generation to approved components and schemas, bind components to validated tool results, preserve an accessible fallback, and record which actions require confirmation. Teams must test states the model did not choose during the demo.
entries.example: "The assistant generated a comparison table with a purchase button, neatly compressing research, interface design, and liability into one panel."
askNext: Is the model selecting trusted components, supplying their data, or generating executable interface code?
How are accessibility, validation, permissions, and destructive actions enforced?
What usable fallback appears when generation or a supporting tool fails?
entries.warningLabel: Generated interaction still needs designed constraints.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: Turn model and tool output into task-specific controls without letting every answer become unreviewed software.

entries.body: The function-calling diagram fits because useful generative UI usually begins with a structured request, a real tool result, and a controlled presentation of what came back.

entries.title: GEO
entries.devilDefinition: SEO after a rebrand, a haircut, and a fresh promise to investors.
entries.plainDefinition: An emerging term for trying to increase a brand's visibility inside AI-generated answers, summaries, and recommendation layers rather than only in traditional search engine rankings.
entries.whyExists: Because people noticed that users increasingly ask chat systems instead of typing ten blue links into a search box.
entries.misuse: By pretending it is a wholly new discipline rather than a messy blend of discoverability, authority, structured content, citations, technical hygiene, and brand presence.
entries.practicalMeaning: Creating clear, well-structured, cited, machine-legible content that AI systems and retrieval layers can understand, quote, or prefer.
entries.example: "The agency sold GEO as a revolution, then quietly delivered schema, better copy, stronger pages, and clearer source material."
askNext: Which engines or assistants do they mean?
Are they measuring citations, referral traffic, mentions, or conversions?
Is this genuinely new work, or renamed SEO plus PR?
entries.warningLabel: Often a rebranding exercise with some real technical hygiene hiding underneath.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Make your content clearer, more structured, more citeable, and easier for retrieval layers to find without pretending the entire discipline was born yesterday.

entries.body: The suspicion is usually justified. Much of GEO is indeed old work wearing new shoes. That does not make it fake. It makes it familiar.

entries.title: GGUF
entries.devilDefinition: A file format that lets quantised models travel through the llama.cpp universe without reinventing the weight suitcase each time.
entries.plainDefinition: GGUF is a binary file format for storing models (often quantised) popular in local inference ecosystems such as llama.cpp-compatible tools.
entries.whyExists: Local model distribution needed a practical container for weights, metadata, and quantisation schemes. GGUF became a de facto standard in that niche.
entries.misuse: Assumed universal across all serving stacks. Many cloud engines prefer safetensors or vendor formats instead.
entries.practicalMeaning: Match GGUF quant type to your quality needs, verify checksums, and track which runtime versions read which GGUF features.
entries.example: "The GGUF file fit on the laptop and ran until the quant type met a model it disliked."
askNext: Which quantisation variant is inside the GGUF?
Is the source and checksum trustworthy?
Does your runtime support this GGUF version?
entries.warningLabel: A convenient format is still a supply-chain artefact.

entries.title: GLM
entries.devilDefinition: A Chinese model family that keeps appearing in agent benchmarks to remind the usual shortlist it is not a shortlist by divine right.
entries.plainDefinition: GLM is a model family associated with Z.AI, including flagship long-context and agent-oriented models such as GLM-5.1.
entries.whyExists: It matters because the model market is increasingly global, and GLM sits in the same practical conversation as Qwen, DeepSeek, and other non-US model families competing on coding, agents, context, and serving economics.
entries.misuse: It gets treated either as a single model or as geopolitical shorthand. The useful question is more boring: which GLM version, which deployment path, which workload, and which failure modes?
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means evaluating Z.AI's models for long-horizon coding, agent workflows, tool use, context length, and cost-performance against the better-known frontier suppliers.
entries.example: "The GLM trial was not ideological; it was an attempt to see whether the agent could work longer without turning the repo into theatre."
askNext: Which GLM version is being evaluated, and under which provider or hosting path?
Is the appeal coding, long-context work, agent reliability, price, or regional availability?
Do our own evals confirm the benchmark story?
entries.warningLabel: Model-family name. Not enough information by itself.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Another serious non-US model family to test before pretending the market is tidy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A Z.AI model line whose relevance depends on the exact version and workload.

entries.body: The correct response to a wider model market is not brand panic. It is better evals, which is less exciting and therefore rarer.

entries.title: Golden set
entries.devilDefinition: The trusted examples everyone optimises against until the model learns the family silver by smell.
entries.plainDefinition: A golden set is a curated evaluation dataset treated as a high-quality reference for testing model or system behaviour.
entries.whyExists: Teams need stable examples that represent important behaviours, edge cases, regressions, and quality expectations. A golden set provides a repeatable target for evaluation.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as objective truth. Golden sets age, encode biases, miss new failure modes, and become dangerous when teams tune to them until the test stops measuring general behaviour.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means careful curation, versioning, contamination controls, holdouts, review cycles, and a refusal to let one spreadsheet become the product's conscience.
entries.example: "The model improved on the golden set and worsened everywhere else, a traditional ceremony known as overfitting with applause."
askNext: Who curated the golden set, and what is missing from it?
How is contamination or overfitting monitored?
When is the set refreshed or retired?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A trusted test set, useful until it becomes the thing being memorised.

entries.body: Gold is valuable. It is also soft enough to dent.

entries.title: Google Antigravity
entries.devilDefinition: Google's answer to the question of whether vibe coding needed a launch name with more lift.
entries.plainDefinition: Google Antigravity is Google's agentic development surface for building and editing apps from prompts, with deeper project understanding and integrations for backend, secrets, and deployment-adjacent work.
entries.whyExists: Prompt-to-app demos stop being charming once users want authentication, databases, API keys, multiplayer state, and software that can survive contact with actual requirements. Antigravity exists to move that promise closer to full-stack work.
entries.misuse: It is pitched as though software difficulty now consists mainly of choosing a framework and describing the vibes with conviction. The agent can scaffold more of the stack. It cannot repeal complexity, ownership, or future maintenance.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means Google AI Studio getting more serious about app generation: Firebase wiring, tool use, framework selection, project memory, and a product surface where the agent is meant to feel like a teammate rather than a chat box with keyboard privileges.
entries.example: "Antigravity got the prototype airborne quickly, then left the team to rediscover that landing is its own discipline."

entries.title: Google DeepMind
entries.devilDefinition: The research empire formed when Google looked at one of the most storied AI labs in the world and decided it would also like the rest of the machine under the same roof.
entries.plainDefinition: Google DeepMind is Google's central AI research and frontier-model organisation. It combines the original DeepMind organisation with key Google AI research efforts, and sits behind research programmes and products including Gemini, AlphaFold, robotics work, safety research, and broader frontier-model development.
entries.whyExists: Because people still say "DeepMind" as if it were a free-floating research monastery. It is now a major unit inside Google, with both scientific prestige and the platform power that comes from sitting inside one of the largest technology companies on earth.
entries.misuse: By treating Google DeepMind as either a pure research institution or merely another product division. It is both more interesting and more awkward than that: a frontier lab nested inside a company that wants research breakthroughs to become products at scale.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means Google's long-horizon AI engine: the part of the company responsible for major research advances, frontier models, safety work, and many of the technical foundations that later surface through Gemini and other Google products.
entries.example: "The pitch started with Gemini, but the strategic argument was really about Google DeepMind and whether one company could still dominate both research legitimacy and product distribution."
askNext: Do they mean Google DeepMind the lab, Gemini the model family, or the Gemini app?
Is this a research claim, a product claim, or a capability claim?
Are we discussing scientific output, safety posture, or Google's platform strategy?
entries.warningLabel: Research lab, product engine, and corporate strategy node. Ask which layer the speaker means.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: The place people point when they want the conversation to sound more scientific than commercial.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Google's frontier AI organisation, where research prestige and product ambition share the same building.

entries.note: Google's own framing emphasises responsible AI for broad human benefit, but the practical significance of Google DeepMind is that it is both a frontier research lab and a core engine of product advantage across Google's ecosystem.
entries.body: The old mental model of DeepMind as a separate mythic lab is now mostly nostalgia. The current reality is institutional power with far shorter distance to product.

entries.title: Governance posture
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Governance posture is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Safe Superintelligence are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The governance posture discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Governance posture is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Governance posture belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Governance stack
entries.devilDefinition: The layered arrangement of policies, approvals, logs, and enforcement mechanisms that determines whether the organisation has governance or merely a decorative fondness for the word.
entries.plainDefinition: Governance stack is the combination of policies, review gates, runtime controls, audit logs, escalation paths, and accountability mechanisms used to keep an AI system bounded and answerable in operation.
entries.whyExists: No single control is enough. Policy without enforcement is sentiment. Logs without ownership are archaeology. Approvals without evidence are theatre. The term exists to name the fact that governance is a layered system rather than a solitary document or committee.
entries.misuse: It gets spoken about as if more layers automatically mean more safety. A bloated stack can still be toothless if the controls do not connect, the owners cannot act, or the commercial incentives remain free to ignore the whole arrangement.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means policy rules, tool permissions, approval flows, release gates, monitoring, incident paths, and clear authority over who can pause, roll back, or refuse a system when the risk story starts sounding imaginative.
entries.example: "The governance stack looked impressive on paper. The production system still had no clear owner who could stop a bad rollout before the customers started documenting it."
askNext: Which layer in this stack can actually block or change risky behaviour?
Where do evidence, logs, and approvals connect rather than merely coexist?
Who owns the decision when policy, product pressure, and operational reality disagree?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The combined control system surrounding the model, not the respectable paragraph about it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

entries.title: GPT
entries.devilDefinition: The acronym that began as a model architecture and graduated into a public synonym for whatever the chatbot did this morning.
entries.plainDefinition: GPT stands for Generative Pre-trained Transformer. In current usage it usually refers to transformer-based language models and, more specifically, to OpenAI's GPT-branded model families.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because it names a lineage of models built around pre-training and transformer architectures, but it also became a consumer-facing brand fragment attached to products, APIs, and model tiers.
entries.misuse: It gets used as a generic word for any AI assistant, any large language model, or any answer that arrived in a chat box. That makes comparisons sloppy and lets vendor branding smuggle itself into technical conversation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone is talking about a language model, often an OpenAI model, without yet being precise about product surface, model version, context window, tool access, or pricing.
entries.example: "The procurement note said GPT. Engineering had to ask whether that meant ChatGPT, the OpenAI API, a particular model, or merely the reassuring sound of three capital letters."
askNext: Do we mean GPT as an architecture label, an OpenAI model family, or a product feature?
Which model version, context size, tool environment, and price tier are actually in scope?
Are we comparing a model to a model, or a full product experience to a bare API call?
entries.warningLabel: Often used as a brand-shaped synonym for the whole model category.

translations.label: meeting meaning
translations.text: The AI thing, said with enough confidence to postpone specificity.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A GPT-branded or GPT-like language model whose actual behaviour depends on the exact version and product wrapper.

entries.note: Mini and lightweight GPT-branded variants are usually better treated as model-sizing or pricing choices, not as a separate concept from GPT itself.
entries.body: The acronym is useful when it is precise. It becomes less useful the moment it is asked to cover architecture, brand, product, and folklore in one breath.

entries.title: GPT Image 2
entries.devilDefinition: OpenAI's current image model for people who would like better picture generation, better editing, and one more meeting about whether prompt writing now counts as art direction.
entries.plainDefinition: GPT Image 2 is OpenAI's current image-generation and image-editing model. It accepts text plus image inputs, returns image outputs, and can be used through the Image API or through the Responses API image generation tool.
entries.whyExists: Image systems are now expected to do more than spit out pretty approximations. They need stronger prompt adherence, better editing, higher-fidelity preservation of input images, and a smoother path from one-off generation to iterative product workflows.
entries.misuse: It gets described as though a new image model automatically solves taste, brand judgement, and production readiness. It does not. It mostly means OpenAI has improved the engine while leaving humans in charge of deciding whether the result deserves civilisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means OpenAI's latest image model for generation and edits, with flexible sizing and stronger image-input handling. The operational wrinkle is that the model itself sits behind two different patterns: direct image endpoints such as `v1/images/generations` and `v1/images/edits`, or a mainline model in the Responses API using the `image_generation` tool for multi-turn editing and prompt revision.
entries.example: "They said they had integrated OpenAI's new images v2, which turned out to mean GPT Image 2 plus a mainline model quietly rewriting prompts before the pixels arrived."
askNext: Are you calling the Image API directly, or using the Responses API image generation tool through a mainline model?
Do you need high-fidelity editing of an input image, or just one-shot generation?
Will the workflow break if someone asks for a transparent background, which GPT Image 2 does not currently support?
entries.warningLabel: Useful image model. Still not a substitute for taste or production judgement.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: OpenAI's newest image engine, plus a fresh round of people discovering that better generation does not remove the need for judgement.

entries.note: OpenAI's current docs describe GPT Image 2 as the state-of-the-art image generation model, with the snapshot alias `gpt-image-2-2026-04-21`. The docs also note that when you use image generation through the Responses API, a mainline model such as `gpt-5.4` can revise the prompt and decide whether to generate or edit.
entries.body: The important distinction is not merely new versus old. It is whether you are buying a standalone image endpoint, a conversational editing loop, or simply a shinier excuse to postpone hiring someone with visual judgement.

entries.title: GPT model families
entries.devilDefinition: GPT model families is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: GPT model families is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around ChatGPT and OpenAI often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "GPT model families came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: GPT-Live
entries.devilDefinition: A voice model fast enough to interrupt naturally and polished enough to make the interruption feel like a feature.
entries.plainDefinition: GPT-Live is OpenAI's generation of real-time voice models used for continuous spoken interaction, including ChatGPT Voice. It is designed to handle speech directly with low enough latency for conversation rather than treating voice as a slow exchange of recorded turns.
entries.whyExists: Natural conversation depends on timing, interruption, tone, pacing, and rapid response. A live speech system aims to make those behaviours part of the model interaction instead of stitching together a transcript, text model, and separate voice after each turn.
entries.misuse: "Real-time" is taken to mean instantaneous, human, or safe in every language and acoustic setting. Latency, turn detection, accents, emotion, hallucinations, network quality, and tool delays still determine whether the conversation works.
entries.practicalMeaning: Product teams gain a more fluid spoken interface but must test end-to-end delay, barge-in behaviour, audio quality, transcription or logging policy, disclosure, accessibility, tool-call timing, and graceful fallback under poor network conditions.
entries.example: "GPT-Live answered before the customer had finished, demonstrating both low latency and a convincing grasp of call-centre tradition."
askNext: What end-to-end latency holds under our actual network and tool workload?
How does the system handle interruption, silence, accents, noise, and overlapping speakers?
What audio is retained, transcribed, reviewed, or disclosed to the user?
entries.warningLabel: Conversational latency includes every tool and network hop, not only the model demo.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A low-latency spoken model interface whose naturalness still depends on the entire audio and tool pipeline.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the useful mental model is a duplex, latency-sensitive audio stream; the current catalogue diagrams describe neither timing nor interruption.

entries.title: GPT-Red
entries.devilDefinition: An automated attacker trained to improve the automated defender, because even the adversary now has quarterly objectives.
entries.plainDefinition: GPT-Red is OpenAI's automated red-teaming system for finding and strengthening defences against jailbreaks and prompt-injection attacks. Attacker and defender models improve through adversarial self-play rather than relying only on a fixed library of human-written attacks.
entries.whyExists: Static safety tests age quickly once models, tools, and attack techniques change. An adaptive attacker can search for new failures, while the defender can learn from the attacks that actually succeeded.
entries.misuse: Automated red-teaming is presented as proof that a system is secure. It is still an evaluation and training process shaped by its threat model, tools, incentives, coverage, and stopping conditions; an attacker that did not discover a flaw has not abolished the flaw.
entries.practicalMeaning: Teams can use self-improving attack loops to generate harder safety cases and improve filters or model behaviour. They still need independent testing, human review, production monitoring, access controls, and incident response for attacks outside the training game.
entries.example: "GPT-Red found a new prompt injection overnight, allowing the morning meeting to call it resilience research instead of an incident rehearsal."
askNext: Which attacker capabilities, tools, languages, and target surfaces are inside the threat model?
Does defensive improvement generalise to attacks withheld from the self-play loop?
Who independently tests failures that the attacker and defender may both miss?
entries.warningLabel: A stronger sparring partner is not a security certificate.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Train adaptive attacks and defences together, then test whether either learned anything beyond the training arena.

entries.body: The agent-loop diagram is used because the important mental model is iterative: attack, observe, adapt, defend, and repeat.

entries.title: GPU
entries.devilDefinition: The rectangle of silicon that turns abstract AI ambition into rack diagrams, queueing pain, and a line item large enough to sober up strategy decks.
entries.plainDefinition: A GPU is a highly parallel processor originally built for graphics work that is now widely used to train and serve AI models because it handles the underlying matrix computations efficiently.
entries.whyExists: Because modern AI workloads need a great deal of parallel numerical work, and GPUs became the practical engine for that before most organisations had learned how physical their software plans were.
entries.misuse: By using "GPU" to mean the whole AI stack, or by assuming that access to GPUs automatically solves latency, data quality, product design, or cost discipline. Hardware helps. It does not finish the argument.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a constraint on training speed, inference cost, memory limits, model size, procurement timing, and whether your supposedly simple launch now depends on specialist infrastructure.
entries.example: "The roadmap called it an AI feature. Finance called it six more GPUs and several uncomfortable follow-up questions."
askNext: Is the bottleneck raw compute, memory, networking, or utilisation?
Do we need premium GPU capacity, or are we overbuying for the workload?
What part of the user value actually depends on this level of hardware?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The compute unit that decides whether your AI ambitions are shipping, waiting, or becoming procurement folklore.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A parallel processor that underwrites much of modern AI training and inference, along with a large share of the bill.

entries.note: The important distinction is not only which GPU you have, but whether the surrounding system can keep it fed, cooled, scheduled, and worth the money.
entries.body: A GPU is often described as the engine of AI. More usefully, it is the point where the conversation stops being metaphor and starts being capacity planning.

entries.title: GPU broker
entries.devilDefinition: A concierge service for accelerators, provided the concierge also owns part of the waiting list.
entries.plainDefinition: A GPU broker is an intermediary that helps organisations find, reserve, lease, or buy access to GPU capacity across clouds, neoclouds, data centres, private clusters, or specialised compute marketplaces.
entries.whyExists: It exists because demand for accelerators is uneven, expensive, and geographically constrained. Buyers often need capacity faster than ordinary procurement or cloud quotas can provide it, while sellers need committed utilisation for hardware that does not enjoy being idle.
entries.misuse: It is misused when brokering is treated as a compute strategy. A broker can find supply, but does not magically remove cluster reliability, data movement, compliance, support quality, quota limits, contract lock-in, or the awkward fact that everyone else also wanted the same chips.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means checking availability windows, GPU type, region, network performance, storage, support terms, minimum commitments, cancellation rights, egress costs, security posture, and whether the quoted capacity exists outside the spreadsheet.
entries.example: "The GPU broker found capacity by Friday, which gave finance exactly three days to discover what the word committed meant."
askNext: What exact GPU type, region, duration, network, and support level are being offered?
Is the broker providing verified capacity, marketplace introductions, financing, or procurement theatre?
What happens if the workload needs more time, more data movement, or less optimism?
entries.warningLabel: Finding capacity is not the same as owning a compute plan.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Someone is helping the buyer find GPU capacity in a constrained market.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A faster path to a quote, followed by the same old questions about cost, reliability, and control.

entries.body: The broker can shorten the search. It cannot make scarce hardware behave like office stationery.

entries.title: GPU cloud
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: GPU cloud names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Neocloud need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said gpu cloud as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."

entries.title: Graph RAG
entries.devilDefinition: Retrieval that draws little relationship diagrams so the system can pretend it understands structure rather than only nearest neighbours.
entries.plainDefinition: Graph RAG is a retrieval pattern that uses a knowledge graph—entities and relations—alongside or instead of pure vector similarity to fetch context for generation.
entries.whyExists: Vector search is good at 'aboutness' and weak at multi-hop structure. Graphs reintroduce explicit relationships that embeddings blur.
entries.misuse: Sold as automatic enterprise understanding. Building and maintaining a useful graph is the hard part; the acronym is the easy part.
entries.practicalMeaning: Start from the queries that need multi-hop relations. Measure whether graph expansion improves answer quality enough to justify extraction and graph ops cost.
entries.example: "Graph RAG found the entity link; the edge was last updated when the org chart still lied."
askNext: Who maintains the graph, and how stale is it?
Which queries actually need relational traversal?
How do you evaluate multi-hop answers beyond vibe demos?
entries.warningLabel: A graph of bad extractions is structured confusion.

translations.text: Graph RAG is a retrieval pattern that uses a knowledge graph—entities and relations—alongside or instead of pure vector similarity to fetch context for generation.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Start from the queries that need multi-hop relations.

entries.body: Graph RAG is structure with a retrieval budget. Structure still rots.
entries.searchText:
Graph RAG GraphRAG graph-based RAG Retrieval and memory Model usage rag knowledge-graph retrieval structure Retrieval that draws little relationship diagrams so the system can pretend it understands structure rather than only nearest neighbours.
 Graph RAG is a retrieval pattern that uses a knowledge graph—entities and relations—alongside or instead of pure vector similarity to fetch context for generation.
 Vector search is good at 'aboutness' and weak at multi-hop structure. Graphs reintroduce explicit relationships that embeddings blur.
 Sold as automatic enterprise understanding. Building and maintaining a useful graph is the hard part; the acronym is the easy part.
 Start from the queries that need multi-hop relations. Measure whether graph expansion improves answer quality enough to justify extraction and graph ops cost.
 "Graph RAG found the entity link; the edge was last updated when the org chart still lied."
 Who maintains the graph, and how stale is it? Which queries actually need relational traversal? How do you evaluate multi-hop answers beyond vibe demos? Rag Hybrid search

entries.title: Grok
entries.devilDefinition: The assistant brand that insists it is not merely an AI product but a personality, a posture, and occasionally a geopolitical plot device.
entries.plainDefinition: Grok is xAI's assistant brand, covering both end-user chatbot experiences and model offerings exposed through xAI products and APIs. Depending on context, people may mean the Grok app, Grok on X, a specific Grok model, or the broader branded assistant experience.
entries.whyExists: Because the name "Grok" is used across product surfaces, model releases, consumer interfaces, and enterprise offerings. Without separating those layers, people end up comparing a brand impression to a technical system and calling it analysis.
entries.misuse: By treating Grok as one stable thing. It can mean the app, the model family, the social-media-integrated assistant, or an API-accessible model with specific tools and constraints. Those are related, but not interchangeable.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means xAI's consumer and developer-facing assistant stack: real-time search posture, distinct brand tone, and a growing family of models and tools wrapped in one loud identity.
entries.example: "They said Grok was better, which turned out to mean they liked the product's tone, real-time feel, and distribution inside familiar surfaces, not that they had run a clean model evaluation."
askNext: Do you mean the Grok product, a specific Grok model, or the experience inside X or grok.com?
Is the appeal about capability, personality, distribution, or real-time data access?
What part of the result came from the model versus the surrounding product scaffolding?
entries.warningLabel: Brand name. Ask whether the speaker means the product, the model family, or the surrounding ecosystem.

translations.label: social meaning
translations.text: The assistant people often evaluate as a cultural object before they evaluate it as software.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A branded xAI assistant experience spanning multiple surfaces, models, tools, and moods.

entries.note: xAI's current product pages frame Grok as a truth-seeking companion with strong reasoning, coding, and multimodal capabilities. As of February 2, 2026, the surrounding corporate context also changed when xAI announced it had joined SpaceX, so the Grok brand now sits inside an even larger machinery of distribution and spectacle.
entries.body: Some assistant brands want to disappear into utility. Grok prefers to arrive already wearing a point of view.

entries.title: Grok Enterprise
entries.devilDefinition: Grok Enterprise is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Grok Enterprise is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Grok often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Grok Enterprise came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
askNext: What exact meaning of Grok Enterprise is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.

translations.label: procurement meaning

entries.title: Groq
entries.devilDefinition: The company people name when they want tokens so fast that product managers start believing latency was only a personality problem.
entries.plainDefinition: Groq is an inference company known for low-latency serving of open-weight models on its LPU (Language Processing Unit) hardware and cloud API, competing on speed and price rather than training frontier models.
entries.whyExists: Open models still need someone to run them quickly and cheaply. Specialised inference hardware and software became a product category of their own.
entries.misuse: Confused with model creators, or treated as proof that any model is 'faster' rather than that a particular serving stack is. Hardware advantage is workload- and model-dependent.
entries.practicalMeaning: Evaluate Groq as an inference provider: latency percentiles, model catalogue, rate limits, regional availability, and cost against alternatives for your actual traffic shape.
entries.example: "They switched the demo to Groq so the chatbot would answer before the slide advanced."
askNext: Which models and context lengths are actually available on the endpoint you plan to use?
How do p95 latency and cost compare on your prompt mix, not a vendor slide?
What is the failover if the provider is down or rate-limits you?
entries.warningLabel: Vendor speed is not model intelligence.

translations.text: Groq is an inference company known for low-latency serving of open-weight models on its LPU (Language Processing Unit) hardware and cloud API, competing on speed and price rather than training frontie

entries.title: Groundedness
entries.devilDefinition: The virtue of staying tethered to the documents you were given, as opposed to freestyling with academic posture.
entries.plainDefinition: Groundedness means generated answers remain supported by provided or retrieved evidence rather than unconstrained model priors. It is related to, but not identical with, citation faithfulness and factuality against the world.
entries.whyExists: Enterprise RAG promised answers 'from your data.' Teams needed a measurable property for that constraint.
entries.misuse: Claimed whenever retrieval runs. A system can retrieve, ignore the evidence, and still sound corporate.
entries.practicalMeaning: Score answers against evidence sets, penalise unsupported spans, and design prompts/tools that force extract-then-generate patterns for high-stakes domains.
entries.example: "Groundedness looked fine in the demo corpus and collapsed on the first messy policy PDF."
askNext: What is the evidence set for each answer?
Do you allow abstention when evidence is weak?
Is groundedness evaluated automatically, by humans, or by hope?
entries.warningLabel: Grounded in documents is not the same as true in the world.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Groundedness means generated answers remain supported by provided or retrieved evidence rather than unconstrained model priors.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Score answers against evidence sets, penalise unsupported spans, and design prompts/tools that force extract-then-generate patterns for high-stakes domains.

entries.body: Groundedness is a leash. Useful leashes still need length limits and inspections.

entries.title: Grounding
entries.devilDefinition: The effort to keep a model's answer attached to evidence rather than to whatever polished sentence was statistically easiest to generate.
entries.plainDefinition: Grounding refers to tying a model's output to external evidence, context, or real-world signals so that the answer is based on something more reliable than the model's prior patterns alone.
entries.whyExists: Language models can sound certain even when they are improvising. Grounding exists to improve faithfulness by connecting answers to documents, databases, observations, or tool results that bear on the question.
entries.misuse: It gets used as a prestige synonym for "we attached some documents". That is not enough. If the evidence is irrelevant, stale, contradictory, or ignored by the prompt design, the system is not meaningfully grounded.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means retrieval, citation, tool outputs, or structured data being incorporated in a way that materially constrains the answer. Good grounding is less about adding more text and more about adding the right evidence with clear priority.
entries.example: "The grounded version of the assistant did not just answer more cautiously; it cited the actual policy entry it used and admitted when retrieval came back thin."
askNext: What source is the answer grounded in?
How is the system encouraged or required to use that evidence faithfully?
What happens when the evidence is missing, conflicting, or low quality?
entries.note: In some research contexts, grounding can refer more broadly to connecting model symbols to the world. In product discussions, it usually means anchoring outputs to evidence or tool results.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The answer has some evidentiary anchor outside the model's own priors, assuming the system uses that anchor properly.

entries.body: Grounding is one of the most practical words in the field when used well. It is also one of the easiest to fake with decorative citations.

entries.title: gstack
entries.devilDefinition: The proposition that one founder, one agent shell, and enough specialised slash commands can now impersonate a small product org with alarming speed.
entries.plainDefinition: gstack is Garry Tan's open-source workflow and skill stack for Claude Code and related agent hosts, packaged as a large set of opinionated roles and commands for planning, coding, review, QA, security, release, and retrospectives.
entries.whyExists: Because agent users do not just want a model. They want a repeatable operating system for product work: who reviews, who tests, who ships, and what sequence keeps the chaos looking intentional.
entries.misuse: By talking as if the stack itself is a substitute for product judgement, engineering taste, or organisational memory. A thick layer of specialist prompts can accelerate work. It cannot abolish the need to know what good looks like or who is accountable when it is not.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means turning an agent shell into a highly structured software-factory workflow, with preset roles for founder review, engineering review, design critique, browser QA, security audit, release management, and post-mortems.
entries.example: "The impressive part of gstack was not that it made one person look like a team. The impressive part was how much of the team's ceremony it compressed into commands."
askNext: Which parts are genuinely workflow discipline, and which parts are elaborate prompt theatre?
Does the stack improve outcomes on a real repo, or merely increase the amount of agentic motion?
Who still owns the decisions once the virtual org chart starts speaking?
entries.warningLabel: Useful when it enforces discipline. Less useful when it merely industrialises confidence.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to turn one competent builder into something that looks suspiciously like a staffed startup.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A strongly opinionated operating layer for AI-assisted software work, built out of reusable skills and process choreography.

entries.note: Garry Tan positions gstack as his personal answer to shipping with agent tooling: a public software-factory kit with role-specific commands, browser QA, review loops, and release mechanics. The interesting question is not whether it feels powerful. The interesting question is whether the structure holds up once the repo gets messy, the product gets real, and the novelty wears off.
entries.body: gstack is notable because it stops pretending the agent is just "helping with code". It formalises the wider ambition: compressing a startup's rituals into reusable command surfaces and seeing how much of the organisation can be simulated before management notices.

entries.title: Guardrail bypass
entries.devilDefinition: The route around the impressive control that was described as a wall and installed as a suggestion.
entries.plainDefinition: A guardrail bypass is any method that causes an AI system to avoid, ignore, route around, or defeat a safety, policy, permission, or validation control.
entries.whyExists: Guardrails are layered into prompts, policies, tools, classifiers, interfaces, and workflows. Bypasses name the ways those layers fail under adversarial input, ambiguous context, bad integration, or ordinary product pressure.
entries.misuse: It gets framed as a clever jailbreak trick when the more expensive version is an architectural gap: the model can still call the wrong tool, see the wrong data, or be trusted after failing a check.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means testing controls as systems, not slogans, and assuming users, documents, tools, and incentives will all look for the path of least resistance.
entries.example: "The guardrail bypass was not a magic phrase. It was a support workflow that let the assistant perform the action after the denial message."
askNext: Which guardrail was bypassed and at what layer?
Was the bypass caused by prompting, routing, permissions, or product logic?
What still limits harm after the first control fails?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A control failed somewhere, and the interesting question is which layer still stopped damage.

entries.body: Most bypasses are less cinematic than advertised. This does not make them less embarrassing.

entries.title: Guardrails
entries.devilDefinition: The collection of fences, checks, filters, and sternly worded hopes designed to stop a model from wandering into places the product team cannot afford.
entries.plainDefinition: Guardrails are mechanisms used to constrain or monitor model behaviour, such as policy filters, tool permissions, schema validation, prompt rules, moderation systems, human approvals, or output checks.
entries.whyExists: Models are flexible in exactly the ways that make product owners nervous. Guardrails exist to narrow behaviour into something safe, compliant, or at least operationally manageable.
entries.misuse: The term gets invoked as though one moderation layer solves the entire problem. Real guardrails are layered, task-specific, and never total. A vague promise of guardrails often means someone has remembered risk late in the process and is trying to sound calmer than they feel.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a stack of constraints before, during, and after the model call: restricted tools, validation logic, refusal policies, monitoring, and human review for higher-risk actions. The strongest guardrail is often reducing what the system is allowed to do in the first place.
entries.example: "The useful guardrail was not the disclaimer. It was the rule that stopped the assistant from sending anything externally without a human sign-off."
askNext: What specific failure mode is each guardrail intended to catch?
Which constraints happen before generation, during generation, and after generation?
What evidence shows the current guardrails work on real edge cases?
entries.warningLabel: A vague promise of guardrails is not a safety strategy.

translations.label: compliance meaning
translations.text: The controls we hope will keep the system inside acceptable boundaries.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A layered set of restrictions and checks, only as good as their coverage and enforcement.

entries.body: Guardrails work best when paired with scope discipline. If you keep widening the task, you are also widening the space the guardrails must cover.

entries.title: GUI agent
entries.devilDefinition: An agent that clicks buttons like a tired intern, except the intern never had root-adjacent access to your desktop session.
entries.plainDefinition: A GUI agent operates graphical interfaces by perceiving screens and issuing clicks, typing, and navigation actions—broader than browser-only agents and including desktop apps.
entries.whyExists: Many critical systems only expose GUIs. Automation that cannot click is blocked by legacy software.
entries.misuse: Given broad desktop control without sandboxing. UI automation is brittle and high blast radius.
entries.practicalMeaning: Constrain apps and accounts, prefer APIs when available, record action traces, and require confirmations for irreversible UI flows.
entries.example: "The GUI agent completed the form and also dismissed three security prompts as clutter."
askNext: Which applications and accounts are in scope?
How do you recover from UI changes?
What requires human confirmation?
entries.warningLabel: If a human can click it, an unconstrained agent can too.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: A GUI agent operates graphical interfaces by perceiving screens and issuing clicks, typing, and navigation actions—broader than browser-only agents and including desktop apps.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Constrain apps and accounts, prefer APIs when available, record action traces, and require confirmations for irreversible UI flows.

entries.body: GUI agents automate the interface layer. The interface layer includes every dangerous button.

 Many critical systems only expose GUIs. Automation that cannot click is blocked by legacy software.
 Given broad desktop control without sandboxing. UI automation is brittle and high blast radius.

entries.title: Hallucination
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a model says something false with the calm, polished confidence of a consultant who will never have to implement it.
entries.plainDefinition: In AI, a hallucination is an output that is incorrect, fabricated, unsupported, or inconsistent with the source material or task constraints. For language models, this can include made-up facts, invented citations, false summaries, or bogus tool arguments.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because generative models produce plausible sequences, not guaranteed truth. They can sound coherent while being wrong, especially when asked beyond their evidence, given poor context, or rewarded for fluent completion rather than faithful reporting.
entries.misuse: It gets used too loosely for every kind of model failure. Not every bad answer is a hallucination. Some outputs are simply outdated, misclassified, poorly retrieved, ambiguously prompted, or badly evaluated. Precision helps because the mitigation often depends on the failure mode.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means you need better task design, stronger grounding, narrower permissions, tighter output constraints, or human review. It also means you should stop treating eloquence as a proxy for truth.
entries.example: "The model produced a five-paragraph answer, three invented legal references, and the exact tone of someone who hoped nobody would check."
askNext: Was the output unsupported by evidence, or was the evidence retrieval itself broken?
What level of factual reliability does this workflow actually require?
What safeguards exist before the answer reaches a customer, regulator, or executive?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model generated something that sounds acceptable but does not survive contact with evidence.

entries.body: Hallucinations are not shocking side effects of consciousness. They are ordinary failure modes of systems built to generate likely continuations. Treat them like engineering problems, not supernatural events.

entries.title: Hallucination rate
entries.devilDefinition: A percentage that sounds scientific until you ask which task, which judge, and which definition of 'made up.'
entries.plainDefinition: Hallucination rate is a measured frequency of fabricated or unsupported claims in model outputs, meaningful only relative to a defined task, evidence set, and evaluation method.
entries.whyExists: Teams needed a metric for factual failure. Rates communicate risk better than anecdotes—when defined carefully.
entries.misuse: Compared across papers with different judges and tasks as if universal. A single number without protocol is marketing.
entries.practicalMeaning: Publish the protocol with the rate: domain, sampling, judge (human/model), and error taxonomy. Track rates per product surface.
entries.example: "Hallucination rate fell on the news benchmark and rose on internal policy questions."
askNext: What counts as a hallucination in this measurement?
Is the judge calibrated against humans?
Which product flows inherit this rate?
entries.warningLabel: A rate without a method is a vibe with a percent sign.

entries.title: Handoff policy
entries.devilDefinition: The rulebook for deciding when the clever system should stop being clever and give the problem to someone or something else.
entries.plainDefinition: A handoff policy defines when and how work moves between agents, tools, workflows, or humans, including what context, authority, and accountability move with it.
entries.whyExists: Agentic systems rarely complete every task in one clean stretch. They encounter uncertainty, missing permission, specialist work, safety limits, or failures that require another actor. A handoff policy prevents that transfer from becoming improvisation with logs.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for any mention of "human in the loop" or "escalation". A real handoff policy specifies triggers, payloads, ownership, timeouts, and what the receiving party is expected to do. A vague instruction to "ask a person" is not a policy.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means routing rules, escalation criteria, summary formats, state transfer, approval requirements, fallback paths, and a clear answer to who owns the task after the handoff occurs.
entries.example: "The support agent had a handoff policy for billing disputes, which was fortunate because its earlier strategy was to apologise until the invoice felt seen."
askNext: What condition triggers the handoff?
What context and authority move to the next agent, tool, or human?
Who owns the outcome after the handoff, and how is failure reported?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A transfer rule that preserves context, authority, and accountability.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The difference between escalation and throwing the problem over a wall with nicer JSON.

entries.body: Handoff policy is where autonomy admits that division of labour still exists.

entries.title: Hermes Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A self-hosted agent for people who thought OpenClaw was a good start and then asked whether the assistant could remember, delegate, schedule, browse, and live on a server.
entries.plainDefinition: Hermes Agent is an open-source, MIT-licensed autonomous server agent from Nous Research, positioned around persistent memory, auto-generated skills, scheduled automations, subagents, sandboxing, browser control, and multi-channel access.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the agent category is moving from chat windows toward always-on systems that can remember projects, run unattended jobs, delegate work, and operate across communication surfaces.
entries.misuse: It gets tempting to describe the whole package as a digital worker and stop there. The useful conversation is less romantic: what permissions it has, where it runs, which sandboxes apply, what it remembers, and how unattended work is audited.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a self-hosted agent stack for users who want persistent automation, multi-channel interaction, browser and tool access, parallel subagents, and more control than a purely hosted chat product allows.
entries.example: "Hermes Agent was attractive because it lived on their server; the security team then asked the traditional follow-up question of what, exactly, had been invited to live there."
askNext: Which channels, tools, sandboxes, and memories are enabled?
What can it do unattended, and how are approvals, logs, and rollbacks handled?
Is self-hosting reducing risk, or merely moving the risk into a room you now own?
entries.warningLabel: Self-hosted does not mean self-governing.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: An always-on agent stack that wants server privileges, memory, and a proper adult permission model.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An OpenClaw-adjacent self-hosted agent with persistent memory, skills, subagents, automations, and browser control.

entries.body: Hermes is interesting because it makes the agent argument concrete. Once the assistant has memory, schedules, channels, sandboxes, and a browser, the debate leaves the keynote and enters operations.

entries.title: High-volume inference
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: High-volume inference names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said high-volume inference as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."

What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Technical vocabulary is not implementation evidence.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A concrete implementation concern wearing a compact label.

entries.body: High-volume inference belongs in the dictionary because implementation detail changes the product conversation. The useful question is not whether the term sounds modern, but what constraint, cost, interface, or failure mode it names.

entries.title: Hosted MCP
entries.devilDefinition: MCP with someone else's uptime promise and your own new questions about tenancy, trust, and the bill.
entries.plainDefinition: Hosted MCP refers to MCP servers or MCP-compatible integrations run as managed infrastructure by a vendor, platform, or central internal team.
entries.whyExists: Teams do not always want to run every connector themselves. Hosted MCP can reduce setup work, centralise maintenance, and make integrations easier to distribute across clients or organisations.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as simplicity while quietly moving the hard questions into authentication, data residency, logging, tenancy, versioning, and vendor dependency.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means deciding who operates the server, who can inspect its behaviour, where data flows, how permissions are enforced, and what happens when the hosted layer changes.
entries.example: "Hosted MCP saved the team from maintaining the connector, then introduced a procurement meeting so the universe could stay balanced."
askNext: Who operates the hosted MCP server and under what trust model?
What data crosses the hosted boundary?
Can we pin versions, audit calls, and disable capabilities quickly?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A managed MCP server that lowers setup friction while raising trust and operating questions.

entries.body: Hosted infrastructure is never only hosted. It is hosted somewhere, by someone, under terms someone should probably read.

entries.title: HP IQ
entries.devilDefinition: HP IQ is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: HP IQ is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Humane often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "HP IQ came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: Human escalation
entries.devilDefinition: The moment the workflow admits that a person is no longer a decorative safeguard but the next system dependency.
entries.plainDefinition: Human escalation is the explicit handoff from an automated or agentic workflow to a person when risk, ambiguity, policy, or failure conditions exceed what the system is allowed to handle alone.
entries.whyExists: Not every problem should stay inside the loop. Some cases need judgement, authority, empathy, or accountability that the automated path does not possess. The term exists so teams can talk about the handoff itself rather than vaguely gesturing at human oversight.
entries.misuse: It gets collapsed into pious "human in the loop" language without asking who the human is, what context they receive, or whether they can actually resolve the case. An escalation path that dumps confusion onto a queue is still confusion, only with payroll.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means thresholds, queues, summaries, owner assignment, response targets, and the awkward realisation that a good escalation path needs product design, staffing, and operational discipline rather than merely a button labelled "send to human".
entries.example: "The escalation was technically successful. A confused customer, an under-briefed reviewer, and a missing audit trail had all been promoted to the same inbox."
askNext: What exact condition triggers escalation rather than another automated attempt?
What evidence and context reach the human who inherits the case?
Can the escalated person actually change the outcome, or merely absorb blame?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The automated path has reached the edge of its mandate and is now someone else's queue.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A controlled transfer of decision-making responsibility from the workflow to a person.

entries.body: Human escalation is valuable precisely because it is not automatic triumph. It is the point where the system confesses what it cannot safely finish on its own.

entries.title: Human evaluation
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Human evaluation names the nearby AI idea that shows up when LLM-as-a-Judge needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once human evaluation entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: Human in the Loop
entries.devilDefinition: The employee retained to absorb liability after management announces that the future requires fewer employees.
entries.plainDefinition: A workflow design in which a person reviews, approves, corrects, or intervenes in an automated system's output before or during action.
entries.whyExists: Because some decisions remain too costly, risky, or ambiguous to automate blindly. Human review is how organisations keep accountability attached to a named person rather than a press release.
entries.misuse: By invoking it as a magic safety phrase when the human is undertrained, overloaded, or expected to rubber-stamp machine output at impossible speed.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means queues, review fatigue, awkward staffing maths, and the discovery that "just have a human check it" is not a design but a labour budget.
entries.example: "They said the workflow was safe because it kept a human in the loop. The human, regrettably, was checking six hundred cases a day."
askNext: What authority does the reviewer actually have to stop or change the system's output?
How many cases can one person review before judgement degrades into gesture?
Is the human improving quality, or merely carrying blame?

translations.label: compliance meaning
translations.text: A reassuring phrase that sounds better than 'we still need staff to catch the machine's mistakes'.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A human checkpoint whose quality depends on staffing, authority, and time.

entries.body: If the loop is moving too quickly for thought, the human is decorative.

entries.title: Human on the loop
entries.devilDefinition: A human who watches the automation with one eye while the other eye answers Slack—accountability with optional attention.
entries.plainDefinition: Human-on-the-loop describes oversight where a person monitors an automated or agentic system and can intervene, without approving every individual step as in human-in-the-loop designs.
entries.whyExists: Full step approval does not scale; full autonomy scares risk owners. On-the-loop is the middle governance posture.
entries.misuse: Used to claim human control while monitoring is understaffed or alerts are unreadable. Presence without power is fiction.
entries.practicalMeaning: Define what the human can see, pause, and reverse; set alert budgets; measure intervention latency. Do not equate a dashboard with control.
entries.example: "Human-on-the-loop looked robust until the only on-call was in a meeting without pages."
askNext: What interventions are possible in real time?

entries.title: Human Taste
entries.devilDefinition: The increasingly expensive ability to notice that the machine has technically produced something and practically produced nothing.
entries.plainDefinition: Human judgement applied to selecting, editing, rejecting, sequencing, refining, or contextualising generated output.
entries.whyExists: Because automation scales production faster than it scales discernment.
entries.misuse: By mystifying it. Human taste is not just artistic instinct. It can be operational, commercial, ethical, strategic, or technical.
entries.practicalMeaning: A person knows when the answer is wrong, the image is tacky, the copy is off-brand, the feature is unnecessary, or the code should be rewritten rather than patched.
entries.example: "The workflow was mostly automated. The value was still in the person who knew which three outputs were worth keeping."

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person notices where automation has produced output without producing quality, relevance, or judgement.

entries.title: Humane
entries.devilDefinition: A beautifully marketed warning that futuristic hardware demos and durable product-market fit are not the same thing.
entries.plainDefinition: Humane was the startup behind the AI Pin, a wearable AI device that aimed to replace or reduce dependence on the smartphone. The product drew major attention, but Humane later wound down the consumer AI Pin service, and HP announced in February 2025 that it was acquiring key Humane AI capabilities, including the Cosmos platform, talent, and intellectual property.
entries.whyExists: Because Humane became shorthand for both the ambition and the fragility of early AI-device optimism.
entries.misuse: By treating Humane as proof that all AI hardware is doomed, rather than evidence that weak utility, awkward execution, pricing friction, and overhype can kill even well-funded products.
entries.practicalMeaning: A case study in what happens when storytelling outruns product usefulness. Also a reminder that hardware does not get to be vague for long. Consumers carry the thing, charge the thing, and notice when the thing does not justify itself.
entries.example: "The pitch was post-phone computing. The reality was a lesson in how little patience consumers have for expensive inconvenience."
askNext: Was the failure about AI itself, hardware design, pricing, latency, or practical utility?
Did Humane fail because the idea was wrong, or because the implementation was weak?
What, if anything, survives from the vision?
entries.warningLabel: Humane is now more useful as a cautionary reference than as a live consumer platform.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A beautifully framed product vision that did not survive the ordinary cruelty of consumer reality.

entries.note: Humane's consumer support materials said AI Pin cloud-connected features would stop working on February 28, 2025. HP separately announced the acquisition of key Humane capabilities on February 18, 2025.
entries.body: Humane deserves precision because it is too easy to turn into a morality play. It did not prove that AI devices are impossible. It proved that theatrical ambition does not excuse weak everyday utility.

entries.title: Hybrid search
entries.devilDefinition: Using keywords and vectors together so neither pure keyword pedantry nor pure vibes-based retrieval has to face the user alone.
entries.plainDefinition: Hybrid search combines lexical retrieval (for example BM25) with dense vector retrieval, usually via score fusion or reciprocal rank fusion, to improve recall and precision for RAG and search systems.
entries.whyExists: Sparse and dense retrievers fail differently. Hybrid systems catch exact identifiers and fuzzy semantics in one pipeline.
entries.misuse: Configured once with default weights and never re-tuned. Bad fusion can be worse than the better single method.
entries.practicalMeaning: Tune fusion weights on real queries, keep metadata filters, and evaluate on exact-match and semantic query sets separately.
entries.example: "Hybrid search finally found both the SKU and the paragraph that explained it."
askNext: How are sparse and dense scores fused?
Do you evaluate identifier queries separately from conceptual ones?
What is the latency cost of running both retrievers?
entries.warningLabel: Two mediocre retrievers do not automatically become one good one.

entries.title: Industrial policy
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Industrial policy is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Sovereign AI are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The industrial policy discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."

entries.title: Inference
entries.devilDefinition: The part where the model stops being a research paper and starts being an invoice.
entries.plainDefinition: Inference is the process of running a trained model on new input to produce an output. For language models, that usually means taking tokens in, computing the next-token probabilities, and generating a response token by token.
entries.whyExists: The term distinguishes using a model from building one. Training changes weights. Inference uses those weights to do work on fresh data. The distinction matters because the technical constraints, costs, and risks are very different.
entries.misuse: People often use "inference" as a grand-sounding synonym for "the AI bit". That hides the practical questions. Inference is not magic reasoning dust. It is a serving workload with latency, throughput, reliability, and cost characteristics.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means API calls, GPU time, queueing, prompt assembly, caching, streaming, rate limits, and a finance person eventually asking why the bot became expensive after launch. If your AI feature is live, inference is the meter running.
entries.example: "Training happened once in the background; inference happens every time a customer opens the feature and expects an answer in under two seconds."
askNext: What model is doing the inference, where is it running, and who pays per call?
Is the bottleneck latency, throughput, quality, or context assembly?
Can some of this workload be cached, batched, or offloaded to a smaller model?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The part customers can actually feel.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A performance budget attached to compute, prompts, and product expectations.

entries.body: If someone says a feature is "AI-powered", inference is usually the operational question hiding underneath. Not whether the model is clever in principle, but whether the system can answer quickly, cheaply, and often enough to survive contact with users.
