 Inference is the process of running a trained model on new input to produce an output. For language models, that usually means taking tokens in, computing the next-token probabilities, and generating a response token by token.
 The term distinguishes using a model from building one. Training changes weights. Inference uses those weights to do work on fresh data. The distinction matters because the technical constraints, costs, and risks are very different.
 People often use "inference" as a grand-sounding synonym for "the AI bit". That hides the practical questions. Inference is not magic reasoning dust. It is a serving workload with latency, throughput, reliability, and cost characteristics.
 In practice it usually means API calls, GPU time, queueing, prompt assembly, caching, streaming, rate limits, and a finance person eventually asking why the bot became expensive after launch. If your AI feature is live, inference is the meter running.
 "Training happened once in the background; inference happens every time a customer opens the feature and expects an answer in under two seconds."
 What model is doing the inference, where is it running, and who pays per call? Is the bottleneck latency, throughput, quality, or context assembly? Can some of this workload be cached, batched, or offloaded to a smaller model?

entries.title: Inference Arbitrage
entries.devilDefinition: Buying intelligence wholesale from one provider, retailing it through a cleaner interface, and hoping the margin survives long enough to become a strategy.
entries.plainDefinition: Inference arbitrage is the practice of profiting from differences in model price, latency, quality, or access by routing demand across providers and packaging the result as a product or service.
entries.whyExists: Model markets are uneven. Providers price differently, regions behave differently, and many users would rather pay one intermediary than personally compare latency tables like a day trader with a prompt addiction.
entries.misuse: The term is stretched when any wrapper, gateway, or comparison site is described as though routing alone were a durable moat. Arbitrage can be real. It is also famously allergic to permanence.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means mixing models behind the scenes, swapping suppliers when pricing shifts, and capturing some spread through UX, bundling, reliability, or customer ignorance. The better businesses add real workflow value. The thinner ones are mostly tariffs with branding.
entries.example: "Their moat turned out to be inference arbitrage with a charming UI and a very aggressive newsletter."
askNext: What part of the margin comes from routing skill, and what part from customer convenience?
How quickly does the advantage disappear if providers cut prices or copy the feature?
Is there product substance here, or mainly a spread between wholesale tokens and retail confidence?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Capture value from differences between model suppliers instead of betting on a single one.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone else is shopping the model market so the customer can pretend not to notice.

entries.body: Arbitrage is not shameful. It is merely less mythic than many decks would prefer.

entries.title: Inference Budget
entries.devilDefinition: The line item where intelligence becomes less abstract and more payable within thirty days.
entries.plainDefinition: An inference budget is the planned or enforced limit on the cost, latency, tokens, model calls, compute, or provider spend used to run AI features in production.
entries.whyExists: Model calls have variable cost and performance characteristics. Without a budget, a successful feature can become a financial incident, and an agent can discover new forms of curiosity at the company's expense.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to token counting. Tokens matter, but so do model choice, retries, tool calls, reasoning effort, cache hits, latency targets, and the number of times a workflow politely tries again.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means per-user limits, per-task ceilings, model routing policies, cache strategy, timeout rules, alerting, and deciding which requests deserve the expensive model before the expensive model decides for itself.
entries.example: "The inference budget survived launch only after the agent was stopped from spending premium reasoning on password reset emails."
askNext: What is the maximum acceptable cost for this user action?
Which requests can use a cheaper model, cache, or shorter reasoning path?
Are retries and tool calls counted in the same budget as the visible model response?

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: Set a ceiling on how much intelligence a workflow is allowed to buy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A practical argument between quality, speed, and the bill.

entries.body: Inference budgets are not anti-intelligence. They are the moment the system admits that cleverness has a marginal cost.

entries.title: Inference Provider
entries.devilDefinition: The company renting you access to intelligence by the token, with latency graphs where the soul used to be.
entries.plainDefinition: An inference provider runs and serves AI models through APIs or managed infrastructure so customers can send inputs and receive outputs without operating the full model stack themselves.
entries.whyExists: Because reliable model serving requires GPUs, batching, scaling, observability, routing, and a tolerance for operational pain that many product teams would rather outsource.
entries.misuse: By blurring the line between building a model and hosting one, or by describing a rented endpoint as proprietary intelligence simply because the invoice arrives under a different logo.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice the choice of inference provider shapes latency, price, regional availability, data handling, model access, and how much of your application's reliability now depends on somebody else's serving layer.
entries.example: "They said they had their own AI platform, which turned out to mean an inference provider, a neat dashboard, and a heroic willingness to rename dependency as strategy."
askNext: Who actually trains the model, and who merely serves it?
What happens to cost and performance when traffic scales?
Which data-handling guarantees survive the extra layer?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone else is handling the GPU headaches, and now they sit in your critical path.

entries.body: Inference providers matter because very few teams want to run model serving properly, and a surprising number still want to sound as if they do.

entries.title: Inference Stack
entries.devilDefinition: The collection of expensive plumbing discovered immediately after somebody says, "It's just one API call."
entries.plainDefinition: An inference stack is the set of systems used to run model requests in production, including providers, routing, caching, prompt assembly, guardrails, observability, evaluation, and cost control.
entries.whyExists: Because shipping AI features requires more than model access. Real applications need reliability, latency control, policy enforcement, failover, measurement, and a defensible explanation for the monthly bill.
entries.misuse: By calling every thin wrapper a stack. A real inference stack handles production constraints. A dropdown of model names and a vibes-based retry loop do not amount to infrastructure.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means SDKs, proxies, model routers, rate limits, prompt templates, redaction, logging, eval pipelines, cache policies, billing dashboards, and repeated arguments over whether quality improved enough to justify the spend.
entries.example: "The inference stack matured rapidly once finance started reading the token invoices with feeling."
askNext: Which parts of the stack protect reliability, and which merely create another place for failure to hide?
How are routing, caching, and fallback policies affecting quality as well as cost?
Who can explain the per-request economics without opening six dashboards and a prayer?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Everything around the model call that keeps production from becoming an expensive hobby.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A layered serving system where cost, latency, safety, and uptime argue with one another on every request.

entries.body: Once the prototypes leave the demo phase, the stack arrives carrying invoices, dashboards, and opinions.

entries.title: INT4
entries.devilDefinition: Four-bit integers for weights so a laptop can host a model that once needed a small power plant—quality optional, marketing mandatory.
entries.plainDefinition: INT4 quantisation stores weights in 4-bit integer formats commonly used for local and edge LLM serving to minimise memory footprint.
entries.whyExists: Consumer hardware and edge devices cannot host full-precision large models. Aggressive quantisation is how they fit.
entries.misuse: Assumed equal to the full model. Task sensitivity varies; some domains degrade sharply at 4-bit.
entries.practicalMeaning: Test on your prompts, prefer methods with calibration, and watch multilingual and coding quality especially.
entries.example: "INT4 made the model fit in RAM and made rare entity names optional."
askNext: Which INT4 method and group size?
What tasks regressed versus higher precision?
Is decode speed actually better on your CPU/GPU?
entries.warningLabel: Fits-in-memory is not passes-your-eval.

entries.title: Integration governance
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Integration governance is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Tool Registry are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The integration governance discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Integration governance is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Integration governance belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Interleaved thinking
entries.devilDefinition: Reasoning allowed to continue after the tool replies, a modest concession to the possibility that reality has amended the original plan.
entries.plainDefinition: Interleaved thinking is a model execution pattern in which reasoning occurs between tool calls as well as before the first call. The model can inspect each result, revise its approach, and decide what to do next instead of following a fully formed tool plan unchanged.
entries.whyExists: Tool results reveal errors, missing data, changed state, and better routes that cannot be known in advance. Reasoning between calls lets an agent adapt to evidence during a task rather than using tools merely to decorate its initial guess.
entries.misuse: It is treated as a guarantee of better judgement or autonomous reliability. More reasoning opportunities can also mean more tokens, latency, wandering plans, unjustified tool calls, and a longer record of the model adapting badly.
entries.practicalMeaning: The runtime preserves the model's reasoning and tool context across a sequence, returns each tool result in the required form, and enforces budgets, approvals, and stop conditions around the loop. Teams should evaluate task success and side effects, not the apparent sophistication of the trace.
entries.example: "Interleaved thinking let the agent revise its plan after the database disagreed, an innovation known elsewhere as paying attention."
askNext: What new evidence may cause the model to revise, stop, or ask for approval?
How are token, latency, tool-call, and side-effect budgets enforced across the loop?
Does evaluation reward correct task completion rather than an impressive reasoning trace?
entries.warningLabel: Adaptive reasoning still needs bounded action.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Let the model reconsider after each tool result, while the application retains control of time, money, and side effects.

entries.body: The agent-loop diagram is the direct mental model: act, observe the result, think again, and either continue or stop. Interleaving improves the opportunity to adapt, not the guarantee of wisdom.

entries.title: Jailbreak
entries.devilDefinition: The art of persuading a safety system that the rules were meant for someone else.
entries.plainDefinition: A jailbreak is a prompt or interaction pattern intended to bypass a model's safety rules, system instructions, or refusal policies and elicit behaviour it is supposed to restrict.
entries.whyExists: It exists because instruction-following systems are probabilistic, layered defences are imperfect, and many users are curious, mischievous, adversarial, or simply unwilling to accept "no" from a machine.
entries.misuse: The term gets stretched to cover any successful prompt tweak. More precisely, a jailbreak is about overriding the intended boundary, not merely asking the question more clearly.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means red-team exercises, defence updates, moderation tuning, prompt hardening, and a permanent reminder that a polite interface does not imply a secure one, especially once tools and external systems are attached.
entries.example: "The jailbreak was funny right up to the moment someone remembered the model also had access to tools."
askNext: What boundary was bypassed: policy, system prompt, tool permission, or all three?
Was this a curiosity, a reproducible exploit, or a path to real-world harm?
How quickly can the defence be patched without breaking ordinary use?
entries.warningLabel: Harmless in theory becomes expensive once the model can act.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: A test of whether the model's stated boundaries survive contact with a determined user.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone found a conversational way around safeguards that were always less absolute than the marketing implied.

entries.note: Not every refusal workaround is a serious security event. The risk changes sharply when the model can call tools, access data, or operate in regulated workflows.
entries.body: The most interesting part is rarely the clever prompt. It is the quiet assumption that the model should have been tougher in the first place.

entries.title: Jalapeño
entries.devilDefinition: A custom inference chip for removing the general-purpose margin from a very specialised appetite for tokens.
entries.plainDefinition: Jalapeño is a custom inference chip developed by OpenAI and Broadcom for serving large language models. It represents a move towards silicon designed around a frontier lab's own inference workloads rather than relying only on general AI accelerators.
entries.whyExists: At large scale, inference cost, power use, memory movement, latency, and supply become strategic constraints. Custom silicon can optimise those trade-offs around a known model stack and reduce dependence on a single accelerator market.
entries.misuse: A custom chip is treated as automatic proof of lower costs or technical independence. Its value depends on software support, yields, packaging, memory, networking, utilisation, deployment volume, and whether the models fit the assumptions built into the hardware.
entries.practicalMeaning: The chip matters less as a benchmark trophy than as a signal about vertical integration. Procurement and infrastructure teams should ask what workloads it serves, where it is deployed, what stack supports it, and whether claimed savings survive full-system costs.
entries.example: "Jalapeño promised cheaper inference once the company had designed the chip, supplied it, integrated it, and achieved the scale described in the first sentence."
askNext: Which models, precisions, batch sizes, and latency targets is the chip designed to serve?
Do cost and efficiency claims include memory, networking, software, cooling, and utilisation?
Where is it deployed at production scale, and what supply dependencies remain?
entries.warningLabel: Custom silicon moves the dependency map; it does not erase it.

translations.label: infrastructure meaning
translations.text: A lab-specific inference accelerator whose economics depend on the whole serving system and enough volume to justify the design.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because none of the current diagrams shows accelerator, memory, network, and serving-stack economics; model routing would answer a different question.

entries.title: Jensen Huang
entries.devilDefinition: The man who sold shovels to the gold rush, then noticed that the shovels were now the kingdom.
entries.plainDefinition: Jensen Huang is the founder and CEO of NVIDIA, the company most closely associated with the compute layer underneath the current AI boom.
entries.whyExists: Because AI eventually becomes a hardware conversation whether the software people like it or not. Jensen's name now stands for compute supply, pricing power, chip strategy, and the awkward fact that ambitious models run on physical constraints, not vibes.
entries.misuse: By speaking as if NVIDIA is AI itself rather than a dominant infrastructure layer beneath much of it. That overstates the company and understates the rest of the stack, but it does capture where the leverage currently sits.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people say "Jensen", they usually mean GPU access, training economics, inference cost, procurement pain, or the broader truth that model ambition can be throttled by silicon and supply chains.
entries.example: "The startup imagined it was building software until it met Jensen and discovered that its roadmap was now partially a conversation about lead times."
askNext: Is this a capability story or a compute story?
Are we discussing training cost, inference efficiency, or supply constraints?
Is NVIDIA enabling the market, pricing the market, or quietly defining it?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The point in the conversation where software optimism collides with hardware invoices.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for the compute bottlenecks, margins, and supply dynamics under the AI stack.

entries.body: If the previous era of software was willing to forget the machine underneath, this one is receiving a stern correction.

entries.title: JSON mode
entries.devilDefinition: A toggle that makes the model sound more like a spreadsheet and less like a novelist, then still invents a field your schema never declared.
entries.plainDefinition: JSON mode is a vendor feature that steers model output toward syntactically valid JSON objects, usually via decoding constraints or strong instruction bias, without guaranteeing that the JSON matches your application schema.
entries.whyExists: Product teams need machine-readable answers more often than essays. A dedicated mode reduces broken braces and free-text apologies when the next step is a parser.
entries.misuse: Treated as schema validation, security boundary, or proof the model 'understands' structure. JSON mode can still emit wrong keys, wrong types, and confidently empty objects.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use it as a decoding bias, then validate against a real schema, repair or reject failures, and log the miss rate. Prefer true structured-output / schema-constrained decoding when the vendor offers it.
entries.example: "JSON mode fixed the curly braces; the invoice total still arrived as a motivational quote."
askNext: Is output validated against a schema after generation?
Do you need constrained decoding, or only a softer JSON bias?
What happens on schema failure: retry, repair, or human review?
entries.warningLabel: JSON-shaped does not mean application-correct.

entries.title: Judge model
entries.devilDefinition: A model asked to grade another model, because peer review was not already delicate enough.
entries.plainDefinition: A judge model is a model used to evaluate, score, compare, or critique outputs from another model or AI system.
entries.whyExists: Human evaluation is expensive and slow. Judge models can help scale assessments of quality, safety, factuality, style, preference, or task success when used carefully.
entries.misuse: They get treated as neutral graders. Judge models bring their own biases, blind spots, prompt sensitivity, training contamination, and taste, often with excellent confidence.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means validating the judge against human review, testing for bias and leakage, calibrating scores, and remembering that automated evaluation is an instrument, not a magistrate.
entries.example: "The judge model preferred the longer answer, which was useful evidence that verbosity had found a robe."
askNext: What human or ground-truth standard validates this judge?
Where does the judge model systematically fail?
Can teams game the judge without improving the product?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A scalable evaluator that still needs evaluation.

entries.body: Automating judgement does not remove judgement. It moves the argument to the judge.

entries.title: Kimi
entries.devilDefinition: Moonshot's name for the assistant, the workspace, the coding tools, and the model family, because one noun is cheaper than four onboarding screens.
entries.plainDefinition: Kimi is Moonshot AI's consumer and developer product brand. It covers an agentic AI workspace, Kimi Work, Kimi Code, web and app experiences, an API platform, and a changing family of Kimi models; the name does not identify one fixed model or capability.
entries.whyExists: Moonshot uses one product family for general assistance, research, knowledge work, presentations, coding, long-running tasks, and model access. A shared brand lets the product combine specialised interfaces and agent workflows without asking users to assemble them independently.
entries.misuse: People attribute a result to "Kimi" without recording whether it came from Kimi K3, an older Kimi model, Work, Code, a swarm or goal workflow, or an API integration. Product scaffolding and model capability then become one flattering but unrepeatable benchmark.
entries.practicalMeaning: Buyers and evaluators should name the active model, surface, tools, effort setting, connected data, and execution mode. Kimi.com, Kimi Work, Kimi Code, and the API can expose different affordances even when they share a model family.
entries.example: "Kimi built the dashboard and fixed the code, which was precise enough for marketing and several nouns short of a reproducible evaluation."
askNext: Which Kimi model, product surface, and effort setting produced this result?
Were tools, Kimi Work, Kimi Code, Swarm, or Goal mode involved?
Can the same workflow be reproduced through the API, or only inside the product?
entries.warningLabel: Product family. Name the model, surface, and tools.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A Moonshot product shell around changing models, tools, and agent workflows rather than one timeless model.

entries.body: The agent-loop diagram fits the product's practical role: Kimi observes context, chooses tools or subtasks, acts, and revises. The model name still needs to be written separately.

entries.title: Kimi K3
entries.devilDefinition: A 2.8-trillion-parameter model built to keep working after the benchmark has run out of comfortable task lengths.
entries.plainDefinition: Kimi K3 is Moonshot AI's 2.8-trillion-parameter flagship mixture-of-experts model. It uses Kimi Delta Attention and Attention Residuals, has native visual understanding and a one-million-token context window, and targets long-horizon coding, knowledge work, reasoning, and multimodal agent tasks.
entries.whyExists: Long software-engineering and research workflows require a model to navigate large bodies of context, use tools repeatedly, reason over visual results, and maintain progress across many steps. K3 scales model capacity and architectural sparsity around those agentic workloads.
entries.misuse: Parameter count, context length, benchmark scores, and "open" status are compressed into one claim of superiority. At launch K3 is available through Kimi products and the API, while Moonshot says the full weights will follow on 27 July 2026; hosted access is not the same thing as downloadable weights.
entries.practicalMeaning: Teams should evaluate K3 in the exact harness, effort mode, tool environment, and task duration they plan to use. They should also distinguish Moonshot's reported results from independent runs and verify whether the promised weights, licences, serving requirements, and partner integrations are available at decision time.
entries.example: "Kimi K3 had a million-token context window, which gave the repository ample room to contain both the relevant code and every irrelevant theory about it."
askNext: Are we using K3 through Kimi, Kimi Code, the API, or released model weights?
Which harness, effort setting, tools, and task duration produced the reported score?
What serving hardware, licence, and independent evaluation apply to the version available today?
entries.warningLabel: Hosted today and open-weight later are different availability states.

translations.label: model meaning
translations.text: A very large sparse multimodal model aimed at sustained agentic work, whose real value still depends on the harness and available release form.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because K3's distinguishing mental model combines sparse expert routing, hybrid attention, multimodality, and long-horizon execution; any single current diagram would reduce that to one marketing bullet.

entries.title: Knowledge Cutoff
entries.devilDefinition: The calendar line after which the model becomes a gifted historical novelist.
entries.plainDefinition: A knowledge cutoff is the latest point in time reflected in a model's internal knowledge without fresh retrieval or live access to newer information.
entries.whyExists: Models are built from data gathered during a finite period. However broad that period was, it still ends somewhere, which means the model's default knowledge eventually becomes stale.
entries.misuse: By treating the cutoff as either a precise magical boundary or as something that no longer matters because the interface looks current. A model can sound up to date long after its internal map stopped being refreshed.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means any question about recent events, changing laws, product releases, prices, or people who change jobs needs fresh sources instead of trusting the model's memory and posture.
entries.example: "The answer sounded current until someone checked the date. It was confidence wearing a knowledge cutoff."
askNext: What information in this workflow changes often enough to outrun the model's built-in knowledge?
Is the system using fresh retrieval, or merely sounding well informed about the past?
How would we notice if the answer stopped at last year's world?

translations.label: user meaning
translations.text: The moment where the system's memory stops and its style keeps going.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A built-in date limit on what the model can know without outside help.

entries.body: The problem is not that models have a past. The problem is that they rarely announce it at the exact moment you most need them to.

entries.title: Knowledge editing
entries.devilDefinition: Surgical rewrites of what a model believes, until the rest of its brain keeps quoting the old textbook.
entries.plainDefinition: Knowledge editing is a family of methods for modifying specific factual associations in a trained model without full retraining, using targeted parameter updates or external memory patches.
entries.whyExists: Facts change and errors ship. Full retraining is slow; editors promise local fixes.
entries.misuse: Treated as reliable memory surgery. Edits can fail to generalise, interfere with related knowledge, or decay under further training.
entries.practicalMeaning: Prefer retrieval for rapidly changing facts. Use editing for narrow static fixes with regression tests across neighbouring knowledge.
entries.example: "Knowledge editing fixed the CEO name and broke the subsidiary list."
askNext: How do you test locality and generality of an edit?
Is retrieval a simpler source of truth?
Who approves factual patches in production?
entries.warningLabel: Models are not databases with clean row updates.

translations.text: Knowledge editing is a family of methods for modifying specific factual associations in a trained model without full retraining, using targeted parameter updates or external memory patches.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Prefer retrieval for rapidly changing facts.

entries.body: Knowledge editing is tempting because finetuning is heavy. Tempting tools need regression suites.

entries.title: Latency
entries.devilDefinition: The awkward silence between "look how magical this is" and the user deciding they no longer care.
entries.plainDefinition: Latency is the elapsed time between making a request to a system and receiving a usable response.
entries.whyExists: AI systems do not merely need to be correct enough. They need to respond quickly enough for the surrounding workflow, interface, and patience of the person waiting.
entries.misuse: By treating latency as a minor engineering detail that can be apologised away with a loading spinner. People are far more forgiving of imperfection than of delay that breaks the rhythm of work.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the difference between a feature that feels helpful and one that feels like a committee meeting. Model size, retrieval, tool calls, routing, and output length all conspire to make waiting expensive.
entries.example: "The model was accurate, elegant, and two seconds too slow, which is another way of saying it was losing."
askNext: What is the maximum delay this workflow can tolerate before people route around it?
Which part of the pipeline is slow: model time, retrieval, tools, or post-processing?
Are we paying for higher capability when the real problem is response speed?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: How long the system makes someone wait before it becomes usable.

entries.title: Le Chat
entries.devilDefinition: Le Chat is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Le Chat is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Mistral AI often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

entries.title: Liar's Dividend
entries.devilDefinition: The social profit earned when fake media becomes plausible enough that real evidence can be waved away as synthetic inconvenience.
entries.plainDefinition: The liar's dividend is the benefit people gain when the existence of convincing AI-generated or manipulated media lets them deny authentic evidence as fake.
entries.whyExists: AI has lowered the cost of producing persuasive synthetic audio, images, and video. That does not only create fake evidence. It also weakens confidence in real evidence by giving bad actors a ready-made excuse.
entries.misuse: The phrase can be used too theatrically, as though trust has already collapsed beyond recovery. Verification, provenance, and forensic methods still matter. The problem is not total epistemic ruin. It is a higher and more expensive burden of proof.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means journalists, courts, platforms, and the public need stronger provenance checks while opportunists claim every inconvenient recording is probably AI. The immediate damage is often delay, confusion, and public doubt rather than perfect deception.
entries.example: "The clip was real, but the liar's dividend arrived on schedule and turned basic verification into a partisan hobby."
askNext: What provenance or verification evidence accompanies this media?
Who benefits from uncertainty around its authenticity?
Are we discussing actual forensic doubts, or opportunistic denial dressed as prudence?
entries.warningLabel: The damage often comes from doubt, not from perfectly convincing fakery.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: Synthetic media makes it easier to dispute authentic evidence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Once fakes are cheap, denial gets cheaper too.

entries.body: The liar's dividend is one of the more serious AI terms because it describes a failure mode with no product demo. It arrives as a shrug, a doubt, and a very convenient excuse.

entries.title: Likeness Rights
entries.devilDefinition: The legal and commercial discovery that a face is not free stock photography because a model can approximate it.
entries.plainDefinition: Likeness rights are the legal, contractual, and commercial interests a person may have in the use of their name, image, voice, performance, identity, or recognisable persona, especially in generated media.
entries.whyExists: They exist because faces, voices, names, and performances carry economic and personal value. AI makes imitation easier to scale, so old questions about permission, endorsement, compensation, and control arrive with cheaper tools and more plausible deniability.
entries.misuse: The term is misused when treated as a universal magic shield. Rights vary by jurisdiction, contract, labour context, publicity law, privacy law, and estate rules. Saying "likeness rights" does not settle who may train, generate, distribute, monetise, or withdraw.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means licences, release forms, union terms, creator contracts, territory limits, usage periods, consent records, compensation, attribution, model-training exclusions, takedown routes, and provenance systems that show what was used.
entries.example: "The product team discovered likeness rights shortly after the generated presenter looked enough like a real actor for the actor's lawyer to notice."
askNext: Which person, performance, voice, image, or persona is being used?
What contract or law gives permission for this exact AI use?
How are scope, compensation, revocation, and downstream reuse handled?
entries.warningLabel: A plausible imitation is still attached to an actual person.

translations.label: legal meaning
translations.text: The rights and contracts governing commercial use of a person's recognisable identity.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The bill that arrives when synthetic media remembers people have lawyers.

entries.body: AI did not invent likeness rights. It merely made forgetting them easier, faster, and more billable.

entries.title: Llama
entries.devilDefinition: The model family that made "open" a competitive move, a developer habit, and a licensing argument in one convenient package.
entries.plainDefinition: Llama is Meta's family of foundation models, released in multiple versions and sizes for research, development, and commercial use under terms that made the models widely deployable without making the conversation any less political.
entries.whyExists: Developers wanted strong deployable models outside closed APIs, and Meta wanted broad ecosystem influence, research visibility, and distribution through everyone else's hardware budget.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a single model, as synonymous with open source, or as proof that downloading weights is the same thing as having deployment solved. "We use Llama" is usually the beginning of the sentence, not the useful part.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means evaluating a specific Llama variant for self-hosting, fine-tuning, or cost control, with all the usual adult questions about licences, throughput, memory, moderation, and whether the chosen size is actually fit for purpose.
entries.example: "They said the stack ran on Llama, which turned out to mean one particular checkpoint, two compatibility caveats, and a spirited debate about memory."
askNext: Which Llama version, size, and licence terms are we talking about?
What problem does self-hosting solve here: cost, privacy, latency, control, or ideology?
Do we have the infrastructure and evaluation discipline to run it well?
entries.warningLabel: Model family, not a monolithic capability.

translations.label: ecosystem meaning
translations.text: A major open-model family that helped turn self-hosting from hobbyism into a default strategic option.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A family of deployable Meta models whose usefulness depends on the exact checkpoint and the operator's competence.

entries.note: Llama's significance is as much strategic as technical. It changed what many teams assumed was possible outside closed-provider APIs.
entries.body: Some products use Llama because they believe in openness. Many more use it because a downloadable model is easier to budget than a permanently rising surprise.

entries.title: LLM
entries.devilDefinition: A statistical text engine promoted from autocomplete to corporate metaphysics.
entries.plainDefinition: LLM stands for large language model: a model trained to predict and generate language, often across many tasks involving text, code, and increasingly tools and multimodal inputs.
entries.whyExists: The field needed a short label for the class of large generative models that sit underneath much of the current AI boom. The acronym is easier to say than the architecture, which is part of its success.
entries.misuse: By using LLM as a synonym for AI, intelligence, or product value. An LLM can be the engine inside a useful system, but the acronym by itself explains very little about whether the result is accurate, safe, or worth paying for.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a flexible language-and-code engine that can draft, classify, summarise, translate, search, and bluff, depending on how it is prompted, constrained, and surrounded by everything else.
entries.example: "They said the product was powered by an LLM as though naming the engine answered the question of whether the vehicle was heading anywhere sensible."
askNext: What specific model behaviour matters here beyond the presence of an LLM?
What surrounding retrieval, tooling, or controls make the system useful?
Is the acronym explaining the product, or merely decorating it?

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: The machine layer whose name is now expected to confer strategic seriousness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A broad class of models for generating and manipulating language, not a guarantee of product merit.

entries.note: The acronym is useful as shorthand. It becomes perfume when treated as an explanation rather than a category.
entries.body: The market loves initials because they compress complexity into something that can fit on a slide and survive a budget meeting.

entries.title: LLM application security
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: LLM application security is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Prompt Firewall are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The llm application security discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."

entries.title: LLM-as-a-Judge
entries.devilDefinition: The practice of asking one stochastic witness whether another stochastic witness deserves a trophy.
entries.plainDefinition: LLM-as-a-judge is an evaluation pattern in which one language model scores, ranks, or compares outputs from another model against a rubric, preference signal, or pairwise choice.
entries.whyExists: Because human evaluation is expensive, slow, and inconveniently unwilling to scale at the pace product teams prefer.
entries.misuse: By treating the judge as neutral, objective, or magically aligned with user value. A model grader can inherit stylistic bias, provider bias, verbosity bias, and a remarkable affection for whatever sounds polished.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it is useful for triage, regression testing, and broad ranking, provided the rubric is clear and the results are anchored to human review on the cases that actually matter.
entries.example: "The model won the internal evals until an actual customer arrived and demonstrated that the judge had confused confidence with usefulness."
askNext: What rubric is the judge following, and who wrote it?
How often do human reviewers disagree with the model's preferences?
Are we using the judge for cheap sorting or pretending it is ground truth?
entries.warningLabel: Helpful for ranking. Dangerous when mistaken for reality.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A scalable but imperfect grading shortcut that still needs humans to calibrate the definition of good.

entries.body: It is a sensible tool if used with modesty. The trouble begins when teams treat automated judgement as the cheaper cousin of judgement itself.

entries.title: LLMOps
entries.devilDefinition: MLOps with chatbots: prompts, evals, traces, and cost dashboards pretending to be a mature discipline while everything is still a notebook somewhere.
entries.plainDefinition: LLMOps is the operational practice of deploying, monitoring, evaluating, versioning, and governing large language model applications in production—covering prompts, models, tools, data, and cost.
entries.whyExists: LLM apps failed in new ways: prompt drift, non-determinism, tool breakage, and token bills. Teams needed a name for the ops surface.
entries.misuse: Used as a job title without metrics, or as vendor suite marketing. Ops is a practice, not a logo.
entries.practicalMeaning: Version prompts and models, log traces, run regression evals, budget tokens, and own on-call for quality regressions—not only HTTP 500s.
entries.example: "LLMOps caught the prompt change that doubled refusals on checkout."
askNext: What is versioned: prompts, models, tools, or only containers?
Which quality metrics page someone at 2 a.m.?
How do you roll back a bad prompt?
entries.warningLabel: Without evals, ops is only infrastructure cosplay.

entries.title: LM Studio
entries.devilDefinition: A desktop studio for running models locally, where downloading the weights is the easy part and fitting them into memory becomes the opening act.
entries.plainDefinition: LM Studio is a desktop and headless runtime for discovering, downloading, running, chatting with, and serving local AI models. It supports GGUF models through llama.cpp, MLX models on Apple Silicon, MCP tools, its own SDKs and APIs, and OpenAI- and Anthropic-compatible endpoints.
entries.whyExists: Local models otherwise require users to assemble runtimes, model files, command-line flags, servers, and interfaces themselves. LM Studio packages those tasks into an approachable application while retaining developer access through a CLI, headless service, and local network APIs.
entries.misuse: LM Studio is confused with a model, a guarantee of privacy, or a fully open-source stack. It is a product that runs separately licensed model weights and runtimes; privacy and openness depend on the model, plugins, network settings, telemetry, and integrations actually used.
entries.practicalMeaning: A user downloads compatible weights, chooses a runtime and memory settings, loads the model, and chats locally or starts a server. Production-minded users must still handle hardware capacity, model licences, authentication, network exposure, updates, evaluation, and the gap between API compatibility and identical behaviour.
entries.example: "LM Studio made the model available on localhost in minutes, after which the team began the longer process of discovering what 'compatible' meant."
askNext: Which model weights, quantisation, runtime, and hardware are actually in use?
Is the server restricted to localhost, authenticated, and safe from unintended network exposure?
Which OpenAI- or Anthropic-compatible features behave differently from the hosted service?
entries.warningLabel: Local runtime, model licence, and privacy posture remain separate.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A friendly local-model workbench and server whose convenience does not repeal memory limits or integration testing.

entries.body: The model-routing diagram fits because LM Studio separates an application or compatible client from the locally selected and loaded model behind its server boundary.

entries.title: Local AI
entries.devilDefinition: AI running close enough to the user that the cloud bill disappears from view and the hardware bill becomes a personal matter.
entries.plainDefinition: Local AI means running some or all model inference, retrieval, storage, or agent tooling on hardware controlled by the user or organisation rather than sending every operation to a third-party hosted service. It ranges from a model on one laptop to a private on-premises cluster.
entries.whyExists: Local execution can improve privacy, latency, offline availability, customisation, cost predictability, and control over models and data. It also lets products work where connectivity, data residency, or external-service dependence is unacceptable.
entries.misuse: "Local" is treated as a complete security and privacy verdict. A local app may still download models, check for updates, call remote tools, send telemetry, expose a network server, or process data through plugins whose boundaries the interface neglects to mention.
entries.practicalMeaning: Teams must specify what runs locally: model inference, embeddings, document parsing, memory, tools, logs, and storage may each live somewhere different. They also inherit model updates, hardware capacity, patching, access control, backups, energy use, and evaluation.
entries.example: "The assistant was local AI because inference stayed on the laptop; the web-search tool, licence server, and crash reporter had made other arrangements."
askNext: Which exact components and data flows stay on hardware we control?
What network calls, remote tools, updates, and telemetry remain enabled?
Who owns model patching, hardware capacity, backups, and access control?
entries.warningLabel: Local is a deployment property, not a privacy certificate.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Move selected AI workloads onto hardware you control and accept the operational work that arrives with them.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the crucial mental model is a deployment and data-flow boundary across local and remote components, which the current catalogue diagrams do not express.

entries.title: Long context
entries.devilDefinition: A context window large enough to hold your entire backlog, and still lose the one sentence that mattered.
entries.plainDefinition: Long context refers to models and serving stacks that accept very large input windows—tens or hundreds of thousands of tokens—so more documents, logs, or history fit in a single prompt.
entries.whyExists: Users hit window limits constantly. Extending context reduces some RAG complexity and enables new workflows over large artefacts.
entries.misuse: Treated as perfect memory and perfect attention. Models still degrade, costs rise, and 'lost in the middle' effects persist.
entries.practicalMeaning: Measure accuracy versus length on your tasks, watch cost and latency, and keep retrieval for corpora larger than any honest window.
entries.example: "Long context swallowed the PDF and still asked for the page that was already there."
askNext: What is effective context quality at your target length?
How does price scale with filled context?
When is retrieval still cheaper and more accurate?
entries.warningLabel: Capacity is not comprehension.

entries.title: LoRA
entries.devilDefinition: Low-rank adapters that let you fine-tune without moving every weight, popular because GPUs and patience are finite.
entries.plainDefinition: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) fine-tunes models by learning small low-rank update matrices injected into layers, leaving most base weights frozen.
entries.whyExists: Full fine-tunes are heavy. LoRA makes adaptation cheaper, more portable, and easier to swap per task.
entries.misuse: Assumed equal to full fine-tuning for every domain. Capacity limits and hyperparameter sensitivity remain real.
entries.practicalMeaning: Tune rank and target modules, evaluate against full FT when stakes are high, and version adapters like code.
entries.example: "LoRA adapted the model to support tone without hosting a second full checkpoint."
askNext: Which modules are adapted, and at what rank?
How do you evaluate adapter interference when stacking?
Where are adapters stored and approved?
entries.warningLabel: Small adapters can still encode large policy changes.

entries.title: Lost in the middle
entries.devilDefinition: The documented habit of models reading the beginning and the end carefully while the middle of your prompt becomes decorative.
entries.plainDefinition: Lost-in-the-middle describes the empirical finding that language models often use information at the start and end of long contexts more reliably than information placed in the middle.
entries.whyExists: Long context marketing met attention and training realities. Practitioners needed a name for positional failure.
entries.misuse: Used as fatalism. Careful ordering, reranking, and retrieval can mitigate; it is a bias, not a law of physics.
entries.practicalMeaning: Put critical instructions and evidence at edges or in structured slots; retrieve fewer better chunks; evaluate with mid-context needles.
entries.example: "Lost-in-the-middle ate the indemnity clause sitting on page fourteen of the pasted contract."
askNext: Where do you place non-negotiable instructions?
Do evals include mid-context facts?
Would retrieval beat stuffing the whole corpus?
entries.warningLabel: Long prompts still have privileged seats.

entries.title: Lovable
entries.devilDefinition: A chat-driven builder for turning "I have an idea" into a working app before anyone asks who will inherit the code.
entries.plainDefinition: Lovable is a product for generating and iterating on web applications from natural-language instructions, with modes for planning, editing code, and increasingly agent-like execution across a project.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many people want software outcomes without local setup, framework literacy, or the traditional rite of discovering three build errors before lunch. Products like this collapse prototyping, editing, and deployment into one highly persuasive loop.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as though describing software and owning software are the same discipline. The tool can absolutely shorten the road to a useful prototype. It cannot repeal the maintenance burden or make architecture an optional hobby.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Lovable usually means faster internal tools, MVPs, marketing sites, and founder demos, plus a fresh opportunity to confuse visible progress with durable engineering.
entries.example: "Lovable got them from idea to live demo in a weekend, which was excellent right up to the moment the product had to become a system."
askNext: Is this a prototype, an internal tool, or something expected to survive real users?
Who understands the resulting code once the initial chat session is over?
Did the tool remove engineering work, or merely defer it to a less festive day?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A short route from idea to demo, ideally before caution schedules a meeting.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A product that makes app creation more accessible while leaving long-term code ownership very much alive.

entries.body: The category is real. So is the temptation to treat software as finished the moment it becomes presentable.

entries.title: Lucy 2.5
entries.devilDefinition: A live video editor that changes the scene while the camera is still filming, leaving post-production with time to reconsider its career.
entries.plainDefinition: Lucy 2.5 is Decart's 720p real-time video-editing model. It transforms a live or streamed video using a text prompt, a reference image, or both, and can add, replace, or remove objects, swap characters and backgrounds, change clothing, and apply styles or effects.
entries.whyExists: Conventional generative video usually produces a clip after a wait. Interactive filters, virtual try-on, live shopping, games, and broadcast effects need output quickly enough to remain part of the live experience.
entries.misuse: "Real-time editing" can sound like ordinary deterministic compositing with better prompts. The model is generating altered frames under latency, consistency, identity, and motion constraints; a convincing short demonstration does not guarantee a stable long session.
entries.practicalMeaning: Developers connect a video stream through Decart's SDK or real-time API, select `lucy-2.5`, and update prompts or reference images during the session. They still need to test delay, frame stability, reference adherence, safety, consent, bandwidth, cost, and failure behaviour on their actual cameras and subjects.
entries.example: "Lucy 2.5 replaced the jacket during the live stream, saving a reshoot and creating several new meetings about likeness rights."
askNext: What end-to-end latency and frame stability hold over a long session on our network?
How closely does the output preserve motion, identity, and the supplied reference image?
What consent, moderation, disclosure, and fallback controls surround live transformation?
entries.warningLabel: Real-time generation still has temporal and consent debt.

translations.label: production meaning
translations.text: Prompt a live 720p video transformation and then measure whether the edited reality remains stable enough to ship.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the useful mental model is a latency-sensitive video stream with temporal feedback, which the catalogue's current diagrams do not show.

entries.title: Mac mini
entries.devilDefinition: The little silver box people buy when they want "serious AI infrastructure" without admitting they mostly mean "a neat machine that sits quietly on a desk."
entries.plainDefinition: Mac mini is Apple's compact desktop computer line. In AI conversations, it usually comes up as a relatively tidy, efficient machine for local inference, lightweight automation, development, and always-on orchestration workloads rather than frontier-scale model training. The current M4 and M4 Pro generation is especially relevant because Apple positions it for on-device AI workloads, and the M4 Pro configuration pushes unified memory and I/O far enough to make it a credible local workhorse within limits.
entries.whyExists: Because not every AI workload needs a server rack, and not every ambitious workflow deserves one either.
entries.misuse: By talking about it as if it were either a toy or a supercomputer. It is neither. It is a practical desktop that can be surprisingly capable within its lane and very ordinary outside it.
entries.practicalMeaning: A good box for coding, running local tools, hosting lightweight agents, testing smaller models, or acting as a stable automation node. It is especially attractive when low noise, power efficiency, and desk-friendly deployment matter more than maximum accelerator firepower.
entries.example: "He did not need a DGX. He needed a Mac mini, some discipline, and a better sense of what counted as production."
askNext: Is the workload inference, orchestration, development, or training?
Will it be running one assistant or many workflows?
Is the constraint compute, RAM, storage, or simply operational clarity?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A practical always-on box for local AI work when you want something more serious than a laptop and much less dramatic than data-centre cosplay.

entries.note: Apple's 2024 Mac mini line pairs M4 or M4 Pro with unified memory, and Apple specifically highlighted on-device AI speed, Thunderbolt 5 on M4 Pro, and higher display and bandwidth ceilings. None of that turns it into a training cluster. It does make it a respectable local node.
entries.body: The Mac mini has become a kind of anti-theatrical AI machine. It is small, quiet, and far more useful when treated as infrastructure than when narrated as destiny.

entries.title: Machine Payments Protocol
entries.devilDefinition: HTTP 402, taken down from the attic and given to agents with wallets.
entries.plainDefinition: Machine Payments Protocol is an open standard, co-authored by Tempo and Stripe, for paying inline with an HTTP request. A service can answer with `402 Payment Required`, after which the client supplies payment credentials and retries the request.
entries.whyExists: Machine clients often need API keys, billing accounts, or a human checkout flow before buying a small unit of service. MPP creates a standard challenge-and-payment exchange for usage-based APIs, agent transactions, and machine-to-machine commerce.
entries.misuse: It is presented as though a payment handshake makes an agent commercially trustworthy. The protocol can move money; it does not decide whether the purchase was authorised, sensible, reversible, or made by software anyone should have employed.
entries.practicalMeaning: A provider declares a price and accepted payment method in an HTTP challenge. The client chooses whether to pay, attaches proof, and retries. Teams still need spending limits, identity, receipts, dispute handling, and a policy for what the agent may buy before lunch.
entries.example: "The research agent used MPP to buy one dataset request, then finance asked why its HTTP client had an expense account."
askNext: Who authorises the payment and sets the per-request and daily limits?
What proof, receipt, refund, and dispute path survives after the agent finishes?
Which payment rails and currencies does the service actually accept?
entries.warningLabel: Payment capability is not purchasing authority.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A standard way for software to meet a price at the API boundary, plus a new reason to define what the software is allowed to spend.

entries.body: The neglected `402` status code has finally found a constituency: software that would like to buy something before a human can schedule procurement.

 "The research agent used MPP to buy one dataset request, then finance asked why its HTTP client had an expense account."
 Who authorises the payment and sets the per-request and daily limits? What proof, receipt, refund, and dispute path survives after the agent finishes? Which payment rails and currencies does the service actually accept? HTTP 402 Payment Required Usage-based billing  Tempo Stripe mpp.dev

entries.title: Managed Agents
entries.devilDefinition: The agent still acts autonomously; the cloud provider merely owns the runtime, the controls, and the invoice it autonomously produces.
entries.plainDefinition: Managed Agents is Google's Gemini API layer for running production agents with provider-operated infrastructure. Its capabilities include background tasks and connections to remote Model Context Protocol servers, so developers can delegate more of the execution lifecycle to the platform.
entries.whyExists: Long-running agents need durable execution, state, tool connectivity, retries, monitoring, security, and resource control. A managed service packages those operational concerns so every team does not build an agent runtime before it can test the agent.
entries.misuse: "Managed" is confused with reliable, governed, or maintenance-free. The provider can operate the runtime, but the customer still owns tool permissions, task design, data policy, evaluation, failure handling, cost limits, and the consequences of agent actions.
entries.practicalMeaning: Developers can submit work that continues beyond a single request and use remote MCP tools through the Gemini platform. They should evaluate portability, observability, state retention, regional controls, pricing, cancellation, identity, and recovery from partial execution.
entries.example: "Managed Agents removed the runtime work, leaving the team free to discover that workflow ownership had not been included in the service."
askNext: Which state, retries, logs, approvals, and cancellation controls does the service manage?
How are remote MCP credentials scoped, rotated, and audited?
Can the workflow move elsewhere without rebuilding its state and tool contracts?
entries.warningLabel: Managed infrastructure does not manage the meaning of the task.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: Rent the agent execution layer, then remain responsible for what the agent is allowed and expected to do.

entries.body: The agent-loop diagram is attached because Managed Agents packages the repeated model, tool, observation, and continuation cycle rather than merely exposing another model endpoint.

entries.title: Managed inference
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: Managed inference names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Model Garden need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said managed inference as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."

entries.title: Manifest
entries.devilDefinition: A model router for agents whose first autonomous act was apparently choosing the most expensive model available.
entries.plainDefinition: Manifest is an open-source LLM routing layer for personal AI agents, designed to sit behind OpenAI-compatible endpoints and route requests to cheaper or more suitable models based on task complexity and policy.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many agents send every request to the same large model, even when the task is tiny, repetitive, or cheap enough to deserve a less theatrical engine.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for model intelligence rather than routing policy. A router can reduce waste and add fallback behaviour, but it cannot make a weak task definition or poor evaluation strategy suddenly competent.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means model-tiering, fallback chains, budget controls, provider abstraction, local or cloud routing, and a sharper view of what each agent request actually costs.
entries.example: "Manifest saved money by routing simple agent chores away from the heroic model, which was a cruel blow to the heroic invoice."
askNext: What signals decide whether a task is simple, complex, or reasoning-heavy?
How are budgets, fallbacks, and provider failures handled?
Does cheaper routing preserve quality on the workloads that actually matter?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Stop paying frontier-model prices for every agent cough.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A routing and budget layer that makes agent spending less mystical and more accountable.

entries.body: The funny thing about autonomy is how quickly it needs accounting. Manifest is what happens when agent ambition meets a spreadsheet that can still feel pain.

entries.title: Manual approval
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Manual approval is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Human in the Loop and Rubber-Stamp Review are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The manual approval discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Manual approval is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Manual approval belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Manus
entries.devilDefinition: An invite-only demonstration that autonomy feels much more plausible when scarcity does some of the marketing.
entries.plainDefinition: Manus is an agent product associated with multi-step task execution across browsing, research, and software tools, marketed as a system that can carry out longer chains of work than a standard chatbot.
entries.whyExists: The appeal is obvious: people do not merely want better answers, they want finished tasks. A product that promises to browse, compare, draft, click, and return with the work already done sits directly on that desire.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as evidence that general-purpose autonomous agents are effectively solved. In reality, a polished demo reel, a waitlist, and a few successful runs do not abolish brittleness, supervision, or task selection.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a carefully packaged long-running agent workflow with browser actions, tool calls, and enough narrative polish to make ordinary automation feel like a historical turning point.
entries.example: "Manus looked like a digital chief of staff until the workflow left the happy path and became a queue with ambition."
askNext: What permissions, applications, and environments does it actually control?
How often does it recover from website changes, edge cases, or ambiguous instructions without human rescue?
Is the impressive part the autonomy, or the fact that someone selected a task where autonomy photographs well?
entries.warningLabel: A product launch and a cultural event, not a proof of solved autonomy.

translations.label: timeline meaning
translations.text: The agent moment has arrived and all remaining doubt is administrative.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A credible but constrained automation product whose strongest feature may be how vividly it dramatises the future.

entries.note: The term often refers as much to the reaction around the product as to the product itself.
entries.body: Manus matters because it compressed three separate things into one launch: agent capability, agent theatre, and agent envy.

entries.title: MCP
entries.devilDefinition: A way of making tools and resources speak a common dialect so the assistant stops needing a bespoke adapter for every cupboard in the house.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a protocol for exposing tools, resources, and related capabilities to AI clients in a standardised way, so assistants can discover and use external systems without each integration being entirely custom.
entries.whyExists: AI applications increasingly need access to files, APIs, documentation, databases, prompts, and tools. Without a shared protocol, every client-server pairing becomes its own integration project. MCP exists to reduce that fragmentation.
entries.misuse: It gets spoken about as though a protocol itself makes systems intelligent, secure, or interoperable by magic. It does not. Standardisation helps, but permissions, trust boundaries, authentication, and product design still matter more than the acronym.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means exposing capabilities through a consistent interface so a client can list resources, call tools, and work with external context more systematically. The real value is reducing bespoke glue and making integrations more legible.
entries.example: "MCP did not solve the assistant's judgement problem. It solved the quieter and more useful problem of not writing one-off connectors forever."
askNext: Which tools and resources are exposed through the protocol?
How are authentication, permissions, and auditability handled?
What part of the integration problem is standardised, and what still remains application-specific?

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A standard way to expose capabilities so clients do not need a fresh bespoke contract every time.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Less adapter sprawl, provided someone still does the hard work on security and product boundaries.

entries.body: Protocols are boring in the most productive possible sense. They do not remove the need for judgement. They remove some of the repetitive integration labour that distracts from the judgement problem.

entries.title: MCP Apps
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a tool result decides it deserves controls, state, and a small front-end team.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP Apps is an official Model Context Protocol extension for interactive user interfaces rendered inside an MCP host. A tool declares a UI resource; after the tool call, the host fetches that resource and renders its HTML in a sandboxed iframe within the conversation.
entries.whyExists: Some tool results are awkward as prose or static structured data. Maps, forms, charts, previews, and multi-step controls are easier to understand and operate as interfaces that remain connected to the tool and conversation context.
entries.misuse: It gets described as though returning HTML makes an MCP server a complete application platform. Hosts still decide support, sandboxes constrain behaviour, and the server still owns the unglamorous work of data, authentication, state, and safe tool design.
entries.practicalMeaning: The server exposes a tool plus a referenced UI resource. A compatible host calls the tool, fetches the resource, and renders it under host-controlled security restrictions. Teams must test host compatibility, content security, accessibility, and what happens when the interface is unavailable.
entries.example: "The MCP tool returned an interactive map, which clarified the result and expanded the threat model in one efficient gesture."
askNext: Which MCP hosts support this app and what is the fallback when they do not?
What data and actions can cross the sandbox boundary?
Does the interface clarify the tool result, or merely relocate the complexity?
entries.warningLabel: Interactive output brings interactive attack surface.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: An MCP tool can return an interface, provided the host agrees to render it and everyone remembers that HTML is executable ambition.

entries.body: Text was never going to contain the tool ecosystem forever. It merely enjoyed a short period of administrative control.

entries.title: MCP client
entries.devilDefinition: The component that dials MCP servers and offers their tools to a model, standing between curiosity and your filesystem.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP client connects to one or more MCP servers, negotiates capabilities, and exposes tools, resources, or prompts to a host application and ultimately to a model.
entries.whyExists: MCP defines roles. The client is the connector that makes server capabilities available inside an app session.
entries.misuse: Run with ambient high privileges and poor server authentication. Clients are part of the trust boundary.
entries.practicalMeaning: Authenticate servers, sandbox tool effects, surface invocations to users, and fail closed on unknown servers.
entries.example: "The MCP client listed three servers; only two were supposed to exist in prod."
askNext: How are servers authenticated and allowed?
What is visible to the model versus the user?
How are tool errors handled?
entries.warningLabel: A client is not a passive wire; it enforces or fails to enforce policy.
