entries.title: MCP Compliance
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a protocol badge is asked to do the emotional labour of a security programme.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP compliance is a claim that a tool, server, client, or platform follows the Model Context Protocol or a related set of compatibility requirements.
entries.whyExists: Protocols need conformance language so implementations can interoperate and buyers can understand which pieces fit together. Compliance can clarify supported capabilities, message shapes, transport expectations, and compatibility with clients.
entries.misuse: It gets stretched into a trust claim. Supporting MCP does not prove a server is secure, well governed, maintained, private, least-privilege, or appropriate for production. It mostly says the plumbing can speak the dialect.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means separating protocol conformance from organisational controls: authentication, permission review, logging, vendor risk, data handling, version pinning, incident response, and the tedious work a badge cannot perform.
entries.example: "The server was MCP compliant, which explained why the client could call it, not why anyone had allowed it access to payroll."
askNext: Which part of MCP is actually supported, and who tested it?
What security, privacy, audit, and permission controls sit outside the compliance claim?
Does compliance mean interoperable, certified, approved, or merely not immediately broken?
entries.warningLabel: Protocol compliance is not security certification.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: The implementation follows enough of the protocol to interoperate.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The compatibility question has been answered; the trust question has merely entered the room.

entries.body: Compliance language is useful when it stays in its lane. The trouble begins when it is promoted from "speaks protocol" to "safe to buy" without doing the intervening paperwork.
entries.searchText:
MCP Compliance Model Context Protocol compliance MCP compliant MCP-compatible compliance Agents and workflows Safety and evaluation Product and vendor terms mcp compliance governance protocols The moment a protocol badge is asked to do the emotional labour of a security programme.
 MCP compliance is a claim that a tool, server, client, or platform follows the Model Context Protocol or a related set of compatibility requirements.
 Protocols need conformance language so implementations can interoperate and buyers can understand which pieces fit together. Compliance can clarify supported capabilities, message shapes, transport expectations, and compatibility with clients.
 It gets stretched into a trust claim. Supporting MCP does not prove a server is secure, well governed, maintained, private, least-privilege, or appropriate for production. It mostly says the plumbing can speak the dialect.
 In practice it means separating protocol conformance from organisational controls: authentication, permission review, logging, vendor risk, data handling, version pinning, incident response, and the tedious work a badge cannot perform.
 "The server was MCP compliant, which explained why the client could call it, not why anyone had allowed it access to payroll."
 Which part of MCP is actually supported, and who tested it? What security, privacy, audit, and permission controls sit outside the compliance claim? Does compliance mean interoperable, certified, approved, or merely not immediately broken? MCP Tool Access Review Safety Washing

entries.title: MCP elicitation
entries.devilDefinition: A protocol path for asking the user, which is healthier than letting the agent invent consent from posture and vibes.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP elicitation is a mechanism for an MCP server to request additional information or confirmation from the user through the client during a workflow.
entries.whyExists: Servers sometimes need missing inputs, choices, approvals, or clarifications. Elicitation provides a structured way to ask for them without bypassing the client or pretending the model can infer everything.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a licence to interrupt people until ambiguity gives up. Asking the user is useful only when the request is clear, bounded, and worth their attention.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means the client needs good UI, permission rules, and logging around the question being asked, the answer received, and the action that answer enables.
entries.example: "MCP elicitation asked the user which account to update before the server performed the action, an innovation also known as not guessing with production data."
askNext: What exactly is the server asking the user to provide?
Does the user understand what their answer will authorise?
How is the elicited input recorded and bounded?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A structured way for a server to ask the user something through the client.

entries.body: Good elicitation is not more questions. It is the one question that stops the system from pretending uncertainty is confidence.

entries.title: MCP host
entries.devilDefinition: The application that owns the MCP client lifecycle and the consent UX—where responsibility sits when a tool goes rogue.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP host is the application (IDE, desktop agent, chat product) that embeds MCP client capabilities, manages connections to MCP servers, and presents permission surfaces to the user.
entries.whyExists: MCP separates servers (tools/resources) from hosts (user-facing apps). Security and UX live largely in the host.
entries.misuse: Blaming only servers for abuse when the host granted broad auto-approve. Host policy is the control plane.
entries.practicalMeaning: Implement least-privilege defaults, clear consent, server allowlists, and visible tool activity. Treat host security as product security.
entries.example: "The MCP host auto-approved a filesystem server and turned a coding session into a data export."
askNext: What does the host auto-approve?
Can users inspect and revoke server access?
How are dangerous tools labelled?
entries.warningLabel: Hosts decide whether MCP is power tools or open doors.

entries.title: MCP Marketplace
entries.devilDefinition: An app store for agent powers, arriving exactly when everyone needs a more adult relationship with install buttons.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP marketplace is a distribution surface where users, developers, vendors, or organisations can discover, publish, install, review, or manage MCP servers and related integrations.
entries.whyExists: MCP turns tool and resource servers into reusable software objects. Once there are enough of them, teams need discovery, packaging, trust signals, versioning, documentation, and a way to decide which integrations deserve to be near a real assistant.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as though marketplace listing equals safety, maintenance, or production readiness. Distribution is not approval. A glossy listing can still expose excessive scopes, stale schemas, weak authentication, or a vendor no one has assessed.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means procurement and security review meeting developer convenience: who authored the server, what it can read or change, how it is updated, what telemetry it emits, who can install it, and how fast it can be removed when enthusiasm ages badly.
entries.example: "The MCP marketplace made the connector easy to install, which was useful until someone asked why the demo weather tool also wanted access to customer exports."
askNext: Who curates, verifies, or removes marketplace listings?
What permissions, data flows, maintenance status, and owner metadata are visible before installation?
Can this marketplace enforce organisational policy, or does it merely decorate the install path?
entries.warningLabel: A marketplace listing is distribution, not due diligence.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A discovery and installation surface for MCP servers and integrations.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The plugin-store problem returns with sharper teeth because the plugins can act.

entries.body: Marketplaces make capability visible. They also make bad trust assumptions scalable, which is a less attractive feature but no less real.

entries.title: MCP prompt
entries.devilDefinition: A reusable instruction bundle, promoted to protocol furniture before someone asks whether it is any good.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP prompt is a reusable prompt template or prompt-like workflow exposed by an MCP server so a client can discover and invoke it with appropriate arguments.
entries.whyExists: Some integrations need repeatable ways to start tasks, collect inputs, or frame a domain-specific workflow. Exposing prompts through MCP lets servers offer those patterns without hiding them in client-specific code.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for business knowledge. A reusable prompt may encode a helpful workflow, but it can also encode yesterday's assumptions with better packaging.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means prompt templates become part of the integration surface and need versioning, ownership, review, and testing like any other operational artifact.
entries.example: "The MCP prompt helped draft the support summary, which was useful until someone noticed it still asked for a field the CRM had retired in March."
askNext: Who owns and reviews this MCP prompt?
What inputs does it require, and how are they validated?
Is this prompt a convenience, a policy surface, or accidental product logic?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A reusable prompt workflow exposed through MCP, and therefore another thing that can become stale.

entries.body: Putting a prompt behind a protocol does not make it less prompt-like. It only gives the maintenance debt a better address.

entries.title: MCP registry
entries.devilDefinition: A catalogue of helpful doors, followed immediately by the old problem of deciding who gets a key.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP registry is a catalogue or discovery surface for MCP servers, tools, resources, prompts, or integrations that clients and organisations can find, evaluate, and configure.
entries.whyExists: As MCP adoption grows, teams need a way to discover available servers and understand what each one exposes. A registry can reduce integration guesswork and make capability discovery less tribal.
entries.misuse: It gets treated like a marketplace where listing implies trust. Discovery is not approval, security review, maintenance, or suitability for production use.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means procurement, security review, version tracking, owner metadata, permission policies, and a process for removing servers that should no longer be near an assistant.
entries.example: "The MCP registry helped the team find the database connector, then ruined the mood by requiring someone to decide whether it was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: Who curates or approves entries in this registry?
What trust, permission, and maintenance signals are visible before installation?
How are risky or abandoned MCP servers removed?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A discovery layer that becomes a governance problem as soon as anyone can install from it.

entries.body: Every registry eventually becomes a policy conversation. The only surprise is how often people are surprised.

entries.title: MCP resource
entries.devilDefinition: Context exposed through a protocol, because apparently even the assistant now needs a filing system with manners.
entries.plainDefinition: An MCP resource is application-provided context exposed through the Model Context Protocol, such as a file, document, record, page, or other readable item that a client can discover or request.
entries.whyExists: MCP separates resources from tools. Tools do things; resources provide context. That distinction helps clients browse, retrieve, display, and reason about external information without turning every read into an action.
entries.misuse: It gets blurred into tool use, as though every available resource is something the model may freely consume. Resources still need permissions, scoping, metadata, and a reason to be used.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a server can expose relevant context in a standardised way, while the client decides what to show, fetch, or pass into the model for a specific task.
entries.example: "The MCP resource exposed the project brief to the assistant; it did not authorise the assistant to rummage through payroll because curiosity felt agentic."
askNext: What resource types does the MCP server expose?
How are permissions and freshness handled for each resource?
Which resources are shown to the user, and which are passed into the model?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Readable context exposed by an MCP server, not a permission slip for unlimited access.

entries.body: Resources are where MCP becomes more useful than a pile of tool buttons. They are also where lazy access control starts to look expensive.

entries.title: MCP roots
entries.devilDefinition: The places a client admits may matter, not a notarised invitation to eat the disk.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP roots are client-provided locations, such as folders or workspace boundaries, that tell an MCP server which areas are relevant to the current context.
entries.whyExists: Servers often need to understand the project, workspace, or file area a user is working in. Roots provide that boundary without requiring the server to infer relevance from the entire machine or account.
entries.misuse: They get confused with permissions. A root can describe relevant scope, but it does not by itself settle what the server may read, write, index, or send elsewhere.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means tooling can be more context-aware while still needing explicit access controls, user consent, audit logs, and conservative defaults around sensitive files.
entries.example: "The client shared the repository root with the MCP server, which was context, not a romantic commitment to every secret in the home directory."
askNext: What locations are being declared as roots?
Which operations are allowed inside those roots?
How does the system prevent a root from becoming silent overreach?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A declared context boundary that still needs real permissions around it.

entries.body: Roots are useful because they name the workspace. They are dangerous when teams start treating naming as authorisation.

entries.title: MCP sampling
entries.devilDefinition: The part where the server asks the client to make the model think, because control flow was apparently too peaceful.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP sampling is the protocol capability that lets an MCP server request model generation through the client, rather than directly calling a model itself.
entries.whyExists: Some servers need model assistance during a workflow but should not own model credentials, user context, or policy decisions. Sampling lets the client mediate that generation request.
entries.misuse: It is easy to describe as the server "using the model", which hides the important part: the client remains the place where user consent, model choice, policy, and context should be controlled.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means the client must decide whether a server's generation request is allowed, what context may be included, and how the resulting text is shown, logged, or passed back.
entries.example: "MCP sampling let the planning server ask for a summary, but the client still decided which model, which files, and which secrets were not invited."
askNext: Which server requests are allowed to trigger sampling?
What context can the server ask the client to include?
How are sampled outputs audited before they influence a workflow?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A server-requested model call mediated by the client, not a free side door to generation.

entries.body: Sampling is useful precisely because the server does not get to become the whole application by asking nicely.

entries.title: MCP Server
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP Server names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around MiniMax need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said mcp server as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."

entries.title: Mechanistic interpretability
entries.devilDefinition: Trying to reverse-engineer the model as if it were software written by a committee of partial differential equations.
entries.plainDefinition: Mechanistic interpretability (MI) is the research programme that aims to explain model behaviour by identifying internal features, circuits, and algorithms implemented in weights and activations.
entries.whyExists: Black-box evals do not explain why models do what they do. MI seeks scientific traction on internals for science and safety.
entries.misuse: Overpromised as near-term audit complete enough for full assurance. Progress is real and incomplete.
entries.practicalMeaning: Track MI findings as research inputs to safety work, not as a substitute for behavioural evals, red teaming, and access controls today.
entries.example: "Mechanistic interpretability found a circuit; product still shipped based on a leaderboard."
askNext: What concrete decision does an MI result change?
How general is the finding across models?
What behavioural tests corroborate the mechanism?
entries.warningLabel: Partial maps are not full certifications.

entries.title: Memory boundary
entries.devilDefinition: The line between useful continuity and an assistant becoming a filing cabinet with social ambitions.
entries.plainDefinition: A memory boundary defines what an AI system may remember, retrieve, update, forget, or expose across sessions, users, tools, and contexts.
entries.whyExists: Persistent memory can make systems more useful, but it also creates privacy, relevance, contamination, and access-control problems. Boundaries keep memory from becoming an unreviewed second database.
entries.misuse: It gets softened into a settings label. The real question is not whether the product "has memory", but what crosses between contexts and who can inspect or delete it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means retention rules, user controls, retrieval scopes, redaction, tenant separation, auditability, and clear defaults for what should not be remembered at all.
entries.example: "The memory boundary stopped the assistant from using a private support detail in a sales draft, which was kind of it."
askNext: What information can be stored as memory?
Who can read, edit, or delete that memory?
How does the system prevent one context's memory from leaking into another?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The rules around what continuity is allowed to survive, where, and for whom.

entries.body: Memory without boundaries is just retention policy written by optimism.

entries.title: Memory hygiene
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Memory hygiene names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Context Poisoning needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

entries.title: Memory Policy
entries.devilDefinition: The quiet constitution deciding what the system remembers, for how long, and which future mistake inherits the privilege.
entries.plainDefinition: Memory policy is the set of rules governing what an AI system stores from interactions, how long it keeps that information, when it retrieves it, and when it must forget or redact it.
entries.whyExists: It exists because continuity is useful and indiscriminate memory is dangerous. Long-running systems can become more helpful by remembering preferences, facts, and prior decisions, but they can also become more wrong, more invasive, and more difficult to debug.
entries.misuse: The term gets reduced to "we have memory now", as if any retained context were inherently intelligent. It also gets used to dress up ordinary logging or vector storage without answering the harder questions about consent, correction, deletion, and sensitive data.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means retention windows, summarisation rules, provenance, retrieval thresholds, redaction filters, user controls, and clear logic for when stale memory should be ignored rather than honoured out of misplaced loyalty.
entries.example: "The assistant felt personal right up until its memory policy allowed one outdated preference to haunt every recommendation for the next three months."
askNext: What information qualifies for memory, and what must never be stored?
How are stale or incorrect memories corrected, deleted, or superseded?
When memory is retrieved, how does the system distinguish relevance from sentimental hoarding?
entries.warningLabel: Useful until it starts remembering the wrong thing with perfect confidence.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: The rules that make continuity feel helpful instead of mildly creepy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A set of retention, retrieval, and deletion decisions nobody can safely leave to improvisation.

entries.body: Memory policy matters because forgetting is part of quality control. A system that never forgets is often just a system that never learned restraint.

entries.title: Memory tool
entries.devilDefinition: A file cabinet the model may update between conversations, with filing standards supplied by whichever application volunteered to become the archivist.
entries.plainDefinition: A memory tool is a client-implemented tool that lets a model read and modify persistent information outside the current conversation. In Anthropic's named implementation, the client provides file-style operations and storage while the model decides what memory to create, retrieve, update, or delete.
entries.whyExists: A context window ends with the request, while useful assistants need preferences, project state, and learned instructions to survive across sessions. A tool gives the model explicit operations over durable storage instead of pretending all prior conversation can remain in every prompt.
entries.misuse: Memory is marketed as though persistence implies accurate recall. A model can store the wrong conclusion, retrieve stale instructions, expose sensitive material, or keep a confident summary long after the underlying facts have changed.
entries.practicalMeaning: The application owns the storage boundary, access controls, retention, deletion, and synchronisation. It should show users what is remembered, separate scopes, defend memory writes from prompt injection, and verify important state against current sources before acting.
entries.example: "The memory tool remembered the user's preferred report format and the obsolete project deadline with equal devotion."
askNext: Where is memory stored, who can inspect it, and which conversations share it?
What may write, update, or delete a memory, and how are hostile instructions filtered?
Which facts must be revalidated against a live source before the agent acts?
entries.warningLabel: Persistent does not mean current, correct, or consented.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: Give the model explicit operations over storage you must secure, explain, expire, and occasionally distrust.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the difficult part is memory scope, provenance, retention, and mutation; the current retrieval diagram would make those governance questions look like a search pipeline.

entries.title: Memory wall
entries.devilDefinition: The point at which giving the system more history, more notes, or more retrieved context starts producing less memory and more administrative clutter.
entries.plainDefinition: The memory wall is the practical limit at which adding more persistent, retrieved, or reusable context stops improving task performance and starts degrading relevance, latency, cost, or maintainability.
entries.whyExists: Systems need ways to retain useful state across turns and sessions, but memory is not an unqualified good. Past a certain point, too much retained material becomes harder to curate, rank, prune, trust, and pay for than it is to use well.
entries.misuse: It gets borrowed lazily from hardware lore or treated as proof that "AI memory just does not work". The useful version is more specific: the wall appears when memory management becomes an editorial and operational problem, not when the product team grows bored of trying.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means stale summaries, duplicated notes, bloated retrieval sets, rising token spend, confused prioritisation, and elaborate memory stores whose main contribution is making the wrong thing easier to fetch repeatedly.
entries.example: "They kept adding memory features until the assistant could remember everything except what mattered now. The memory wall had arrived with excellent storage metrics."
askNext: Which retained context is still improving outcomes, and which merely survives by inertia?
How is stale or low-value memory pruned, compacted, or re-ranked over time?
Is the bottleneck model capacity, retrieval quality, or the cost of curating what the system remembers?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The retained context has become harder to curate than to collect.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A limit where more memory stops helping the task and starts burdening the system.

entries.body: The memory wall is not an argument against memory. It is an argument against pretending retention automatically produces recall, relevance, or judgement.

entries.title: Meta
entries.devilDefinition: Meta is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Meta is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Llama often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Meta came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: Microsoft AI
entries.devilDefinition: The part of Microsoft tasked with making "Copilot everywhere" sound less like a slogan and more like the natural order of software.
entries.plainDefinition: Microsoft AI is the organisation Microsoft formed in 2024 to advance Copilot and its consumer AI products and research. It is distinct from Microsoft as a whole, and also distinct from Azure's infrastructure role or OpenAI's position as a model partner.
entries.whyExists: Because Microsoft needed a named unit for the part of its AI strategy focused on product experience, consumer interfaces, and turning model access into habitual software use.
entries.misuse: By using "Microsoft AI" to mean Microsoft generally, Azure specifically, or OpenAI by implication. The company has several overlapping AI layers; this term refers to the product-and-research organisation built around Copilot and related consumer experiences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the organisational machinery behind Microsoft's bid to make AI a persistent interface layer across its consumer products, with Copilot as the flagship expression of that strategy.
entries.example: "They said Microsoft AI as if that explained the architecture. In reality it explained the product thesis: put a companion layer in front of as much software as possible."
askNext: Do they mean Microsoft AI, Azure AI infrastructure, or Microsoft's OpenAI partnership?
Is the topic model capability, product interface, or distribution?
How much of the value comes from Copilot branding versus actual workflow integration?
entries.warningLabel: Organisation name. Not a synonym for Azure, OpenAI, or every AI feature Microsoft ships.

translations.label: enterprise meaning
translations.text: The part of Microsoft trying to turn AI into a default interface, not merely an optional feature.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Microsoft's dedicated organisation for turning model capability into sticky products people actually use.

entries.note: Microsoft announced the organisation on March 19, 2024, framing it around Copilot and other consumer AI products and research. That matters because "Microsoft AI" is a specific organisational bet, not a vague brand halo.
entries.body: If OpenAI is the supplier in Microsoft's story, Microsoft AI is the packaging department with imperial ambitions.

entries.title: Microsoft Copilot
entries.devilDefinition: Software sold as an assistant and deployed as a new layer of management: supervising the machine that was supposed to save you time.
entries.plainDefinition: Microsoft Copilot is Microsoft's umbrella brand for AI assistants and generative features across workplace and consumer products, including Microsoft 365, Windows, developer tools, and business software.
entries.whyExists: Because Microsoft wants AI to become the default interface layer across the software people already pay for, turning model access into subscription expansion and habitual workflow presence.
entries.misuse: By using "Copilot" as if it named one coherent product or guaranteed productivity. In reality it is a family resemblance: similar branding spread across many surfaces with uneven usefulness.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means an AI sidebar, a drafting assistant, a meeting-summary machine, a permissions headache, and a fresh round of arguments about whether the licence justifies itself.
entries.example: "The rollout promised a copilot for every employee. What most people got was a new button and one more thing to evaluate before sending an email."
askNext: Which Copilot are we discussing, in which product, with which permissions and data access?
What measurable work improved after adoption rather than merely feeling more modern?
Is this replacing friction, or adding a branded detour to familiar tasks?
entries.warningLabel: Umbrella product label. Always ask which Copilot and in which software surface.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: Proof that the office suite now has an AI strategy attached to it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A branded assistant layer whose value depends heavily on product surface, permissions, and user patience.

entries.note: The important question is rarely whether Copilot exists. It is whether a specific Copilot meaningfully improves a specific workflow enough to survive the invoice.
entries.body: The branding suggests a tireless co-worker. The operating reality is closer to a software committee member.

entries.title: Midjourney
entries.devilDefinition: A picture factory for turning "I have no visual taste" into "I have a campaign."
entries.plainDefinition: Midjourney is an image-generation product known for stylised visual output, rapid iteration, and strong adoption among designers, marketers, and hobbyists.
entries.whyExists: Because visual ideation is expensive, taste is unevenly distributed, and many people would like concept art, campaign imagery, or mood boards before hiring anyone who can draw.
entries.misuse: By treating it as either the death of design or the same thing as design. It is neither. It is a very powerful image engine whose output still requires selection, direction, and legal and brand judgement.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means mood boards at industrial speed, more options than anyone needed, and a sudden premium on the human ability to reject what merely looks expensive.
entries.example: "Midjourney gave the team one hundred glorious directions, which unfortunately left them needing a designer more than they had an hour earlier."
askNext: Is the goal ideation, final artwork, or merely spectacle for a pitch deck?
Who is making the taste decisions after the model floods the room with options?
What rights, brand constraints, and production steps still sit outside the image generator?

translations.label: marketing meaning
translations.text: A way to make the brand look visionary before the brand has decided what it believes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An image tool that dramatically lowers the cost of visual exploration while raising the need for editorial restraint.

entries.body: The machine can flood the page with possibility. Someone still has to decide which possibility deserves civilisation.

Midjourney Midjourney image model MJ Product and vendor terms Cultural terms Model usage images design creative tools A picture factory for turning "I have no visual taste" into "I have a campaign."
 Midjourney is an image-generation product known for stylised visual output, rapid iteration, and strong adoption among designers, marketers, and hobbyists.
 Because visual ideation is expensive, taste is unevenly distributed, and many people would like concept art, campaign imagery, or mood boards before hiring anyone who can draw.
 By treating it as either the death of design or the same thing as design. It is neither. It is a very powerful image engine whose output still requires selection, direction, and legal and brand judgement.
 In practice it usually means mood boards at industrial speed, more options than anyone needed, and a sudden premium on the human ability to reject what merely looks expensive.
 "Midjourney gave the team one hundred glorious directions, which unfortunately left them needing a designer more than they had an hour earlier."
 Is the goal ideation, final artwork, or merely spectacle for a pitch deck? Who is making the taste decisions after the model floods the room with options? What rights, brand constraints, and production steps still sit outside the image generator? Concept art Mood board Creative direction  Image generation Styles Mood boards

entries.title: Mini Apps
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Mini Apps names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Telegram needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once mini apps entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: MiniMax
entries.devilDefinition: A model vendor whose product surface suggests the future will be multimodal, agentic, and administratively impossible to describe in one sentence.
entries.plainDefinition: MiniMax is an AI company and model platform offering text, speech, video, image, music, file, and agent-facing API capabilities, including official MCP server implementations for some modalities.
entries.whyExists: It exists because model platforms are becoming bundles of capabilities rather than single chat endpoints. Teams increasingly buy text, voice, media generation, tool calling, and integrations from the same vendor shelf.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to another model name when the operational question is broader: which modality, which API, which latency, which rights, and which integration surface are actually being used?
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a vendor option for multimodal generation, voice and media workflows, agent integrations, and OpenAI or Anthropic SDK-compatible access paths.
entries.example: "MiniMax entered the architecture review as a text model and left carrying voice, video, MCP, and three new procurement questions."
askNext: Which MiniMax capability is in scope: text, speech, video, image, music, or MCP integration?
What are the latency, licensing, data, and moderation constraints for that modality?
Does the unified vendor surface reduce complexity, or merely collect it in one account?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: One vendor trying to cover more of the generative stack than plain chat.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A multimodal API supplier whose exact relevance depends on the workload, not the brand.

entries.body: The vendor map gets less neat every month. MiniMax is one of the reminders that "model provider" now often means "small department store".

entries.title: Mistral AI
entries.devilDefinition: The French company that managed to make "European frontier AI" sound like a commercial category rather than a wistful policy memo.
entries.plainDefinition: Mistral AI is a French AI company building models, products, and platform services, with a public identity built around efficient frontier systems, enterprise deployment, and a more open posture than many large closed-model competitors.
entries.whyExists: Because the market wanted proof that serious model-building could happen outside the dominant US labs, and because Mistral made strategic use of openness, efficiency, and European identity to become that proof.
entries.misuse: By describing Mistral AI as either purely open-source or purely a geopolitical symbol. It is a commercial model company making selective openness part of its strategy, not a charity for developer ideals.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a vendor choice for teams that want competitive models, deployment flexibility, open-weight options, and some combination of cost discipline, control, and distance from the biggest US platforms.
entries.example: "They said they wanted Mistral for openness. What they really wanted was optionality, speed, and a way to avoid handing every strategic decision to the usual American suspects."
askNext: Do they want Mistral for model quality, deployment control, openness, or pricing?
Which product do they mean: API models, Le Chat, enterprise platform, or open-weight releases?
Is the attraction technical fit or strategic independence?
entries.warningLabel: Do not confuse a selectively open commercial strategy with pure open-source idealism.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to say we want frontier capability without sounding resigned to the usual stack.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A serious model vendor using efficiency, optionality, and selective openness as competitive weapons.

entries.note: Mistral's own language emphasises accessible frontier AI, and the Mistral 3 line makes the selective-open strategy more explicit: large frontier models, smaller deployment-friendly models, and enough openness to be strategically useful without becoming simple charity.
entries.body: It is one of the clearest examples of how "open" in AI rarely means simple purity. It usually means a strategy, a pricing posture, and a market wedge wearing noble clothes.

entries.title: Mixture of Experts
entries.devilDefinition: An architecture for having enormous parameter counts without insisting every token use the whole cathedral.
entries.plainDefinition: A mixture-of-experts model is a neural architecture in which a routing mechanism activates only a subset of specialised sub-networks for each token or input, allowing higher total capacity without using all parameters on every pass.
entries.whyExists: Because model builders want more capacity and performance without paying the full compute cost of dense models at every step. Sparse activation offers a way to scale capability more economically, at least in theory.
entries.misuse: By treating "experts" as if the model contains little professors with stable domains of knowledge. It is an optimisation strategy, not a panel discussion. The routing can help a great deal, but it does not grant wisdom by committee.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means better parameter-efficiency on paper, more complex training and serving behaviour, routing overhead, and a fresh set of engineering questions about load balancing, latency spikes, and uneven quality.
entries.example: "The model was a mixture of experts, which sounded philosophical until the serving team had to explain the routing bottlenecks."
askNext: How many experts exist, and how many are active per token?
What did sparse routing improve relative to a dense baseline at the same cost?
Did the architecture move the bottleneck from compute to systems complexity?

translations.label: conference meaning
translations.text: A way to say the model is huge without admitting every request does not touch most of it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Sparse activation traded some compute cost for substantially more systems complexity.

entries.body: The name flatters the machinery. What matters is not that there are experts, but whether the router sends work to the right places often enough to justify the complications.

entries.title: Model access
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Model access names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Closed Weight needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once model access entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: Model Armor
entries.devilDefinition: A safety layer with a heroic name, because apparently "input and output protection" lacked chainmail.
entries.plainDefinition: Model Armor is Google Cloud's product surface for adding prompt, response, and content protection controls around AI applications.
entries.whyExists: Organisations deploying AI systems need ways to inspect prompts and outputs for policy violations, unsafe content, prompt injection risks, and other application-level safety concerns.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for universal protection. A safety product can reduce some classes of risk, but it does not remove the need for permissions, architecture, logging, testing, and common sense.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means adding a managed safety layer to AI traffic, then deciding which policies apply, what gets blocked, how false positives are handled, and who reviews the exceptions.
entries.example: "Model Armor blocked the prompt injection attempt, which was useful and still not a reason to give the assistant write access to everything with a dashboard."
askNext: Which prompts and responses pass through this protection layer?
What policies are enforced, and who tunes them?
What happens when protection blocks legitimate work or misses a real attack?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A managed safety layer, not armour against every failure mode with a model nearby.

entries.body: Armour helps. It does not make charging at the problem a strategy.

entries.title: Model card
entries.devilDefinition: The nutrition label for a model that vendors fill in with virtues and leave blank where the allergens would be.
entries.plainDefinition: A model card is a documentation artefact describing a model's intended use, training data overview, evaluation results, limitations, and ethical considerations, popularised as a transparency practice.
entries.whyExists: Models shipped without shared context about scope and failure modes. Cards created a standard-ish place to put that context.
entries.misuse: Treated as complete safety proof or marketing brochure. A card can be outdated, selective, or disconnected from the deployed checkpoint.
entries.practicalMeaning: Require cards for production models, version them with checkpoints, and link to live eval dashboards rather than static PDFs alone.
entries.example: "The model card said 'not for medical use' on page nine of the onboarding that nobody finished."
askNext: Does the card match the exact deployed revision?
Which evals are reported, and which are missing?
Who updates the card when fine-tunes ship?
entries.warningLabel: Documentation is not runtime enforcement.

entries.title: Model Collapse
entries.devilDefinition: The fear that if the machines keep learning from one another's homework, the class average will eventually become the curriculum.
entries.plainDefinition: Model collapse is the hypothesised degradation that can occur when models are trained repeatedly on synthetic or model-generated data without enough fresh, high-quality human data to preserve diversity and fidelity.
entries.whyExists: Because synthetic data is useful, cheap, and increasingly tempting at scale, but feedback loops can distort distributions, wash out edge cases, and quietly teach systems to imitate their own errors.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a guaranteed apocalypse or as proof that synthetic data is worthless. Synthetic data can be valuable. The concern is about unmanaged feedback loops, weak provenance, and replacing judgement with volume.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means teams need stronger data lineage, better curation, and honest evaluation before they let generated material dominate the training mix just because it is abundant and conveniently machine-readable.
entries.example: "The lab was not worried about synthetic data in principle. It was worried about model collapse from feeding the next system a buffet of the previous system's bad habits."
askNext: How much of the training mix is synthetic, and how is its quality measured?
What prevents generated noise from becoming self-confirming doctrine?
Are we preserving human-created edge cases or optimising them out of existence?

translations.label: internet meaning
translations.text: The machines are eating too much of their own content and may start to resemble it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Poorly managed recursive data pipelines can flatten quality faster than the dashboards admit.

entries.body: The phrase has become a general anxiety container, which is understandable. Still, the useful question is not whether collapse is coming in the abstract, but whether your data pipeline has the manners to notice contamination before it institutionalises it.

entries.title: Model distillation
entries.devilDefinition: Teaching a smaller model to imitate a larger one so you can serve almost the same answers with a better bill—almost being the expensive word.
entries.plainDefinition: Model distillation trains a student model to match a teacher model's outputs or behaviours, transferring capability into a smaller or cheaper model.
entries.whyExists: Large models are costly to serve. Distillation is a primary way to compress capability for deployment.
entries.misuse: Assumed complete transfer. Students miss long tails, tool skills, and safety behaviours unless those are explicitly distilled and tested.
entries.practicalMeaning: Distil on task distributions you care about, evaluate safety and capability separately, and monitor production divergence from the teacher.
entries.example: "Model distillation kept the demo smart and dropped the edge-case refusals."
askNext: What teacher behaviours were included in training?
Which evals compare student and teacher?
Is the student allowed to drift via later fine-tunes?
entries.warningLabel: Imitation is selective unless you force breadth.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Distil on task distributions you care about, evaluate safety and capability separately, and monitor production divergence from the teacher.

entries.body: Distillation is apprenticeship at scale. Apprentices skip lessons you do not teach.

entries.title: Model drift
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a system that used to behave one way quietly acquires new manners, new vices, or a fresh interpretation of your instructions, and no dashboard feels personally responsible.
entries.plainDefinition: Model drift is the change in a model system's behaviour over time, whether because the underlying model changed, surrounding prompts and tools changed, traffic changed, or the environment stopped being the one the original evaluation had in mind.
entries.whyExists: AI systems are not static artefacts. Providers update models, teams revise prompts, tools change their schemas, and user behaviour wanders off. The term exists because quality can move even when nobody thinks they touched the main lever.
entries.misuse: It gets used as a polite synonym for "something got worse". Sometimes the issue is genuine behaviour drift. Sometimes it is bad retrieval, a broken tool, a prompt edit, or a team discovering that last month's benchmark did not survive contact with this month's traffic.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means running comparisons over time, holding out stable evaluation sets, watching sensitive slices rather than averages, and keeping enough version history to identify whether the model changed or the system around it did.
entries.example: "The team called it model drift because that sounded dignified. The incident report later revealed a prompt change, a tool timeout, and optimism."
askNext: Which part of the system changed: the base model, the prompt stack, the tools, the routing, or the traffic?
What stable evaluation set shows the behaviour actually moved rather than merely encountering a harder week?
Can we roll back or replay the previous configuration to isolate the change?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The system changed somewhere, and now you must prove where before someone blames the wrong component.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A behaviour change whose cause may live in the model, the scaffolding, or the surrounding workflow.

entries.body: Model drift deserves a score of five because teams confuse the label with the diagnosis. The phrase is useful only if it narrows the search rather than sanctifying the shrug.

entries.title: Model edit
entries.devilDefinition: A targeted parameter tweak sold as mind surgery, sometimes leaving the patient with new superstitions.
entries.plainDefinition: A model edit is a specific change to a model's parameters or associated memory to alter particular behaviours or facts, encompassing techniques like rank-one updates, adapter patches, or constrained fine-tunes.
entries.whyExists: Teams need fixes smaller than full retraining when a model is mostly fine but wrong in a critical place.
entries.misuse: Stacked edits without regression testing. Multiple local fixes can interact unpredictably.
entries.practicalMeaning: Version every edit, run neighbouring-fact and capability regressions, and prefer external knowledge for volatile facts.
entries.example: "The model edit corrected the pricing table and invented a new discount for fun."
askNext: What regression suite guards each edit?
Is the edit reversible?
Would retrieval solve this more cleanly?
entries.warningLabel: Small diffs can still have large behavioural radii.

entries.title: Model Garden
entries.devilDefinition: A cloud menu where every model is arranged like a tasteful orchard and billed like enterprise software.
entries.plainDefinition: Model garden is a vendor term for a managed catalogue of models that customers can browse, provision, fine-tune, and call through a cloud platform.
entries.whyExists: Because buyers want more than one model, clouds want to be the place where that choice gets made, and nobody enjoys integrating a fresh API every time the leaderboard twitches.
entries.misuse: By making model access sound like curation solved the hard part. A catalogue is useful. It does not answer which model is trustworthy, economical, regionally available, or appropriate for a real workload.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a procurement-and-deployment layer with billing, quotas, IAM, regions, logging, and just enough abstraction to turn model shopping into infrastructure policy.
entries.example: "The model garden looked abundant until the architecture review reduced it to the three options legal, security, and latency could tolerate."
askNext: Which models are actually production-ready for our constraints?
What vendor lock-in sits behind the pleasant catalogue language?
Are we choosing a model, or quietly choosing an entire cloud relationship?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A cloud-controlled shelf of models that turns experimentation into something procurement can invoice.

entries.body: The garden metaphor is doing heavy work here. It suggests abundance, order, and gentle stewardship. The operational reality is still quotas, contracts, and somebody arguing about egress.

entries.title: Model hosting
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: Model hosting names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Inference Provider and Weights need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said model hosting as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."

entries.title: Model Inventory
entries.devilDefinition: The list every governance programme asks for after discovering the organisation has been shipping intelligence through spreadsheets, vendor portals, and brave endpoints with no forwarding address.
entries.plainDefinition: A model inventory is a maintained catalogue of models, AI systems, wrappers, endpoints, vendors, owners, versions, uses, data dependencies, risk tiers, and deployment status.
entries.whyExists: It exists because an organisation cannot govern, monitor, retire, insure, or defend systems it cannot name. As AI spreads through products and departments, the inventory becomes the map before the controls can be more than theatre.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to a spreadsheet made once for an audit, or narrowed to only the models built by the central AI team. The inconvenient work is finding the bought tools, embedded features, shadow pilots, spreadsheet miracles, and vendor models already touching business decisions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means ownership records, risk classification, approved uses, data lineage, model versions, vendor contracts, deployment locations, monitoring status, incident history, and a process for updating the record when the system changes.
entries.example: "The model inventory began as governance and ended as archaeology, with procurement holding the torch."
askNext: Which models, wrappers, endpoints, and vendor features are actually included?
Who owns each system after launch, and who can retire it?
How often is the inventory reconciled against production, procurement, and shadow use?
entries.warningLabel: If the inventory is only a document, the estate will continue living elsewhere.

translations.label: governance meaning
translations.text: A living record of which AI systems exist, who owns them, what they touch, and how risky they are.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The first uncomfortable admission that the organisation has more AI than its policy remembers.

entries.body: The inventory is not the control. It is the moment the control stops pretending the estate is smaller than it is.

entries.title: Model provider routing
entries.devilDefinition: Model provider routing is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Model provider routing is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Cline often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

entries.title: Model Risk Management
entries.devilDefinition: The discipline of discovering that a model can be accurate, useful, approved, and still a liability with a login.
entries.plainDefinition: Model risk management is the set of governance, validation, monitoring, documentation, and control practices used to identify and manage risks created by models in business processes.
entries.whyExists: Models can fail through bias, drift, misuse, bad data, overconfidence, hidden assumptions, weak validation, unclear ownership, and operational dependence. AI has made those risks broader, faster, and harder to keep politely inside spreadsheets.
entries.misuse: It is misused when teams equate risk management with a launch checklist or a policy document. The useful version follows the model through design, data, testing, approval, deployment, monitoring, change control, and retirement.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means inventories, risk tiering, independent validation, evaluation coverage, monitoring, incident handling, version control, human oversight, documentation, and clear accountability when model behaviour affects money, rights, safety, or operations.
entries.example: "The chatbot entered model risk management when its confident summaries started shaping refunds, escalations, and the legal team's blood pressure."
askNext: Which risks are tracked before and after deployment?
Who validates the model independently, and what evidence can they inspect?
What monitoring, incident, change-control, and retirement process exists?
entries.warningLabel: A checklist is not a control if nobody can see the model failing.

translations.label: risk meaning
translations.text: The controls that keep model behaviour visible, validated, monitored, and accountable.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The moment an AI feature stops being a demo and starts needing adult supervision.

entries.body: Model risk management is not there to make models less interesting. It is there because interesting failures tend to arrive with stakeholders.

entries.title: Model rollback
entries.devilDefinition: The dignified way of saying that production reality declined the invitation to share your release optimism.
entries.plainDefinition: Model rollback is the controlled reversion from a newly deployed model, prompt stack, or routing configuration back to a previous version after regressions, cost surprises, or safety issues appear.
entries.whyExists: AI releases do not fail only by crashing. They also fail by becoming slower, stranger, costlier, or less trustworthy in ways that benchmarks and demos did not predict. The term exists because recovery needs to be designed before the awkwardness arrives.
entries.misuse: It gets imagined as a simple button. Real rollback is harder when model changes came bundled with prompt edits, schema changes, routing tweaks, tool updates, or silently shifted provider behaviour. Reverting the label is not the same as restoring the old system.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means version pinning, release gates, shadow tests, archived prompts, configuration history, and evidence good enough to decide whether the problem lives in the model, the workflow, or the surrounding environment.
entries.example: "The rollback was delayed because nobody had preserved the previous prompt bundle, proving once again that release confidence and release hygiene are not twins."
askNext: What exactly must be restored for this rollback to recreate the previous behaviour?
Which regressions or risk signals are strong enough to trigger reversal?
Can the team compare the old and new configurations cleanly, or only nostalgically?

translations.label: release meaning
translations.text: Return the system to the last version that deserved trust.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A recovery path that only works if the team kept version history, configuration discipline, and the courage to use them.

entries.body: Model rollback is one of those ideas everyone supports in principle and neglects in implementation. Then production develops opinions, and the omission becomes educational.

entries.title: Model Router
entries.devilDefinition: The switchboard that promises the right model for every job and occasionally adds a thrilling new place for policy mistakes to hide.
entries.plainDefinition: A model router is a software layer that selects among multiple models or providers for a given request based on rules or signals such as task type, latency, cost, availability, policy, or fallback behaviour.
entries.whyExists: Because no single model is best at everything, outages happen, prices move, and product teams would prefer not to hard-code every bet into the application layer.
entries.misuse: By marketing routing itself as intelligence, or by implying that a multi-model stack is automatically well evaluated. A router can express good operational judgement. It can also just spread uncertainty across more vendors.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means heuristics, policy rules, retries, provider abstractions, observability, and a permanent negotiation between quality, speed, and the monthly bill.
entries.example: "The model router kept the app online during the outage, then introduced a fresh round of debugging because two providers interpreted the same tool schema differently."
askNext: What signals drive routing decisions, and were they benchmarked on real workloads?
What is the fallback policy when the preferred model fails or drifts?
Does the router reduce operational risk, or merely distribute it?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: One control plane to decide whose model invoice gets the request.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An operational policy engine standing between product promises and vendor behaviour.

entries.note: Vendors often sell model routers as a neutral convenience layer. In practice the interesting part is what routing policy the customer can inspect, override, and trust.
entries.body: The appeal is obvious. So is the trap. Once routing becomes strategic, the application has acquired a small foreign policy department.

entries.title: Model routing
entries.devilDefinition: Sending easy questions to the cheap model and hard ones to the expensive model, until the router becomes the new untested model.
entries.plainDefinition: Model routing is the practice of directing each request to a chosen model or endpoint based on rules, classifiers, or policies—typically to balance cost, latency, capability, and risk.
entries.whyExists: One model is rarely optimal for every query. Systems needed a control plane for 'good enough versus best available' without rewriting the product for each call.
entries.misuse: Presented as free optimisation. Bad routers misclassify hard tasks onto weak models, or thrash between endpoints until latency and bills both rise.
entries.practicalMeaning: Define routing features (length, tool need, risk tier), log decisions, measure quality per route, and keep a safe fallback. Treat the router as production logic with tests.
entries.example: "Model routing saved money until customer support tickets all got the thrifty brain."
askNext: What signals decide the route, and how are they tested?
What is the fallback when the primary model fails?
Do you measure task success per route, not only average cost?
entries.warningLabel: The router can become the product’s quiet single point of judgement.

entries.title: Model signing
entries.devilDefinition: Cryptographic attestation that a weight file is the one you meant to deploy, not a surprise remix from an untrusted mirror.
entries.plainDefinition: Model signing attaches cryptographic signatures or attestations to model artefacts so deployers can verify integrity and authenticity before loading weights into production.
entries.whyExists: Weights are large binaries moved across registries and clouds. Integrity checks reduce tampering and mix-up risk.
entries.misuse: Treated as proof the model is safe or unbiased. Signatures prove provenance of bits, not goodness of behaviour.
entries.practicalMeaning: Sign at publish time, verify at deploy time, manage keys properly, and fail closed on mismatch.
entries.example: "Model signing blocked a checkpoint that had the right name and the wrong hash."
askNext: Who holds signing keys, and how are they rotated?
Does every environment verify before load?
What is the process for emergency hotfixes?
entries.warningLabel: A valid signature can still belong to a terrible model.

entries.title: Model spec
entries.devilDefinition: A public document describing how the model ought to behave, which is at least more honest than pretending the behaviour emerged from pure mathematics and good breeding.
entries.plainDefinition: A model spec is a provider-authored behavioural specification that describes the intended priorities, refusal patterns, boundaries, and instruction-handling rules for a model or model family.
entries.whyExists: Modern models are expected to be useful, safe, steerable, and publicly defensible at once. A specification exists so providers can state what behaviour they are trying to produce and give developers a reference for the rules sitting behind the pleasant interface.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a guarantee, a safety proof, or a substitute for evaluation. A spec can clarify intent. It cannot ensure that every model version, product surface, or edge case will obey the text as faithfully as legal and marketing might prefer.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means reading the provider's stated priorities, understanding how instruction hierarchy and refusals are supposed to work, and designing prompts, product expectations, and escalation paths around the fact that the model has house rules you did not personally write.
entries.example: "The product team called it a bug. The model spec called it a refusal. The user called it a waste of time."
askNext: Which behaviours in this product come from our prompt stack, and which come from the provider's published rules?
How closely does the live model match the current spec in the cases we actually care about?
What happens operationally when the provider updates the spec before we update our expectations?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: The provider's declared behavioural contract, minus the comforting fantasy that declarations enforce themselves.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A document you should read before accusing the model of breaking rules that were never yours to set.

entries.body: Model specs matter because the era of purely generic models is over. The model arrives with prior instructions, prior politics, and prior notions of what counts as responsible behaviour.

entries.title: Model supply chain
entries.devilDefinition: Everything your model depends on before it answers—datasets, base checkpoints, adapters, tools, and the intern who uploaded a weight file.
entries.plainDefinition: The model supply chain is the web of datasets, base models, fine-tunes, evaluation sets, tools, and distribution channels that produce and deliver a deployed model system.
entries.whyExists: Incidents and regulations made it obvious that models are assembled, not immaculately conceived. Supply chain is where risk accumulates.
entries.misuse: Reduced to a logo slide of vendors. Real chains include transitive data licences and unofficial mirrors.
entries.practicalMeaning: Inventory components, pin versions, verify artefacts, track licences, and require approval for new upstream sources.
entries.example: "The model supply chain included a base model, two adapters, and a dataset nobody could licence."
askNext: Can you list every artefact in the deployed system?
Which upstreams can change without your knowledge?
What verification exists at each hop?
entries.warningLabel: You inherit the weakest verified link.

entries.title: Model theft
entries.devilDefinition: Stealing a model by copying weights, distilling behaviour, or walking out with a checkpoint on a drive—IP crime with FLOPs.
entries.plainDefinition: Model theft is unauthorised acquisition of proprietary model weights or close behavioural clones through extraction attacks, insider exfiltration, or insecure distribution.
entries.whyExists: Models are valuable IP and dual-use artefacts. Theft enables competitors and adversaries to skip training costs.
entries.misuse: Applied only to cinematic weight heists. API extraction and distillation can also approximate proprietary behaviour.
entries.practicalMeaning: Control weight access, monitor anomalous query patterns, rate-limit and watermark where useful, and treat fine-tune outputs as sensitive.
entries.example: "Model theft concerns rose after the internal checkpoint appeared on a public mirror."
askNext: Who can download weights, and is that logged?
Do APIs show extraction-like traffic?
What is the incident response for leaked checkpoints?
entries.warningLabel: If weights are easy to copy, assume someone will try.

entries.title: Multimodal
entries.devilDefinition: Able to juggle text, images, audio, or video, which sounds far more coherent than the reality of several data types sharing one product team.
entries.plainDefinition: Multimodal refers to models or systems that can process, generate, or jointly reason over more than one data modality, such as text, image, audio, or video.
entries.whyExists: Human tasks rarely arrive as clean text alone. Multimodal systems exist because many useful applications involve documents, screenshots, spoken language, visual context, or combinations of these.
entries.misuse: It gets waved around as a prestige adjective even when a product merely accepts an image upload and produces a caption. True multimodal capability is not just accepting multiple inputs. It is being able to use them meaningfully for the task at hand.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a model or stack that can ingest several types of data, map them into compatible internal representations, and produce outputs that reflect those combined signals. The hard part is often not modality support itself but maintaining reliability across them.
entries.example: "The feature was multimodal in the useful sense: it could read the chart, listen to the spoken explanation, and answer questions about the mismatch."
askNext: Which modalities can the system actually handle in production?
Does the model merely accept multiple formats, or does it use them jointly to improve the outcome?
How has performance been evaluated across the different input types?
entries.note: Multimodal can describe both input and output capabilities. A system that reads images and writes text is multimodal, even if it does not generate images in return.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system works across more than one type of data, with all the messy evaluation problems that implies.

entries.body: The adjective matters less than the task. The real question is whether the extra modality materially improves the outcome or simply decorates the feature list.

entries.title: Multimodal RAG
entries.devilDefinition: Retrieval that can fetch images, audio, or video clips so the model can misread more kinds of evidence at once.
entries.plainDefinition: Multimodal RAG retrieves and conditions generation on non-text or mixed modalities—images, audio, video, PDFs—using embeddings, captions, or specialised indexes alongside text retrieval.
entries.whyExists: Enterprise knowledge is not only markdown. Products needed retrieval that matches how evidence actually exists.
entries.misuse: Assuming a text RAG pipeline plus a vision model is enough. Chunking, alignment, OCR quality, and latency dominate outcomes.
entries.practicalMeaning: Separate indexes per modality when needed, invest in parsing, and evaluate with multimodal questions, not text-only benchmarks.
entries.example: "Multimodal RAG retrieved the right diagram and the model still described last year's version."
askNext: How are images and pages chunked and captioned?
What is end-to-end latency with large media?
How do you cite non-text evidence for users?
entries.warningLabel: More modalities multiply failure modes.

entries.title: Mustafa Suleyman
entries.devilDefinition: The fluent translator of machine intelligence into product language for people who would rather adopt a companion than study a model card.
entries.plainDefinition: Mustafa Suleyman is the CEO of Microsoft AI and a prominent founder-operator associated with productising advanced AI into mass-market interfaces, assistants, and companion-style experiences.
entries.whyExists: Because AI is not only a research contest. It is also a packaging contest, an interface contest, and a trust contest. Suleyman's name often marks the point where the conversation shifts from raw capability to how intelligence is turned into habit.
entries.misuse: By treating "companion" language as either obviously profound or obviously absurd. It is better understood as a serious product thesis about persistence, personalisation, convenience, and software that wants to sit between the user and everything else.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Mustafa Suleyman, they usually mean AI as a persistent product layer: more assistant, less query box; more relationship, less transaction.
entries.example: "The roadmap had gone full Mustafa: less tool, more companion, with enough memory and polish to make dependence sound like convenience."
askNext: Is this about interface design, product strategy, or model capability?
Does the vision depend on trust, habit, or lock-in?
Are we building a workflow tool, a companion, or a polite way to blur the line?

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A sign that the discussion is moving from model performance to user relationship design.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for the thesis that AI should feel less like software you visit and more like software that stays with you.

entries.body: It is easier to mock the companion idea than to notice how many major products are inching toward it anyway.
