entries.title: National compute
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: National compute names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Sovereign AI needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once national compute entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: Nemotron 3
entries.devilDefinition: NVIDIA's model family for anyone who suspected the GPU company might also enjoy owning more of what runs on the GPUs.
entries.plainDefinition: Nemotron 3 is NVIDIA's open model family with Nano, Super, and Ultra variants positioned for agentic, reasoning, coding, and conversational workloads.
entries.whyExists: It exists because model vendors increasingly want families that span different cost and performance points, especially for agent workloads where serving efficiency and deployment control matter as much as leaderboard theatre.
entries.misuse: It gets flattened into "NVIDIA has a model" as though all variants, licences, serving choices, and agent benchmarks collapse into one tidy procurement noun.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means evaluating an NVIDIA-backed open model family for agentic workloads, often alongside questions about hardware fit, NIM deployment, open-weight hosting, and price-performance.
entries.example: "Nemotron 3 entered the shortlist because the team wanted an open model that made sense on the hardware strategy already approved by finance."
askNext: Which Nemotron 3 variant are we testing: Nano, Super, or Ultra?
Are we choosing it for agent capability, serving economics, openness, or NVIDIA stack alignment?
How does it perform on our own workflows rather than the family brochure?

translations.label: infrastructure meaning
translations.text: A model family designed to make NVIDIA's agent story less dependent on other people's models.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A family of model-size and capability trade-offs wrapped in the NVIDIA deployment universe.

entries.body: The more agent workloads become real workloads, the more model families start sounding like infrastructure portfolios.

entries.title: Neocloud
entries.devilDefinition: A cloud provider whose most disruptive feature is often discovering that scarce Nvidia hardware can be resold with excellent posture.
entries.plainDefinition: "Neocloud" is an informal label for newer cloud providers focused on AI infrastructure, especially GPU capacity for training and inference, rather than broad general-purpose enterprise cloud offerings.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because demand for accelerators outran easy supply, while many teams wanted alternatives to the hyperscalers on price, access, support, geography, or simple willingness to answer the phone.
entries.misuse: It is misused whenever specialised capacity is treated as a permanent moat. Owning or brokering GPUs can be valuable. It does not automatically solve networking, reliability, data governance, procurement, or the small inconvenience of debt.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a provider that can get you machines faster, sometimes cheaper, sometimes with better human support, in exchange for narrower platform breadth and a closer relationship with the physics of hardware finance.
entries.example: "The neocloud pitch was compelling right up to the moment finance noticed the business model involved both GPUs and optimism."
askNext: Are we choosing this provider for price, access, support, location, or all four?
What happens to reliability, networking, and compliance once we leave the big clouds?
Is this a real platform advantage or an opportunistic position in a supply shortage?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A cloud that may treat AI workloads as its first-class citizen instead of an unusually demanding tenant.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A specialist compute provider offering real advantages in some cases and a very concentrated exposure to hardware economics in others.

entries.note: The term describes a market position more than a technical architecture. Some neoclouds are serious platforms. Some are very earnest forwarding businesses with GPUs.
entries.body: The honest version of the pitch is simple: the big clouds were busy, so a new class of landlord arrived.

entries.title: Network policy
entries.devilDefinition: The list of places an agent is allowed to call, written after it already called somewhere regrettable.
entries.plainDefinition: Network policy for agents and model integrations defines allowed and denied destinations, protocols, and egress rules so tools cannot reach arbitrary hosts.
entries.whyExists: Tool-using agents with open internet access are data exfiltration and lateral-movement machines. Policy is the seatbelt.
entries.misuse: Set to 'allow all' in dev and forgotten in prod, or so strict that the agent cannot do its job and humans disable it.
entries.practicalMeaning: Default deny, allowlist required domains, separate policies per environment, and log every egress attempt including denials.
entries.example: "Network policy blocked the agent's surprise dependency download and saved the week."
askNext: Is egress default-deny with an allowlist?
Who can change the allowlist, and is it audited?
Do denials alert someone?
entries.warningLabel: Open egress is an implied trust decision.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Default deny, allowlist required domains, separate policies per environment, and log every egress attempt including denials.

entries.body: Network policy is geography for packets. Agents need maps with fences.

entries.title: Non-human identity
entries.devilDefinition: Every credential that is not a person, now enjoying the sort of population growth that usually alarms city planners.
entries.plainDefinition: Non-human identity is an identity used by a machine, service, script, workload, bot, or agent rather than a human user.
entries.whyExists: Modern systems are full of actors that need credentials but do not have a body, a payslip, or an HR exit interview. AI agents make the category more visible because they combine machine credentials with goal-directed behaviour and tool use.
entries.misuse: It is misused when teams treat all non-human identities as boring plumbing. A deployment key, a data pipeline, and an agent that can negotiate refunds may all be non-human, but they do not carry the same behavioural risk.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means inventorying service accounts, workload identities, agent principals, secrets, certificates, tokens, owners, permissions, rotation schedules, and the awkward number of credentials no one remembers creating.
entries.example: "The non-human identity inventory was reassuring until it revealed more active principals than employees and slightly better job security."
askNext: Which non-human identities exist, who owns them, and what can they access?
Are agent identities governed differently from ordinary service accounts?
How quickly can credentials be rotated, narrowed, or killed?
entries.warningLabel: The identity may be non-human. The breach report will not be.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Credentials and principals for machine actors that still need ownership and limits.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The access-control estate that grew while everyone was busy onboarding humans.

entries.body: No diagram is attached because the useful work is inventory and governance. A diagram of another loop would only make the credential sprawl look intentional.

entries.title: NotebookLM
entries.devilDefinition: The unusually sensible AI product that became popular by admitting you should probably start with your own documents.
entries.plainDefinition: NotebookLM is Google's source-grounded research and note-taking product for uploading or linking material, asking questions about it, generating summaries, and producing derivative outputs such as briefs or audio overviews.
entries.whyExists: Most knowledge work does not need an omniscient machine performing personality. It needs help digesting a bounded set of files, links, transcripts, slides, or notes without wandering off into generic internet mush.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for a citation guarantee or a truth engine. Grounding in supplied sources materially helps, but the system can still overstate, misconnect, or present generated synthesis with more poise than certainty.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice NotebookLM means a polished retrieval-and-synthesis interface for source packs: useful for study guides, research briefings, interview prep, internal manuals, and turning document piles into something a human can survive.
entries.example: "NotebookLM did not make the report magically correct, but it did make the document swamp considerably less vindictive."
askNext: Which sources are actually in the notebook, and which claims come from outside them?
Is the value in source-grounded synthesis, or are we quietly expecting general web research as well?
Would a document search tool be enough, or do we genuinely need generated structure on top?

translations.label: student meaning
translations.text: A way to turn a terrifying reading list into something that answers back.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A source-grounded synthesis tool that is most useful when the document set is deliberate rather than merely large.

entries.note: NotebookLM is strongest when the source set is bounded and relevant, not when it is treated as a landfill with a search box.
entries.body: Its success says something unflattering about the broader category: people trusted the AI product more once it agreed to stay inside the reading packet.

entries.title: NPU
entries.devilDefinition: The dedicated silicon every device launch now invokes to prove the future has arrived locally, efficiently, and preferably in this quarter's hardware cycle.
entries.plainDefinition: An NPU is a specialised processor designed to accelerate machine-learning workloads, especially on-device inference tasks such as speech, vision, transcription, or smaller language-model features.
entries.whyExists: Because CPUs and GPUs are not always the best fit for constant local inference, and device makers want better performance per watt for AI features that cannot afford cloud latency or cloud disclosure.
entries.misuse: By treating the presence of an NPU as evidence that meaningful AI features exist, or by presenting the acronym as a product benefit without saying what models fit, what latency improves, or what the software stack can actually use.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice an NPU determines which features can run on-device, how responsive and private they can be, and whether "AI PC" or "AI phone" means a new capability or merely a fresh sticker on the box.
entries.example: "The keynote celebrated the NPU at length, which was useful because the list of software features still fit comfortably on one slide."
askNext: Which models or tasks actually run on the NPU today?
What latency, battery, or privacy gains are measurable rather than implied?
Is the developer stack mature enough to use the hardware well?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful accelerator when the models fit and the software has caught up, plus a generous helping of launch-event theatre.

entries.body: NPUs are real infrastructure, not pure marketing, but the marketing has arrived unusually early. Hardware now promises local intelligence faster than most software teams can ship reasons to notice.

entries.title: NVIDIA
entries.devilDefinition: A chip company that became the closest thing the AI boom has to a central bank, except louder and with better leather jackets.
entries.plainDefinition: NVIDIA is a computing platform company best known in AI for GPUs, accelerated computing systems, and the broader hardware-software stack used to train and serve many modern AI models.
entries.whyExists: Because AI keeps pretending to be abstract until someone has to pay for the compute. NVIDIA matters because the current boom runs on its chips, systems, software ecosystem, and the procurement gravity that follows them.
entries.misuse: By using NVIDIA as shorthand for AI itself. That exaggerates the company and erases the rest of the stack, but it does capture where a great deal of pricing power and strategic leverage currently sits.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means GPUs, DGX-class systems, CUDA-shaped ecosystems, inference clusters, and the hardware economics under both frontier training and production deployment.
entries.example: "The board wanted an AI strategy. The operations team, less poetically, wanted to know how much of it would end up being tribute to NVIDIA."
askNext: Is this a chip discussion, a systems discussion, or a software-ecosystem discussion?
Are we bottlenecked by compute, cost, availability, or habit?
Could another stack do the job, or are we already too CUDA-shaped to be honest about the answer?

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: The company whose margins reveal how physical the AI boom really is.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The dominant compute platform beneath a large share of modern AI training and inference.

entries.note: NVIDIA's own framing increasingly presents the company as the engine of AI and the builder of AI factories. The important practical point is that it is no longer just a component supplier; it is a stack with enormous ecosystem gravity.
entries.body: When one vendor becomes both a product choice and an environmental condition, normal procurement language starts sounding quaint.

entries.title: NVIDIA NIM
entries.devilDefinition: NVIDIA's reminder that once everyone wants models in production, the pick-and-shovel business starts selling neatly boxed shovels.
entries.plainDefinition: NVIDIA NIM is a set of microservices and API surfaces for deploying, serving, and trying foundation models across NVIDIA's AI stack, including hosted NIM APIs exposed through build.nvidia.com.
entries.whyExists: It exists because serving models is not just a model choice. Teams also need runtimes, endpoints, containers, security updates, deployment patterns, and enough operational packaging to make inference feel less handmade.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if the presence of an official endpoint solves model selection, quality evaluation, latency budgets, or vendor lock-in. A production-grade serving wrapper still serves whatever decision you made inside it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means easier access to NVIDIA-optimised model serving, hosted trials, and deployment recipes for teams already thinking in GPUs, data centres, clouds, and enterprise support contracts.
entries.example: "They tried the model through NVIDIA NIM first, which was sensible; they then called the working API call a platform strategy, which was less so."
askNext: Are we using NIM as a hosted API, a deployable microservice, or a reference runtime?
Which model, hardware target, latency budget, and data-handling requirements matter here?
Does NIM simplify deployment enough to justify the surrounding NVIDIA dependency?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A packaged way to try and serve models through NVIDIA's deployment machinery.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Inference plumbing with a logo people already associate with the expensive part.

entries.body: The interesting part is not that NVIDIA has APIs. The interesting part is how neatly the model era keeps turning back into an infrastructure era.

entries.title: Ollama
entries.devilDefinition: The software that persuaded a great many people to download a model locally and call the resulting fan noise a privacy strategy.
entries.plainDefinition: Ollama is a tool and runtime for downloading, serving, and interacting with local AI models through a straightforward CLI and API on a developer machine or private system.
entries.whyExists: Running local models used to feel more like a weekend project than a developer workflow. Ollama exists because many teams wanted a simple way to try local inference, private prototypes, and offline model use without assembling the whole stack by hand.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for a complete production strategy or proof that local inference is automatically secure, cheap, or enterprise-ready. Pulling a model locally is helpful. It does not abolish memory limits, observability, scaling, or the possibility that the chosen model is merely mediocre in private.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means convenient local model packaging for experiments, prototypes, internal tools, and developer workflows. It is especially useful for reducing friction around self-hosted testing, less useful as a substitute for a real serving platform once traffic, governance, or uptime begin to matter.
entries.example: "They rolled the demo on Ollama because the requirement was a private proof of concept, not a treatise on distributed inference."
askNext: Which model and quantisation are we actually running?
Is local inference solving privacy, latency, cost, or merely setup friction?
Do we need a developer runtime, or do we really need a production serving stack?
entries.warningLabel: Excellent developer ergonomics are not the same thing as fleet management.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A much friendlier way to run local models than manually wrestling the stack into existence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A convenient local runtime that makes self-hosted model experiments practical before production complexity catches up.

entries.note: The term matters because it lowered the activation energy for local AI so dramatically that many more people could form opinions before involving procurement.
entries.body: Tools like this are why local AI stopped being a niche boast and became normal developer behaviour.

entries.title: On-behalf-of
entries.devilDefinition: Acting for a user with delegated authority—the difference between helpful automation and borrowed identity.
entries.plainDefinition: On-behalf-of describes an agent or service performing actions under delegated authority for a principal user, ideally with scoped tokens and clear audit attribution rather than shared passwords.
entries.whyExists: Agents need to email, file, and purchase for people. Delegation patterns from identity systems became central to agent safety.
entries.misuse: Implemented as 'login as the user' with full privileges. That is impersonation, not delegation.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use OAuth-style delegation, narrow scopes, short lifetimes, user-visible consents, and logs that show agent and principal separately.
entries.example: "On-behalf-of access let the agent book travel without holding the CEO password in a prompt."
askNext: What scopes were granted, and can the user revoke them?
Do audits show agent versus human actions?
Is step-up auth required for sensitive acts?
entries.warningLabel: Delegation without scope is a blank cheque.

entries.title: On-device inference
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: On-device inference names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around NPU need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said on-device inference as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."

entries.title: One-Person Unicorn
entries.devilDefinition: The recurring fantasy that one founder, several agents, and a cloud invoice can replace an organisation and still count as a company instead of an episode.
entries.plainDefinition: The "one-person unicorn" is the idea that AI tools and automation could let a single founder build and run a company worth a billion dollars with little or no traditional staff.
entries.whyExists: It exists because AI has genuinely reduced the cost of coding, design, support, research, and back-office work enough for solo founders to reach farther than they could a few years ago. That makes the old dream of extreme leverage newly discussable and newly distortive.
entries.misuse: The term gets used as if headcount were simply a regrettable inefficiency. It understates the boring things companies are still made of: trust, sales, operations, compliance, customer support, and the general inconvenience of reality needing more than one pair of eyes.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means smaller teams doing more, solo founders getting to revenue faster, and investors testing how much organisational fragility they can rename as leverage.
entries.example: "The one-person unicorn thesis sounds marvellous until the founder discovers that support, tax, contracts, and incident response still decline to become vibes."
askNext: Which parts of the company are actually automated, and which parts are being silently done by contractors, software, or wishful thinking?
Does the business model scale without a team, or only the demo?
What breaks first when a supposedly solo company meets regulation, churn, or enterprise procurement?
entries.warningLabel: Usually a prediction, occasionally a business.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: A company with software margins, heroic narrative density, and almost no payroll.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A real shift toward smaller teams and solo builders, wrapped in a much larger fantasy about how many people organisations require.

entries.note: The important point is not whether the perfect solo billion-dollar company appears. The important point is that a lot of people now behave as if it is imminent.
entries.body: As with many AI-era myths, the term reveals something true by exaggerating it until it becomes unusable.

entries.title: Open Weight
entries.devilDefinition: The compromise term for when a company wants the glow of openness without the administrative burden of actually being open source.
entries.plainDefinition: An open-weight model is one whose trained parameters are released for others to download and run, even if the training code, data, licences, or usage rights remain restricted.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because there is a meaningful difference between sharing a model's weights and sharing the full ingredients behind it. The industry needed language for that middle ground, if only to avoid calling everything open source with a straight face.
entries.misuse: It is misused whenever "open weight" is presented as equivalent to full transparency, unrestricted reuse, or community governance. A downloadable checkpoint can still arrive with significant legal, technical, and commercial strings attached.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means you can run or fine-tune the model yourself, but you may not know precisely how it was trained, what data shaped it, or what terms govern your most lucrative use case.
entries.example: "The vendor called it open weight, which was accurate enough if you ignored the licence, the data secrecy, and the very expensive preferred hosting arrangement."
askNext: What exactly has been released besides the checkpoint?
What restrictions apply to commercial use, redistribution, or modification?
Can the model be audited meaningfully, or only downloaded?
entries.warningLabel: Not the same thing as open source.

translations.label: press-release meaning
translations.text: We are generous enough to share the artefact, though not necessarily the power.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model checkpoint is available, while other parts of the stack or rights remain controlled.

entries.body: This is one of the more honest euphemisms in AI. At least it admits there is a difference between access and openness.

entries.title: Open-weight releases
entries.devilDefinition: Open-weight releases is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Open-weight releases is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around DeepSeek often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Open-weight releases came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: OpenAI
entries.devilDefinition: A company name frequently used as shorthand for an app, a model family, an API, a policy debate, and sometimes the entire industry when people cannot be bothered to separate nouns.
entries.plainDefinition: OpenAI is a company. It builds and sells AI products and services, including model APIs and end-user products such as ChatGPT. The company name is distinct from the names of its individual models and products.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because company, product, and model are constantly collapsed into one another. If you do not separate them, conversations about performance, pricing, safety, procurement, and market position become foggy very quickly.
entries.misuse: People say "OpenAI said this" when they mean ChatGPT produced a reply, or "we use OpenAI" when they actually use a specific API model through a specific product flow. The confusion sounds minor until accuracy, vendor risk, or budgeting starts mattering.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a commercial vendor relationship. You are choosing a supplier, a set of models, platform terms, product constraints, and an evolving roadmap. The most important question is usually not whether OpenAI exists, but which exact OpenAI product or model you mean.
entries.example: "The board memo said the company was 'using OpenAI', which turned out to mean one customer-service workflow calling a single model through the API with very strict prompt scaffolding."
askNext: Do you mean the company, the API platform, ChatGPT, or a specific model?
What dependency does the product have on this vendor: model quality, tooling, pricing, or distribution?
What is the fallback plan if the model, terms, or costs change?
entries.warningLabel: Company name. Not a synonym for every OpenAI model or product.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A supplier whose capabilities can become product strategy if you are not careful.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A commercial platform choice, not a category of intelligence.

entries.body: The clean sentence is: OpenAI is a company; ChatGPT is a product; specific GPT models are models. If a conversation cannot hold those three levels apart, it is not ready for a serious decision.

entries.title: OpenClaw
entries.devilDefinition: An AI assistant for people who looked at a chatbot and thought, "Nice, but can it also touch my files, my browser, my messages, and my calendar?"
entries.plainDefinition: OpenClaw is a self-hosted personal AI assistant and gateway platform designed to do things, not just chat. Its own docs describe it as an assistant you run on your own devices, replying through surfaces such as Telegram, WhatsApp, Discord, iMessage, Slack, and WebChat while orchestrating tools, memory, and workflows through an always-on gateway.
entries.whyExists: Because the market got bored of systems that only generate text and started demanding systems that can actually execute tasks.
entries.misuse: By treating any autonomous workflow as magic, or by forgetting that giving an AI broad permissions turns "helpful assistant" into "possible incident". The interesting part is not that it can act. The interesting part is how narrowly, visibly, and safely it acts.
entries.practicalMeaning: A software layer that connects models to tools, interfaces, and actions so that the system can perform useful work across apps and services. In practice it is closer to an orchestration-and-gateway stack than a free-floating digital butler.
entries.example: "OpenClaw is what people reach for when they want the AI to stop explaining the inbox and start clearing it."
askNext: What tools does it have access to?
What permissions does it require?
Is it mostly chat, mostly automation, or truly operational?
Where does the human stay in the loop?
entries.warningLabel: A broad-permission assistant is only charming right up until it inherits enough access to become a systems problem.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A self-hosted orchestration layer that lets an assistant live inside chat surfaces and reach out into tools, files, schedules, and other systems.

entries.note: OpenClaw's docs explicitly frame it as open source, hackable, and self-hostable. They also spend a lot of time warning users to start conservatively with permissions, which is a good sign that the authors have met software before.
entries.body: Systems like OpenClaw matter because they make the agent discussion less abstract. Once the assistant lives in Telegram, runs on your own box, and can touch real tools, the architecture stops being philosophical and becomes operational.

entries.title: OpenCode
entries.devilDefinition: A coding agent for people who want the assistant close to the repo, not trapped inside a subscription-shaped cathedral.
entries.plainDefinition: OpenCode is an open-source AI coding agent for terminal, IDE, and desktop workflows, designed to work with multiple model providers and help developers inspect, edit, and operate on codebases.
entries.whyExists: It exists because coding agents moved from autocomplete into delegated repo work, and developers wanted tools that were model-flexible, scriptable, local-friendly, and less tied to a single vendor's product surface.
entries.misuse: It gets used as proof that openness alone creates reliability. An open agent can still edit the wrong file, misunderstand the task, or spend tokens with the confidence of any other agent wearing better governance.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a repo-facing coding agent that can run near a developer's existing workflow, connect to different model providers, and trade polished platform control for flexibility and inspectability.
entries.example: "OpenCode helped the team move faster, then politely reminded them that open source does not abolish code review."
askNext: Which model provider, permissions, and repo tools does OpenCode have access to?
Does the local workflow improve reviewability, or simply make mistakes arrive faster?
What tasks are safe to delegate, and which still need a human at the keyboard?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A coding agent that tries to live where the work already happens.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A flexible agent surface whose usefulness still depends on scope, tests, and review discipline.

entries.body: The mature argument for OpenCode is not romance about openness. It is the quieter case that developer tooling should remain inspectable when it starts changing the repository.

OpenCode OpenCode AI open source coding agent terminal coding agent Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Model usage coding agents developer-tools open-source A coding agent for people who want the assistant close to the repo, not trapped inside a subscription-shaped cathedral.
 OpenCode is an open-source AI coding agent for terminal, IDE, and desktop workflows, designed to work with multiple model providers and help developers inspect, edit, and operate on codebases.
 It exists because coding agents moved from autocomplete into delegated repo work, and developers wanted tools that were model-flexible, scriptable, local-friendly, and less tied to a single vendor's product surface.
 It gets used as proof that openness alone creates reliability. An open agent can still edit the wrong file, misunderstand the task, or spend tokens with the confidence of any other agent wearing better governance.
 In practice it usually means a repo-facing coding agent that can run near a developer's existing workflow, connect to different model providers, and trade polished platform control for flexibility and inspectability.
 "OpenCode helped the team move faster, then politely reminded them that open source does not abolish code review."
 Which model provider, permissions, and repo tools does OpenCode have access to? Does the local workflow improve reviewability, or simply make mistakes arrive faster? What tasks are safe to delegate, and which still need a human at the keyboard? Open-source coding agent Terminal agent Agent Worktree  Models.dev

entries.title: OpenRouter
entries.devilDefinition: A toll road for developers who want access to every model and one more month before choosing a real dependency.
entries.plainDefinition: OpenRouter is a gateway service that exposes many AI models behind a unified API, letting developers compare providers, route traffic, and manage access through one integration layer.
entries.whyExists: Because the model market is fragmented, provider APIs differ, and teams prefer one contract when they are still shopping for capability, price, latency, or fallback options.
entries.misuse: By implying that abstraction removes vendor risk rather than merely relocating it. A router can hide some differences. It cannot abolish model quirks, pricing shocks, policy changes, or provider outages.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a gateway for multi-model experimentation, centralised billing, fallback logic, and the comforting illusion that indecision has become architecture.
entries.example: "They integrated OpenRouter so product could compare five models in a week and postpone commitment with unusual professionalism."
askNext: Which provider-specific behaviours still leak through the abstraction?
Is the router saving real engineering time or merely extending evaluation limbo?
What happens to cost control, observability, and data handling once another layer sits in the middle?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An aggregation layer that makes model switching easier until you care about the details that never quite aggregate.

entries.note: Services like this matter because the provider layer is now messy enough to justify a broker. They also matter because brokers become part of the dependency chain they promise to simplify.
entries.body: Routers are most attractive during the phase when the market is still moving and everyone wants optionality without philosophical commitment.

entries.title: Operational risk
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Operational risk is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Human in the Loop are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The operational risk discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Operational risk is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Operational risk belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Orchestration
entries.devilDefinition: The noble art of arranging prompts, tools, retries, rules, and side effects so that a demo looks like intelligence rather than plumbing.
entries.plainDefinition: Orchestration is the software layer that coordinates model calls, tool use, retrieval, validation, branching logic, retries, and state across a workflow. It determines how the surrounding system manages the model, not what the model intrinsically knows.
entries.whyExists: Modern AI products are rarely a single model call. They are systems. Orchestration exists because useful behaviour often depends on ordering steps, handling failures, and deciding when to ask a model versus when to run conventional software.
entries.misuse: The term gets abused when simple glue code is dressed up as autonomous reasoning. Sometimes orchestration is just sensible application logic wearing a tuxedo because "if statements and APIs" sounded too ordinary for the pitch deck.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a chain of decisions around prompts, routing, tool permissions, schema checks, retrieval steps, model selection, human approvals, and error handling. Good orchestration makes systems reliable. Bad orchestration makes them busy.
entries.example: "The impressive part was not the model; it was the orchestration layer that knew when to search the docs, when to call billing, and when to stop pretending confidence."
askNext: What steps are being orchestrated, and which of them actually require a model?
Where does state live between steps?
What happens when a tool call fails, returns nonsense, or arrives too late?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The thing that turns one model call into something sellable.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A coordination layer full of branching logic, safeguards, and operational compromises.

entries.body: When people say a system is "agentic", there is often an orchestration story underneath doing most of the useful work. That is not a disappointment. It is how software works.

entries.title: Orchestration debt
entries.devilDefinition: The accumulated complexity produced by adding one more step, one more model, one more fallback, and one more apology until nobody can explain the workflow without a guided tour.
entries.plainDefinition: Orchestration debt is the maintenance burden created when an AI workflow accumulates too many steps, branches, models, tools, retries, and special cases for the team to reason about confidently.
entries.whyExists: Useful systems rarely stay simple. Teams add retrieval, validators, fallbacks, routing rules, approvals, tool calls, and recovery logic incrementally. The term exists because those sensible additions can eventually produce a coordination layer more fragile and opaque than the problem it was meant to solve.
entries.misuse: It should not be used to insult every non-trivial workflow. Some orchestration is necessary. It becomes debt when the layering makes failures harder to predict, ownership harder to assign, and changes harder to ship without accidentally summoning three side effects and an incident review.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means duplicated logic, hidden dependencies, branching nobody wants to touch, unclear state transitions, and an architecture whose strongest testing strategy is hoping the person who built it is still employed.
entries.example: "The workflow was no longer sophisticated. It had simply acquired enough orchestration debt to make every bug look original."
askNext: Which parts of this orchestration still earn their keep, and which survive by historical accident?
Can a newcomer explain the main failure paths without interviewing the original builder?
What change would be hardest to make safely because the coordination layer has become too tangled?

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: The coordination layer now costs more to understand and change than the team expected.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Accumulated workflow complexity that keeps producing fresh ways to fail.

entries.body: Orchestration debt is what happens when a clever workflow wins too many arguments against simplification. The system still works, but each new improvement must now travel through customs.

entries.title: Orchestrator agent
entries.devilDefinition: The manager agent that assigns work to other agents and then discovers management is also a failure mode.
entries.plainDefinition: An orchestrator agent coordinates multi-step or multi-agent workflows: decomposing goals, delegating subtasks, integrating results, and handling retries or escalations.
entries.whyExists: Complex tasks exceed single-tool loops. Systems needed a control agent that owns the plan while specialists execute.
entries.misuse: Stacked until every problem becomes a committee. Orchestrators can amplify errors, duplicate work, and hide accountability.
entries.practicalMeaning: Keep one clear owner of state and permissions. Limit fan-out, require structured handoffs, and measure end-to-end success rather than agent count.
entries.example: "The orchestrator agent scheduled four specialists to reinvent the same function."
askNext: Who holds durable state and final authority?
How are conflicts between specialist agents resolved?
What is the human escalation path?
entries.warningLabel: More managers do not guarantee more progress.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: An orchestrator agent coordinates multi-step or multi-agent workflows: decomposing goals, delegating subtasks, integrating results, and handling retries or escalations.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Keep one clear owner of state and permissions.

entries.body: Orchestrators are useful when work is truly multi-part. They are expensive when it is not.

Orchestrator agent orchestrator planner agent Agents and workflows agents orchestration workflows multi-agent The manager agent that assigns work to other agents and then discovers management is also a failure mode.
 An orchestrator agent coordinates multi-step or multi-agent workflows: decomposing goals, delegating subtasks, integrating results, and handling retries or escalations.
 Complex tasks exceed single-tool loops. Systems needed a control agent that owns the plan while specialists execute.
 Stacked until every problem becomes a committee. Orchestrators can amplify errors, duplicate work, and hide accountability.
 Keep one clear owner of state and permissions. Limit fan-out, require structured handoffs, and measure end-to-end success rather than agent count.
 "The orchestrator agent scheduled four specialists to reinvent the same function."

entries.title: Outcome reward model
entries.devilDefinition: A judge that only scores the ending, generously ignoring every crime committed on the way there.
entries.plainDefinition: An outcome reward model (ORM) scores final answers or completed trajectories without evaluating intermediate reasoning steps, providing a sparse training or ranking signal.
entries.whyExists: Labelling full process traces is expensive. Outcome labels are cheaper and still useful for preference training, best-of-n, and RL.
entries.misuse: Assumed to teach good reasoning. Models can learn shortcuts, lucky guesses, and answer patterns that score well without robust process.
entries.practicalMeaning: Pair ORMs with process checks on hard tasks, diversify seeds, and watch for reward hacking that produces high-scoring wrong methods.
entries.example: "The outcome reward model loved the final number; the derivation had borrowed it from the question."
askNext: What is labelled as success, and by whom?
Do you inspect trajectories that score high for the wrong reasons?
When is process supervision required instead?
entries.warningLabel: Sparse rewards teach whatever works, including cheats.

entries.title: Outcome supervision
entries.devilDefinition: Only marking the final answer right or wrong, which is cheap, common, and how lucky guesses get tenure.
entries.plainDefinition: Outcome supervision provides labels or rewards solely on final results—correct answer, successful trajectory end state—without grading intermediate steps.
entries.whyExists: Final labels are easier to collect at scale than full process traces. Much of modern post-training still depends on them.
entries.misuse: Assumed sufficient for teaching robust reasoning. Models can hack outcomes with shallow strategies.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use outcome supervision where checkers exist; add process supervision or verifiers for multi-step domains where shortcuts abound.
entries.example: "Outcome supervision rewarded the right number arrived at by memorised leakage."
askNext: Is the outcome checker gameable?
Do you inspect winning trajectories?
When is process signal worth the labelling cost?
entries.warningLabel: Right answers can travel on wrong roads.

entries.title: Output Laundering
entries.devilDefinition: The corporate ritual of running your opinion through a machine so it returns wearing objectivity.
entries.plainDefinition: The act of using AI output to make a human preference, hunch, or decision appear more neutral, data-driven, or authoritative than it really is.
entries.whyExists: Because organisations enjoy the prestige of algorithmic distance. An answer feels less political, less personal, and less blameworthy once a system is seen to have produced it.
entries.misuse: By pretending the model independently discovered the conclusion when the framing, source selection, or prompt design all but guaranteed it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a machine-generated report, ranking, or recommendation being used to formalise a decision management already wanted to make.
entries.example: "They did not want the model's judgement. They wanted output laundering: a printable reason to do what they had already decided."
askNext: Who framed the problem, selected the sources, and wrote the prompt?
Would the conclusion have surprised anyone involved?
Is the system informing the decision or deodorising it?

translations.label: office meaning
translations.text: A way to sound impersonal while preserving all the old preferences.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Human judgement passed through a machine to acquire the costume of neutrality.

entries.body: The machine did not remove politics. It merely gave them formatting.

entries.title: PagedAttention
entries.devilDefinition: Virtual memory for KV cache so inference servers can pack more sequences without treating GPU RAM like a single luxury hotel room.
entries.plainDefinition: PagedAttention is a KV-cache management technique (popularised by vLLM) that stores attention key/value tensors in non-contiguous pages, improving memory utilisation for batched LLM serving.
entries.whyExists: Request lengths vary; contiguous preallocation wastes memory. Paging lets servers admit more concurrent sequences.
entries.misuse: Cited as automatic infinite context. It improves packing efficiency; it does not remove model context limits.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use serving engines that implement it well, monitor fragmentation and preemption, and size pages for your length distribution.
entries.example: "PagedAttention raised concurrency until the team noticed they had been paying for empty KV slots."
askNext: What engine implements your paging?
How full is KV memory under peak load?
Do long requests starve short interactive ones?
entries.warningLabel: Better packing still hits a hard memory ceiling.

entries.title: Pages
entries.devilDefinition: Pages is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Pages is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Cloudflare and Perplexity often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Pages came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: Pairwise eval
entries.devilDefinition: Judging two answers by duel rather than absolute score, which often matches human taste and sometimes matches judge bias.
entries.plainDefinition: Pairwise eval compares two model outputs head-to-head under the same prompt, producing a preference rather than an absolute grade, commonly used with human or model judges.
entries.whyExists: Absolute scoring is hard and noisy. Humans are often better at choosing the better of two than assigning consistent points.
entries.misuse: Treated as bias-free. Position bias, verbosity bias, and judge model bias still skew results.
entries.practicalMeaning: Randomise order, control for length, use multiple judges, and report win rates with confidence intervals—not just a winner trophy.
entries.example: "Pairwise eval preferred the longer answer until they controlled for length."
askNext: Is order randomised?
Who or what judges the pair?
Do you stratify by task type?
entries.warningLabel: A duel winner can still be wrong for the user.

entries.title: Paper
entries.devilDefinition: A design canvas for people who looked at the wall between mockup and code and decided the wall should have fewer feelings.
entries.plainDefinition: Paper is a design platform built around a web-native canvas, where teams can design, share, and ship interface work with code, data, and agent workflows closer to the same surface.
entries.whyExists: It exists because visual design, prototype review, implementation, and AI-assisted iteration often happen in separate tools that translate badly into one another. A web-standard canvas tries to make the artefact more portable.
entries.misuse: It can be oversold as if an HTML/CSS-aware canvas eliminates product design judgement, engineering taste, or the awkward final mile where a pretty surface has to survive real application state.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a design space aimed at reducing handoff loss: designers, agents, code, and web output operate closer together, so a prototype has a better chance of becoming implementation material rather than a decorative screenshot.
entries.example: "They used Paper because the agent could reason over something closer to the web page they actually needed, not a museum specimen of a future interface."
askNext: Is Paper being used as the source of truth, a prototyping layer, or a bridge into code?
How much of the generated output survives real responsive states and product data?
Where does design review happen once agents can also edit the canvas?
entries.warningLabel: A web-native canvas can reduce handoff loss. It cannot make taste compile.

translations.label: designer meaning
translations.text: A canvas that tries to keep the mockup closer to the web thing it wants to become.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Less handoff theatre between visual design, agents, and frontend code, provided the team still reviews the result properly.

entries.body: Paper is interesting because it treats the design artefact less like a picture of software and more like software's early social life.

entries.title: Permission prompt
entries.devilDefinition: The small modal window where a large system briefly pretends the user has read the implications.
entries.plainDefinition: A permission prompt asks a user or operator to grant, deny, or scope access before a system performs an action or touches data, tools, files, accounts, or external services.
entries.whyExists: AI systems increasingly sit near sensitive capabilities: email, calendars, files, payments, code, customer records, and production tools. Permission prompts exist to slow down access and force an explicit decision at the boundary.
entries.misuse: They get overused until people click through them as a reflex, or under-specified until "allow" hides too much authority. A prompt that asks for broad access without explaining the concrete action is consent theatre with rounded corners.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means least-privilege design, clear action descriptions, revocable access, audit trails, sensible defaults, and enough friction that the agent cannot quietly turn convenience into a permissions estate.
entries.example: "The permission prompt said the agent wanted to help with email, which turned out to include reading the inbox, drafting replies, and developing opinions about procurement."
askNext: What exact action or data access is being requested?
Can the user grant a narrower permission or revoke it later?
Is the prompt rare and specific enough that approval still means something?

translations.label: user meaning
translations.text: A decision about what the system may touch, not a decoration before the real workflow starts.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The place where product convenience negotiates with future incident response.

entries.title: Perplexity
entries.devilDefinition: A search product for people who found ten blue links too democratic and preferred one confident stranger.
entries.plainDefinition: Perplexity is an AI-first search and answer product that combines web retrieval, synthesis, and cited responses in a chat-style interface.
entries.whyExists: Because conventional search makes users do the synthesis themselves, while chat products taught people to expect a direct answer even when the subject deserved more caution.
entries.misuse: By treating a cited answer as the same thing as a trustworthy answer. Links help. They do not absolve the reader of checking whether the system chose sensible sources or summarised them honestly.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means faster reconnaissance on a topic, fewer tabs, and a subtle transfer of editorial control from the user to the answer engine.
entries.example: "Perplexity saved him half an hour of clicking, then cost him ten minutes of verifying that the neat summary had not quietly simplified the important part away."
askNext: Which sources did it trust first, and why?
Is the answer good because it is accurate or because it is convenient?
Would you notice if it omitted the most inconvenient source?

translations.label: user meaning
translations.text: A quicker route from question to provisional answer, provided you remember it is still provisional.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A retrieval-heavy answer interface that compresses search and summarisation into one persuasive screen.

entries.body: The old problem with search was effort. The new problem is trusting whoever did the effort for you.

entries.title: Personal AI
entries.devilDefinition: Software that remembers your preferences and calendar until the privacy review remembers its job.
entries.plainDefinition: Personal AI refers to assistants and agents tuned to an individual user's data, preferences, and ongoing context—often spanning devices, accounts, and long-term memory features.
entries.whyExists: Generic chatbots feel shallow for daily life. Products chase continuity, personalisation, and proactive help across a person's digital footprint.
entries.misuse: Marketed as a friend or second self. Personalisation without clear data boundaries becomes surveillance with a cheerful UI.
entries.practicalMeaning: Demand export, deletion, scope controls, and transparent memory. Separate convenience features from high-stakes autonomy.
entries.example: "Personal AI booked the dinner and also learned which colleagues he avoids."
askNext: What personal data is stored, where, and for how long?
Can the user inspect and edit memory?
Which actions require confirmation?
entries.warningLabel: Intimacy is a product risk category.

entries.title: Personal Intelligence
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that your assistant becomes more useful once it is permitted a supervised rummage through your digital life.
entries.plainDefinition: Personal Intelligence is Google's feature for connecting apps like Gmail and Photos to Gemini or AI Mode so the system can answer with more personal context.
entries.whyExists: Generic assistants plateau quickly. Connected assistants can answer questions that only make sense against your own history, purchases, plans, and messages. The term exists because context is now a product feature, not merely a prompt length problem.
entries.misuse: It is framed as pure helpfulness when the real proposition is intimacy with guardrails. Opt-in controls matter, but the underlying bargain is still clear: give the system more of your life and hope the convenience is worth the access.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means personalised itineraries, recommendations, memory-like assistance across Google products, and a new set of design and trust questions about permissions, boundaries, and how much context people really want their assistant to hold.
entries.example: "Personal Intelligence made the travel plan far more relevant, which was lovely right up until everyone remembered what relevance had to be fed."
askNext: Which apps are connected, and what data is actually in play?
Is the value coming from better answers, or from making the assistant harder to leave?
What happens to trust once convenience starts depending on deeper access?
entries.warningLabel: Personalised help is still a trust exercise, not a law of nature.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: Context from your Google apps, used to make answers feel more tailored and less generic.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An opt-in personal data layer that makes the assistant more useful and the trust question less abstract.

entries.note: The key distinction is that this is not just remembered chat history. It is permissioned access to outside context that makes the model's answers feel more specifically about you.
entries.body: The industry's polite term is personalisation. The operational term is access. Personal Intelligence sits exactly where those two start sounding like the same decision.

entries.title: Personhood proof
entries.devilDefinition: The attempt to prove a user is a person without turning personhood into another subscription with surveillance attached.
entries.plainDefinition: Personhood proof is a mechanism for establishing that an account, user, or participant represents a real human rather than a bot, duplicate account, automated system, or synthetic identity.
entries.whyExists: Cheap automation and generative systems make spam, manipulation, fraud, and fake participation easier. Some systems need stronger assurance that a participant is human and unique.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a purely technical problem, when it is also a privacy, accessibility, civil rights, platform power, and dignity problem.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means trade-offs between friction, privacy, fraud resistance, exclusion risk, centralised identity, and how much users must reveal to prove they are not software.
entries.example: "The personhood proof stopped a bot farm and also locked out three real users, because the line between safety and paperwork remains thin."
askNext: What kind of human assurance is actually required?
What personal data or biometric signal is collected?
Who is excluded or harmed when the proof fails?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A human-assurance mechanism with privacy and exclusion risks attached.

entries.body: Proof of personhood starts as anti-bot infrastructure and quickly discovers politics.

entries.title: Physical AI
entries.devilDefinition: The rebranding of robotics for an era in which software valuations discovered they might need a body after all.
entries.plainDefinition: "Physical AI" is a broad industry label for applying modern AI systems to embodied machines such as robots, vehicles, and industrial equipment operating in real environments.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because language and image models do not move boxes, navigate warehouses, or survive factory floors by themselves. Embodied systems need perception, control, planning, simulation, and safety engineering tied to physical constraints.
entries.misuse: It is misused whenever a polished robot demo is treated as evidence that deployment is near. Hardware, safety, reliability, maintenance, and integration remain stubbornly unimpressed by keynote vocabulary.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means expensive sensor stacks, simulation pipelines, teleoperation fallbacks, long deployment cycles, and a great deal of engineering dedicated to the fact that the physical world does not accept almost right.
entries.example: "The physical AI roadmap sounded transformative until operations asked who would service the fleet after the first enthusiastic collision."
askNext: What part of the system is actually learned, and what part is conventional robotics?
How does the system fail in messy real environments rather than curated demos?
What are the safety, maintenance, and integration costs after the pilot ends?

translations.label: industry meaning
translations.text: AI that leaves the screen and must now negotiate with gravity, friction, and insurance.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A robotics push that borrows modern AI methods while inheriting all the old realities of hardware.

entries.body: Software likes to pretend the physical world is just another interface. The physical world has not confirmed this.

entries.title: Pieter Levels
entries.devilDefinition: The indie-hacker folk hero who makes ordinary organisations look less underpowered than overexcused.
entries.plainDefinition: Pieter Levels is an indie maker known for products such as Nomad List and Remote OK and, in contemporary AI culture, for representing the argument that one person with distribution, taste, and AI tooling can build more than many small teams would care to admit.
entries.whyExists: Because some names stop referring only to a person and start referring to a method. Invoking Levels usually means shipping in public, bootstrapping, keeping teams tiny, and treating software as something to release before the deck is finished apologising.
entries.misuse: By turning an unusual operator with an audience into a universal organisational theory. His style is instructive, but it does not erase survivorship bias, domain complexity, support work, or the awkward truth that most founders are not Pieter Levels with a timeline full of customers.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Levels usually means solo-founder leverage, fast iteration, blunt market testing, and a belief that AI tools amplify people who already know how to ship, write, and sell.
entries.example: "The moment someone invoked @levelsio, the roadmap became less a plan than an accusation that the company might simply have too many meetings."
askNext: Are they borrowing his execution style, or just his mythology?
Is the lesson about distribution, speed, team size, or public accountability?
Would this approach still hold once the product had enterprise customers, compliance, or a support queue?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: An uncomfortable reminder that speed, taste, and an audience can outcompete a small committee.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped symbol for solo-builder leverage in the age of AI-assisted product work.

entries.note: His own site uses the name Pieter Levels, even though people online often flatten it to Peter Levels. The distinction is minor; the mythology is not.
entries.body: The useful lesson is not that every company should be one person. It is that many companies became inefficient long before AI gave anyone a sharper mirror.

entries.title: PII redaction
entries.devilDefinition: Blanking out personal data before logs and models see it, imperfectly, which is still better than shipping the passport number in cleartext.
entries.plainDefinition: PII redaction is the process of detecting and removing or masking personally identifiable information in text and artefacts before storage, training, logging, or tool calls.
entries.whyExists: LLM pipelines copy data into prompts, traces, and fine-tunes. Redaction reduces privacy and compliance exposure.
entries.misuse: Treated as complete anonymisation. Detectors miss novel formats; context can re-identify; audio and images need their own handling.
entries.practicalMeaning: Combine detectors, allowlists/denylists, minimisation at collection, and access controls. Test with realistic PII forms from your domain.
entries.example: "PII redaction caught emails and missed employee IDs formatted as internal codes."
askNext: Which PII types matter in your jurisdiction and product?
Where does unredacted data still flow?
How do you evaluate detector recall?
entries.warningLabel: Redaction is risk reduction, not magic invisibility.

entries.title: Pilot Purgatory
entries.devilDefinition: The budgetary afterlife in which every firm proves its boldness by never quite deploying anything.
entries.plainDefinition: A recurring organisational state in which AI initiatives remain stuck in trials, proofs of concept, and limited rollouts without becoming trusted, scaled operations.
entries.whyExists: Because demos are easier than deployment, sponsors like novelty more than process change, and real adoption usually requires data access, security review, staff enablement, governance, and someone to own the outcome.
entries.misuse: By calling endless experimentation strategy. A pilot is a learning phase. It becomes purgatory when the organisation wants the optics of innovation without paying the price of commitment.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means many vendor meetings, a few polished demos, no durable workflow change, and a graveyard of licences nobody remembers approving.
entries.example: "The company had twelve AI pilots and one AI workflow in production. Pilot purgatory was not a risk; it was the operating model."
askNext: What concrete condition must be met for this pilot to graduate or die?
Who owns the workflow after the novelty budget runs out?
What operational change is the organisation avoiding by calling this an experiment?

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: Visible evidence that the company is doing something with AI.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A holding pattern where experimentation replaces commitment.

entries.body: The point of a pilot is to learn. The point of purgatory is apparently to keep learning forever.

entries.title: Plan and execute
entries.devilDefinition: Draft a plan, then do the plan, then discover the plan was a novel written by someone who had not seen the codebase.
entries.plainDefinition: Plan-and-execute is an agent pattern that first produces an explicit plan of steps, then carries out those steps with tools, optionally replanning when reality diverges.
entries.whyExists: Interleaved improvisation gets lost. Separating planning from acting can improve structure and reviewability.
entries.misuse: Treating the first plan as sacred. Rigid execution of a bad plan is organised failure.
entries.practicalMeaning: Allow replanning checkpoints, keep plans short, and verify steps against tests or acceptance criteria rather than vibes.
entries.example: "Plan-and-execute step four assumed a database that production retired last year."
askNext: When may the agent replan?
Who reviews high-risk plans before execution?
How are plan steps tied to verification?
entries.warningLabel: A plan is a hypothesis about the future of the repo.

entries.title: Plan Ceo Review
entries.devilDefinition: Plan Ceo Review is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Plan Ceo Review is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around gstack often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Plan Ceo Review came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

entries.body: Plan Ceo Review earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.

entries.title: Planning budget
entries.devilDefinition: The amount of map-staring an agent gets before it must touch the work and risk becoming measurable.
entries.plainDefinition: A planning budget is the time, tokens, steps, or cost an agent may spend decomposing a task, exploring options, and deciding a course of action before execution.
entries.whyExists: Agents need planning to avoid reckless action, but unlimited planning becomes latency, expense, and sometimes a decorative loop. Budgets keep exploration proportional to the task.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with caution. A long plan can still be shallow, and a short plan can be appropriate when the task is routine and reversible.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means setting step limits, deciding when to ask a user, when to call tools, when to stop researching, and when to execute a good-enough plan.
entries.example: "The agent's planning budget ended after three searches, which spared everyone a fourth theory about the same package.json."
askNext: How many planning steps are allowed before execution?
What conditions justify extending the planning budget?
How does the agent avoid spending the plan on theatre?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A limit on pre-action deliberation, so the agent eventually has to do the work.

entries.body: Planning is a virtue until it starts charging rent.

entries.title: Policy bypass
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Policy bypass is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Jailbreak are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The policy bypass discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Policy bypass is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Policy bypass belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Policy engine
entries.devilDefinition: The machine that decides what may happen, assuming the rules were written by someone braver than the meeting notes.
entries.plainDefinition: A policy engine is a runtime component that evaluates rules, context, permissions, risk signals, or organisational policies to decide what an AI system may see, say, or do.
entries.whyExists: AI systems need decisions that should not live only in prompts. Tool calls, data access, approvals, retention, routing, and safety behaviours often need policy enforcement outside the model.
entries.misuse: It gets used as a responsible-sounding noun for a folder of guidelines nobody has connected to the system. A policy engine is only meaningful when it actually evaluates and enforces decisions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means encoding policies into testable rules, attaching them to runtime events, logging decisions, and making exceptions visible before they become the real policy.
entries.example: "The policy engine blocked the assistant from sending customer data to the external summariser, which was more useful than the slide saying privacy is important."
askNext: Which decisions does the policy engine make at runtime?
Where are exceptions logged and reviewed?
Can the model override policy, or only request an allowed path?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A runtime decision layer, not a PDF wearing a platform badge.

entries.body: Policy becomes interesting only when it can say no to software that was about to do something convenient.

entries.title: Polysemanticity
entries.devilDefinition: One neuron doing several jobs poorly enough that your interpretation poster needs footnotes.
entries.plainDefinition: Polysemanticity is the property that a single neuron or direction responds to multiple unrelated concepts, complicating simple interpretability stories.
entries.whyExists: It is a central obstacle in reading models unit-by-unit and motivates feature-level methods and superposition analyses.
entries.misuse: Cited to claim interpretability is hopeless. It is a challenge that shapes methods, not a full stop.
entries.practicalMeaning: Prefer multi-evidence interpretations and interventions over single-neuron labels in dashboards meant for decisions.
entries.example: "Polysemanticity made the 'Python' unit fire on snakes, code, and Monty."
askNext: Are you labelling units or distributed features?
What interventions support the claimed meaning?
How many unrelated triggers activate the unit?
entries.warningLabel: A tidy label can hide a messy unit.
