entries.title: Post-Training
entries.devilDefinition: All the work done after pretraining so the base model can stop sounding raw, evasive, or commercially inconvenient.
entries.plainDefinition: Post-training is the phase after base-model pretraining where a model is further adapted through methods such as supervised fine-tuning, preference optimisation, safety tuning, distillation, tool-use training, or reasoning-focused tuning.
entries.whyExists: Because a pretrained model may have broad knowledge yet still be poor at following instructions, using tools, refusing unsafe requests, formatting outputs, or behaving in ways customers and policy teams can live with.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a minor polish step. In modern systems, post-training often shapes the behaviour users actually experience. The base model matters. So does everything done afterwards to civilise it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means expensive data curation, human or synthetic preference signals, eval loops, reward design, refusal policies, task-specific tuning, and long arguments about whether capability gains came from the base model or the scaffolding layered on top.
entries.example: "The pretraining got the headlines. The post-training taught the model how to survive product management."
askNext: Which behaviours come from the base model, and which were imposed during post-training?
What data or feedback signals shaped instruction following, refusals, and tool behaviour?
Did post-training improve real task quality or merely make the model easier to demo?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The long and consequential process of turning a broadly trained model into a commercially usable one.

entries.note: The term covers a family of methods. It is not synonymous with RLHF, even though RLHF remains one of its better-known ingredients.
entries.body: The modern model story is often written twice: once in pretraining papers and again in post-training release notes.

entries.title: Power-constrained AI
entries.devilDefinition: The moment the roadmap discovers the socket is not a metaphor.
entries.plainDefinition: Power-constrained AI describes AI growth, deployment, or operations limited by electricity supply, grid capacity, substations, cooling, energy pricing, or the ability to place compute where enough power is available.
entries.whyExists: It exists because scaling AI is no longer only a question of better models and more chips. Dense accelerator clusters need dependable electricity, and the grid has its own release schedule.
entries.misuse: It is misused when power is treated as a temporary facilities nuisance or as a simple morality play. Constraints can involve transmission, peak demand, cooling, permitting, pricing, emissions targets, workload scheduling, and whether the proposed site can actually be connected.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means capacity planning, power purchase agreements, demand response, model and hardware efficiency, inference batching, workload placement, cooling design, queueing, and occasional humility in front of municipal infrastructure.
entries.example: "The team had the model, the chips, and the customer demand; power-constrained AI arrived when the grid declined the invitation."
askNext: Is the constraint generation, transmission, substation capacity, cooling, price, permitting, or emissions policy?
Can efficiency, scheduling, batching, model choice, or placement reduce the power problem?

entries.body: Power-constrained AI is a useful antidote to cloud-shaped thinking. Somewhere, the abstract workload still wants a very real plug.
entries.searchText:
Power-constrained AI energy-constrained AI electricity-constrained AI Economics and operations of AI Infrastructure and deployment power compute data-centres economics The moment the roadmap discovers the socket is not a metaphor.
 Power-constrained AI describes AI growth, deployment, or operations limited by electricity supply, grid capacity, substations, cooling, energy pricing, or the ability to place compute where enough power is available.
 It exists because scaling AI is no longer only a question of better models and more chips. Dense accelerator clusters need dependable electricity, and the grid has its own release schedule.
 It is misused when power is treated as a temporary facilities nuisance or as a simple morality play. Constraints can involve transmission, peak demand, cooling, permitting, pricing, emissions targets, workload scheduling, and whether the proposed site can actually be connected.
 In practice it means capacity planning, power purchase agreements, demand response, model and hardware efficiency, inference batching, workload placement, cooling design, queueing, and occasional humility in front of municipal infrastructure.
 "The team had the model, the chips, and the customer demand; power-constrained AI arrived when the grid declined the invitation."

entries.title: Prefill/decode split
entries.devilDefinition: The architectural admission that reading the prompt and inventing the next token are different jobs, even when marketing still sells them as one smooth thought.
entries.plainDefinition: Prefill/decode split is a serving architecture pattern that separates the compute-heavy processing of the input prompt (prefill) from the sequential generation of output tokens (decode), often so each phase can be scheduled, batched, cached, or hardware-tuned differently.
entries.whyExists: Prompt processing is typically parallel and memory-bandwidth hungry; token generation is sequential and latency-sensitive. Treating them as one uniform workload wastes GPUs, confuses capacity planning, and makes "tokens per second" sound more honest than it is.
entries.misuse: It is treated as a guarantee of lower cost or magic speed. A split only helps when the scheduler, KV cache, batching, and hardware placement actually exploit the different shapes of work. Naming the phases is not the same as running them well.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means sizing and routing prefill and decode separately, watching TTFT versus inter-token latency, deciding where KV state lives, and refusing to judge the whole stack by a single tokens-per-second slide.
entries.example: "The prefill/decode split looked elegant on the architecture diagram until every long prompt still queued behind someone else's multi-turn novel."
askNext: Are prefill and decode measured, scheduled, and capacity-planned as separate workloads?
Where does the KV cache live between prefill and decode, and who pays for keeping it warm?
Which user-visible metric actually improved: time to first token, tokens per second, cost, or none of the above?
entries.warningLabel: Splitting phases is an engineering choice, not proof the serving problem is solved.

translations.label: serving meaning
translations.text: Prompt processing and token generation are different shapes of work and may be scheduled apart.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A serving redesign that only pays off once KV cache, batching, and capacity math catch up.

entries.body: No diagram is attached because the useful picture is a capacity and latency trade, not a single product flow. Prefill/decode split is infrastructure honesty dressed as architecture.

entries.title: Pretraining
entries.devilDefinition: The long, expensive next-token education that creates a base model before anyone teaches it manners.
entries.plainDefinition: Pretraining is large-scale unsupervised (usually next-token) learning on broad corpora that produces a base language model later adapted by fine-tuning, instruction tuning, or alignment.
entries.whyExists: General capabilities emerge from broad statistical learning at scale. Pretraining is still the foundation of modern LLMs.
entries.misuse: Used loosely for any training. Post-training and pretraining have different data, costs, and risks.
entries.practicalMeaning: Track data mix, scale, compute, and evals for base models; treat pretraining runs as major capital and governance events.
entries.example: "Pretraining finished; the model could complete code and also complete bad ideas fluently."
askNext: What data mix and filters were used?
Which base evals define success?
What post-training is planned to shape behaviour?
entries.warningLabel: Base capability is not product readiness.

entries.title: Private inference
entries.devilDefinition: The label vendors reach for when "trust us" has been asked to wear a blazer.
entries.plainDefinition: Private inference is a broad claim that model inference is run with privacy protections around user data, prompts, outputs, or business context. It can refer to on-device execution, isolated cloud tenancy, no-training policies, encryption, confidential computing, local models, or some mixture of those things.
entries.whyExists: It exists because organisations want AI features without casually donating sensitive data, customer records, trade secrets, or regulated workflows to every inference provider in the chain.
entries.misuse: It gets abused by collapsing very different privacy models into one comforting adjective. "Private" may mean the model runs on the device, the vendor does not train on the request, the traffic is encrypted, the data stays in a region, or simply that the sales deck has learned the mood of the room.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means pinning the exact privacy claim: where inference runs, who operates it, what data leaves the device or tenant, whether prompts are retained, who holds keys, what is logged, and what legal or technical controls back the promise.
entries.example: "The private inference feature sounded settled until procurement asked whether private meant on-device, encrypted, isolated, or merely not used for training."
askNext: Which privacy guarantee is being claimed: location, retention, training exclusion, isolation, encryption, or access control?
What data leaves the user's device, tenant, region, or organisation during inference?
Can the vendor show the policy and the architecture, or only the adjective?
entries.warningLabel: Private is not a technical architecture by itself.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: The AI feature should not casually expose sensitive inputs to the provider or the world.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A bundle of privacy claims that must be separated before the risk can be understood.

entries.body: Private inference is a useful phrase only after it stops being a fog machine. Ask which privacy property is doing the work.

entries.title: Pro Search
entries.devilDefinition: Pro Search is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Pro Search is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Perplexity often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Pro Search came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: Proactive agent
entries.devilDefinition: Software that acts before you ask, then hopes the aftermath still looks like service.
entries.plainDefinition: A proactive agent is an AI system designed to initiate actions, suggestions, or workflows without waiting for a fresh user turn, usually based on schedules, events, monitored context, or prior goals.
entries.whyExists: Many valuable tasks arrive as reminders, status changes, deadlines, and half-finished work rather than as neat chat prompts. Product teams want agents that notice and move, not only answer.
entries.misuse: Proactivity is sold as intelligence. Without policy, budgets, interruption design, and clear stop conditions, it becomes spam with write access. Acting first is easy; acting correctly without supervision is the hard part.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means defining triggers, permission scopes, spend limits, notification rules, approval gates for irreversible steps, and a way for users to see why the agent woke up and how to put it back to sleep.
entries.example: "The proactive agent booked the follow-up, rewrote the brief, and only then asked whether anyone still wanted the meeting."
askNext: What events are allowed to wake this agent, and who can change that list?
Which actions require approval, and which can run unattended within a budget?
How does a user inspect, undo, or permanently silence a proactive behaviour?
entries.warningLabel: Initiative without policy is just unsolicited automation.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: The agent starts work from events or goals, not only from a new prompt.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Background triggers plus permissions, budgets, and an interrupt button that actually works.

entries.body: The agent-loop diagram fits because proactivity is still observe, decide, act, and report — only the first step no longer waits for the user to type.

entries.title: Proactive notification
entries.devilDefinition: The agent messaging you first—useful when it is right, hostile when it has learned your weakest attention hours.
entries.plainDefinition: Proactive notification is agent- or system-initiated alerting that surfaces updates, risks, or completed work without a user query, requiring ranking, quiet hours, and false-alarm control.
entries.whyExists: Async agents need a way to return results and escalate. Proactivity is the product surface for that return path.
entries.misuse: Enabled for everything. Notification spam trains users to ignore the one alert that mattered.
entries.practicalMeaning: Rank by severity and user preference, enforce quiet hours, require clear actions, and measure mute rates as a product metric.
entries.example: "Proactive notification announced a successful lint fix at 03:12 and lost the team's trust."
askNext: What severity warrants breaking quiet hours?
Can users tune topics and channels?
How do you measure false-alarm rate?
entries.warningLabel: Attention is a scarce resource; agents can loot it.

entries.title: Procedural memory
entries.devilDefinition: Remembering how to do the thing next time, which sounds wise until the procedure is last quarter's brittle workaround.
entries.plainDefinition: Procedural memory in agent systems is retained knowledge of how to perform tasks—skills, playbooks, tool sequences, and habits—as distinct from episodic records of what happened in a particular run.
entries.whyExists: Agents that only store transcripts relearn the same steps. Teams wanted a name for reusable how-to knowledge that can be loaded as skills or policies.
entries.misuse: Treated as guaranteed competence. A stored procedure can be outdated, unsafe, or optimised for the wrong environment.
entries.practicalMeaning: Version skills, scope when they apply, test them like code, and separate them from raw conversation logs. Prefer explicit skill libraries over mystery memory blobs.
entries.example: "Procedural memory made the agent deploy faster—and reapply the same broken migration."
askNext: How are procedures authored, versioned, and retired?
Can the agent explain which skill it used and why?
What prevents a bad procedure from becoming institutional memory?
entries.warningLabel: Reusable steps are still only as good as their last review.

entries.title: Process reward model
entries.devilDefinition: A model paid to grade the working, not only the answer, so training can scold the journey as well as the destination.
entries.plainDefinition: A process reward model (PRM) scores intermediate reasoning steps or partial solutions, providing denser training or search signal than outcome-only rewards that judge only the final answer.
entries.whyExists: Hard tasks have long traces. Waiting until the end to say 'wrong' wastes signal and encourages lucky final guesses after messy reasoning.
entries.misuse: Treated as objective truth about reasoning. PRMs can be gamed, miscalibrated, or biased toward verbose-looking steps.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use PRMs in search, RL, or critique loops with careful step labels and held-out process evals. Monitor for reward hacking that invents plausible junk steps.
entries.example: "The process reward model loved step three; step three was a confident detour into fiction."
askNext: Who labelled the good steps, and on what task distribution?
Do you evaluate process quality separately from final accuracy?
What stops the policy from optimising PRM quirks?
entries.warningLabel: Step scores are still opinions with weights.

entries.title: Process supervision
entries.devilDefinition: Grading the steps, not only the final answer, so models learn that the journey can also be wrong.
entries.plainDefinition: Process supervision provides feedback on intermediate reasoning steps or actions—via humans or models—rather than supervising only final outcomes.
entries.whyExists: Outcome-only labels are sparse and allow lucky correctness. Process labels teach better trajectories on hard multi-step tasks.
entries.misuse: Assumed objective. Process labels encode annotator taste and can reward verbose-looking but empty steps.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use process supervision where steps are meaningful and checkable; combine with outcome metrics; watch for reward hacking of the process judge.
entries.example: "Process supervision improved proofs until students learned to write steps the grader liked."
askNext: Who labels steps, and with what rubric?
Do you measure final task success separately?
What gaming behaviours appear?
entries.warningLabel: Supervising process is still supervising a proxy.

entries.title: ProgramBench
entries.devilDefinition: A clean-room coding benchmark that gives the agent the manual and the binary, then asks it to rediscover why the original codebase had so many files.
entries.plainDefinition: ProgramBench evaluates whether software-engineering agents can rebuild complete programs from scratch. Each task provides a compiled executable and documentation but not the source; the agent must design and implement a replacement whose behaviour is checked by hidden tests.
entries.whyExists: Most coding benchmarks ask agents to patch an existing repository with its architecture already supplied. ProgramBench tests holistic software construction: requirements discovery, black-box experimentation, architecture, implementation, integration, and behavioural compatibility.
entries.misuse: Partial behavioural agreement is described as "rebuilding the program" even when almost no tasks are fully resolved. Hidden tests can expose missing behaviour, but they cannot prove semantic equivalence over every possible input or reward maintainable architecture automatically.
entries.practicalMeaning: The public benchmark contains 200 tasks derived from programs to be reconstructed under clean-room constraints. Results distinguish fully resolved instances from "almost" resolved ones that pass at least 95% of behavioural tests; those two columns should not be merged for a friendlier headline.
entries.example: "The agent rebuilt 97% of the program's observed behaviour, leaving the remaining 3% to contain authentication and the month-end close."
askNext: Is the reported number fully resolved or at least 95% behaviourally resolved?
What black-box access, documentation, internet, and decompilation rules applied?
Do the hidden tests cover the behaviours that matter outside the benchmark?
entries.warningLabel: Almost resolved is not the same column as rebuilt.

translations.label: evaluation meaning
translations.text: Recreate a whole program from its executable behaviour and documentation, then let hidden tests find the missing contract.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because ProgramBench's key mental model is clean-room reconstruction against a hidden behavioural oracle, not an ordinary tool-calling workflow.

entries.title: Programmatic tool calling
entries.devilDefinition: Let the model write the orchestration code, thereby saving round trips and concentrating the incident report.
entries.plainDefinition: Programmatic tool calling lets a model write code that invokes tools inside a controlled execution container. Intermediate tool results can be filtered, combined, or aggregated there before only the useful output is returned to the model.
entries.whyExists: A workflow with many ordinary tool calls requires repeated model turns and can pour large intermediate results into context. Code can perform loops, branching, joins, and filtering more cheaply while preserving the model for planning and interpretation.
entries.misuse: It is described as though fewer model round trips mean less complexity. The complexity has moved into generated code, container policy, tool permissions, runtime limits, and debugging traces—places where it can now fail at machine speed.
entries.practicalMeaning: The model writes a script, a sandbox runs it, permitted tools execute through controlled call paths, and only selected results return to context. Teams need explicit allowlists, time and resource limits, auditable calls, deterministic fixtures, and a firm answer to whether generated code may touch the network.
entries.example: "One generated script replaced twenty tool turns and gave the security review a pleasingly compact object of concern."
askNext: Which tools, networks, files, and secrets can the execution container access?
How are generated scripts, intermediate calls, and failures traced?
Does the workflow save tokens without hiding decisions that require review?
entries.warningLabel: Efficient orchestration is still code execution.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: Run a generated tool-using script in a sandbox so the model sees the answer, not every intermediate spreadsheet row.

entries.body: The optimisation is real. So is the old systems principle that anything allowed to execute code will eventually become the most interesting part of the architecture.
entries.searchText:
Programmatic tool calling  Agents and workflows Model usage tools code execution sandbox agents Let the model write the orchestration code, thereby saving round trips and concentrating the incident report.
 Programmatic tool calling lets a model write code that invokes tools inside a controlled execution container. Intermediate tool results can be filtered, combined, or aggregated there before only the useful output is returned to the model.
 A workflow with many ordinary tool calls requires repeated model turns and can pour large intermediate results into context. Code can perform loops, branching, joins, and filtering more cheaply while preserving the model for planning and interpretation.
 It is described as though fewer model round trips mean less complexity. The complexity has moved into generated code, container policy, tool permissions, runtime limits, and debugging traces—places where it can now fail at machine speed.
 The model writes a script, a sandbox runs it, permitted tools execute through controlled call paths, and only selected results return to context. Teams need explicit allowlists, time and resource limits, auditable calls, deterministic fixtures, and a firm answer to whether generated code may touch the network.
 "One generated script replaced twenty tool turns and gave the security review a pleasingly compact object of concern."

entries.title: Project Glasswing
entries.devilDefinition: A security initiative built on the now-plausible fear that the same model helping defenders today could industrialise attackers tomorrow.
entries.plainDefinition: Project Glasswing is Anthropic's programme for giving selected partners early access to Claude Mythos Preview to find and fix vulnerabilities in critical software and infrastructure.
entries.whyExists: Models have become strong enough at code and systems reasoning that cybersecurity is no longer just another benchmark story. If a model can inspect, understand, and alter complex software unusually well, defence and offence both start moving faster.
entries.misuse: It gets treated either as proof that cyber doom is already here or as proof that gated access itself counts as a sufficient safety strategy. Neither conclusion is especially adult. Restricted preview buys time. It does not settle the broader governance problem.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a closed partner programme, usage credits, system cards, research-preview language, and a highly managed attempt to steer frontier capability toward defensive work before the surrounding market catches up in less polite ways.
entries.example: "Project Glasswing was the moment the industry stopped speaking about coding gains in the abstract and started sounding like it had met a regulator."
askNext: What capability is being gated here, and how much of it is genuinely new?
Who gets access first, and what public benefit follows from that privilege?
Is this a real defensive programme, or an unusually sober product launch?
entries.warningLabel: Restricted access is a control, not a theory of the future.

translations.label: security-team meaning
translations.text: A preview programme for using a frontier model to harden important systems before everyone else gets similar capability.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A controlled early-access lane for a model that made the cyber conversation feel less theoretical.

entries.note: Project Glasswing is an initiative, not a model. The model inside the story is Claude Mythos Preview.
entries.body: The important shift is not the partner list. It is that capability is now being framed as something the industry must manage before it fully commodifies.

entries.title: Prompt
entries.devilDefinition: A politely worded attempt to steer a probability machine into behaving as if it has read the brief and cares.
entries.plainDefinition: A prompt is the input text, and sometimes structured context, provided to a model in order to shape its output. In LLM systems this can include instructions, examples, retrieved documents, tool results, formatting rules, and the user's actual request.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because the way you frame input materially affects output. Language models do not simply receive a question. They respond to the entire text context they are given.
entries.misuse: It gets treated either as wizardry or as trivia. Both are wrong. Prompt quality matters, but prompting is not a substitute for missing product design, missing data, or missing control logic. A good prompt cannot rescue a bad system forever.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the assembled context sent to the model: system instructions, user text, retrieved snippets, examples, tool schemas, output rules, and maybe the remnants of last week's prompt experiments still haunting production.
entries.example: "The issue was not that the model was weak; the prompt was half policy manual, half legal disclaimer, and no longer resembled a usable instruction."
askNext: What exact context is being sent to the model?
Which parts of the prompt are fixed system behaviour versus user input?
Is the prompt compensating for a missing tool, missing retrieval step, or missing product rule?
entries.note: In image generation, "prompt" often refers to descriptive text steering the style or content of an output. In LLM products, it usually refers to the full assembled input context, not just the visible user message.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A carefully packed bag of instructions, examples, constraints, and context, all fighting for the model's attention.

entries.body: The important operational question is rarely "what prompt did you type?" It is "what exactly did the system send?" Those are not always the same thing.

entries.title: Prompt Architecture
entries.devilDefinition: The attempt to turn a growing pile of instructions into something more defensible than folklore and copy-paste.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt architecture is the deliberate structure of system prompts, task instructions, examples, tool schemas, retrieved context, and output constraints across a model-driven workflow.
entries.whyExists: It exists because complex AI systems no longer send one neat instruction and hope for the best. They assemble multiple layers of context, and the order, boundaries, and ownership of those layers affect reliability more than anyone wanted to admit in the early prompt-engineering era.
entries.misuse: It gets used as a grand title for very long prompts and very little discipline. Sometimes the work really is architectural. Sometimes the phrase is just a more respectable way of saying nobody wants to untangle the string anymore.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means separating stable policy from task-level instructions, defining insertion points for retrieval and tool results, deciding what gets versioned, and preventing one helpful addition after another from turning the whole prompt into legal compost.
entries.example: "Their prompt architecture improved the moment they stopped letting every new requirement attach itself to the same immortal system message."
askNext: Which parts of the prompt are fixed policy, and which parts are task-specific assembly?
Where do retrieved context, examples, and tool outputs enter the stack?
What happens when you remove one section: collapse, improvement, or merely a shorter superstition?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Designing the prompt stack so the model receives fewer contradictions and the team receives fewer mysteries.

entries.body: Prompt architecture is useful when it names structure rather than swagger. If the system still consists of one swollen string and a prayer, the architecture remains aspirational.

entries.title: Prompt Caching
entries.devilDefinition: The serving trick where you pay full price once so the model can stop rereading your sacred system prompt on every request.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt caching is a technique where a provider reuses the already processed prefix of repeated prompts, reducing latency and input cost when the beginning of the request stays the same.
entries.whyExists: It exists because long instructions, tool schemas, examples, and reference context are expensive to process from scratch every time. If large parts of a prompt repeat, caching lets systems avoid doing the same preparatory work over and over.
entries.misuse: It gets pitched as free speed or magical memory. In reality it only helps when prompts share the right prefix, the provider's cache keeps it warm, and the traffic pattern is disciplined enough to hit it again before it expires.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means putting stable instructions first, volatile user data later, watching cache hit rates, and discovering that prompt order has quietly become a finance decision.
entries.example: "Prompt caching finally started paying off once they stopped putting the customer-specific chaos before the reusable instructions."
askNext: Which part of the prompt is actually stable enough to be reused?
How long do cache hits survive on this provider before they quietly disappear?
Are you measuring real latency and cost gains, or just admiring the idea of efficiency?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: Reuse the expensive beginning of a prompt so the invoice and the wait both shrink a little.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A provider-side shortcut that rewards repeated prompt prefixes and punishes sloppy prompt layout.

entries.note: The cache is not your application's memory. It is an opportunistic discount for repeated prompt structure.
entries.body: Prompt caching is one of those useful terms that sounds more glamorous than it is. The glamour fades the moment someone randomises the first thousand tokens and wonders why the savings have left the building.

entries.title: Prompt debt
entries.devilDefinition: The future cost of every expedient line added to a prompt because shipping today felt nobler than understanding tomorrow.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt debt is the accumulated maintenance burden, fragility, and hidden coupling created when prompts, examples, and guardrails are patched repeatedly without clear structure, tests, ownership, or retirement.
entries.whyExists: Prompting is fast, legible, and dangerously hospitable to tactical fixes. Teams keep adding caveats, examples, and exceptions because it works now, while the eventual complexity invoice waits with unusual patience.
entries.misuse: It gets romanticised as artisanal prompt craft or dismissed as not real engineering because it lives in text. In truth, prompt debt is ordinary technical debt wearing conversational clothing.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means contradictory instructions, duplicated examples, stale references, unexplained magic phrases, harder debugging, slower onboarding, and a model that obeys yesterday's emergency patch long after everyone forgets why it was invited.
entries.example: "The prompt looked comprehensive until the fifth exception contradicted the second principle and the model began choosing its favourite manager."
askNext: Who owns this prompt, and what tests prove each instruction still matters?
Which lines were added as temporary patches and never retired?
Would a smaller prompt plus cleaner tools or policy logic solve the problem more honestly?

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: A text-shaped pile of shortcuts that will eventually demand architecture.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The prompt still works, but nobody can explain why without opening four old incident documents.

entries.body: Prompt debt matters because prompts are now product logic by other means. Treating them as disposable notes is a good way to acquire a permanent archaeology project.

entries.title: Prompt Engineer
entries.devilDefinition: A person who discovered that giving clearer instructions becomes prestigious once the recipient is nonhuman.
entries.plainDefinition: A person who specialises in shaping prompts, examples, constraints, and interaction patterns to produce better results from AI systems.
entries.whyExists: Because generated output depends heavily on framing. Clear instructions, useful examples, and well-chosen constraints often matter more than people expect.
entries.misuse: By inflating careful prompting into a mystical profession or pretending it removes the need for domain expertise, testing, and workflow design.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means part writer, part editor, part product thinker: someone trying to make a probabilistic system behave consistently enough for actual use.
entries.example: "They hired a prompt engineer. What they really needed was someone to specify the task clearly enough that the rest of the team would stop contradicting itself."
askNext: Are they improving prompts, examples, system constraints, or the workflow around them?
Can the approach produce repeatable gains, or is it artisanal luck with version numbers?
Would the task improve just as much if the humans were clearer with one another?

translations.label: recruiter meaning
translations.text: A modern job title for the old skill of specifying work clearly and spotting bad drafts quickly.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone improving outcomes by making instructions more explicit and usable.

entries.body: The title sounds futuristic. The useful part is often ordinary professional clarity.

entries.title: Prompt Firewall
entries.devilDefinition: A security product that promises to stand between your model and human creativity, a famously restful place to stand.
entries.plainDefinition: A prompt firewall is a protective layer that inspects model inputs, outputs, tool calls, or retrieved content for policy violations, prompt injection, data leakage, unsafe instructions, and other risky patterns.
entries.whyExists: AI systems accept natural language from users, documents, tools, and websites. That makes ordinary application boundaries porous, so teams need checks that understand text, intent, context, and the strange confidence of a poisoned instruction.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as a complete shield. A firewall can reduce known classes of risk, but it cannot replace threat modelling, least-privilege tools, red-team testing, or the habit of not handing production powers to a chatbot with a clipboard.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means classifiers, rules, allowlists, deny lists, logging, human review paths, and careful placement around retrieval and tool execution. The hard part is blocking the right things without turning the product into a polite refusal machine.
entries.example: "The prompt firewall caught the obvious injection and missed the support document that asked the agent to email payroll with theatrical confidence."
askNext: Where is the firewall placed: before retrieval, after retrieval, before tools, after tools, or merely in the sales deck?
What happens when the firewall is unsure?
Are blocked requests audited, or are they just counted until the dashboard feels serious?
entries.warningLabel: A control layer, not an absolution layer.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Inspect AI traffic for risky instructions, leakage, and tool abuse.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful guardrail that becomes dangerous when everyone starts treating it as the whole fence.

entries.body: The prompt firewall is most convincing when it produces boring evidence: what it blocked, what it missed, and what the system still refuses to trust it with.

 A prompt firewall is a protective layer that inspects model inputs, outputs, tool calls, or retrieved content for policy violations, prompt injection, data leakage, unsafe instructions, and other risky patterns.
 AI systems accept natural language from users, documents, tools, and websites. That makes ordinary application boundaries porous, so teams need checks that understand text, intent, context, and the strange confidence of a poisoned instruction.
 It gets sold as a complete shield. A firewall can reduce known classes of risk, but it cannot replace threat modelling, least-privilege tools, red-team testing, or the habit of not handing production powers to a chatbot with a clipboard.
 In practice it means classifiers, rules, allowlists, deny lists, logging, human review paths, and careful placement around retrieval and tool execution. The hard part is blocking the right things without turning the product into a polite refusal machine.
 "The prompt firewall caught the obvious injection and missed the support document that asked the agent to email payroll with theatrical confidence."
 Where is the firewall placed: before retrieval, after retrieval, before tools, after tools, or merely in the sales deck? What happens when the firewall is unsure? Are blocked requests audited, or are they just counted until the dashboard feels serious? LLM application security Data loss prevention Policy enforcement

entries.title: Prompt Injection
entries.devilDefinition: The trick of slipping a second brief into the machine and watching it forget who is supposed to be in charge.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt injection is a failure mode or attack in which untrusted text changes a model's behaviour by smuggling in instructions that override, confuse, or redirect the intended prompt.
entries.whyExists: Because language models read instructions, data, and hostile text through the same basic channel. They do not naturally maintain the clean boundaries software engineers would very much prefer.
entries.misuse: By treating it as an exotic hacker trick rather than a structural property of model-based systems, or by using it to describe every wrong answer regardless of whether instructions were actually manipulated.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means any tool-using or retrieval-heavy system must assume that documents, web pages, emails, and user content may contain adversarial instructions that need filtering, isolation, or refusal.
entries.example: "The agent was fine until the support ticket helpfully instructed it to ignore policy, reveal the hidden prompt, and email the database to itself."
askNext: What untrusted text can reach the model, and in what priority order?
Which tools or permissions become dangerous if the model follows hostile instructions?
What separates trusted instructions from retrieved or user-supplied content?
entries.warningLabel: Not a niche curiosity. A design constraint.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Untrusted text has found a way to impersonate part of the system's instructions.

entries.body: Prompt injection matters because it collapses a comfortable fiction. The model does not reliably know which text is policy, which is data, and which is a trap wearing the manners of documentation.

entries.title: Prompt injection scanner
entries.devilDefinition: A detector for hostile instructions, useful until someone mistakes a smoke alarm for the fire brigade.
entries.plainDefinition: A prompt injection scanner is a tool or model-based check that looks for malicious, conflicting, or policy-breaking instructions in content before that content influences an AI system.
entries.whyExists: AI systems increasingly ingest web pages, documents, emails, tickets, and tool outputs. Some of that content will contain instructions designed to override the system, leak data, or steer actions.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a complete defence. Scanners can catch known patterns and suspicious language, but adversarial content adapts, and many attacks work by context rather than obvious wording.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means one layer in a wider safety design: content isolation, tool permissions, output checks, audit logs, least privilege, and careful handling of untrusted text.
entries.example: "The prompt injection scanner flagged the instruction telling the assistant to ignore security policy, which was helpful but not a reason to let the email call payroll."
askNext: What kinds of injection does the scanner actually detect?
What happens when the scanner is uncertain?
Which controls still protect the system when scanning misses an attack?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful warning layer, not a guarantee that untrusted text has become safe.

entries.body: Detection is not absolution. It is merely the part of the system willing to admit it has seen trouble before.

entries.title: Prompt scaffold
entries.devilDefinition: The temporary structure teams build around a model so the workflow can stand upright before the surrounding software has fully learned to do its job.
entries.plainDefinition: A prompt scaffold is the structured set of instructions, examples, delimiters, and output constraints used to support a workflow without being the workflow itself.
entries.whyExists: Models respond better when tasks are framed clearly. Prompt scaffolds exist because careful structure can stabilise behaviour, reduce ambiguity, and make an immature workflow usable before every surrounding control has been turned into software.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as permanent architecture. A scaffold is helpful precisely because it is a support structure, not a substitute for real validation, permissions, retrieval quality, or product logic. When a team starts asking the prompt to carry the whole building, the prompt begins to sag.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means ordered instructions, explicit roles, few-shot examples, output schemas, reminders about tool use, and the gradual discovery that some recurring prompt discipline deserves to migrate into code, policy, or interface design.
entries.example: "The prompt scaffold made the assistant usable. The trouble began when the team mistook that usability for a reason never to build the missing product controls."
askNext: Which parts of this scaffold are genuinely clarifying the task, and which are compensating for missing system design?
What elements belong in code, policy, or UI rather than in ever longer instructions?
If the scaffold disappeared, what behaviour would the workflow actually lose?

translations.label: builder meaning
translations.text: Useful prompt structure that keeps the workflow upright while the rest of the system catches up.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A support frame for model behaviour, not an excuse to leave system design unfinished forever.

entries.body: Prompt scaffolds are respectable. The pathology begins when they become load-bearing for problems the application ought to own more directly.

entries.title: PromptOps
entries.devilDefinition: Treating prompts like production code after enough production incidents taught you they already were.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt ops is the practice of versioning, testing, reviewing, and monitoring prompts and prompt templates as operational artefacts in LLM applications.
entries.whyExists: Tiny prompt edits change behaviour. Teams needed change control, evals, and ownership rather than wiki folklore.
entries.misuse: Reduced to a prompt CMS without tests, or over-processed so experiments freeze. Ops without metrics is bureaucracy.
entries.practicalMeaning: Store prompts in git or a versioned store, require eval gates on material changes, and attribute production traces to prompt versions.
entries.example: "Prompt ops caught the comma that made the agent skip authentication checks."
askNext: Who can edit production prompts?
What eval suite runs before merge?
Can you roll back to last week's wording in minutes?
entries.warningLabel: Unversioned prompts are unowned behaviour.

entries.title: Provenance
entries.devilDefinition: The story of where an artefact came from, which everyone demands after the artefact has already been trusted.
entries.plainDefinition: Provenance is recorded origin and transformation history for data, models, media, or outputs—who created them, from what, when, and under which processes—used for trust, compliance, and debugging.
entries.whyExists: Synthetic media, training data disputes, and supply-chain risk made origin claims load-bearing.
entries.misuse: Confused with authenticity guarantees. Provenance metadata can be missing, forged, or stripped in transit.
entries.practicalMeaning: Capture provenance at creation time, sign where possible, and design products that preserve it through transforms. Combine with policy, not as a sole defence.
entries.example: "Provenance showed the image came from the marketing pipeline, not the satellite."
askNext: What events are logged in the provenance chain?
Can metadata be stripped by normal export paths?
Who verifies signatures or attestations?
entries.warningLabel: History that is easy to erase is not history you can bet on.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Provenance is recorded origin and transformation history for data, models, media, or outputs—who created them, from what, when, and under which processes—used for trust, compliance, and debugging.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Capture provenance at creation time, sign where possible, and design products that preserve it through transforms.

entries.body: Provenance is receipts. Systems that discard receipts are choosing amnesia.

entries.title: Provenance signal
entries.devilDefinition: A clue about where content came from, not a courtroom transcript with better metadata.
entries.plainDefinition: A provenance signal is any marker, metadata, watermark, credential, signature, or contextual clue that helps indicate the origin, history, or authenticity of content.
entries.whyExists: Synthetic media, edited content, reposts, and automated generation make origin harder to judge. Provenance signals help systems and people assess where content came from and whether it has been altered.
entries.misuse: They get mistaken for proof. A signal can be missing, stripped, forged, misread, or detached from the thing it was meant to describe.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means combining metadata, platform context, cryptographic checks, editorial judgement, and scepticism rather than outsourcing trust to one badge.
entries.example: "The provenance signal suggested the image had edit history, which was useful until the platform treated that suggestion as divine testimony."
askNext: What does this provenance signal actually prove?
Can the signal be removed, forged, or separated from the content?
Which decisions rely on this signal alone?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful clue about origin, not the final verdict.

entries.body: Signals help. They do not relieve anyone of judgement, which remains unpopular but necessary.

entries.title: Provider Abstraction
entries.devilDefinition: The layer promising freedom from vendor lock-in while quietly becoming a new dependency with opinions of its own.
entries.plainDefinition: Provider abstraction is a software layer that normalises differences between model or inference providers so an application can switch vendors, route requests, or support multiple back ends with less app-level change.
entries.whyExists: It exists because providers differ in APIs, pricing, uptime, rate limits, model access, and feature support, while product teams would still like to behave as though swapping them were a sign of maturity rather than a fresh integration programme.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as effortless interchangeability. In reality, you can abstract the API more easily than you can abstract behaviour, tool support, latency patterns, safety systems, or the awkward fact that different models fail in different accents.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means adapter layers, schema translation, feature fallbacks, observability, routing policy, and constant arguments over whether to target the least common denominator or preserve the parts each provider is unusually good at.
entries.example: "Their provider abstraction made it easy to switch endpoints and slightly less easy to explain why the outputs changed character the moment finance wanted a cheaper option."
askNext: Which provider differences are truly hidden, and which still leak into the product?
What breaks when you switch models: quality, tool behaviour, latency, or compliance?
Are you abstracting the transport, the feature set, or the user experience?
entries.warningLabel: Portability usually stops where model behaviour starts.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: One interface over several providers, plus a fresh category of mismatch tickets.

entries.note: A provider abstraction can reduce switching cost and still fail to make providers meaningfully equivalent.
entries.body: Provider abstraction is often sensible engineering. It becomes theology when teams speak as if a shared interface has abolished the underlying differences that still decide cost, quality, and risk.

entries.title: Provider routing
entries.devilDefinition: Provider routing is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Provider routing is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around AI Gateway and OpenRouter often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Provider routing came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: QLoRA
entries.devilDefinition: LoRA on a quantised base so fine-tuning fits on hardware that finance will actually approve.
entries.plainDefinition: QLoRA is a fine-tuning method that freezes a quantised base model (commonly 4-bit) and trains LoRA adapters in higher precision, enabling large-model adaptation on smaller GPUs.
entries.whyExists: Full fine-tunes of large models are inaccessible to many teams. QLoRA made high-parameter adaptation practical on modest hardware.
entries.misuse: Assumed identical to full-precision full fine-tuning. Quantisation and adapter limits can reduce quality on hard tasks.
entries.practicalMeaning: Follow established recipes for rank, quant type, and learning rates; evaluate carefully; watch for training instability.
entries.example: "QLoRA fine-tuned the 70B on one GPU and still needed a week of eval arguments."
askNext: Which quantisation and LoRA config are you using?
How close is quality to a stronger FT baseline?
Are adapters versioned and reviewed?
entries.warningLabel: Accessible fine-tuning is still fine-tuning with consequences.

entries.title: Quantisation
entries.devilDefinition: Storing weights with fewer bits so inference gets cheaper and everyone argues about how much quality you actually sold.
entries.plainDefinition: Quantisation reduces the numeric precision of model weights and/or activations (for example to 8-bit or 4-bit formats) to cut memory and often increase throughput, with some accuracy trade-offs.
entries.whyExists: Large models strain memory bandwidth and capacity. Lower precision is one of the main practical compression levers for serving.
entries.misuse: Assumed lossless, or compared across methods without task metrics. A '4-bit model' is not a single quality class.
entries.practicalMeaning: Benchmark your tasks after quantisation, watch outliers and long-context behaviour, and pick formats your hardware actually accelerates.
entries.example: "Quantisation fit the model on one GPU and made the accounting bot forget how invoices work."
askNext: Which scheme and bit-width, and calibrated how?
What task metrics regressed?
Is the runtime using real low-precision kernels?
entries.warningLabel: Smaller weights are not automatically the same model.

entries.title: Qwen
entries.devilDefinition: A model family people mention either as evidence that the field is more global than the usual brand shortlist, or because they would like you to know they read beyond the obvious vendors.
entries.plainDefinition: Qwen is a family of models associated with Alibaba Cloud and related research and product efforts. It can refer to multiple models and variants rather than a single unified system.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions of AI often collapse into a very small US-centric vendor set. Qwen is one of the model families that reminds people the landscape is broader, more competitive, and more operationally varied than the headlines imply.
entries.misuse: It gets used as if "Qwen" alone were enough information. In reality, capability, licensing, deployment fit, and tool support depend on the specific model and version. Saying "we benchmarked Qwen" is only the beginning of the sentence.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means evaluating one of several model variants for cost, quality, deployment flexibility, coding, agent workflows, multimodal use, or localisation needs. The right comparison is not with a brand in general, but with a task and a constraint set.
entries.example: "They were not adopting Qwen as an ideology; they were testing whether a particular variant gave them an acceptable quality-cost trade-off for multilingual support."
askNext: Which Qwen model or version are we talking about?

entries.title: Rabbit
entries.devilDefinition: A bright orange reminder that "AI hardware" often means "a chatbot with industrial design and stronger opinions about your workflow."
entries.plainDefinition: Rabbit is an AI hardware and software company best known for the rabbit r1, a small device built around voice interaction, app actions, and an operating layer called rabbitOS. Its original pitch was that AI should not just answer questions, but also help perform actions across services and workflows; its current positioning also leans into rabbitOS 2, DLAM, and third-party integrations such as OpenClaw on r1.
entries.whyExists: Because every few years the industry rediscovers the dream of a dedicated personal assistant device and insists that this time it will be different.
entries.misuse: By talking about Rabbit as if it solved agentic computing in general, rather than shipping one very visible attempt at turning AI into a standalone consumer device. The company is a case study, not a general law of the interface future.
entries.practicalMeaning: A bet that people want a dedicated AI-native device, not just another app on a phone. Also a bet that action matters more than chat, and that orchestration can be packaged as consumer hardware without becoming a niche curiosity.
entries.example: "The demo made Rabbit look like the future. The harder question was whether that future needed another piece of hardware in your pocket."
askNext: Is Rabbit interesting because of the hardware, the action layer, or the marketing?
Does it do something meaningfully better than a phone plus apps?
Is the value in the device, or in the orchestration layer behind it?
entries.warningLabel: Often discussed as if it were a general theory of AI devices, rather than one company shipping one very opinionated product.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A consumer device trying to make AI action feel native enough to justify existing separately from the phone.

entries.note: Rabbit's own positioning has shifted over time. The original story centred on rabbitOS and a Large Action Model; the current product pages also emphasise rabbitOS 2, DLAM, and third-party agent integrations on r1.
entries.body:
Rabbit is useful partly because it makes an old ambition newly legible. People do not merely want smarter chat. They want fewer menus, fewer tabs, and fewer tiny acts of digital housekeeping.

The risk, as ever, is that "fewer apps" becomes "one more device".

entries.title: RAG
entries.devilDefinition: A system for giving the model open-book conditions and then acting surprised that the book selection matters.
entries.plainDefinition: RAG stands for retrieval-augmented generation. It refers to a pattern in which a system retrieves relevant external information, usually documents or records, and inserts that information into the model's context before the model generates an answer.
entries.whyExists: Base models do not reliably know your internal documents, latest policies, or private data. RAG exists so a system can supplement the model with fresh, domain-specific, or proprietary information at query time.
entries.misuse: People describe almost any search-plus-model feature as RAG, even when retrieval quality is poor, documents are shoved into the prompt indiscriminately, or there is no meaningful grounding at all. Slapping documents near a model is not the same as building a reliable retrieval system.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means chunking documents, creating embeddings, storing vectors, retrieving candidates, reranking or filtering them, and then deciding what enters the prompt. The model is only as grounded as those earlier steps allow.
entries.example: "The team said they had solved hallucinations with RAG. What they had actually solved was the first paragraph of the sales demo."
askNext: How are documents chunked, embedded, retrieved, and filtered?
What evidence shows the retrieved material is actually relevant?
What happens when retrieval fails or returns contradictory context?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The practical way to make a general model look domain-aware.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A search and context assembly problem sitting in front of a model call.

entries.body: RAG is valuable because it is ordinary. It lets you improve answers with better retrieval, better curation, and better context handling rather than pretending the base model magically absorbed your SharePoint.

entries.title: RAG Audit Trail
entries.devilDefinition: The receipt for the answer, requested shortly after the answer has become expensive.
entries.plainDefinition: A RAG audit trail is the record of which query, sources, chunks, rankings, filters, citations, model inputs, and answer outputs were used when a retrieval-augmented system produced a response.
entries.whyExists: Once RAG answers influence support, legal, compliance, medical, financial, or operational decisions, teams need to know what evidence was retrieved, what was excluded, and how the final answer was grounded.
entries.misuse: It is confused with showing citations in the user interface. Citations are useful, but an audit trail also needs retriever state, permissions, versions, timestamps, filters, model inputs, and enough detail to reproduce or investigate the answer.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means logging retrieval requests, source identities, chunk IDs, ranking scores, permission filters, prompt context, generated claims, cited sources, blocked sources, model versions, and retention rules that will survive contact with compliance.
entries.example: "The RAG audit trail showed that the assistant cited the policy correctly and ignored the newer exception nobody had indexed."
askNext: Can a disputed answer be reconstructed from logged retrieval and generation records?
Are filtered, blocked, stale, and cited sources all visible to reviewers?
How long is the evidence retained, and who may inspect it?
entries.warningLabel: A citation is not an audit trail; it is the visible tip of one.

translations.label: compliance meaning
translations.text: Evidence that shows how a retrieved answer was assembled, grounded, filtered, and produced.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The logs you wish you had collected before someone asked why the answer was trusted.

entries.body: A RAG audit trail is less exciting than a fluent answer. This is why it tends to matter after the fluent answer has done something memorable.

entries.title: Ranked AI
entries.devilDefinition: The SEO pitch reborn for a world where the customer asks a chatbot and the website waits at home for visitors who may never arrive.
entries.plainDefinition: Ranked AI is a service positioned around AI search and SEO, helping businesses improve visibility in AI-mediated discovery surfaces as well as traditional search.
entries.whyExists: It exists because brands are learning that being visible in Google results is no longer the whole discovery problem. AI answers, summaries, citations, and recommendations increasingly shape whether a business is mentioned at all.
entries.misuse: It is easy to sell as a guaranteed way to "rank in AI", as if assistants had one stable leaderboard. In reality, AI visibility depends on source quality, structured information, authority, citations, query phrasing, and the messy retrieval habits of each system.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Ranked AI belongs to the wider GEO and AI SEO market: audits, content optimisation, citation strategy, monitoring, and the hard work of making a brand easier for answer engines to understand and trust.
entries.example: "Ranked AI promised visibility in AI search, which sounded new until the work plan included clearer pages, better citations, and the old religion of making useful content."
askNext: Which AI surfaces are being measured?
Are they tracking citations, mentions, referrals, conversions, or ordinary rankings?
What evidence shows the work changes AI answers rather than just producing more SEO copy?
entries.warningLabel: AI search visibility is real. Guaranteed AI ranking is usually theatre.

translations.label: agency meaning
translations.text: Make the brand easier for answer engines to find, cite, and recommend.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A newer wrapper around discoverability work, with some real AI-search monitoring and plenty of old SEO discipline underneath.

entries.body: The phrase "rank in AI" is useful mostly because it reveals who has mistaken a probability cloud for a leaderboard.

entries.title: Ranking system
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Ranking system names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Output Laundering needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once ranking system entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: Rate Limit
entries.devilDefinition: The provider's quiet way of reminding your agent that enthusiasm is not capacity planning.
entries.plainDefinition: A rate limit is a cap on how much an API or service can be used over a time period, often measured in requests, input tokens, output tokens, concurrent jobs, or other throughput units.
entries.whyExists: It exists to protect shared infrastructure, control abuse, preserve service quality, and make sure one ambitious integration does not turn a provider's capacity plan into a public experiment.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with spend limits, context limits, or general product restrictions. Those may interact, but they are not the same thing: one controls throughput, another controls money, and another controls how much the model can read at once.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means backoff logic, queues, retries, request shaping, model tier choices, budget alerts, and the discovery that your "real-time" agent is now waiting politely behind its own success.
entries.example: "The demo scaled beautifully until the rate limit arrived and taught the agent the ancient discipline of waiting."
askNext: Which limit are we hitting: requests, input tokens, output tokens, concurrency, spend, or context?
Do we have backoff, queueing, fallbacks, or a cheaper model tier for overflow?
Is the limit a provider constraint, a workspace setting, or self-inflicted traffic design?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The service is not down; it is declining to be consumed quite so rapidly.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A throughput ceiling that becomes an architecture concern as soon as the product works.

entries.note: Usage limits is the umbrella phrase people often use in conversation. For engineering decisions, separate rate limits, spend limits, context limits, and feature caps before designing the workaround.
entries.body: Rate limits are rarely glamorous. They are, however, one of the first signs that a prototype has wandered into production clothing.

entries.title: ReAct pattern
entries.devilDefinition: Reason, act, observe, repeat—until the agent has thought out loud enough to justify the API bill. Not the JavaScript library.
entries.plainDefinition: The ReAct pattern interleaves natural-language reasoning with tool actions and observations in a loop, so an agent can plan and gather information step by step.
entries.whyExists: Pure chain-of-thought cannot query systems; pure tool calls lack flexible planning text. ReAct combined both in a widely copied recipe.
entries.misuse: Confused with React the UI framework, or treated as the only agent architecture. Verbose traces can leak and cost without improving outcomes.
entries.practicalMeaning: Bound loop iterations, structure tool schemas tightly, and evaluate whether explicit reasoning text helps versus tool-only policies on your tasks.
entries.example: "The ReAct pattern found the answer in three tools and six paragraphs of self-narration."
askNext: What stops infinite reason-act loops?
Are reasoning traces logged securely?
Would a planner/executor split be clearer?
entries.warningLabel: A popular pattern is still a pattern, not a product.

entries.title: Reasoning budget
entries.devilDefinition: The amount of thought a model is allowed before finance, latency, or policy asks it to develop conviction.
entries.plainDefinition: A reasoning budget is a limit on the computation, tokens, time, cost, or policy allowance a model may spend on deliberation before producing an answer or action.
entries.whyExists: More reasoning can improve some tasks, but it costs money, latency, energy, and sometimes user patience. Systems need controls that decide when deeper reasoning is worth the trade-off.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a quality dial that only turns upward. More deliberation can still reason from bad premises, overfit a neat plan, or spend handsomely to reach the wrong conclusion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means task routing, cost controls, latency targets, confidence thresholds, escalation rules, and a clear idea of which work deserves slower, more expensive thought.
entries.example: "The refund bot got a higher reasoning budget for disputed cases and a lower one for checking whether Tuesday comes after Monday."
askNext: Which tasks receive a larger reasoning budget, and why?
What happens when the budget is exhausted?
Are we buying better judgement, or merely a longer explanation?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A spending limit on deliberation, not a guarantee that the answer deserves its confidence.

entries.body: The budget buys the opportunity to think. It does not buy wisdom in bulk.

entries.title: Reasoning Effort
entries.devilDefinition: The polite little dial that lets you spend extra time and money so the model can look more thoughtful before disappointing you with greater ceremony.
entries.plainDefinition: Reasoning effort is a model setting that asks a reasoning-capable model to spend more or less internal computation before answering, usually trading speed and cost for stronger performance on harder tasks.
entries.whyExists: It exists because not every prompt deserves the same amount of computational attention. A quick classification task and a multi-step coding problem should not be billed as though they require identical intellectual theatre.
entries.misuse: It is often treated as a truth slider. Higher effort can improve performance on difficult tasks, but it does not guarantee correctness, judgement, or freedom from the model's usual bad habits.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means deciding whether the task is worth the latency and token burn. Low effort is sensible for routine work. Higher effort is better reserved for coding, planning, maths, and the kinds of questions people later pretend were obvious.
entries.example: "They turned reasoning effort to high for a simple extraction job, which was a costly way to discover the value of restraint."
askNext: What kinds of tasks genuinely improve when the model spends longer reasoning?
How much latency and cost does a higher setting add in production?
Are you buying better performance, or just a slower answer with nicer posture?

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A user-facing way to expose the trade-off between speed, cost, and extra internal computation.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A knob for deciding when a task deserves more machine time than your budget wanted to give it.

entries.warningLabel: Useful control, unreliable status symbol.
entries.body: The term is genuinely practical. It only becomes ridiculous when people start treating a compute budget as a personality trait.

entries.title: Reasoning Model
entries.devilDefinition: A model marketed as thoughtful because it spends longer arranging its confidence.
entries.plainDefinition: A model or mode designed to spend more compute and intermediate steps on harder tasks so it can perform better on structured problems, multi-step work, or tool-heavy tasks.
entries.whyExists: Because not every prompt is a pattern-matching snack. Some tasks benefit when the system takes more time, explores alternatives, or uses tools before committing to an answer.
entries.misuse: By treating "reasoning" as a synonym for correctness, judgement, or wisdom. Extra deliberation can improve results, but it can also produce a longer route to the same wrong conclusion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slower responses, higher cost, and better odds on thorny tasks if the problem is well framed and the evaluation is honest.
entries.example: "They upgraded to the reasoning model and were delighted by the improved maths, right up to the point they asked it a vague strategic question."
askNext: What kinds of tasks measurably improve when the system takes longer?
How much extra latency and cost are we accepting for that gain?
Is the task truly hard, or merely badly specified?

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A premium mode for harder questions and a premium invoice for asking them.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A slower model path that can improve structured task performance without guaranteeing sound judgement.

entries.note: The phrase is useful if it names a measurable operating trade-off. It becomes perfume when it is used as a personality trait.
entries.body: The machine may take longer. That does not mean it has become profound.

entries.title: Reasoning Tokens
entries.devilDefinition: Tokens consumed while the model privately arranges its confidence and your invoice publicly matures.
entries.plainDefinition: "Reasoning tokens" is a loose product and billing term for tokens spent on internal deliberation or intermediate reasoning steps, especially in models that use extra compute before producing a visible answer.
entries.whyExists: Because reasoning-oriented model modes often consume more compute than the final response length suggests, and providers need some way to describe or meter the extra budget.
entries.misuse: By talking as though more hidden tokens automatically mean better judgement. Sometimes the model is genuinely doing harder work. Sometimes it is taking the scenic route to the wrong answer and charging for the view.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slower responses, higher usage cost, budget controls for internal deliberation, and partial visibility at best into where the extra compute went.
entries.example: "The answer looked short, but the usage report showed enough reasoning tokens to suggest the model had held an expensive internal committee meeting first."
askNext: How does the provider define and expose reasoning-token usage?
What quality gain justified the extra latency and spend?
Can the task be scoped more tightly so the model thinks less and performs better?
entries.note: Providers do not expose this concept consistently. Sometimes it is a direct meter, sometimes a vague usage hint, and sometimes mostly a marketing aroma around slower models.

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: The model appears to have thought harder than the budget approved.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Extra compute was burned somewhere between the prompt and the answer, and you may only get partial receipts.

entries.body: The useful question is not whether the model thought. The useful question is whether the additional hidden work improved anything a customer could notice.

entries.title: Reasoning Trace
entries.devilDefinition: The part of the model's inner monologue vendors alternately market as proof of thought and hide as a trade secret.
entries.plainDefinition: A reasoning trace is the sequence of intermediate deliberative steps a model produces while solving a task, whether shown directly, summarised for the user, or kept internal by the provider.
entries.whyExists: It exists because some reasoning-oriented systems allocate extra compute to intermediate steps before producing a final answer, and those steps can improve performance on harder tasks.
entries.misuse: The term is abused whenever a long answer is treated as evidence of deep reasoning, or when a visible summary is mistaken for the full internal process. What you see may be curated explanation rather than raw cognition.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means more latency, more tokens, higher cost, better performance on some tasks, and a new set of arguments about transparency, gaming, privacy, and whether exposing traces helps users or merely helps them feel impressed.
entries.example: "The product demo featured a reasoning trace, which was excellent theatre and only partial evidence."
askNext: Are users seeing the raw intermediate steps, a provider summary, or nothing at all?
Does the extra reasoning budget improve outcomes on this task enough to justify the cost?
Could exposing the trace create privacy, security, or prompt-gaming problems?

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: Evidence that the model did more than instantly guess.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A partly visible deliberation process whose usefulness depends on what is shown, hidden, and priced.

entries.note: Providers differ sharply in how much of a reasoning trace they reveal. Some expose summaries, some expose raw steps, and some expose only the bill.
entries.body: The trace often tells you something. It just does not always tell you the thing the demo implies.
