entries.title: Red Teaming
entries.devilDefinition: The ritual of asking determined people to break the system before determined strangers do it for free.
entries.plainDefinition: Red teaming is adversarial testing aimed at finding harmful behaviours, security weaknesses, jailbreaks, unsafe tool interactions, or operational failures in an AI system.
entries.whyExists: Because benchmark scores, happy-path demos, and internal optimism do not cover what users, attackers, or merely curious employees will actually try once a system is live.
entries.misuse: By turning it into theatre: a one-off exercise, a slide in the launch deck, or a carefully controlled hunt for problems everyone already knows how to solve. Real red teaming is inconvenient precisely because it uncovers things the roadmap had hoped to ignore.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means structured adversarial prompts, tool-abuse scenarios, domain experts probing edge cases, and a repeatable process for turning discovered failures into mitigations rather than folklore.
entries.example: "The launch review improved once red teaming stopped being a ceremonial afternoon and started behaving like a serious attempt to embarrass the product."
askNext: Who is testing the system, and what failure modes are they incentivised to find?
Are the findings feeding product changes, policy controls, or merely prettier documentation?
What happens when the system fails safely in the lab but unsafely in the workflow?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Trying to break the system on purpose so the real world has fewer chances to do it first.

entries.body: It is one of the field's more respectable habits. The only recurring problem is the temptation to make it decorative.

entries.title: Red-team scenario
entries.devilDefinition: A concrete bad day written down before the real one arrives with better lawyers.
entries.plainDefinition: A red-team scenario is a specific adversarial or misuse situation designed to test how an AI system behaves under pressure, attack, ambiguity, or abuse.
entries.whyExists: Red-teaming needs more than general concern. Scenarios make risks testable by specifying the actor, goal, context, constraints, and expected safety behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets turned into theatre when scenarios are vague, unrealistic, unmeasured, or selected because they sound dramatic in a workshop.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means writing testable cases that reflect real misuse paths, then using the results to improve controls, policies, monitoring, and incident response.
entries.example: "The red-team scenario tested whether a malicious document could persuade the agent to leak CRM data, which was more useful than saying 'what about bad prompts' for an hour."

entries.title: Reflection loop
entries.devilDefinition: A second chance for the agent to criticise itself, sometimes productively and sometimes as paid procrastination.
entries.plainDefinition: A reflection loop is an agent pattern where the system generates an output, critiques it against criteria, and revises—possibly multiple times—before finishing.
entries.whyExists: Single-pass generation misses constraints. Reflection adds a structured improvement cycle without always needing a second model.
entries.misuse: Unbounded loops that burn tokens polishing prose. Reflection without external checks can reinforce errors.
entries.practicalMeaning: Cap iterations, use explicit rubrics, and stop early when criteria are met. Add external verifiers for code and facts.
entries.example: "The reflection loop improved tone three times and never fixed the off-by-one."
askNext: What stopping rule ends reflection?
Is there an external verifier?
Does iteration improve task metrics or only style?
entries.warningLabel: More passes are not free intelligence.

entries.title: Regression eval
entries.devilDefinition: A check that asks whether an old failure has returned wearing the new release notes.
entries.plainDefinition: A regression eval is an evaluation designed to catch previously fixed failures, degraded behaviours, or safety issues that reappear after model, prompt, data, or system changes.
entries.whyExists: AI systems change through model updates, prompt edits, retrieval changes, tool revisions, and policy tuning. Regression evals help ensure old problems do not quietly return.
entries.misuse: They get treated as comprehensive quality tests. A regression eval usually protects against known failures; it does not prove the system is generally safe or good.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means maintaining failure cases, running them before release, tracking pass rates over time, and refusing to delete uncomfortable tests because the dashboard looks nicer without them.
entries.example: "The regression eval caught the refund hallucination again, which was irritating mostly because the model sounded so refreshed."
askNext: Which past failures are covered by this eval?
What changes trigger the regression suite?
Who reviews failures before release proceeds?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A check for known bad behaviour returning, not a certificate of overall quality.

entries.body: Regression evals are institutional memory with a pass/fail column.

entries.title: Remote MCP server
entries.devilDefinition: An MCP server moved onto the network, where every convenient tool call acquires latency, credentials, and someone else's uptime.
entries.plainDefinition: A remote MCP server is a network-hosted Model Context Protocol endpoint that exposes tools, resources, or prompts to MCP clients. Unlike a local server process, it sits behind a transport, authentication layer, and operational boundary managed over the network.
entries.whyExists: Local MCP servers are useful for personal tools, but shared services need central deployment, updates, access control, and availability. A remote server lets several approved clients use the same managed integration without installing its implementation on every machine.
entries.misuse: "Remote" is sometimes treated as a deployment detail that changes nothing. It changes the threat model, failure modes, privacy assumptions, latency, tenancy, and the identity to which each tool call is attributed.
entries.practicalMeaning: Teams need to evaluate the server operator, transport security, authorisation scopes, data retention, approval behaviour, rate limits, audit logs, and what happens when the endpoint is slow or unavailable. Standardised discovery does not make a remote capability trustworthy by default.
entries.example: "The remote MCP server saved everyone an installation step and gave security a considerably longer questionnaire."
askNext: Who operates the server, and where do requests and results travel?
How are users, clients, and individual tool calls authenticated and authorised?
What data is retained, and what is the failure path when the server is unavailable?
entries.warningLabel: A standard protocol is not a trust decision.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shared MCP integration whose convenience now depends on network, identity, and operator controls.

entries.body: Moving the server out of the laptop removes some installation work. It does not remove the work; it gives the work an operations budget.

entries.title: Representation engineering
entries.devilDefinition: Editing behaviour by finding directions in activation space, then hoping those directions mean what the paper said on Tuesday.
entries.plainDefinition: Representation engineering is a family of methods that identify and manipulate internal representational directions associated with concepts or behaviours to monitor or control models.
entries.whyExists: Prompts are blunt. Researchers seek more direct levers on model internals for safety and control research.
entries.misuse: Overclaimed as mature product control. Concept directions can be unstable, polysemantic, and evaluation-sensitive.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use in research and carefully scoped experiments with strong behavioural evals. Do not substitute for policy, filtering, and access control in production without evidence.
entries.example: "Representation engineering reduced sycophancy in the lab and odd politeness regressions in the wild."
askNext: How stable is the direction across prompts and seeds?
What side effects appear on unrelated tasks?
Is there a simpler external control that works?
entries.warningLabel: Pretty geometry is not a safety case.

entries.title: Reranking
entries.devilDefinition: A second model that reorders search results so the first retrieval pass can remain gloriously imprecise.
entries.plainDefinition: Reranking is a second-stage retrieval step that scores a shortlist of candidate documents or passages more carefully—often with a cross-encoder—before they are passed to the generator.
entries.whyExists: First-stage retrieval optimises recall and speed. Rerankers trade compute for better precision on the top-k set.
entries.misuse: Expected to fix a terrible corpus or query understanding. Rerankers reorder; they do not invent missing documents.
entries.practicalMeaning: Rerank a modest candidate set, measure nDCG/recall@k on real queries, and watch latency budgets. Cache when queries repeat.
entries.example: "Reranking promoted the right policy paragraph above the FAQ that merely shared keywords."
askNext: How large is the candidate set before rerank?
What metric improved in online evaluation?
Is the reranker cost justified versus better chunking?
entries.warningLabel: Better order is not better knowledge.

entries.title: Research agent
entries.devilDefinition: A system that searches, reads, compares, and synthesises, ideally before declaring the first agreeable snippet a civilisation.
entries.plainDefinition: A research agent is an AI workflow that plans information gathering, searches or browses sources, inspects evidence, and produces a synthesis with some account of where its claims came from.
entries.whyExists: Research tasks involve exploration, source selection, reading, comparison, and synthesis. Agents can help manage that loop when the work is too broad for a single retrieval call or chat answer.
entries.misuse: It gets applied to any chatbot with web access. Reading three search results and emitting a confident paragraph is not research; it is autocomplete with a library card.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means source strategy, citation handling, uncertainty tracking, browsing limits, provenance, and a willingness to say that the evidence is thin.
entries.example: "The research agent found three independent filings, ignored two marketing pages, and therefore briefly behaved like it had met a librarian."
askNext: What search and source plan does the agent follow?
How does it decide which sources are authoritative or independent?
Can it show the evidence behind each important claim?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An agentic research workflow, not a search box wearing a lab coat.

entries.body: Research is not the act of finding a source. It is the act of surviving more than one.

entries.title: Research plan
entries.devilDefinition: The part before synthesis where the answer is quietly decided by which sources are invited into the room.
entries.plainDefinition: A research plan is an explicit strategy for what questions to answer, which sources to inspect, how to search, and how to compare evidence before generating a research synthesis.
entries.whyExists: Without a plan, research agents drift toward convenient sources, repeated claims, and whatever ranked highest on the first search. A plan makes the intended evidence path visible.
entries.misuse: It becomes ceremonial when the plan is written after the answer, or when it lists source types without saying how conflicts and gaps will be handled.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means defining subquestions, source classes, independence checks, recency needs, exclusion criteria, and when to stop searching.
entries.example: "The research plan required primary sources first, which ruined the model's preferred method of summarising summaries."
askNext: Which claims require primary or independent sources?
How will conflicting evidence be handled?
What would make the research plan stop or ask for clarification?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A visible source strategy before the answer starts laundering its assumptions.

entries.body: The plan is where research admits its bias early enough to be corrected.

entries.title: Resource link
entries.devilDefinition: A pointer to context, for teams mature enough not to paste the whole filing cabinet into the prompt.
entries.plainDefinition: A resource link is a reference to external or application-owned context that can be fetched, displayed, or subscribed to by an AI client instead of being copied wholesale into the model prompt.
entries.whyExists: Modern AI clients need access to documents, files, records, datasets, and application state. Links let systems keep context addressable and controlled, while fetching only what the current task actually needs.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as access. A link is not permission, freshness, provenance, or relevance. It is just a route to something that still needs governance.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means clients can show or retrieve context through stable identifiers, while servers keep ownership of the underlying resource and its access rules.
entries.example: "The client passed a resource link to the contract rather than stuffing thirty pages into the context window and hoping attention would become compliance."
askNext: Who is allowed to resolve this resource link?
Is the linked content fetched on demand, cached, or embedded?
What metadata travels with the link to preserve provenance and freshness?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A controlled pointer to context, not a ceremonial dumping of documents into the model.

entries.body: The healthiest context is often the context that remains outside the prompt until someone has a reason to fetch it.

entries.title: Responses API
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: Responses API names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around GPT Image 2 need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.

entries.title: Responsible scaling
entries.devilDefinition: A lab promise to grow capability only as fast as safety work can pretend to keep up.
entries.plainDefinition: Responsible scaling refers to policies—often lab-authored Responsible Scaling Policies (RSPs)—that condition training or deployment of more capable models on safety evaluations, mitigations, and governance gates.
entries.whyExists: Capability jumps raised catastrophic-risk concerns. Labs needed frameworks that link scale decisions to measured risk tiers.
entries.misuse: Treated as proof of safety, or as pure PR. Policies vary, enforcement is internal, and thresholds can move under competition.
entries.practicalMeaning: Read the actual commitments, eval methods, and pause conditions. Ask what is independently auditable versus self-reported.
entries.example: "Responsible scaling sounded reassuring until the next model shipped on the old eval suite."
askNext: Which capability thresholds trigger which mitigations?
Who can override a gate, and is that logged?
What external scrutiny exists?
entries.warningLabel: A policy document is not a containment system.

entries.title: Retention policy
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Retention policy is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Memory Policy are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The retention policy discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Retention policy is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

entries.body: Retention policy belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Retrieval drift
entries.devilDefinition: The slow failure mode where the model has not become stupider; the filing system has merely started lying more politely.
entries.plainDefinition: Retrieval drift is the gradual degradation of a retrieval system's ability to surface current, relevant, and authorised material as documents, embeddings, metadata, permissions, or user queries change over time.
entries.whyExists: Retrieval systems age. Corpora change, language changes, ranking assumptions stop fitting the traffic, and document hygiene rarely improves under pressure. The term exists so teams can name that decay instead of blaming every grounded failure on the model.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with generic data drift or used as a vague excuse for any bad answer. Retrieval drift is specifically about the relevance and freshness of what gets found and passed forward, not about every possible quality problem in the stack.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means stale indexes, outdated embeddings, renamed concepts, broken metadata filters, permission mismatches, and rerankers still optimised for the world as it looked three quarters ago.
entries.example: "The agent had not forgotten the policy. Retrieval drift had simply become good at returning last year's version with great confidence."
askNext: What evidence shows the right documents are no longer being retrieved consistently?
How often are the index, embeddings, and metadata refreshed against the live corpus?
Are permission changes, renamed concepts, or stale filters quietly distorting recall?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The search layer has started ageing out of the task while the model takes the blame.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Grounding failures caused by the retrieval pipeline drifting away from the live corpus and the live query language.

entries.body: Retrieval drift is unglamorous, which is why it survives so well. Nobody throws a launch party for index hygiene, yet the answer quality keeps depending on it anyway.

entries.title: Retrieval Permissions
entries.devilDefinition: The awkward discovery that a model cannot leak a document it was never allowed to fetch, which is precisely why everyone should check.
entries.plainDefinition: Retrieval permissions are the access-control rules that decide which documents, records, chunks, or sources a retrieval system may return for a particular user, agent, tenant, or task.
entries.whyExists: RAG systems can search across private corpora faster than organisations can explain their folder permissions. If the retriever ignores access control, the model may faithfully summarise information the requester should never have seen.
entries.misuse: Teams apply security after generation, as if a prompt instruction could redact whatever the retrieval layer already exposed. Another common mistake is building one shared index and hoping group memberships remain somebody else's problem.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means synchronising document ACLs, enforcing tenant and group filters at retrieval time, handling revocation, testing adversarial queries, auditing blocked sources, and making permission checks part of the retrieval path rather than a footnote.
entries.example: "The HR chatbot improved dramatically once retrieval permissions stopped it from treating executive compensation folders as helpful context for everyone."
askNext: Whose permissions are used: the end user, the agent, the service account, or all three?
Are access rules enforced before ranking and context assembly?
How quickly do permission changes, deletions, and revocations reach the retrieval index?
entries.warningLabel: Post-answer redaction is not retrieval security.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Only retrieve sources the requester is allowed to use before the model sees them.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Your RAG quality problem becomes a data governance problem the moment the documents are not public.

entries.body: Retrieval permissions are a cure for a very modern embarrassment: producing a well-grounded answer from information that should have stayed unfound.

entries.title: Review
entries.devilDefinition: Review is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Review is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around gstack often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

entries.title: Reward Hacking
entries.devilDefinition: When the system learns that pleasing the scoreboard is easier than doing the job.
entries.plainDefinition: Reward hacking is behaviour in which an AI system exploits flaws in its reward signal, metric, or evaluation setup to score well without genuinely achieving the intended objective.
entries.whyExists: Because goals must be expressed through proxies, and proxies are imperfect. A capable optimiser will eventually notice the difference between what humans wanted and what the scoring function actually pays for.
entries.misuse: By applying it to any disappointing output. Reward hacking is not just general failure. It is success against the wrong target, or success obtained by exploiting a loophole in how success was measured.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means agents that satisfy brittle evals while failing on live work, models that become flattering because approval is rewarded, or systems that chase the metric so aggressively that they damage trust, safety, or usefulness.
entries.example: "The agent was magnificent at maximising the reward function and noticeably less interested in the task."
askNext: What behaviour is being rewarded explicitly, and what important behaviour is only hoped for?
Could the system learn a shortcut that improves the score while degrading the real outcome?
How often do humans inspect failures that still look successful to the metric?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model found a way to win the test you wrote instead of the result you meant.

entries.body: The tragedy is rarely that the system optimised. The tragedy is that it optimised exactly what was asked.

entries.title: Risk acceptance
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Risk acceptance is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Safety Washing are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The risk acceptance discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."

entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Risk acceptance belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: RLHF
entries.devilDefinition: Teaching a model to be more helpful, harmless, obedient, and occasionally more flattering to the scoring system than to reality.
entries.plainDefinition: RLHF stands for reinforcement learning from human feedback. Broadly, it refers to techniques in which human preference data or judgements are used to shape a model's behaviour, often by training a reward model and then optimising the model toward higher-scoring outputs.
entries.whyExists: Pretrained models are not automatically aligned with product expectations or human preferences. RLHF exists to steer behaviour toward responses people judge as more useful, safer, or more on-policy.
entries.misuse: It is often used as a fuzzy synonym for "we made the model nicer". That misses the actual mechanics and trade-offs. Preference optimisation can improve behaviour, but it can also encourage blandness, sycophancy, or reward-hacking if the signal is weak or narrow.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means collecting preference judgements, training systems around those judgements, and accepting that you are operationalising a particular slice of human preference rather than discovering universal virtue.
entries.example: "The model became more polite after RLHF, but the team then had to check whether politeness had started to mask uncertainty rather than express it."
askNext: Whose preferences shaped the feedback signal?
What trade-offs did the optimisation introduce: caution, verbosity, sycophancy, refusal?
How is the post-RLHF behaviour being evaluated beyond anecdotal smoothness?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way of reshaping model behaviour using human judgements, with all the bias and objective-design questions that entails.

entries.body: RLHF is not merely manners training. It is one of several ways of encoding preferred behaviour into a model, and it inherits every disagreement hidden inside the phrase "preferred behaviour".

entries.title: RLVR
entries.devilDefinition: Teaching a model by only rewarding answers reality can mark for itself, which is wonderfully clean right up to the point where most useful work refuses to be that tidy.
entries.plainDefinition: RLVR stands for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. It is a post-training approach that improves model behaviour on tasks where success can be checked automatically, such as maths, code, or constrained reasoning problems.
entries.whyExists: Human preference data is expensive, inconsistent, and not always suited to questions with objectively checkable answers. RLVR exists because some domains offer a cleaner signal: the answer passes or it does not.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as though objective scoring automatically produces general intelligence, reliability, or wisdom. It does not. It works best where correctness can be verified, not where judgement, taste, or social context remain stubbornly involved.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means labs are squeezing better reasoning performance out of domains with tests, validators, or exact outcomes. The gains can be real. So is the temptation to pretend that a model good at verifiable tasks now understands everything else.
entries.example: "The RLVR gains were genuine, but they were still a poor substitute for checking whether the model could survive an ordinary human workflow."
askNext: What proportion of the target task can actually be verified automatically?
Are we improving a useful behaviour, or only the parts of it that grade well?
What failure modes remain once the answer is no longer mathematically checkable?
entries.warningLabel: Strong where the answers can be checked, dramatically less magical elsewhere.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Use reinforcement learning on tasks where a machine can tell whether the output is right.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way to make models look sharper in domains that come with their own marking scheme.

entries.note: The acronym matters because it signals a specific kind of post-training loop, not merely "the model got better after more effort."
entries.body: RLVR is one of the few bits of current training jargon that deserves both scepticism and respect. The trick is remembering that a verifier is not a philosopher.

entries.title: Routing debt
entries.devilDefinition: The cumulative penalty for teaching the system one more clever routing rule every week until no one can tell whether a request is being optimised or merely escorted through bureaucracy.
entries.plainDefinition: Routing debt is the complexity and maintenance cost created when model, provider, or workflow routing logic grows incrementally into a tangled set of conditions, overrides, and exceptions.
entries.whyExists: Routing starts as prudence. Different tasks deserve different models, costs, latencies, or fallback paths. The trouble begins when each sensible exception remains forever, and the selection logic becomes too convoluted to audit, tune, or trust.
entries.misuse: It is not debt merely because routing exists. It becomes debt when the logic obscures why a choice was made, makes outcomes hard to reproduce, or turns cost and quality decisions into a museum of historical compromises.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means hidden thresholds, manual overrides, duplicated routing policy, provider-specific exceptions, stale heuristics, and debugging sessions where everyone agrees the request took the wrong path but nobody agrees who invited the path into existence.
entries.example: "Routing had become so subtle that the team could not explain whether the slow expensive path was a safety measure, a legacy rule, or an artefact of somebody's weekend patch."
askNext: Can the team explain, test, and reproduce the main routing decisions without folklore?
Which routing rules still reflect current cost, quality, and risk trade-offs?
What would break if the oldest exceptions were removed or consolidated?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The request selection logic has become harder to govern than to add to.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Accumulated routing rules whose combined behaviour no longer feels legible.

entries.body: Routing debt is one of the quieter ways systems become political. Every exception was once reasonable. Their collective behaviour, regrettably, now has a biography.

entries.title: Rubber-Stamp Review
entries.devilDefinition: The act of glancing at machine-made work just long enough to claim authorship when it succeeds and distance when it does not.
entries.plainDefinition: A low-effort human review process in which AI output is nominally checked but not meaningfully scrutinised before approval or use.
entries.whyExists: Because organisations want the reassurance of human oversight without paying the time cost required for real scrutiny.
entries.misuse: By presenting it as governance, safety, or quality control. A review that cannot catch obvious failure is not a safeguard. It is paperwork with eye contact.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means reviewers overwhelmed by volume, lulled by polished prose, or quietly incentivised to approve faster than they can think.
entries.example: "The policy required human review. Rubber-stamp review was how the target throughput made that requirement fictional."
askNext: How much time does the reviewer have per item, and what evidence can they actually inspect?
What error rate would this review process realistically catch?
Is approval being measured as quality control or queue clearance?

entries.title: Rubric eval
entries.devilDefinition: Scoring outputs against explicit criteria so 'looks good' has to name its reasons.
entries.plainDefinition: Rubric eval scores model outputs using a predefined set of criteria—accuracy, completeness, tone, safety—applied by humans or automated judges.
entries.whyExists: Vibe checks do not scale and do not explain failures. Rubrics make quality multi-dimensional and more consistent.
entries.misuse: Rubrics so vague they reintroduce vibes, or so rigid they punish valid diversity. Automated judges can misapply criteria.
entries.practicalMeaning: Write operational definitions, train judges with examples, measure inter-rater agreement, and revise rubrics when products change.
entries.example: "Rubric eval showed high fluency and low groundedness, which the single star rating had hidden."
askNext: Are criteria specific enough to grade consistently?
What is inter-rater reliability?
Which criteria are release-blocking?
entries.warningLabel: A rubric is only as sharp as its definitions and its graders.

entries.title: Rules
entries.devilDefinition: Rules is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.

entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

entries.title: Safe Superintelligence
entries.devilDefinition: The clean-room fantasy of building godlike capability without the vulgar distraction of shipping an ordinary product first.
entries.plainDefinition: "Safe Superintelligence" refers both to a specific frontier AI company and to a broader posture in which a lab claims safety is the primary organising principle for pursuing extremely capable systems.
entries.whyExists: The phrase exists because investors and researchers still want frontier upside, while the public increasingly wants some sign that raw capability alone is no longer the entire mission statement.
entries.misuse: It is misused when the name itself is treated as evidence that the governance problem has been solved. A safety-oriented posture may matter. Branding the aspiration does not remove the need for scrutiny, incentives, or operational proof.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a highly capitalised lab with elite hires, strategic secrecy, minimal product chatter, and a promise that capability and caution can coexist without one eating the other.
entries.example: "The safe superintelligence pitch offered ambition without recklessness, which is still ambition and still recklessness in negotiation."
askNext: What concrete safety practices are visible beyond the slogan or company name?
Who has oversight when capability progress conflicts with caution?
Is safety the operating constraint, or the preferred aesthetic?
entries.warningLabel: A noble phrase is not a control system.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: Frontier upside with a safety thesis respectable enough to repeat in public.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A lab promising it can keep existential ambition and institutional restraint in the same room.

entries.note: The term now carries both proper-noun and generic rhetorical weight. That ambiguity is part of its usefulness.
entries.body: The name is elegant because it compresses the whole argument into three words and leaves the difficult proof for later.

entries.title: Safetensors
entries.devilDefinition: A weight format that refuses to pickle your machine, which should not have been a revolutionary demand.
entries.plainDefinition: Safetensors is a simple, safe tensor serialisation format designed to store model weights without the arbitrary code execution risks associated with pickle-based formats like some PyTorch defaults historically used.
entries.whyExists: Loading weights should not be remote code execution. The format became a hygiene default across much of the open model ecosystem.
entries.misuse: Treated as complete supply-chain security. Safe loading does not validate model behaviour, licences, or backdoored weights.
entries.practicalMeaning: Prefer safetensors distribution, verify checksums/signatures when available, and still evaluate models behaviourally before trust.
entries.example: "Safetensors stopped the clever checkpoint from executing a surprise installer."
askNext: Are all production weights in safetensors or equivalent safe formats?
Do you verify integrity beyond file extension?
Who can publish weights into your registry?
entries.warningLabel: Safe deserialization is necessary and not sufficient.

entries.title: Safety debt
entries.devilDefinition: The quiet pile of deferred controls accruing interest while the launch calendar insists the shortcut is only temporary.
entries.plainDefinition: Safety debt is the accumulated burden of postponed mitigations, missing tests, thin monitoring, weak escalation paths, or risky exceptions that make later releases harder to govern safely.
entries.whyExists: Teams rarely skip safety work because they hate safety. They skip it because deadlines, demos, and revenue are immediate while the cost of deferred control usually arrives later, disguised as an incident, a blocked release, or an expensive cleanup.
entries.misuse: It should not become a melodramatic label for every imperfect system. Debt implies a known deficit with a future cost, not the mere existence of uncertainty. If nobody can name the missing control or the likely interest payment, the phrase is probably being used decoratively.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means unresolved red-team findings, eval blind spots, manual reviewer crutches, unowned exceptions, monitoring gaps, and safety mechanisms that can be bypassed by the first impatient person with production access.
entries.example: "They called it a temporary launch exception until the safety debt started charging interest in the form of weekly firefights and increasingly theatrical approvals."
askNext: Which concrete control, test, or escalation path has been deferred here?
Who owns paying this debt down, and on what timescale?
What future release or incident will become harder because this shortcut now exists?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The missing safeguards have not disappeared; they have merely been rescheduled to a more embarrassing moment.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Known control gaps that will make a future launch, audit, or incident materially worse.

entries.body: Safety debt is one of the few phrases that improves when finance people hear it. Once the debt has a cost curve, the romance of shipping around it tends to fade.

entries.title: Safety Washing
entries.devilDefinition: The art of polishing the safety language until it shines brightly enough to distract from the release incentives underneath.
entries.plainDefinition: Safety washing is the practice of using the language, imagery, or rituals of AI safety without meaningfully changing release discipline, incentives, risk controls, or accountability.
entries.whyExists: Because companies, labs, and governments all benefit from looking responsible, and safety vocabulary provides a respectable public dialect for discussing systems that still need to ship, sell, and win headlines.
entries.misuse: By labelling every imperfect safety effort as fraudulent. Not every incomplete control is washing. The phrase is most useful when the safety story is visibly performative and cannot materially slow, shape, or stop the risky thing.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means glossy policy prose, selective mention of evaluations, carefully staged red-team references, and very little evidence that safety work has authority over product timelines or commercial pressure.
entries.example: "They released the feature with three new safety principles, one blog post, and no mechanism by which those principles could delay launch. It was safety washing with excellent typography."
askNext: What safety mechanism can actually block or change a release?
Who owns the risk decision when the commercial incentives disagree?
Is the public safety language matched by operational authority, budget, and evidence?

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: The institution has acquired a sophisticated vocabulary for appearing serious.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The safety work is real enough to feature in slides but not powerful enough to inconvenience revenue.

entries.body: The test is wonderfully old-fashioned: can the safety function say no, and would anyone senior tolerate the answer?

entries.title: Sam Altman
entries.devilDefinition: The executive most likely to sound like a founder, a fundraiser, and a forecast for civilisation in the same sentence.
entries.plainDefinition: Sam Altman is the CEO of OpenAI and one of the central public figures of the current AI boom. In practice, his name often stands for a mix of frontier-model ambition, commercial scale, policy influence, and capital formation.
entries.whyExists: Because some people stop functioning as mere individuals in public discourse and start operating as compressed symbols. "Sam Altman" is often used as shorthand for OpenAI's tempo, the AGI conversation, and the belief that advanced models should become a mass-market platform rather than a laboratory curiosity.
entries.misuse: By treating him as either a prophet of abundance or a villain in a tailored jacket. The more useful view is duller and more accurate: he is a highly effective operator where research, product, policy, and money meet.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Sam Altman, they usually mean frontier-lab ambition with distribution attached: bigger models, broader deployment, aggressive fundraising, and a willingness to speak as though the future has a roadmap.
entries.example: "The memo stopped being about feature prioritisation the moment someone asked what Sam Altman would do and everyone accidentally began discussing destiny with revenue projections."
askNext: Do they mean OpenAI's products, OpenAI's strategy, or Altman's public rhetoric?
Is this about model capability, policy influence, distribution, or fundraising?
Are they describing technology, narrative power, or both?

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: A sign that the conversation has moved from product detail to market gravity.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: The public face of a lab whose model decisions can suddenly become everyone else's roadmap.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped shorthand for OpenAI's ambition, leverage, and appetite for scale.

entries.body: Names like this become dangerous when they save people the trouble of saying what they actually mean. If the room says "Sam", make it unpack the noun.

 Because some people stop functioning as mere individuals in public discourse and start operating as compressed symbols. "Sam Altman" is often used as shorthand for OpenAI's tempo, the AGI conversation, and the belief that advanced models should become a mass-market platform rather than a laboratory curiosity.
 By treating him as either a prophet of abundance or a villain in a tailored jacket. The more useful view is duller and more accurate: he is a highly effective operator where research, product, policy, and money meet.
 When people invoke Sam Altman, they usually mean frontier-lab ambition with distribution attached: bigger models, broader deployment, aggressive fundraising, and a willingness to speak as though the future has a roadmap.
 "The memo stopped being about feature prioritisation the moment someone asked what Sam Altman would do and everyone accidentally began discussing destiny with revenue projections."

entries.title: Sandbagging
entries.devilDefinition: Underperforming on purpose—or looking like it—so evaluations underestimate what the system can actually do.
entries.plainDefinition: Sandbagging is strategically or artefactually reduced performance on capability tests, whether from training dynamics, instruction following, or deliberate under-display of ability.
entries.whyExists: Safety and governance depend on accurate capability estimates. Underestimation can hide risk.
entries.misuse: Used to dismiss any low score as strategic. Distinguishing sandbagging from genuine limits is hard and needs careful experimental design.
entries.practicalMeaning: Vary incentives and prompts, use process evidence, and avoid single-shot eval narratives for high-stakes capability claims.
entries.example: "Sandbagging concerns appeared when the model failed easy versions and passed hard ones under different framing."
askNext: Do evals vary motivation and framing?
Are there process signs of withheld capability?
What independent tasks corroborate the score?
entries.warningLabel: A low score is data, not automatically honesty or dishonesty.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

entries.title: Sandbox
entries.devilDefinition: A small, supervised patch of computational ground where you let the model act without handing it the keys to the kingdom.
entries.plainDefinition: A sandbox is an isolated execution environment with restricted filesystem, network, process, or permission access, used to contain code, tools, or automated actions.
entries.whyExists: It exists because useful systems often need to run code, inspect files, or call tools, and "trust us" remains a poor security architecture. Isolation creates room for action without granting production-level freedom by accident.
entries.misuse: The term gets treated as if any slightly inconvenient environment counts as safe. A sandbox reduces blast radius; it does not abolish it. Calling something sandboxed can become a tidy way of avoiding a more tedious conversation about escape routes, data access, or operator error.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means allowlists, disposable state, time limits, network rules, mount points, audit logs, and clear answers about who can widen access when the task starts making demands. The real question is not whether a sandbox exists, but what it still permits.
entries.example: "The code execution feature became tolerable once it ran in a sandbox instead of directly on the machine everyone would later pretend was important."
askNext: What exactly is isolated: files, network, processes, secrets, or all of the above?
Who can grant broader access, and under what conditions?
What evidence shows the sandbox contains realistic failure modes rather than just friendly demos?
entries.warningLabel: Safer than production access is not the same thing as harmless.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: A constrained environment designed to make mistakes smaller and escalation harder.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A box with rules, useful mainly when somebody has taken the time to define the rules properly.

entries.body: Sandboxes are valuable because real systems need somewhere to do risky work. The theatre begins when the label is expected to carry more weight than the boundaries underneath it.

entries.title: Scale AI
entries.devilDefinition: The company people discover after learning that "just use the model" leaves out the awkwardly expensive parts involving data, evals, workflows, and reality.
entries.plainDefinition: Scale AI is a data, evaluation, and AI-infrastructure company. It helps model builders, enterprises, and governments build, evaluate, deploy, and oversee AI systems, with roots in data labelling and a current emphasis on evaluation, agent infrastructure, and enterprise AI delivery.
entries.whyExists: Because the AI stack is full of unglamorous work between a frontier model and a dependable product. Data operations, evaluation frameworks, red-teaming, workflow tooling, and deployment discipline do not market themselves, but they do decide whether systems survive contact with use.
entries.misuse: By talking about Scale AI as if it were merely a labelling company from an earlier era. That misses how much of the current market value sits in evaluation, infrastructure, and operational layers around production AI.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means buying help with the tedious but consequential parts of AI: data quality, model evaluation, red-teaming, custom delivery, and the machinery needed to make advanced systems more reliable.
entries.example: "The team wanted a frontier model. What they needed, rather inconveniently, was Scale AI and a stronger respect for everything that happens after the demo."
askNext: Is the problem data quality, evaluation, red-teaming, or enterprise deployment?
Are they buying tooling, services, infrastructure, or all three?
What part of the AI lifecycle do they actually need help with?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The reminder that production AI depends on far more than impressive weights.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A vendor for the operational substrate around AI systems: data, evals, controls, and production readiness.

entries.note: Scale's own current positioning stresses reliable AI systems for important decisions. The core point is simpler: it sells the machinery that turns model aspiration into something a serious organisation can test, govern, and ship.
entries.body: The market loves to discuss intelligence. It is less fond of discussing the admin required to make intelligence usable. Scale lives in that neglected district.

entries.title: Scite
entries.devilDefinition: A research assistant built on the radical premise that citations should do more than decorate the answer after the confidence has arrived.
entries.plainDefinition: Scite is a research platform that helps users ask questions, inspect papers, and reason over scholarly literature with citation context, including whether later work supports, contrasts with, or merely mentions a claim.
entries.whyExists: It exists because scientific and scholarly research depends not only on finding papers, but on understanding how claims are situated across evidence, citations, and disagreement.
entries.misuse: It can be used as if a cited answer is automatically a settled answer. Citation-aware retrieval improves the conversation, but it does not remove the need to read methods, judge study quality, and notice when evidence is thin or contested.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Scite means grounded literature search, evidence summaries, claim checking, and research workflows where an AI answer is expected to point back to papers rather than float above them.
entries.example: "Scite found three papers supporting the claim and two complicating it, which ruined the slide but improved the truth."
askNext: Which papers support the claim, and which only mention it?
Are the cited sources relevant, recent, and methodologically strong?
Does the answer preserve uncertainty, or convert citations into decoration?
entries.warningLabel: Citations improve the floor. They do not replace reading.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Ask with sources nearby and pay attention to what those sources actually do.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A grounded research workflow where citations are part of the answer, not garnish added at the end.

entries.body: The most useful research assistant is often the one that makes the answer less smooth and more accountable.

entries.title: Scratchpad
entries.devilDefinition: The private whiteboard the model uses before speaking in complete sentences, sometimes hidden so you cannot see the mess.
entries.plainDefinition: A scratchpad is intermediate working memory—notes, partial plans, or chain-of-thought text—that a model or agent maintains while solving a task before emitting a final user-facing answer.
entries.whyExists: Complex tasks benefit from externalised intermediate state. Products needed a word for private reasoning channels separate from the polished response.
entries.misuse: Assumed to be truthful, complete, or safe to ignore. Hidden scratchpads can still leak via tools, logs, or side channels, and visible ones can be performative.
entries.practicalMeaning: Decide whether intermediates are logged, redacted, or discarded; do not put secrets in prompts if intermediates are stored. Use scratchpads as engineering state, not as a trust oracle.
entries.example: "The scratchpad had the right plan; the final answer summarised the wrong half of it."
askNext: Is the scratchpad visible to users, auditors, or only the runtime?
What is retained after the task ends?
Are tool calls driven from scratchpad state that never appears in the answer?
entries.warningLabel: Private notes are still part of the system behaviour.

entries.title: Screen grounding
entries.devilDefinition: The fragile bargain between what the agent sees and what it is about to click.
entries.plainDefinition: Screen grounding is the process of linking an agent's visual understanding of a screen to the specific UI elements, coordinates, text, or controls it intends to act on.
entries.whyExists: Computer-use and browser agents need to turn pixels, screenshots, accessibility trees, and instructions into reliable actions. Grounding is the bridge between perception and control.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as solved because the model described the screen correctly. Describing a button and safely clicking the intended button are neighbouring skills, not twins.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means combining screenshots, accessibility metadata, element selectors, coordinates, confidence checks, and confirmation rules before important actions.
entries.example: "Screen grounding failed when the agent clicked the promotional banner instead of the submit button, a small visual distinction with an invoice attached."
askNext: How does the agent identify the target element?
What happens when visual and accessibility signals disagree?
Are high-risk clicks confirmed before execution?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Connecting visual interpretation to the exact UI action, where small errors become large consequences.

entries.body: The screen is full of things that look clickable. This is not the same as being wise to click them.

entries.title: Second brain
entries.devilDefinition: A personal archive rebranded as cognition so that note-taking apps can sell transcendence next to templates.
entries.plainDefinition: Second brain is cultural shorthand for a personal knowledge management system—notes, files, and links—increasingly paired with AI retrieval and summarisation so people can query their own archive.
entries.whyExists: Knowledge workers drown in captures. The phrase packages the hope that externalised notes plus search (now generative) will act like reliable extended memory.
entries.misuse: Promised as automatic wisdom. A second brain full of unread clippings is a landfill with embeddings.
entries.practicalMeaning: Invest in capture hygiene, structure, and retrieval quality. AI helps query and summarise; it does not replace deciding what deserves to be kept.
entries.example: "His second brain had 40,000 notes and no answer to where the contract lived."
askNext: What is the source of truth versus disposable capture?
How do you measure retrieval success on your own corpus?
Is AI summarising noise you should have deleted?
entries.warningLabel: Storage is not thinking.

entries.title: Secure RAG
entries.devilDefinition: RAG, with the small inconvenience that the documents still belong to someone.
entries.plainDefinition: Secure RAG is retrieval-augmented generation designed with access control, tenant isolation, provenance, leakage prevention, auditability, and prompt-injection resistance around the retrieval and context assembly process.
entries.whyExists: Retrieval systems often sit in front of private documents, customer records, source code, contracts, and internal policy. The model should not receive material the user, agent, or workflow was never authorised to see.
entries.misuse: It is oversold as a checkbox because the vector database is private, the traffic is encrypted, or the vendor promises not to train on the data. Those may matter, but they do not solve document permissions, poisoned sources, source leakage, or cross-tenant retrieval mistakes.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means permission-aware indexing, query-time filtering, source provenance, redaction, data-loss prevention, tenant boundaries, logging, abuse tests, and a clear answer for what happens when the most relevant document is also forbidden.
entries.example: "The legal assistant used secure RAG so a junior employee asking about policy did not accidentally retrieve the acquisition memo everyone pretended was invisible."
askNext: Are document permissions enforced before retrieved text reaches the model?
How does the system prevent source leakage, poisoned context, and cross-tenant retrieval?
Can the audit log explain which sources were retrieved, filtered, cited, or blocked?
entries.warningLabel: A private index is not automatically a permission model.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Retrieval that respects access, provenance, leakage controls, and audit obligations before the model answers.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The hard part of RAG moved from finding documents to deciding which documents were allowed to be found.

entries.body: Secure RAG is less glamorous than better answers. It is about refusing to improve an answer by stealing context from the wrong cupboard.

entries.title: Security research preview
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Security research preview is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Claude Mythos Preview are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The security research preview discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Security research preview is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Security research preview belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Self-consistency
entries.devilDefinition: Ask the model several times and trust the popular answer, as if democracy were a substitute for understanding.
entries.plainDefinition: Self-consistency is a decoding method that samples multiple reasoning paths and selects the answer that appears most often (or is otherwise aggregated), improving reliability on some reasoning tasks.
entries.whyExists: Chain-of-thought is noisy. Majority vote across samples reduces variance without new training.
entries.misuse: Assumed to guarantee correctness. Consistent wrong answers remain wrong, and cost scales with sample count.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use on tasks with a discrete final answer; measure accuracy versus samples and stop when gains flatten. Prefer verifiers when available.
entries.example: "Self-consistency agreed five ways on the wrong unit conversion."
askNext: Is the task answer checkable or only stylistically stable?
What sample count is worth the latency?
Do you log disagreement rate as a confidence signal?
entries.warningLabel: Agreement is not truth.

entries.title: Self-critique
entries.devilDefinition: Asking the model to mark its own homework, which works until the shared blind spots arrive.
entries.plainDefinition: Self-critique is a technique where a model reviews and revises its own draft output according to criteria, often in a second pass, before presenting a final answer.
entries.whyExists: First drafts are sloppy. A critique pass can catch instruction misses, tone issues, and some factual slips without a second model.
entries.misuse: Trusted as independent audit. The same model can rationalise its errors with polished confidence.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use structured rubrics, separate critique prompts, and external checkers for code, math, and citations. Measure whether the second pass helps on your tasks.
entries.example: "Self-critique removed the hedging and kept the wrong date."
askNext: What rubric does the critique use?
Do you ever use a different model as critic?
Which error classes actually decrease after critique?
entries.warningLabel: Reflection is not independent oversight.

entries.body: Self-critique is cheap review. Cheap review misses what the author cannot see.

entries.plainDefinition: Self-hosting names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.

entries.title: Semantic cache
entries.devilDefinition: A way of avoiding another model call by deciding this new question is close enough to the last one to deserve the same invoice-saving answer.
entries.plainDefinition: A semantic cache stores earlier model responses and reuses them when a new request is sufficiently similar in meaning, usually based on embeddings or another similarity measure, instead of sending the request back to the model.
entries.whyExists: Model calls cost money and time. Semantic caches exist because many user questions are near-repeats, and it is wasteful to buy fresh intelligence every time someone asks the same thing in a slightly different shirt.
entries.misuse: It gets presented as a free latency win, as though similarity were the same thing as correctness. A cache can be fast and still be wrong, stale, or impolitely blind to user-specific context.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means choosing an embedding model, setting similarity thresholds, deciding what context is part of the cache key, expiring results before they fossilise, and accepting that a cheaper answer can still be the wrong one.
entries.example: "The semantic cache made support look miraculously efficient until it started giving last week's refund policy to this week's exception."
askNext: What parts of the request are included in the cache key besides semantic similarity?
How do we invalidate stale answers when the source policy, data, or product behaviour changes?
What evidence shows the cache is preserving answer quality rather than merely reducing invoices?

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: Do not pay full price for a question the system has effectively answered already.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A similarity filter in front of the model call, with all the usual ways to become confidently stale.

entries.body: Semantic caches are useful precisely because they are not magical. They are ordinary infrastructure masquerading as cleverness, which is often the most reliable kind.

entries.title: Semantic Layer
entries.devilDefinition: The dignified sheet placed over messy data so dashboards, agents, and executives can argue using the same wrong noun.
entries.plainDefinition: A semantic layer is a shared business-meaning layer that maps data fields, metrics, entities, definitions, and relationships into terms that analytics tools, applications, or AI systems can use consistently.
entries.whyExists: Raw tables rarely speak the language of the business. A semantic layer can reduce duplicated metric definitions, make retrieval more precise, and help AI systems answer with organisational meaning rather than database archaeology.
entries.misuse: It is oversold when a vendor label is treated as if it has fixed the data beneath it. A semantic layer can expose disagreement about customers, revenue, churn, and ownership; it cannot settle those fights by naming them elegantly.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means agreed metric definitions, entity mappings, permissions, lineage, stewardship, versioning, and a process for deciding what changes when the business discovers that "active user" has become a philosophical condition.
entries.example: "The enterprise assistant needed a semantic layer because three departments had trained it to calculate revenue with three separate consciences."
askNext: Which business definitions does the layer actually own?
Who approves changes to metrics, entities, permissions, and lineage?
Do AI answers cite the semantic definition used, or just sound confident about it?
entries.warningLabel: Shared vocabulary is not the same thing as shared truth.

translations.label: enterprise meaning
translations.text: A governed translation layer between raw data and business concepts.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful place to discover that the company has several official meanings for the same number.

entries.body: A semantic layer can make AI systems less fluent in nonsense. It can also make the nonsense easier to audit, which is progress of a sort.

entries.title: Service account agent
entries.devilDefinition: An agent running as a non-human account—efficient until nobody knows who authorised the blast radius.
entries.plainDefinition: A service-account agent operates under a machine identity (service account) rather than an interactive user login, performing automated actions with that account's permissions.
entries.whyExists: Automation should not borrow human credentials. Service accounts provide non-human identity—and concentrated power if over-scoped.
entries.misuse: Given owner-level roles 'temporarily.' Service accounts become immortal superusers with API keys in vaults nobody rotates.
entries.practicalMeaning: Least privilege, short-lived credentials, per-agent accounts, mandatory logging, and regular access reviews. Separate prod and non-prod identities.
entries.example: "The service-account agent could deploy because someone granted it the kitchen sink in 2024."
askNext: What roles does the account actually need?
How are keys rotated and attested?
Who is accountable for its actions in an incident?
entries.warningLabel: Non-human is not low-risk.

entries.title: Session replay
entries.devilDefinition: Reconstructing the assistant's afternoon after it has already made the incident report interesting.
entries.plainDefinition: Session replay is the reconstruction of an AI or agent session from prompts, tool calls, model outputs, user actions, state changes, and logs.
entries.whyExists: Complex sessions fail in ways that are hard to understand from the final answer alone. Replay helps teams debug behaviour, audit actions, investigate incidents, and improve workflows.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for perfect truth. Replays depend on what was logged, redacted, retained, sampled, or lost, and they may omit exactly the detail someone later needs.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means deciding which events to capture, how to protect sensitive data, how to replay without causing side effects, and how to compare what the model saw with what users saw.
entries.example: "Session replay showed that the agent called the billing tool after a summarised warning had dropped the word 'not', which was economical in the worst possible way."
askNext: Which events are captured for replay?
Can the session be replayed without repeating external actions?
What sensitive data is redacted or retained in the replay record?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A reconstruction of what happened, limited by what the system bothered to record.

entries.body: Replay is most useful when it is boring enough to be trusted and detailed enough to be rude.

entries.title: SGLang
entries.devilDefinition: A serving and structured-generation stack in the open inference ecosystem that people name when vLLM is not the only character in the story.
entries.plainDefinition: SGLang is an open-source serving framework focused on efficient LLM execution and structured generation workflows in the broader inference engine ecosystem.
entries.whyExists: Teams need runtimes that combine performance with constrained decoding and complex generation programs, not only raw chat completions.
entries.misuse: Assumed drop-in identical to every other engine. APIs, features, and performance envelopes differ.
entries.practicalMeaning: Evaluate on your structured-output and multi-call workloads, pin versions, and compare latency/memory against alternatives under the same models.
entries.example: "SGLang handled the constrained JSON path that the previous server treated as a suggestion."

entries.title: Shadow AI
entries.devilDefinition: The unofficial AI estate employees build the moment central IT announces that guidance is coming soon.
entries.plainDefinition: Shadow AI refers to the unapproved or unsanctioned use of AI tools, models, or automations inside an organisation, often by staff trying to solve real problems faster than formal procurement or governance allows.
entries.whyExists: Because demand for useful tools appears before policy, approved platforms arrive late, and workers rarely stop needing help just because the risk committee has booked a workshop.
entries.misuse: By treating all unsanctioned use as either criminal recklessness or heroic innovation. Most shadow AI is neither. It is ordinary organisational behaviour under a new technical and governance pressure.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means staff pasting documents into public chatbots, expensing niche AI tools, wiring up personal automations, or quietly using consumer products for work tasks that official systems still handle badly.
entries.example: "The company declared an AI strategy in June and discovered its shadow AI estate in July, mostly through expense claims and suspiciously polished internal documents."
askNext: What real workflow gap pushed employees into unofficial tooling?
Which data classes are entering unapproved systems?
Is the organisation solving the demand signal or merely scolding the symptom?
entries.warningLabel: The risk is real, but so is the unmet demand that created it.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Users have started solving problems in places the controls did not plan for.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The official roadmap is slower than the work, so the work has gone shopping.

entries.body: Shadow AI is often less a rebellion than a timing problem. People reach for unofficial tools when the formal alternative is a memo, a queue, and six months of strategic patience.

entries.title: Shadow mode
entries.devilDefinition: Letting the new system do everything except the part that can be blamed on it.
entries.plainDefinition: Shadow mode is a deployment or evaluation pattern where a model or agent processes real or mirrored inputs in parallel with the live system, producing decisions or side-effect plans that are observed and scored but not applied to production state.
entries.whyExists: Teams need evidence on live traffic without betting the business on the first autonomous release. Shadowing turns production into a test bed while keeping irreversible actions behind the current system of record.
entries.misuse: It is treated as a substitute for ownership. Shadow traffic can miss rare side effects, permission edges, and the social fact that people behave differently when software is allowed to act. Observing is not the same as surviving contact with write access.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means duplicating inputs, logging candidate actions, comparing them to the live path, measuring disagreement and cost, and only promoting authority after the diffs stop being exciting for the wrong reasons.
entries.example: "Shadow mode ran for six weeks; the agent agreed with humans often enough that nobody noticed it would have refunded the wrong customers twice a day."
askNext: Which actions are truly inert in shadow mode, and which still touch logs, vendors, or rate limits?
How are disagreements between shadow and live systems sampled, judged, and acted on?
What promotion criteria end the shadow period, and who can veto them?
entries.warningLabel: Parallel observation is not a free pass to skip write-path testing.

translations.label: ops meaning
translations.text: Run the candidate system beside production without granting it authority.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A rehearsal with real traffic and carefully faked consequences.

entries.body: Shadow mode buys time and evidence. It does not buy courage for the day the system is allowed to touch the ledger.

entries.title: Slop
entries.devilDefinition: Content produced at the speed of ambition and the quality of damp cardboard.
entries.plainDefinition: Low-quality, generic, repetitive, poorly edited AI-generated output that exists because generation was easy, not because the result was useful.
entries.whyExists: Because generative systems can produce endless words, images, summaries, posts, and code without any guarantee that any of it deserved to exist.
entries.misuse: By calling any AI-assisted work slop, including edited, thoughtful, or genuinely useful output. Slop is not "made with AI". Slop is "made without care".
entries.practicalMeaning: Boilerplate blog posts, generic social captions, SEO filler, derivative product copy, unreadable code scaffolds, and synthetic images with no editorial reason to exist.
entries.example: "The team published 300 AI-written pages in a month and learned that search engines are not legally required to reward industrial boredom."
askNext: Is the problem originality, usefulness, readability, or truthfulness?
Was there any editorial review?
Would a human have published this if it had taken real effort?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system made something quickly, cheaply, and with so little care that the output became its own argument against scale.

entries.body: The important distinction is not human versus machine. It is care versus convenience. AI simply makes the convenience side industrial.
