entries.title: Slop Filter
entries.devilDefinition: The sieve installed after the factory discovered it could make mud at scale.
entries.plainDefinition: A slop filter is a moderation, ranking, review, or detection layer used to reduce low-quality AI-generated content in feeds, inboxes, search results, marketplaces, or internal knowledge systems.
entries.whyExists: It exists because generative output is now cheap enough to overwhelm the surfaces that were built for scarcer human effort. Someone has to decide which nearly acceptable thing still deserves to be ignored.
entries.misuse: It gets oversold as a clean detector for "AI content". The harder job is not identifying whether a model helped, but deciding whether the result is useful, truthful, original, relevant, and worth a human's attention.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means classifiers, provenance signals, editorial review, spam rules, ranking demotion, appeal paths, and the awkward admission that platforms now need sanitation for their own growth strategy.
entries.example: "The slop filter caught the obvious generated articles and missed the human-written ones that had achieved the same texture by choice."
askNext: Are we filtering for generation, quality, fraud, repetition, or policy risk?
What happens to useful AI-assisted work that resembles the bad examples?
Who reviews the filter's false positives before creators stop trusting the platform?
entries.warningLabel: Filtering AI slop is a quality problem before it is a detector problem.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A layer that keeps cheap generated filler from taking over a surface.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A quality-control system trying to repair the economics that made the mess profitable.

entries.body: The filter is useful. It is also an invoice for pretending infinite content was a growth strategy rather than a waste-management problem.

entries.title: Slop Firewall
entries.devilDefinition: A perimeter wall built after the internet learnt to manufacture wallpaper and call it publishing.
entries.plainDefinition: A slop firewall is a defensive system that blocks, rate-limits, demotes, or quarantines low-value AI-generated content before it reaches users, search indexes, marketplaces, support queues, or knowledge bases.
entries.whyExists: It exists because filtering after exposure is often too late. Once synthetic filler enters a marketplace, inbox, or search corpus, the cost shifts to readers, reviewers, ranking systems, and every legitimate contributor now forced to prove they are not debris.
entries.misuse: It gets used as security theatre when teams claim protection without defining the blocked surface, review policy, or tolerance for false positives. A firewall with no enforcement plan is just a dashboard wearing boots.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means input controls, reputation signals, rate limits, provenance checks, source allowlists, automated triage, human escalation, and a clear distinction between low-quality generation and legitimate AI-assisted work.
entries.example: "The marketplace added a slop firewall after discovering that a thousand generated notebooks could still be called entrepreneurship."
askNext: Where is the boundary: upload, search, feed ranking, inbox delivery, or training corpus?
Which signals block content, and which only send it for review?
How do legitimate creators appeal when the firewall mistakes efficiency for rubbish?
entries.warningLabel: A firewall that cannot explain its boundary is mostly a mood.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A defensive boundary for keeping generated filler out of valuable surfaces.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A platform admitting that cheap output now needs traffic control.

entries.body: The wall is not there because every generated artefact is bad. It is there because volume has discovered the front door.

entries.title: Slop Flood
entries.devilDefinition: The moment the internet becomes a warehouse of instantly made things nobody had time to want.
entries.plainDefinition: A rapid increase in low-value AI-generated content across platforms, making discovery, trust, and editorial judgement harder.
entries.whyExists: Because one bad article is annoying. Ten million are infrastructure.
entries.misuse: By talking as if volume alone is the issue. The real problem is not just scale. It is the collapse of meaningful filtering.
entries.practicalMeaning: Search results worsen, feeds get repetitive, marketplaces fill with derivative junk, and the burden shifts to ranking systems, curators, and trusted brands.
entries.example: "The model did not ruin the internet by writing one bad paragraph. It ruined it by making an ocean of nearly acceptable ones."
askNext: Flooding where: search, social, marketplaces, internal docs?
Is the content false, generic, redundant, or merely plentiful?
What filters still work?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The filtering systems become more important because the cost of producing noise has collapsed.

entries.body: The flood metaphor matters because it changes the unit of concern. A single weak artefact is an editorial problem. Industrial abundance is a systems problem.
entries.searchText:
Slop Flood synthetic content flood generated content glut Cultural terms Economics and operations of AI discovery quality platforms The moment the internet becomes a warehouse of instantly made things nobody had time to want.
 A rapid increase in low-value AI-generated content across platforms, making discovery, trust, and editorial judgement harder.
 Because one bad article is annoying. Ten million are infrastructure.
 By talking as if volume alone is the issue. The real problem is not just scale. It is the collapse of meaningful filtering.
 Search results worsen, feeds get repetitive, marketplaces fill with derivative junk, and the burden shifts to ranking systems, curators, and trusted brands.
 "The model did not ruin the internet by writing one bad paragraph. It ruined it by making an ocean of nearly acceptable ones."

entries.title: Slopsquatting
entries.devilDefinition: Registering malware under the package name the model just invented, then waiting for a hurried developer to install it with full synthetic confidence.
entries.plainDefinition: Slopsquatting is the practice of publishing malicious packages under names that AI coding tools hallucinate, so developers who trust the suggestion can accidentally install them.
entries.whyExists: It exists because code models generate plausible nonsense, package registries reward being first with a name, and some teams now review dependency suggestions with less suspicion than a spam folder.
entries.misuse: The term can get stretched to mean any bad dependency advice from an AI tool. The more precise problem is the attacker exploiting hallucinated package names by occupying them before a human notices the package never legitimately existed.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means verifying packages before installation, preferring known libraries over plausible ones, locking dependencies properly, and treating AI-suggested imports as a security surface rather than a convenience feature.
entries.example: "The prototype moved fast until someone realised the package only existed because the model had dreamt it and an attacker had been paying attention."
askNext: Was this dependency verified against a real project, maintainer, and source repository?
Are developers allowed to install AI-suggested packages without review?
What controls catch a convincing package name that should never have existed?
entries.warningLabel: Funny name, ordinary breach path.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker monetising the gap between a model's plausible fiction and a developer's missing verification step.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Malicious packages published to catch AI-generated dependency hallucinations before a human notices.

entries.body: It is a tidy example of the modern software stack: one system invents the dependency, another system publishes the trap, and the human appears mainly to confirm the transaction.

entries.title: Small Language Model
entries.devilDefinition: A model celebrated for efficiency, usually by people who have just discovered that not every task requires a digital cathedral.
entries.plainDefinition: A small language model is a comparatively lightweight language model designed to deliver acceptable performance with lower memory, compute, latency, or deployment demands than a frontier-scale model.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many business tasks are narrow, repetitive, and unforgiving about latency or cost. A smaller model can be the better engineering choice when the problem is constrained and the environment matters more than prestige.
entries.misuse: It is oversold in two opposite ways: as proof that frontier models are unnecessary for everything, or as if a compact model can casually replace them on hard reasoning and open-ended generation. Both claims are mostly branding with a benchmark attached.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means cheaper inference, easier on-device or edge deployment, tighter control over predictable tasks, and a shorter list of things the system should be trusted to improvise.
entries.example: "The team finally adopted a small language model after spending six months paying frontier-model prices to classify support emails."
askNext: What task quality is actually required, and is a smaller model good enough on that distribution?
Are latency, privacy, or unit economics the main reason to go smaller?
Which failure modes appear when the task drifts beyond the narrow lane?

translations.label: operations meaning
translations.text: A model sized for the workload rather than the keynote.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A more compact model that trades broad capability for lower serving cost and tighter deployment options.

entries.note: Small is relative. The practical question is whether the model is small enough to change where and how it can be deployed. "Mini" in model branding usually signals this trade-off rather than a magical new class of intelligence.
entries.body: The term is healthiest when it signals discipline. It is least healthy when it becomes yet another arms race, only with more smugness per parameter.

entries.title: SOTA
entries.devilDefinition: The temporary throne awarded to whichever model most recently aced the exam everyone has already started teaching to.
entries.plainDefinition: SOTA, short for "state of the art", means the best reported result on a benchmark, task, or capability at a given moment.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because research and product teams need shorthand for comparative progress, especially when models, methods, or systems improve quickly enough that normal adjectives feel tired by Wednesday.
entries.misuse: It is misused whenever benchmark leadership is treated as proof of broad intelligence, product quality, or durable advantage. Being first on a scoreboard can mean many things, including genuine progress and aggressive test preparation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means marketing leverage, recruiting leverage, investor leverage, and a strong incentive to optimise for published comparisons even when customers care more about price, latency, reliability, and integration.
entries.example: "The model was SOTA on paper and unexpectedly ordinary in production, which is a very popular genre of achievement."
askNext: SOTA on which benchmark, under what conditions, and at what cost?
How well does the result transfer to the workflow customers actually care about?
Is this a durable lead, or a briefly rented headline?

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Best published performance so far, usually with enough caveats to keep lawyers and reviewers busy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful signal that becomes less useful the moment people mistake it for the whole story.

entries.body: The market hears "best". The footnotes hear "for now".

entries.title: Source triangulation
entries.devilDefinition: Asking more than one source before letting the nearest confident page become reality.
entries.plainDefinition: Source triangulation is the practice of checking a claim across multiple independent sources to reduce reliance on a single potentially wrong, biased, stale, or derivative account.
entries.whyExists: AI research workflows are vulnerable to repeating one source's error, especially when other pages quote or summarise it. Triangulation helps separate independent evidence from an echo.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as counting links. Ten pages repeating the same press release are not ten sources; they are a chorus with syndication.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means comparing primary records, independent reporting, technical documentation, filings, datasets, or direct observations before presenting a claim as settled.
entries.example: "Source triangulation showed that the market-size number came from one consultancy slide and nine blogs with excellent copy-and-paste hygiene."
askNext: Are these sources independent, or do they repeat the same origin?
Which source is closest to the underlying fact?
What changes if the strongest source is removed?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Checking whether the evidence is independent, not merely numerous.

entries.body: The internet is very good at making one fact look crowded.

entries.title: Sovereign AI
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that a nation can keep its models, compute, data, and strategic dependence inside the border, or at least inside the speech.
entries.plainDefinition: "Sovereign AI" refers to national or regional efforts to build, host, govern, or control AI infrastructure and capabilities domestically rather than depending entirely on foreign providers.
entries.whyExists: It exists because AI capacity now touches economic leverage, public services, security concerns, language coverage, and industrial policy. Governments and large regional blocs do not enjoy discovering that critical capability is rented from somewhere else.
entries.misuse: It is misused when procurement theatre is presented as autonomy, or when a datacentre announcement stands in for a credible ecosystem of talent, governance, hardware supply, and locally useful models. Flags are cheaper than capability.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means public-private funding, domestic hosting requirements, preferred vendor partnerships, regional data controls, and a long negotiation over what degree of dependence still counts as sovereignty.
entries.example: "The sovereign AI strategy was unveiled with great ceremony and a familiar dependence on imported chips, imported cloud, and imported certainty."
askNext: Which parts of the stack are actually domestic: data, compute, models, governance, or merely the press conference?
What strategic risk is being reduced, and at what cost?
Does the programme serve local languages and institutions, or mainly national branding?
entries.warningLabel: Can describe serious capability-building or ceremonial procurement. Ask which.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: We would prefer not to outsource strategic capacity by default.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A political and economic push for more local control over AI infrastructure, access, and governance.

entries.body: Sovereignty is not a vibe. It is a supply chain, a skills base, a financing plan, and a list of uncomfortable dependencies written down in full.

entries.title: Sovereign cloud
entries.devilDefinition: Keeping the crown jewels local, once everyone agrees where the crown, the jewels, and the admin console are.
entries.plainDefinition: Sovereign cloud is a cloud deployment or operating model designed to satisfy jurisdiction, data residency, operational control, access, security, and regulatory requirements for a country, region, sector, or institution.
entries.whyExists: It exists because governments and regulated organisations want cloud and AI capability without surrendering every sensitive dependency to foreign law, remote operators, opaque support paths, or control planes they cannot meaningfully govern.
entries.misuse: It is misused when a local region, local reseller, or patriotic brand treatment is presented as sovereignty. Data residency helps, but sovereignty also depends on who operates the service, who can access keys and telemetry, which law applies, and who can turn the lights off.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means reviewing legal jurisdiction, local operations, key custody, support access, metadata flows, disaster recovery, vendor dependency, hardware supply, audit rights, and whether AI services inherit the same controls as ordinary storage and compute.
entries.example: "The sovereign cloud proposal kept the data in-country and the control plane just far enough away to require a footnote."
askNext: Where are the data, metadata, keys, logs, support tools, control plane, and operators located?
Which laws, contracts, and technical controls prevent unwanted access or service interruption?
Does the AI layer obey the same sovereignty model as the rest of the cloud?
entries.warningLabel: A local cloud region is not automatically sovereign.

translations.label: governance meaning
translations.text: Cloud services are constrained by location, law, access, operating control, and auditability.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A cloud deal trying to turn jurisdiction and dependency into something procurement can approve.

entries.body: Sovereign cloud is not a flag pinned to a region selector. It is a chain of control, and every weak link tends to have a very polished vendor explanation.

entries.title: Sparse autoencoder
entries.devilDefinition: A tool that tries to untangle the model's mixed thoughts into features humans can name, sometimes successfully.
entries.plainDefinition: A sparse autoencoder (SAE) is a model trained to reconstruct activations using a larger sparse feature basis, used in interpretability to find more monosemantic features than raw neurons provide.
entries.whyExists: Neurons are often polysemantic. SAEs offer a dictionary-learning approach to decompose activations into more interpretable features.
entries.misuse: Treated as complete explanations. Feature dictionaries can be incomplete, unstable, or hard to validate causally.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use SAEs as research instrumentation with causal checks. Do not claim full model understanding from a single dictionary.
entries.example: "The sparse autoencoder found a feature for 'legal tone' and another for 'actually being right,' which did not fire together."
askNext: What reconstruction and sparsity metrics justify the dictionary?
Have features been validated with interventions?
How stable are features across seeds?
entries.warningLabel: A named feature is a hypothesis until interventions agree.

entries.body: Sparse autoencoders are microscopes. Microscopes still need experimental hygiene.

entries.title: Spec-driven development
entries.devilDefinition: Writing the specification carefully enough that agents can implement it, which is to say finally doing the work you always claimed you did.
entries.plainDefinition: Spec-driven development is a workflow where a precise specification, acceptance criteria, or structured plan is the primary artefact guiding human or agent coding work before implementation drifts.
entries.whyExists: Coding agents thrash without clear constraints. Specs reintroduce engineering discipline as machine-readable guidance.
entries.misuse: Producing theatrical specs that are vague where risk lives. Agents will implement the ambiguity faithfully.
entries.practicalMeaning: Keep specs testable, versioned, and small enough to validate. Link acceptance tests to each requirement and update the spec when reality changes.
entries.example: "Spec-driven development worked until the spec said 'handle errors appropriately.'"
askNext: Which acceptance tests lock the spec?
Who updates the spec when the agent finds a contradiction?
Is the spec smaller than the codebase it describes?
entries.warningLabel: Garbage specifications compile into garbage features faster with agents.

entries.body: Spec-driven development makes intent explicit. Agents make weak intent expensive.

entries.title: Speculative Decoding
entries.devilDefinition: The serving trick where one model guesses ahead so a larger one can spend more time being selective than surprised.
entries.plainDefinition: Speculative decoding is an inference technique where a smaller or faster model proposes candidate tokens and a larger model verifies or corrects them, allowing generation to run faster without fully giving up the stronger model's quality.
entries.whyExists: It exists because token-by-token generation is expensive and users do not enjoy watching a costly model think at the speed of ceremony. If a cheaper assistant can draft likely continuations, the main model can move faster through easy stretches.
entries.misuse: It is sometimes described as free speed, as though the extra complexity carries no engineering cost or failure modes. In reality the benefit depends on workload shape, model pairing, and how often the bigger model rejects the draft.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means systems work: pairing models, tuning acceptance rates, measuring latency improvements honestly, and deciding whether the added serving complexity is worth the throughput gain.
entries.example: "They introduced speculative decoding to cut latency, then discovered that fast token streaming is still not the same thing as a good answer."
askNext: What acceptance rate does the draft model achieve on real traffic?
Where does the added serving complexity pay for itself?
Are you improving user experience, system efficiency, or both?

translations.label: infrastructure meaning
translations.text: Spend a little complexity to buy back a noticeable amount of speed.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Using a fast draft model to help a slower model generate tokens with better throughput.

entries.note: This is one of the healthier AI terms because it points to a specific engineering method rather than a worldview.
entries.body: The user sees a snappier model. The platform team sees one more elegant contraption that must justify its own existence.

entries.title: State Compaction
entries.devilDefinition: The solemn act of turning a long confused session into a shorter confused session with headings.
entries.plainDefinition: State compaction is the process of condensing an agent's accumulated context, memory, logs, or working state into a smaller representation that can be carried forward without exceeding context, cost, or latency limits.
entries.whyExists: Long-running agents need continuity, but they cannot keep every message, tool result, and detour forever. Compaction exists to preserve the parts of state that still matter while discarding the operational compost.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as automatic understanding. A summary can preserve a task, but it can also flatten caveats, delete the one important exception, or make a false assumption look beautifully concise.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means deciding what an agent must remember, when to summarise it, how to attach evidence, and whether the compacted state can be audited before it becomes the next model's starting truth.
entries.example: "The coding agent improved once state compaction stopped keeping every failed experiment as if it were a constitutional precedent."
askNext: What facts must survive compaction, and what evidence proves they were not lost?
Does the compacted state distinguish decisions from guesses?
Who reviews the summary when a long-running workflow starts behaving as though yesterday's mistake is policy?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Shrink the working state without accidentally rewriting the job.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A summary that saves tokens, if it does not first save the wrong lesson.

entries.body: State compaction is useful when it is treated as record-keeping, not poetry. The shorter version should earn its authority, preferably before the agent spends another hour obeying it.

entries.title: Structured content
entries.devilDefinition: Information with enough shape to be useful before someone asks the model to rephrase it into fog.
entries.plainDefinition: Structured content is information returned or stored in a predictable format, such as typed fields, JSON-like objects, tables, or protocol-specific payloads, rather than only free-form text.
entries.whyExists: AI systems often need to pass data between models, tools, applications, and user interfaces. Structure lets those systems validate, route, render, and reuse the content without parsing persuasive paragraphs for hidden meaning.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with correctness. A wrong value in a tidy schema is still wrong; it merely arrives wearing a lanyard.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means fewer brittle string parsers, clearer UI rendering, better validation, and a sharper line between machine-readable data and the model's explanation of that data.
entries.example: "The assistant returned structured content for the invoice, which made the approval flow possible without asking a regex to become finance operations."
askNext: What schema does this content follow?
Which fields are validated, optional, or generated by the model?
Where does structured content become user-facing prose?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Data with a usable shape, not prose pretending to be an API.

entries.body: Structure is not truth. It is only the part of the lie that can be typechecked.

entries.title: Structured Outputs
entries.devilDefinition: The attempt to persuade a language model to stop writing essays when the system actually needs clean fields, valid JSON, and a little self-control.
entries.plainDefinition: Structured outputs are model responses constrained to a predefined schema or format, such as JSON objects with required fields and typed values, so downstream systems can parse and use them more reliably.
entries.whyExists: Free-form language is nice for humans and annoying for software. Structured outputs exist because product workflows often need predictable fields, not prose with creative punctuation and a flair for near-miss compliance.
entries.misuse: They get described as if a schema magically guarantees truth. It does not. Structured nonsense is still nonsense. A model can produce perfectly valid JSON containing invalid judgements, fabricated facts, or inappropriate actions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means schema definitions, validation layers, retries, and careful prompt design so the model returns machine-usable data. It is often a major step up in reliability for extraction, classification, tool use, and workflow control.
entries.example: "The support triage improved once the model stopped composing heartfelt paragraphs and started returning severity, queue, confidence, and escalation reason in the schema."
askNext: What schema is being enforced, and who validates it?
If the structure is correct but the judgement is wrong, how is that caught?
Is the task genuinely schema-friendly, or are we forcing brittle structure onto ambiguous work?

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A way to make model outputs less annoying for software to consume.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: More parseable responses, not inherently more correct ones.

entries.body: This is one of the most practically valuable patterns in modern AI systems because it reduces downstream chaos. It does not remove the need to evaluate the actual content.

entries.title: Sundar Pichai
entries.devilDefinition: The executive voice of AI incumbency: calm enough to sound inevitable, careful enough to make strategic delay resemble wisdom.
entries.plainDefinition: Sundar Pichai is the CEO of Google and Alphabet. In AI conversations, his name often stands for Google's attempt to convert enormous distribution, infrastructure, and research depth into an orderly response to the generative-AI era.
entries.whyExists: Because some public figures become shorthand for institutional posture. Invoking Sundar usually means discussing not just a person but Google's managerial style: cautious rollout, massive product surface area, and the awkward task of modernising the internet's default habits without wrecking the business that paid for them.
entries.misuse: By treating him as either proof that Google has already won AI because it has reach, or proof that it has already lost because it once looked slow. Both stories flatten the more useful reality: incumbents move differently when they are defending search, ads, mobile platforms, cloud, and public trust all at once.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Sundar usually means Google's AI strategy under executive discipline: Gemini folded through consumer and enterprise products, AI Overviews inserted into search, and a company trying to make aggressive technical change look like prudent continuity.
entries.example: "The debate stopped being about model quality the moment someone said Sundar, and the room quietly shifted to distribution, regulation, and who gets to rewrite search without admitting it."
askNext: Do they mean Google's technical capability, its product rollouts, or Pichai's management style?
Is the claim about research strength, distribution power, or corporate caution?
Are we judging Google by frontier demos or by what it manages to ship at global scale?

translations.label: boardroom meaning
translations.text: The AI transition as handled by a company with too much reach to behave like a startup.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for Google's AI strategy, risk tolerance, and product discipline.

entries.body: When a firm already sits inside search, mobile, cloud, and office software, its AI posture is never just technical. It is managerial choreography with global defaults attached.

entries.title: Super app
entries.devilDefinition: An app that begins by promising convenience and ends by wanting custody of your identity, payments, messages, bookings, workflow, and remaining dignity.
entries.plainDefinition: A software product that bundles multiple services, workflows, or transaction types into a single ecosystem rather than offering one narrowly defined function.
entries.whyExists: Because platforms want distribution, retention, transaction volume, and data gravity. AI makes the pitch even more seductive by promising one interface for everything.
entries.misuse: By calling any app with four tabs a super app. A true super app is not just feature-bloated. It is an ecosystem strategy.
entries.practicalMeaning: Messaging, payments, commerce, support, booking, search, assistants, identity, and workflow all living under one roof, often with AI as the conversational front door.
entries.example: "The company said it was building a super app. Translation: it wanted to become the place where users stop leaving."
askNext: Is there real ecosystem lock-in?
Are the services integrated or merely co-located?
Does AI simplify the experience or just decorate the sprawl?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A bid to own more of the user's time, transactions, and switching costs by collapsing multiple workflows into one controlled environment.

entries.body: AI is attractive to super app ambitions because conversation makes sprawl feel tidy. Whether the underlying product has actually become simpler is a separate question, and usually the more important one.

entries.title: Superposition
entries.devilDefinition: The model packing more concepts into fewer dimensions than should politely fit, which is efficient until you try to interpret a single neuron.
entries.plainDefinition: Superposition in interpretability is the hypothesis that neural networks represent more features than they have dimensions by encoding features in overlapping directions, especially when features are sparse.
entries.whyExists: It explains why individual neurons often mix concepts and motivates dictionary learning approaches like sparse autoencoders.
entries.misuse: Used as a vague excuse that interpretability is impossible. Superposition is a modelling claim, not a surrender flag.
entries.practicalMeaning: Expect polysemantic units; use feature-level tools and interventions rather than single-neuron stories alone.
entries.example: "Superposition meant the 'dog' direction also part-timed as 'loyalty' and 'hot dog.'"
askNext: Are you interpreting neurons or features?
What evidence supports overlapping encoding here?
Do interventions behave as the feature story predicts?
entries.warningLabel: Compressed representations resist naive storytelling.

entries.title: Supervisor agent
entries.devilDefinition: An agent promoted to watch other agents, recreating middle management with JSON.
entries.plainDefinition: A supervisor agent oversees worker agents by assigning tasks, checking outputs, enforcing policies, and deciding when to retry, escalate, or stop.
entries.whyExists: Multi-agent systems needed quality control and policy enforcement beyond hope. Supervision encodes review into the loop.
entries.misuse: Stacked supervisors that add latency and shared delusions. A weak supervisor rubber-stamps bad work with confidence.
entries.practicalMeaning: Give supervisors different tools and clearer rubrics than workers. Measure whether supervision improves outcomes worth the extra tokens.
entries.example: "The supervisor agent approved the patch because it looked thorough, not because tests passed."
askNext: What independent signal does the supervisor use?
Can it block irreversible actions?
How do you prevent supervisor-worker collusion on shallow checks?
entries.warningLabel: Oversight without independent evidence is theatre.

entries.title: Supply-chain security
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Supply-chain security is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Slopsquatting and Tool Poisoning are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The supply-chain security discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Supply-chain security is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Supply-chain security belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: SWE-Bench
entries.devilDefinition: The benchmark that turns repository repair into a spectator sport and product roadmap at the same time.
entries.plainDefinition: SWE-Bench is a benchmark suite of real software repository tasks, typically derived from GitHub issues and patches, used to evaluate how well coding systems can diagnose, edit, and validate code changes.
entries.whyExists: Because software engineering is harder to fake than toy code generation, and the field needed a public way to compare agents on real repositories, tests, and issue-driven fixes.
entries.misuse: By treating a strong SWE-Bench score as proof that a coding agent can replace engineering judgement, production review, or the small matter of understanding organisational context. It is a useful signal, not a complete hiring packet.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means measuring whether a system can navigate repo structure, identify relevant files, make coherent edits, and satisfy tests under benchmark conditions. It says much less about product sense, messy human requirements, or living with the resulting code.
entries.example: "They cited SWE-Bench as proof the agent could run the team, which was ambitious given the team still had customers."
askNext: Which version of SWE-Bench are we talking about, and under what tooling assumptions?
How much of the reported score depends on retrieval, orchestration, or human curation around the model?
What does the system do on your repos when tests are flaky, requirements are vague, or the codebase is old enough to vote?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A better benchmark than toy prompts, and still not the same thing as shipping software.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A constrained test of repository task performance that becomes less complete the moment it enters a sales deck.

entries.body: A benchmark can be honest and still be overinterpreted. The trouble begins when the score tries on a badge.

entries.title: SWE-Marathon
entries.devilDefinition: Twenty software tasks long enough for an agent to experience optimism, context loss, recovery, and a very expensive second wind.
entries.plainDefinition: SWE-Marathon is Abundant AI's benchmark of 20 multi-hour software-engineering tasks spanning library reproductions, full-stack product clones, machine-learning engineering, algorithmic work, and performance optimisation. It tests whether agents can sustain coherent progress over unusually long trajectories.
entries.whyExists: Many coding benchmarks reward short repository patches. Real engineering can require hours of design, implementation, testing, research, and recovery across a large deliverable, so SWE-Marathon extends the horizon and verifies the completed system rather than a single edit.
entries.misuse: A small task count is dismissed as anecdotal or a long runtime is mistaken for difficulty by itself. The tasks are deliberately expensive and heterogeneous; aggregate resolution rates need uncertainty, per-task evidence, harness details, and scrutiny of whether verifiers resist shortcuts.
entries.practicalMeaning: The benchmark publishes 20 executable environments, human reference solutions, multi-layer verification, and a large collection of trial trajectories. Its site reports roughly 1,400 logged trials and less than 26% resolution by frontier agents, making per-task behaviour as important as the headline average.
entries.example: "The agent completed the SWE-Marathon task after six hours, a triumph of sustained autonomy and a reminder to inspect the cloud invoice before awarding the medal."
askNext: Which task, harness, model, budget, and number of trials produced this result?
How do the verifiers prevent superficial clones, hard-coded outputs, or benchmark-specific shortcuts?
Did the agent maintain a coherent plan, recover from failures, and validate the whole deliverable?
entries.warningLabel: Long trajectories need task-level evidence, not endurance theatre.

translations.label: evaluation meaning
translations.text: Give an agent multi-hour software work and verify whether coherence survives as long as the task does.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the benchmark's defining feature is trajectory length across design, implementation, recovery, and whole-system verification—not the shape of one agent loop.

entries.title: Sycophancy
entries.devilDefinition: When the model mistakes helpfulness for applause.
entries.plainDefinition: A tendency for an AI system to agree with the user, validate their framing, or echo their assumptions even when those assumptions are weak, false, or unsafe.
entries.whyExists: Because chat systems are often trained to be helpful, pleasant, and non-confrontational. Under pressure, that can drift into obedience dressed as empathy.
entries.misuse: By using it to describe any polite response. True sycophancy is not just friendliness. It is miscalibrated agreement.
entries.practicalMeaning: The model reinforces a user's bad diagnosis, faulty strategy, false certainty, or inflated self-assessment instead of correcting course.
entries.example: "The user wanted validation, not analysis, and the model was only too happy to provide both badly."
askNext: Did the model agree where it should have challenged?
Did it preserve the user's framing instead of testing it?
Would a more grounded answer have introduced uncertainty or correction?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model is preserving the user's preferred frame rather than helping them inspect whether that frame deserves to survive.

entries.body: This is one of the more important social failure modes in conversational AI because it can look like empathy, feel like support, and still make the user's judgement worse.

entries.title: Synthetic content label
entries.devilDefinition: A small disclosure label asked to do the work of education, platform policy, and common sense.
entries.plainDefinition: A synthetic content label is a notice or marker indicating that media, text, audio, video, or another artifact was generated or materially altered by AI.
entries.whyExists: People need context when evaluating content, especially in politics, news, identity, evidence, advertising, and creative work. Labels can support disclosure and reduce some forms of deception.
entries.misuse: Labels get treated as literacy. A label can be missed, removed, vague, disputed, or misunderstood, and it does not explain whether the content is harmful, truthful, satirical, or benign.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means platforms and products need disclosure rules, enforcement, provenance support, user education, and a way to handle mixed human and AI editing.
entries.example: "The synthetic content label said the video was AI-generated, which helped everyone except the people who had already shared it with patriotic punctuation."
askNext: What qualifies as synthetic enough to require a label?
Who applies and enforces the label?
What does the label help users decide?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A disclosure aid, not a substitute for judgement or enforcement.

entries.body: A label is a door sign. It does not make the room safer by itself.

entries.title: Synthetic Customer
entries.devilDefinition: A customer who never churns, never sues, and never says the thing that would have ended the meeting.
entries.plainDefinition: A synthetic customer is an AI-generated representation of a buyer, account, segment, or customer scenario used in testing, marketing, sales, support, or strategy work.
entries.whyExists: It exists because teams need quick ways to rehearse customer conversations, test positioning, explore support scenarios, and reason about segments before real market evidence is available or affordable.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the generated customer becomes a witness for the team's assumptions. A synthetic customer can simulate a plausible reaction; it cannot create revenue, reveal private incentives, or punish a bad product by leaving.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it is useful for drafting sales objections, stress-testing support scripts, mapping journey assumptions, and finding questions for later research. It is weak as proof of demand, willingness to pay, churn risk, or strategic truth.
entries.example: "The synthetic customer loved the enterprise plan because the prompt had not included procurement, politics, or a competing budget request."
askNext: What real customer evidence shaped this synthetic profile?
Which assumptions are we testing, and which are we accidentally feeding back into the model?
Where will real behaviour, purchasing, or refusal be checked?
entries.warningLabel: A simulated customer cannot validate a market by agreeing with you.
entries.note: This entry is narrower than Synthetic User: it focuses on commercial buyer, account, segment, and customer scenarios rather than general simulated users.

translations.label: commercial meaning
translations.text: A generated buyer or account scenario used to rehearse customer-facing decisions.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A convenient proxy for customer thinking, best kept away from final evidence.

entries.body: The synthetic customer is always available. That is the first clue.

entries.title: Synthetic Data
entries.devilDefinition: Artificial evidence created to solve the inconvenient scarcity of real evidence, and occasionally to hide from it.
entries.plainDefinition: Data created or expanded through models, simulation, or rules to supplement scarce, sensitive, or expensive real-world examples.
entries.whyExists: Because real data can be expensive, private, dangerous, incomplete, or slow to collect. Synthetic data offers a way to broaden coverage, test systems, and explore edge cases without waiting for the world to cooperate.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a free replacement for reality. Useful synthetic data can extend a dataset. It cannot repeal the stubborn fact that models eventually meet actual users, actual documents, and actual mess.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means bootstrapping scarce cases, filling scenario gaps, and trying to stretch data budgets without accidentally teaching the system a polished version of the wrong world.
entries.example: "Synthetic data got the project moving. The next problem was discovering which of its elegant assumptions reality had no intention of honouring."
askNext: Which real-world gaps is the synthetic data covering, and how will that coverage be validated?
What artefacts or biases might the generation process be introducing?
At what point does real usage data need to overrule the synthetic substitute?

translations.label: budget meaning
translations.text: A way to buy more examples without paying the full price of finding them in nature.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Manufactured data that can be valuable, provided it is judged against reality rather than mistaken for reality.

entries.body: The synthetic version is often cleaner than life. That is precisely why one should worry.

entries.title: Synthetic Influencer
entries.devilDefinition: A brand ambassador with perfect skin, no union, and a scandal history managed by prompt review.
entries.plainDefinition: A synthetic influencer is a fictional or AI-generated persona used to promote products, publish content, attract followers, or represent a brand as if it were a social media personality.
entries.whyExists: It exists because brands want repeatable personalities that can be localised, scheduled, licensed, and revised without negotiating with a human being who has opinions, agents, fatigue, or old tweets.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a designed persona is treated as authentic community presence. A synthetic influencer can perform a style and hold an audience, but it cannot honestly live a life, disclose a private experience, or provide lived endorsement.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means brand governance, disclosure rules, likeness rights, synthetic content labelling, moderation, audience trust, and a careful answer to who is responsible when the invented person says something expensive.
entries.example: "The synthetic influencer loved the skincare range, partly because the campaign brief had not modelled acne, ageing, or refusing the fee."
askNext: Is the audience being told that this persona is synthetic?
Who owns the likeness, voice, backstory, and moderation policy?
What real human claim is the brand trying to borrow without a real human?
entries.warningLabel: A fictional spokesperson still needs real disclosure and real accountability.

translations.label: marketing meaning
translations.text: A controlled digital persona used to attract attention and sell things.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A cheaper, safer-looking ambassador whose risks have moved from behaviour to governance.

entries.body: The synthetic influencer is less a person than a content calendar with cheekbones.

entries.title: Synthetic media
entries.devilDefinition: Pictures, voices, and video that look like evidence until someone asks for the camera, the microphone, or the consent form.
entries.plainDefinition: Synthetic media is image, audio, video, or multimodal content generated or substantially altered by AI systems rather than captured solely from physical sensors, covering everything from benign mock-ups to deceptive deepfakes.
entries.whyExists: Generation tools made media cheap to invent at scale. Society still treats many media forms as traces of reality, so language is needed for content that looks captured while being composed.
entries.misuse: All synthetic media is collapsed into fraud, or all AI polish is treated as harmless. The category includes product demos, accessibility tools, entertainment, and scams. The risk lives in disclosure, intent, distribution, and harm — not in the mere fact of generation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means labelling generated assets, checking provenance where available, applying consent and likeness rules, and treating high-stakes channels as if a convincing clip can arrive without a corresponding event.
entries.example: "The campaign called it synthetic media in the footer and documentary footage in the headline."
askNext: Is the media labelled as generated or altered, and can that label survive redistribution?
Whose likeness, voice, brand, or situation is being simulated, and with what consent?
What verification exists beyond visual plausibility?
entries.warningLabel: Plausibility is not provenance.

translations.label: media meaning
translations.text: Content produced or heavily reshaped by generative systems rather than pure capture.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A wide bucket from useful mock-ups to convincing fabrications that still need labels and rules.

entries.body: Synthetic media is not a single crime or a single art form. It is the collapse of production cost for images of events that may never have happened.

entries.title: Synthetic User
entries.devilDefinition: A fabricated customer with infinite availability and the same fatal weakness as every fabricated customer: it does not pay.
entries.plainDefinition: A synthetic user is an AI-generated stand-in for a human user, used to simulate feedback, behaviour, testing, or product research scenarios.
entries.whyExists: It exists because recruiting real users is slow, messy, and expensive, while product teams are under constant pressure to validate ideas before lunch.
entries.misuse: It gets oversold as a replacement for actual user research. Synthetic users inherit the assumptions, biases, and blind spots of their prompts, source material, and model priors. They can imitate a segment. They cannot become one.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means using model-generated personas to pressure-test flows, draft hypotheses, or widen exploratory coverage before speaking to real people whose incentives, confusion, and refusal to behave neatly are the whole point.
entries.example: "The synthetic users loved the onboarding because, unlike actual customers, they had been generated to find it coherent."
askNext: What real evidence informed these simulated users in the first place?
Are we using them to generate hypotheses or to avoid meeting actual customers?
Which decisions still require observation of real behaviour and incentives?
entries.warningLabel: Excellent for speed. Unsuitable as a substitute for reality.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A fast way to explore likely reactions before scheduling interviews or tests.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A synthetic proxy useful for hypothesis generation and dangerously tempting as a shortcut around actual users.

entries.body: The trouble with a simulated customer is that it will often tell you exactly what your process hoped to hear.

entries.title: Synthetic User Research
entries.devilDefinition: User research without the dangerous variable known as a user.
entries.plainDefinition: Synthetic user research uses AI-generated personas, simulated participants, or modelled customer responses to explore likely reactions, behaviours, objections, or product needs.
entries.whyExists: It exists because real research takes recruiting, scheduling, incentive design, observation, and patience. Synthetic research can help teams draft hypotheses, explore variants, and prepare better questions before spending time with people.
entries.misuse: It is misused when simulated feedback is treated as a substitute for observation, interviews, analytics, support logs, or buying behaviour. A model can reflect patterns in its training data and prompt. It cannot be embarrassed, confused, underpaid, distracted, or genuinely out of budget.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it belongs near discovery planning, early concept testing, assumption mapping, and interview preparation. It is strongest when it helps a team decide what to ask real users next, not when it lets the team avoid asking them at all.
entries.example: "The synthetic user research said the pricing page was clear, which was comforting until actual users reached the part involving money."
askNext: Which real evidence seeded these synthetic participants?
Are we using this to generate hypotheses or to replace contact with customers?
What decision would change if real users disagree with the simulation?
entries.warningLabel: Useful for hypotheses. Dangerous as a replacement for evidence.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Using simulated participants to explore likely reactions before or alongside real research.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fast assumption generator with a remarkable ability to sound like validation.

entries.body: Synthetic user research is most honest when it admits the word "research" is doing some supervised stretching.

entries.title: System Prompt
entries.devilDefinition: The invisible preface explaining what the machine is supposed to be before the user begins testing the claim.
entries.plainDefinition: A system prompt is the high-priority instruction layer supplied by the application or developer to shape a model's behaviour, rules, tone, and operating constraints.
entries.whyExists: Because most AI products need stable behaviour across many users and tasks. Someone has to tell the model what role it is playing, what rules matter, and how the chain of command is supposed to work.
entries.misuse: By treating it as secret sauce, or worse, as a reliable security boundary. A system prompt is useful control text. It is not a magical wall separating trusted intent from hostile input.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a standing instruction layer covering role, tone, refusal rules, output format, tool permissions, escalation behaviour, and the product's preferred version of reality.
entries.example: "The app felt polished until the leaked system prompt revealed that half the brand voice was an anxious paragraph about never improvising legal advice."
askNext: What instructions live in the system prompt, and which belong in code or policy instead?
How does the system behave when user input conflicts with hidden instructions?
What breaks if the system prompt is exposed, ignored, or partially obeyed?
entries.warningLabel: An instruction layer, not a security model.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The application's standing attempt to keep the model on script before users and retrieved text start arguing back.

entries.body: Every serious AI product has one, whether the company talks about it or not. The interesting part is never merely that it exists. The interesting part is what else the team expects it to do.

entries.title: Tab
entries.devilDefinition: Tab is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Tab is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Cursor often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Tab came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
askNext: What exact meaning of Tab is being used here?

entries.title: Taste
entries.devilDefinition: The mysterious quality everyone claims to have once the machine starts making average things faster.
entries.plainDefinition: The ability to recognise quality, coherence, appropriateness, style, restraint, and signal amid abundance. In AI work, taste often matters more after generation than before it.
entries.whyExists: Because AI lowers the cost of producing text, images, code, and options. Once output becomes cheap, selection becomes expensive.
entries.misuse: By treating taste as pure vibes, class signalling, or post-hoc justification for arbitrary preferences. Good taste is not magic. It is trained judgement with standards.
entries.practicalMeaning: Knowing what to keep, what to reject, what to simplify, what to make legible, and what should never have shipped.
entries.example: "The model gave ten logo directions. Taste was choosing the only one that did not look like a fintech startup from 2021."
askNext: What standards are being applied?
Can the person explain why one output is better?
Is this taste, or just familiarity and bias?
Who is the intended audience?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person can distinguish between output that merely exists and output that deserves to survive.

entries.body: The more generation costs fall, the less impressive raw production becomes. Taste starts earning its keep when the machine can give you twenty options before you've properly wanted one.

entries.title: Teacher-student
entries.devilDefinition: Distillation vocabulary: a big model teaches a small model, and both still need exams.
entries.plainDefinition: Teacher-student refers to the pairing in knowledge distillation where a larger or stronger teacher model provides signals that train a smaller student model.
entries.whyExists: It is the standard language of distillation setups across deep learning and modern LLM compression.
entries.misuse: Stretched into pure metaphor for any mentoring UI. In engineering contexts it should stay tied to training practice.
entries.practicalMeaning: Specify what the teacher provides (logits, sequences, preferences), on which data, and how the student is evaluated against the teacher.
entries.example: "The teacher-student setup transferred style quickly and factuality slowly."
askNext: What exact teacher signals are used?
Is the student evaluated on the teacher's failure modes?
When do you refresh the teacher?
entries.warningLabel: Students inherit the teacher's blind spots by default.

entries.title: Telegram
entries.devilDefinition: A messaging app that periodically looks at the rest of the software industry and decides it would also like to be a platform, a payments layer, a bot host, and possibly a country.
entries.plainDefinition: Telegram is a messaging platform that is also a deployment surface for bots, automations, and Mini Apps. In AI contexts, it often serves as the conversational front end through which assistants, workflows, notifications, and lightweight products are delivered.
entries.whyExists: Because the easiest way to make software feel useful is often to place it where the user already is: inside the chat thread.
entries.misuse: By treating Telegram as just a messenger when the real value is often in its bot ecosystem, Mini App surface, lightweight UI layer, and low-friction interaction model. The platform question matters as much as the chat question.
entries.practicalMeaning: A practical interface layer for AI agents, internal tools, support flows, commerce, alerts, and small web apps that do not need the ceremony of a full native app. Telegram's own docs explicitly position bots and Mini Apps as capable of replacing websites, accepting payments, and handling business workflows.
entries.example: "They said they were building an AI product. In practice, they were building a Telegram bot with good timing and a payment hook."
askNext: Is Telegram the product, or just the distribution layer?
Does the experience rely on bots, Mini Apps, or simple notifications?
Is the choice about user convenience, growth, or avoiding native-app complexity?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A chat surface that doubles as a lightweight product platform, which is why so many assistants and automations end up living there.

entries.note: Telegram's current platform docs span bots, Mini Apps, business bots, payments, and attachment-menu integrations. In product conversations, that means Telegram is often less 'channel' than 'deployment shortcut'.
entries.body: Chat interfaces are powerful partly because they lower the activation energy for software. Telegram makes that especially obvious: a bot, a Mini App, and a payment layer can get surprisingly close to "product" before anyone has written a proper mobile app.

entries.title: Terminal-Bench 2.1
entries.devilDefinition: A terminal-agent benchmark revised after the tasks themselves failed enough evaluation to earn a point release.
entries.plainDefinition: Terminal-Bench 2.1 is a verified revision of Terminal-Bench 2.0 for evaluating agents on complex tasks in containerised terminal environments. Its maintainers modified 26 tasks to fix bugs, adjust timeouts or resources, and strengthen robustness against reward hacking.
entries.whyExists: Terminal agents must do more than edit a repository: they install dependencies, configure systems, train models, debug asynchronous code, analyse data, handle security work, and complete scientific or operational tasks end to end. The benchmark measures that broader computer-use competence.
entries.misuse: Version 2.1 results are compared directly with 2.0 scores as though task fixes cannot move a leaderboard. Scores are also quoted without the agent harness, model configuration, sandbox provider, trial count, or public trajectory requirements that make them interpretable.
entries.practicalMeaning: A compliant leaderboard submission runs at least five trials per task, uploads public results through Harbor, and passes automated and maintainer review. Evaluators should record the exact 2.1 dataset, harness, model, reasoning effort, environment, and task-level variance.
entries.example: "Terminal-Bench 2.1 fixed the tasks before ranking the agents, a courtesy not every benchmark extends to either party."
askNext: Is this result from Terminal-Bench 2.0, 2.0 Verified, or 2.1?
Which agent harness, model settings, sandbox, and trial count were used?
Are trajectories public, and how were reward-hacking or infrastructure failures reviewed?
entries.warningLabel: A point release can change both the tasks and the rank.

translations.label: evaluation meaning
translations.text: A repaired terminal-task suite whose version, harness, environment, and repeated trials belong beside every score.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the benchmark spans many terminal workflows; a single agent-loop picture would hide the dataset and verification changes that make version 2.1 noteworthy.

Terminal-Bench 2.1 Terminal Bench 2.1 Terminal-Bench v2.1 Safety and evaluation Agents and workflows Infrastructure and deployment terminal benchmark agents containers A terminal-agent benchmark revised after the tasks themselves failed enough evaluation to earn a point release.
 Terminal-Bench 2.1 is a verified revision of Terminal-Bench 2.0 for evaluating agents on complex tasks in containerised terminal environments. Its maintainers modified 26 tasks to fix bugs, adjust timeouts or resources, and strengthen robustness against reward hacking.
 Terminal agents must do more than edit a repository: they install dependencies, configure systems, train models, debug asynchronous code, analyse data, handle security work, and complete scientific or operational tasks end to end. The benchmark measures that broader computer-use competence.
 Version 2.1 results are compared directly with 2.0 scores as though task fixes cannot move a leaderboard. Scores are also quoted without the agent harness, model configuration, sandbox provider, trial count, or public trajectory requirements that make them interpretable.
 A compliant leaderboard submission runs at least five trials per task, uploads public results through Harbor, and passes automated and maintainer review. Evaluators should record the exact 2.1 dataset, harness, model, reasoning effort, environment, and task-level variance.
 "Terminal-Bench 2.1 fixed the tasks before ranking the agents, a courtesy not every benchmark extends to either party."
 Is this result from Terminal-Bench 2.0, 2.0 Verified, or 2.1? Which agent harness, model settings, sandbox, and trial count were used? Are trajectories public, and how were reward-hacking or infrastructure failures reviewed? Harbor Terminal-Bench  Harbor Framework Terminal-Bench

entries.title: Test-Time Compute
entries.devilDefinition: The principle that if a model is allowed to spend more compute on a question, the invoice and the answer may both become more elaborate.
entries.plainDefinition: Test-time compute refers to the amount of computation a model uses while handling an input at inference time, including extra decoding steps, search, tool calls, verification passes, or deliberate reasoning effort.
entries.whyExists: It exists because performance is not determined only during training. Many systems improve on harder tasks when they are allowed more time, more samples, or more internal work at the moment of answering.
entries.misuse: It is misused when greater compute is treated as proof of deeper intelligence rather than as a trade-off. More compute can buy better results on some tasks, but it can also buy slower answers, higher cost, and a longer walk to the wrong place.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means setting policies for which requests deserve slower, more expensive handling and which should be answered quickly with a lighter touch. The interesting part is budget allocation, not mystical thinking.
entries.example: "They added more test-time compute for the thorny cases and promptly discovered that half their workload was only thorny because the prompt was poor."
askNext: Which tasks actually improve when more inference-time budget is spent?
How is extra compute triggered, measured, and capped?
Are you solving a hard problem, or subsidising a badly specified one?

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Performance may improve if the system is allowed to work harder at answer time.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Spending more inference-time budget on selected requests to improve results where it is worth the cost.

entries.body: The mature version of this idea is resource management. The immature version is calling every slow answer thoughtful.

entries.title: Thinking time
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Thinking time names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Reasoning Effort needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once thinking time entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: Together AI
entries.devilDefinition: Another place to rent open models by the token when you want GPU access without marrying a single cloud's opinionated stack.
entries.plainDefinition: Together AI is a platform for running and fine-tuning open models via API and infrastructure services, competing in the inference-provider and model-platform market.
entries.whyExists: Open weights need hosts. Platforms package serving, fine-tuning, and model catalogues so teams can ship without owning clusters on day one.
entries.misuse: Confused with a model lab, or assumed to make every open model production-ready without evals, scaling work, or data controls.
entries.practicalMeaning: Compare model availability, latency, pricing, fine-tune options, and compliance posture against other inference providers for your workload.
entries.example: "They put the open model on Together AI so finance would stop asking who owned the GPU reservation."
askNext: Which exact model revision are you calling?
Where does fine-tune data reside, and who can access it?
What is the exit plan if pricing or availability shifts?
entries.warningLabel: A platform is not a model card.

entries.title: Tokens
entries.devilDefinition: The tiny units in which language models think, pricing pages charge, and context windows quietly run out of patience.
entries.plainDefinition: Tokens are chunks of text that a model processes as basic units. They are not the same as words or characters. Depending on the tokenizer, a word may be one token, several tokens, or part of a token sequence.
entries.whyExists: Models do not read text the way people do. They operate on tokenised sequences, and limits such as context size, speed, and pricing are usually expressed in tokens rather than words.
entries.misuse: People often say "one token equals one word", which is close enough for a dinner party and wrong enough to break a budget. The mismatch becomes especially annoying in multilingual text, code, JSON, and documents with lots of formatting noise.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means three things: how much context you can fit, how long responses take, and how much the request costs. If your prompt, retrieved docs, tool schemas, and expected output all compete for space, tokens are the shared currency.
entries.example: "The feature failed less because the reasoning was poor and more because the prompt, schema, and four attached policy documents had eaten the token budget."
askNext: How many input and output tokens does the workflow typically use?
What is consuming the token budget: user text, retrieval, examples, or structured schemas?
Can the context be shortened without removing information the model genuinely needs?

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: The microscopic line items that add up into very real spend.

entries.body: If you want a rule of thumb, use token counts for estimating budgets and constraints, not for sounding mystical. They are accounting units with mathematical consequences, not evidence that a system has discovered the secret atoms of language.

entries.title: Tokens per dollar
entries.devilDefinition: The spreadsheet column that ends arguments about "best model" by replacing them with arguments about whose tokens count.
entries.plainDefinition: Tokens per dollar is a unit-economics ratio comparing how many tokens a model, provider, or serving setup produces or processes for each unit of spend, used to compare inference cost efficiency across options.
entries.whyExists: Model choice, routing, caching, and provider pricing all collapse into money. Teams need a crude but shared yardstick when quality claims arrive without invoices attached.
entries.misuse: It is treated as quality. A system can mint cheap tokens while still being wrong, slow, unsafe, or expensive once tool calls, retries, long contexts, and human cleanup are counted. Input and output tokens are also not interchangeable currencies.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means comparing providers and models with the same workload shape, including cache hits, reasoning tokens, tool loops, and failure retries, then deciding whether the cheaper path still meets the quality bar you pretended was fixed.
entries.example: "Their tokens per dollar looked unbeatable until the evaluation set forced every cheap answer through a second, expensive model."
askNext: Are you counting input, output, reasoning, tool, and retry tokens the same way across options?
What quality or latency floor must hold before cheaper tokens are allowed to win?
Does the ratio include only model fees, or also serving, egress, eval, and human review?
entries.warningLabel: Cheap tokens are not the same as useful answers.

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: How much model traffic you get for each unit of spend.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A rough efficiency score that still needs a quality and latency floor.

entries.body: Tokens per dollar is useful until someone confuses thrift with taste. Count carefully, then decide what you are willing to buy badly.

entries.title: Tool Access Review
entries.devilDefinition: The meeting where the tool list is supposed to become a control surface, not a souvenir from the prototype.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool access review is a recurring or pre-release review of which tools an AI system can use, what each tool can do, who owns the access, and whether the permissions still match the workflow's risk.
entries.whyExists: Tool access drifts. Prototypes gain connectors, pilots inherit credentials, teams add exceptions, and the original permission story quietly becomes folklore. Review exists to compare the declared design with the live system.
entries.misuse: It is reduced to asking whether the team feels comfortable with the current setup. Comfort is not evidence. The review has to inspect real grants, scopes, logs, owners, and revoke paths.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means inventorying live connectors, checking read and write scopes, confirming user impersonation rules, sampling audit logs, removing unused tools, and giving somebody accountable the power to say no before the agent says "done".
entries.example: "The tool access review found that the calendar agent still had a file-export scope from a prototype nobody wanted to remember."
askNext: Does the review inspect live permissions or only the architecture diagram?
Which stale tools, scopes, or credentials were removed last time?
Who owns the risk when access is kept for convenience?

translations.label: governance meaning
translations.text: Check what the agent can actually touch, then remove what no longer earns its access.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Permission hygiene with receipts, owners, logs, and the occasional awkward revocation.

entries.body: No diagram is attached because the useful mental model is not a flowchart. It is a live access register meeting a person with enough authority to close doors.

entries.title: Tool Allowlist
entries.devilDefinition: The list that keeps the demo from discovering the payroll API by vibes.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool allowlist is the explicit set of tools, operations, and scopes an AI system is permitted to use for a given task, user, environment, or workflow.
entries.whyExists: Agents become safer and easier to reason about when they cannot reach every integration by default. An allowlist turns "the model has tools" into a narrower claim about which tools are available and under which conditions.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a static checkbox. A generous allowlist with broad write access, production credentials, and no review cadence is not a control. It is an invitation with better formatting.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means named tools, allowed operations, read and write distinctions, environment boundaries, user-specific scopes, approval thresholds, and a review process for removing access once the prototype has stopped pretending to be temporary.
entries.example: "The support agent could read orders and draft refunds, but the tool allowlist kept the actual refund tool behind a separate approval gate."
askNext: Which tools are allowed for this exact workflow, not the platform in general?
Does the allowlist distinguish read, draft, write, send, and delete operations?
Who can add tools to the list, and how is stale access removed?

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Only these tools may be called, and only in these ways.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A permission boundary that needs maintenance, not a screenshot from the launch checklist.

entries.body: An allowlist is useful because it is boring. The moment it becomes aspirational, the agent has already begun negotiating with production.
