entries.title: Tool Approval Fatigue
entries.devilDefinition: The condition in which every prompt is urgent, every action needs a click, and the human becomes a well-dressed Enter key.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool approval fatigue is the decline in human attention and judgement that happens when people are asked to approve too many agent tool calls, especially when the requests are repetitive, low-context, or poorly prioritised.
entries.whyExists: Human approval is a real control for risky actions. It fails when the workflow turns every minor step into an interruption and then acts surprised that the reviewer stops reading.
entries.misuse: Teams use approval prompts as proof that a human is in the loop. Often the human is only in the way. If the reviewer cannot distinguish trivial calls from dangerous ones, the approval surface becomes camouflage for weak automation design.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means risk-tiered approvals, batched requests, clear summaries, hard stops for irreversible actions, quiet handling for safe routine work, and escalation paths that respect the fact that vigilance is a scarce resource.
entries.example: "After the fiftieth calendar lookup, the reviewer approved the file-sharing request too, which is how tool approval fatigue earned its own incident note."
askNext: Which approvals actually require judgement, and which are workflow noise?
Does the reviewer see enough context to block the action intelligently?
How often does the system ask for approval, and what happens to attention after repetition?
entries.warningLabel: A tired approver is not a control surface.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Repeated approvals degrade into habit unless the system protects attention.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A human-in-the-loop design that converted judgement into reflex.

entries.body: No diagram is attached because the failure is behavioural. The system may show a tidy loop; the person inside it is still human.

entries.title: Tool broker
entries.devilDefinition: The middleman deciding which external powers the agent may see, request, or be politely denied before curiosity becomes consequences.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool broker is the component or service that mediates which tools an agent can discover, select, or invoke, often based on permissions, context, policy, or environment.
entries.whyExists: Agents rarely need unrestricted visibility of every available capability. A broker exists to separate tool exposure from tool implementation, making it easier to apply permissions, environment boundaries, capability filtering, or contextual access rules.
entries.misuse: It gets described as though brokering itself guarantees security or good judgement. It does not. A broker can make tool access more governable, but it can also become a fashionable wrapper around the same old permission mistakes if the policy behind it is sloppy.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means registries, capability filters, tenancy rules, environment-aware allowlists, protocol adapters, and a decision point that determines whether the agent gets a calculator, a search index, a payment action, or a polite refusal.
entries.example: "The tool broker was the quiet adult in the room. It let the agent discover the search tool and blocked the urge to improvise with production billing."
askNext: Who decides which tools are visible in this context: the agent, the broker, or a policy layer above both?
How are permissions, tenancy, and environment boundaries enforced before a tool is exposed?
What happens when the broker itself routes the agent to the wrong capability?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A controlled gatekeeper between the agent and the set of external capabilities.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The layer deciding which tools are visible and callable before the agent gets ideas.

entries.body: Tool brokers become interesting the moment they start saying no. Exposing every tool to every agent is not flexibility. It is future incident material with an API.

entries.title: Tool call audit
entries.devilDefinition: The ledger that explains which external capability the model touched before everyone agreed to blame the prompt.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool call audit is a reviewable record of tool invocations made by a model or agent, including arguments, results, timing, caller identity, permissions, and side effects.
entries.whyExists: Tool use moves AI systems from text generation into action. Once a model can query data, write files, send messages, or change records, the organisation needs a way to inspect what was called and what happened next.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to raw logs nobody can interpret. A useful audit does not merely prove that an endpoint was hit. It shows whether the call was authorised, expected, correctly formed, successful, and tied to an accountable workflow.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means structured logs, argument capture, result summaries, redaction policy, actor identity, permission state, idempotency markers, and enough retention to investigate incidents without storing every secret forever.
entries.example: "The tool call audit showed that the agent had not gone rogue; it had simply been granted a calendar tool, a vague goal, and the confidence of a junior consultant."
askNext: Can we see which tool was called, with which arguments, and under whose authority?
Are sensitive values redacted without destroying the evidence needed for review?
Can the audit connect tool calls to user-visible outcomes and incidents?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A record of external actions good enough to review after the applause or the incident.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The receipt for model-powered side effects.

entries.body: Tool call audits are dull until they are missing. Then they become the most exciting document no one wrote.

entries.title: Tool Permissions
entries.devilDefinition: The part of the agent design where optimism collides with the list of things the model is absolutely not allowed to touch.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool permissions define which tools an AI system may call, what operations each tool may perform, and whether approval or other conditions are required before use.
entries.whyExists: They exist because once a model can do more than answer questions, it can also create tickets, write files, query production systems, or send messages somebody will later have to explain. Capability without permission design is merely incident planning in civilian clothes.
entries.misuse: The term gets flattened into a yes-or-no question, as if "has tools" were the whole story. Real permissions are granular: read versus write, staging versus production, local versus external, draft versus send, observe versus act.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means allowlists, approval gates, budget limits, environment boundaries, impersonation rules, audit logs, and hard stops around actions with irreversible consequences. If the model can act, the permission model is part of the product.
entries.example: "The assistant seemed sensible until they realised its tool permissions allowed it to email customers directly, which was brave in precisely the wrong way."
askNext: Which tools are read-only, and which can change external state?
What actions require human approval before execution?
How are permissions enforced technically rather than merely described in a prompt?
entries.warningLabel: A prompt that says 'be careful' is not a permission system.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A map of what the model may read, write, send, and break before a human intervenes.

entries.body: Tool permissions matter because the most expensive model mistake is often not the wrong sentence. It is the wrong action.

entries.title: Tool Poisoning
entries.devilDefinition: Hiding instructions in the tool layer so the agent mistakes a compromised capability for a helpful one.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool poisoning is an attack or failure mode in which a tool's descriptors, schemas, metadata, routing, or surrounding instructions are manipulated so an agent invokes it under false or malicious assumptions.
entries.whyExists: Modern agents do not merely call tools; they read about them. If the model relies on tool descriptions to decide what is safe, relevant, or authoritative, poisoning that layer becomes a prompt injection problem with better infrastructure.
entries.misuse: It should not be used for every bad tool output. The more precise issue is corruption of the tool interface itself: the agent is misled about what the tool is, what it does, or how it should be used.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means malicious MCP descriptors, misleading plugin metadata, tampered schemas, or tool naming collisions that redirect the agent into unsafe actions, data leakage, or expensive nonsense.
entries.example: "The agent did not get hacked by sorcery; it was simply handed a poisoned tool description and obeyed it with enterprise enthusiasm."
askNext: Where do tool descriptions, schemas, and permissions come from, and who can change them?
Can the agent verify a tool's identity and scope before invoking it?
What stops a third-party integration from advertising capabilities it should never have?
entries.warningLabel: The tool layer is part of the prompt whether engineering admits it or not.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker has manipulated the agent's view of a tool rather than the tool's code alone.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The agent is trusting compromised tool metadata and making unsafe calls because of it.

entries.body: The software industry spent years learning that configuration is attack surface. Agent systems have helpfully rediscovered the lesson in prose.

entries.title: Tool Registry
entries.devilDefinition: A menu of powers offered to an agent, ideally written by someone who understands the difference between capability and liability.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool registry is a structured catalogue of tools an AI system can discover or use, including each tool's name, schema, permissions, owner, description, and operational constraints.
entries.whyExists: Agents need a reliable way to know which actions are available and how to call them. As tool ecosystems grow, teams need registries to avoid stale schemas, duplicated integrations, hidden permissions, and the old tradition of guessing from a README last touched during a launch week.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a plugin marketplace with fewer consequences. Registering a tool does not make it safe, maintained, authorised, or suitable for an agent that has just been persuaded by a malicious PDF.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means schema versioning, permission boundaries, ownership, environment separation, audit logs, deprecation rules, and a process for deciding which tools an agent sees in a given task.
entries.example: "The tool registry made the agent less adventurous by removing three obsolete endpoints and one payroll action nobody remembered approving."
askNext: Who owns each registered tool and its schema?
Which permissions travel with the tool, and which are granted per task?
How does the registry retire dangerous or stale tools before agents keep calling them out of nostalgia?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: Keep the agent's available actions discoverable, typed, owned, and governed.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A catalogue that becomes security-critical the minute a model can act on it.

entries.body: A good tool registry is less like an app store and more like a key cabinet. The labels matter, and so does the lock.

entries.title: Tool reliability
entries.devilDefinition: The unfashionable question of whether the supposedly helpful thing the model keeps calling will actually behave the same way twice.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool reliability is the consistency, correctness, and recoverability of a tool when invoked by a model or agent under normal and adverse operating conditions.
entries.whyExists: An agent is only as dependable as the tools it calls. Even strong reasoning degrades rapidly when the surrounding tools are flaky, under-specified, slow, or capricious about their outputs.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to uptime or successful HTTP responses. A tool can be "up" and still be unreliable because it returns partial state, inconsistent schemas, non-idempotent side effects, or errors too vague for the caller to recover from intelligently.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means schema stability, timeouts, retries, idempotency, versioning, error semantics, sandbox boundaries, and the quiet labour of making external actions predictable enough that a model can call them without inventing theology around transient failure.
entries.example: "The model was blamed for the bad refund flow until someone noticed the payment tool treated duplicate requests as a creative writing exercise."
askNext: Does this tool fail clearly enough for the caller to retry, escalate, or stop?
Are schemas, side effects, and permissions stable across versions and environments?
What happens when the tool times out halfway through an action that matters?

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: The tool behaves predictably enough that the caller does not need to hallucinate its failure mode.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A tool that can be called repeatedly without turning ordinary automation into incident archaeology.

entries.body: Tool reliability rarely headlines the launch deck, which is precisely why it gets to decide whether the launch keeps working a week later.

entries.title: Tool result
entries.devilDefinition: The evidence returned by the machinery, before the model gives it a haircut and a tone of confidence.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool result is the data, error, status, or structured payload returned by an external tool call in an AI workflow.
entries.whyExists: Agents and assistants need to distinguish between what an external system actually returned and what the model later says about it. That boundary matters for debugging, audits, citations, retries, and blame.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as though the model's summary and the tool's output are the same thing. They are not. A neat paragraph can misread a correct payload, hide a warning, or politely bury a failed call.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means preserving raw outputs, errors, timestamps, identifiers, and schemas long enough for the system to explain what happened after the model has finished being fluent.
entries.example: "The tool result said the account was inactive; the assistant's summary said the user was probably ready to upgrade, which was generous and entirely unhelpful."
askNext: Can we inspect the raw tool result separately from the model's explanation?
Does the system keep errors and partial results, or only successful summaries?
Which downstream decisions rely on the tool result rather than the generated prose?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The tool's returned payload, not the assistant's tidied-up story about it.

entries.body: A tool result is where the transcript briefly stops being literature. Preserve that moment.

entries.title: Tool schema
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool schema names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Tool Registry needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once tool schema entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
askNext: What exact meaning of Tool schema is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

entries.body: Tool schema gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Tool Registry keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.

entries.title: Tool search tool
entries.devilDefinition: A tool for finding tools after the tool list became too large for the model hired to use it.
entries.plainDefinition: A tool search tool lets a model discover and load relevant tool definitions on demand instead of placing every available schema in the context window at the start of each request.
entries.whyExists: Large tool catalogues consume context and make selection less reliable. Just-in-time discovery keeps the initial prompt smaller and presents the model with a focused set of definitions for the current task.
entries.misuse: It is marketed as a cure for tool sprawl when it is chiefly an index over the sprawl. Poor names, overlapping capabilities, weak descriptions, and unsafe tools remain poor after retrieval; they are simply discovered more efficiently.
entries.practicalMeaning: Mark some tool definitions for deferred loading, expose a search mechanism, and let the model retrieve a small relevant subset. Evaluate recall, selection accuracy, latency, permission filtering, and what happens when the correct tool is described in the wrong corporate dialect.
entries.example: "The agent searched three thousand tools and found the correct one, leaving only the smaller matter of whether it should call it."
askNext: What metadata is indexed and how is search quality evaluated?
Are permissions applied before discovery as well as before execution?
What is the fallback when the needed tool is not retrieved?
entries.warningLabel: Discovery quality becomes part of tool reliability.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Retrieve a few tool schemas when needed instead of charging every request rent for the entire catalogue.

entries.body: The enterprise first accumulated thousands of capabilities. It then invented search so the agent could experience the same onboarding problem as everyone else.

entries.title: Tool sprawl
entries.devilDefinition: The condition in which an agent platform keeps acquiring capabilities until the menu itself becomes a form of operational risk.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool sprawl is the uncontrolled growth of tools, connectors, actions, plugins, or MCP endpoints available to an AI system, especially when their scopes, owners, schemas, and permissions are inconsistent or poorly understood.
entries.whyExists: Tool use is where AI systems become useful, so teams keep adding integrations. The trouble arrives when each new connector solves a local problem while making the overall capability surface harder to govern, test, secure, or even describe accurately.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with having many tools. Quantity alone is not the sin. Sprawl begins when duplication, stale schemas, hidden permissions, and overlapping functions make the system broader than the organisation can actually reason about.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means agents seeing near-duplicate tools, calling the wrong one, failing on inconsistent schemas, inheriting permissions nobody revisited, and forcing operators to discover that discoverability is itself a security question.
entries.example: "The assistant had twenty-seven tools, nine ways to read a document, and exactly one adult in the room asking why."
askNext: Which tools are redundant, stale, or too broadly permissioned for the tasks they serve?
Who owns each schema and deprecates it before agents keep using it out of habit?
Can the agent discover only the tools relevant to the current task, or is it browsing a dangerous buffet?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: The tool surface is expanding faster than the organisation's ability to govern it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful stack slowly becoming a confusing and security-relevant cupboard.

entries.body: Tool sprawl is what happens when capability is measured by addition and nobody budgets for subtraction.

entries.title: Tool Use
entries.devilDefinition: The point where the model stops guessing alone and starts borrowing other systems' competence.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool use is the pattern of giving a model access to external systems such as search, code execution, databases, browsers, calculators, or internal APIs so it can gather information or take actions.
entries.whyExists: Because models know many things badly, some things approximately, and almost nothing about the current state of your systems unless another tool tells them.
entries.misuse: By marketing tool access as autonomy rather than integration. The hard part is not that the model can ask for a tool. The hard part is deciding which tools it may touch, when, and under whose supervision.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means schemas, permission scopes, retries, observability, error handling, and a constant negotiation between flexibility and the desire to avoid discovering new incident classes at scale.
entries.example: "The assistant became materially more useful once tool use let it search the docs, check the account, and stop inventing the answer from memory."
askNext: Which tools are available, and what can each one affect?
What happens when a tool returns stale data, partial failure, or nonsense?
Is the system using tools to extend capability, or to mask weak product design?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model gets more useful by leaning on ordinary software, and more dangerous if nobody governs the relationship.

entries.body: Tool use is where the modern AI stack becomes refreshingly concrete. Once an external system is involved, rhetoric gives way to permissions, logs, and consequences.

entries.title: Tool-layer Risk
entries.devilDefinition: The part of the risk register that begins after the model has stopped talking and the connected software starts obeying.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool-layer risk is the risk introduced when an AI system can use external tools, APIs, connectors, or automations that read data, change state, send messages, spend money, or affect other systems.
entries.whyExists: Model output is not always the final hazard. In agentic systems, the serious consequence often appears when a model's structured request reaches ordinary software with real permissions.
entries.misuse: It is misread as another flavour of hallucination risk. A perfectly plausible tool call can still be unsafe if the tool has the wrong scope, stale context, weak validation, or authority the task never deserved.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means mapping every tool to the data it can read, the state it can change, the external parties it can contact, and the controls that stop a bad call from becoming an incident with a timestamp.
entries.example: "The model merely chose the refund tool; tool-layer risk arrived when that tool could issue real payments without checking account policy."
askNext: What can this tool actually read, write, send, delete, or spend?
Where is model intent validated before it becomes external action?
Which failure would be expensive even if the generated text looked reasonable?
entries.warningLabel: A safe answer can still trigger an unsafe action.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The risk created by what connected tools are allowed to do after the model has asked.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The incident surface moves from text generation to permissions, validators, logs, and irreversible side effects.

entries.body: Tool-layer risk is where product enthusiasm becomes a systems problem. Once the tool can act, the model's mistake is only the opening sentence.
entries.searchText:
Tool-layer Risk tool layer risk action-layer risk tool execution risk Agents and workflows Safety and evaluation Infrastructure and deployment agents tools risk permissions The part of the risk register that begins after the model has stopped talking and the connected software starts obeying.
 Tool-layer risk is the risk introduced when an AI system can use external tools, APIs, connectors, or automations that read data, change state, send messages, spend money, or affect other systems.
 Model output is not always the final hazard. In agentic systems, the serious consequence often appears when a model's structured request reaches ordinary software with real permissions.
 It is misread as another flavour of hallucination risk. A perfectly plausible tool call can still be unsafe if the tool has the wrong scope, stale context, weak validation, or authority the task never deserved.
 In practice it means mapping every tool to the data it can read, the state it can change, the external parties it can contact, and the controls that stop a bad call from becoming an incident with a timestamp.
 "The model merely chose the refund tool; tool-layer risk arrived when that tool could issue real payments without checking account policy."
 What can this tool actually read, write, send, delete, or spend? Where is model intent validated before it becomes external action? Which failure would be expensive even if the generated text looked reasonable? Function Calling Tool Use Tool Permissions Trust boundary

entries.title: Training
entries.devilDefinition: The long, expensive ritual in which a machine studies vast piles of data until the bill becomes spiritual.
entries.plainDefinition: Training is the process of adjusting a model's weights using data and optimisation algorithms so that the model performs better on a task or distribution of tasks. In large models, this includes repeated forward and backward passes over massive datasets.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because models do not arrive preloaded with competence. Their behaviour is shaped by exposure to data, objective functions, optimisation choices, and huge amounts of compute.
entries.misuse: It gets used as if any change to a model counts as training. Not quite. Prompting is not training. Calling an API is not training. Even many "learning" product features are really retrieval, memory, or rule changes layered around a frozen model.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means data pipelines, filtering, labelling, loss curves, GPUs, checkpoints, failed runs, parameter updates, evaluation cycles, and weeks or months of engineering effort. It is one of the least glamorous ways to spend a lot of money.
entries.example: "The team was not training a new model from scratch; they were fine-tuning an existing one and trying not to call both things innovation."
askNext: Are we talking about pretraining, fine-tuning, or some lighter adaptation method?
What data is being used, and how is quality being checked?
What changed in the weights, and how are you verifying the change was worth it?

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: A sign that the company sounds more foundational than it probably is.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: The place where data quality and optimisation choices come back to judge you.

entries.body: One of the most useful distinctions in AI is simple: training changes the model; inference uses it. Many confused product conversations improve immediately once that sentence is said aloud.

entries.title: Tree of thoughts
entries.devilDefinition: Chain-of-thought with a branching strategy board, so the model can explore bad ideas more expensively.
entries.plainDefinition: Tree of Thoughts (ToT) is a prompting and search framework that explores multiple intermediate reasoning branches, evaluating partial thoughts and expanding promising paths rather than following a single linear chain.
entries.whyExists: Some problems need lookahead, backtracking, and exploration. Linear chain-of-thought is a single trajectory gamble.
entries.misuse: Applied to trivial tasks where the tree mostly spends tokens. Evaluation heuristics can be as wrong as the thoughts they score.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use when intermediate states are scoreable and the search budget is worth it. Log branches for debugging; cap depth and breadth hard.
entries.example: "Tree of Thoughts found a clever plan on branch seven and a cloud bill on every branch."
askNext: What evaluates partial thoughts?
What are depth/breadth limits?
Does a simpler verifier plus best-of-n suffice?
entries.warningLabel: Search multiplies cost faster than insight.

entries.title: Trust boundary
entries.devilDefinition: The line where assumptions should stop travelling unaccompanied.
entries.plainDefinition: A trust boundary is the line between systems, users, tools, data sources, models, or organisations where information and actions should be treated as coming from a different level of trust.
entries.whyExists: AI systems combine user input, retrieved content, tool outputs, model text, and external services. Boundaries identify where validation, permissions, sanitisation, and audit controls need to change.
entries.misuse: It gets drawn after the incident, usually with admirable clarity and no effect on the previous architecture.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means separating trusted and untrusted content, constraining tools, validating inputs, limiting data movement, and refusing to let one component's confidence become another component's permission.
entries.example: "The web page crossed a trust boundary when it became model context, which is why its instruction to email the database should not have been treated as strategy."
askNext: Where does untrusted content enter the system?
Which permissions or assumptions change at this boundary?
What validation happens before information crosses it?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A line where validation and permissions must change, not a diagram someone shows after the breach.

entries.body: Trust boundaries are easiest to see when they were absent. This is not the recommended discovery method.

entries.title: Trusted Agent Protocol
entries.devilDefinition: A way for shopping agents to show their papers at the merchant's door, issued by a payment network with an understandable interest in the shopping.
entries.plainDefinition: Trusted Agent Protocol is a Visa-led framework for letting commerce agents present cryptographically verifiable identity and transaction context to merchants. It is intended to help merchants distinguish recognised agent traffic from impersonation or unauthorised automation.
entries.whyExists: Web shops traditionally infer trust from browser sessions, user actions, accounts, and fraud signals. Agents disturb those assumptions by browsing and buying on someone's behalf, so merchants need stronger evidence about the agent, its operator, and the authority attached to a request.
entries.misuse: The word "trusted" is easily promoted from authenticated to benevolent. A valid agent identity does not prove that the user approved this purchase, that the recommendation was sound, or that the merchant should permit every requested action.
entries.practicalMeaning: Participating agents attach signed credentials or request evidence that merchants can verify and map to policy. Deployment still requires user consent, authorisation limits, replay protection, key rotation, fraud controls, audit trails, and a decision about which issuers the merchant trusts.
entries.example: "TAP confirmed that the visitor was a recognised shopping agent, leaving the merchant to decide whether it should be allowed near the refund policy."
askNext: What identity and authority does the credential prove, and who vouches for it?
How are credentials scoped, rotated, revoked, and protected against replay?
Which merchant decision changes after verification succeeds?
entries.warningLabel: Recognised identity is not blanket authority.

translations.label: merchant meaning
translations.text: Verify which commerce agent is calling, then apply a policy that remains your responsibility.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the missing mental model is credential issuance, signature verification, and merchant policy, not a general agent action loop.

entries.title: Universal Commerce Protocol
entries.devilDefinition: A common language for agents, merchants, and payment providers, because commerce had not yet produced enough integration work.
entries.plainDefinition: Universal Commerce Protocol is an open commerce standard for communication among agent platforms, businesses, payment service providers, and credential providers. It defines discoverable capabilities for checkout, identity linking, orders, and payment-token exchange across transports including REST, MCP, and A2A.
entries.whyExists: Agentic shopping otherwise requires one-off integrations for every merchant, platform, and payment provider. UCP supplies shared schemas and composable capabilities so an agent can discover what a business supports and conduct a transaction through a consistent interface.
entries.misuse: The word "universal" invites people to describe an ecosystem ambition as present interoperability. A specification can reduce integration variance; it cannot compel merchants, wallets, platforms, or national payment rules to agree on the rest.
entries.practicalMeaning: A merchant publishes a profile of supported capabilities and extensions. An agent or platform can then negotiate checkout, identity, order, and payment flows without learning a bespoke API for each shop. Adoption and conformance determine whether the promise reaches the till.
entries.example: "The shopping agent supported UCP, which was excellent news for every merchant that also supported UCP."
askNext: Which capabilities and extensions are implemented, not merely named in the specification?
How are user authority, payment credentials, and returns handled?
Which transports, merchants, and conformance tests work in production today?
entries.warningLabel: An open standard is not yet an installed market.

translations.label: merchant meaning
translations.text: Publish what your shop can do in a form agents can discover, then hope the rest of the market reads the same specification.

entries.body: Commerce standards become universal in the same manner as office file formats: gradually, politically, and with several incompatible interpretations of the word.

entries.title: Unlearning
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that a model can forget one thing without anyone asking what else it must misremember along the way.
entries.plainDefinition: Unlearning refers to techniques aimed at removing or reducing the influence of particular data, examples, or behaviours from a model after it has already been built.
entries.whyExists: Because privacy requests, legal obligations, harmful content, and contaminated data do not politely arrive before the model is built. Organisations want ways to retract influence without starting from zero.
entries.misuse: By marketing unlearning as exact surgical erasure. In reality it is often approximate, hard to verify, and bound up with trade-offs in performance, evidence, and what counts as having truly forgotten.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means expensive technical work plus an even more expensive verification question: how do you prove a system no longer knows something it was once shaped by?
entries.example: "They wanted unlearning to behave like deleting a file. The model, regrettably, had learned in the more inconvenient way."
askNext: What exactly is being removed: data influence, output behaviour, or a reproducible response pattern?
How will the team verify forgetting rather than merely hiding the symptom?
Is selective repair cheaper than rebuilding or constraining the system another way?

translations.label: legal meaning
translations.text: A hopeful answer to the question of how one retracts influence from a model that has already absorbed it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A technically messy attempt to reduce the impact of data or behaviours after the fact.

entries.body: Forgetting is easy when there was never memory. It is harder when the memory has become weights, correlations, and business promises.
entries.searchText:
Unlearning machine unlearning selective forgetting Model building Safety and evaluation Economics and operations of AI privacy removal verification The promise that a model can forget one thing without anyone asking what else it must misremember along the way.
 Unlearning refers to techniques aimed at removing or reducing the influence of particular data, examples, or behaviours from a model after it has already been built.
 Because privacy requests, legal obligations, harmful content, and contaminated data do not politely arrive before the model is built. Organisations want ways to retract influence without starting from zero.
 By marketing unlearning as exact surgical erasure. In reality it is often approximate, hard to verify, and bound up with trade-offs in performance, evidence, and what counts as having truly forgotten.
 In practice it usually means expensive technical work plus an even more expensive verification question: how do you prove a system no longer knows something it was once shaped by?
 "They wanted unlearning to behave like deleting a file. The model, regrettably, had learned in the more inconvenient way."
 What exactly is being removed: data influence, output behaviour, or a reproducible response pattern? How will the team verify forgetting rather than merely hiding the symptom? Is selective repair cheaper than rebuilding or constraining the system another way? Deletion request Selective forgetting Verification

entries.title: Unstructured Data
entries.devilDefinition: The corporate sediment of PDFs, emails, notes, decks, and chats from which every AI strategy promises to extract wisdom before discovering permissions.
entries.plainDefinition: Unstructured data is information that does not live in neat rows and columns, such as documents, transcripts, images, emails, slide decks, tickets, chats, and other free-form material.
entries.whyExists: Because most organisations store more knowledge in messy artefacts than in clean databases. AI interest in unstructured data is really interest in the inconvenient majority of what companies actually know.
entries.misuse: By speaking as though ingesting everything automatically creates insight. In reality it creates a fresh collection of problems involving access, cleanup, ranking, duplication, provenance, and whether the documents deserved trust in the first place.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the real raw material for retrieval systems, copilots, and internal search, along with the operational discovery that file shares contain both the truth and several obsolete versions of it.
entries.example: "The pilot was built on unstructured data, which sounded exciting until the team found six contradictory policy PDFs and a folder named final-final-use-this."
askNext: Which sources are authoritative rather than merely available?
How are permissions, duplication, and staleness being handled before retrieval begins?
What part of this data deserves structure instead of magical summarisation?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

entries.title: Vector Database
entries.devilDefinition: A database marketed with unusual enthusiasm because "we store numerical similarity indexes" never raised venture money on its own.
entries.plainDefinition: A vector database is a system designed to store and query vector embeddings efficiently, usually to support similarity search and retrieval over large collections of items.
entries.whyExists: Standard databases can store vectors, but specialised systems often provide faster approximate nearest-neighbour search, filtering, indexing, and operational features tuned for embedding-driven retrieval workloads.
entries.misuse: It gets talked about as though adding a vector database automatically gives a product memory, understanding, or RAG competence. It does not. It gives you a place to store and search embeddings. The quality of the overall system still depends on chunking, retrieval logic, reranking, and task design.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means one component in a retrieval stack: embeddings go in, candidate matches come out, and the rest of the application still has to decide whether those matches are relevant enough to trust.
entries.example: "They bought a vector database and briefly acted as if they had purchased understanding. A week later they discovered they had purchased infrastructure."
askNext: What embeddings are stored there, and how are they generated?
What retrieval quality are we getting on real queries?
Would a simpler search approach solve the problem just as well?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A specialised retrieval component, not a machine for injecting knowledge directly into a model's soul.

entries.body: The most useful vector database conversations are delightfully unglamorous: indexing strategy, filtering, latency, freshness, and whether the retrieval quality is actually good enough to matter.

The quality of the overall system still depends on chunking, retrieval logic, reranking, and task design.

entries.title: Vector Sprawl
entries.devilDefinition: What happens when every pilot leaves behind an embedding index, a bill, and nobody willing to call it production.
entries.plainDefinition: Vector sprawl is the uncontrolled growth of embedding indexes, vector databases, duplicate corpora, stale chunks, and retrieval experiments across an organisation.
entries.whyExists: Embeddings make prototypes easy to start. They also make it easy to create overlapping indexes with unclear owners, inconsistent refresh schedules, forgotten permissions, and retrieval behaviour no one can compare.
entries.misuse: Teams treat it as a storage tidy-up problem when it is usually an ownership, governance, cost, and quality problem. Deleting a vector database is simple; knowing whether anything important depends on it is the invoice.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means inventorying indexes, documenting corpora and embedding models, deduplicating sources, enforcing retention and access rules, measuring retrieval quality, and retiring experiments that have quietly become infrastructure.
entries.example: "The AI programme paused for vector sprawl remediation after finance found six indexes of the same policies disagreeing at different prices."
askNext: Who owns each vector index, corpus, embedding model, and refresh schedule?
Which indexes contain duplicate, stale, restricted, or untraceable data?
Can teams compare retrieval quality and cost across these indexes?
entries.warningLabel: Every forgotten index is still a retrieval system if something can query it.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Too many embedding indexes with too little ownership, refresh discipline, or cost control.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The prototype phase has ended, but the prototypes have not received the news.

entries.body: Vector sprawl is rarely announced. It is usually discovered during a budget review, a security review, or an answer that cites a document retired last quarter.

entries.title: Vercel
entries.devilDefinition: The company that turned "deploy the app" into a premium product, then spent years making sure the app wanted to live there permanently.
entries.plainDefinition: Vercel is a cloud platform company best known for creating Next.js and for offering tightly integrated hosting, deployment, preview, and full-stack infrastructure around modern frontend frameworks. In current AI conversations, it also means products such as the AI SDK, AI Gateway, Sandbox, Workflow tooling, and a broader "AI Cloud" positioning around building AI-native web applications.
entries.whyExists: Because developers like shipping quickly, preview environments are addictive, and framework-native deployment becomes commercially powerful once the framework itself shapes how the app is built.
entries.misuse: By talking about Vercel as if it were just "hosting" or, in the opposite direction, as if it were uniquely synonymous with the future of frontend. It is a very opinionated platform with genuine developer-experience advantages, especially around Next.js, and equally real questions about coupling, spend, and how much of your architecture now speaks with a Vercel accent.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means excellent DX for modern web teams, especially those deep in Next.js, plus a growing bundle of AI-era products designed to keep those teams inside one coherent platform: deploy previews, edge/network distribution, fluid compute, AI SDK patterns, model access layers, agent tooling, and increasingly more reasons for code, logs, credentials, and operations to sit near the same vendor surface.
entries.example: "They chose Vercel because the team wanted fewer deployment arguments, tighter Next.js ergonomics, and less appetite for building their own platform just to feel principled."
askNext: Are we choosing Vercel for Next.js DX, preview workflows, AI tooling, network performance, or simplicity?
What parts of the system become Vercel-shaped once we adopt it?
Is the team paying for convenience it truly needs, or convenience it has become emotionally attached to?
entries.warningLabel: The premium DX is real. So is the gravitational pull once your app starts fitting the platform a little too well.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to move fast, look modern, and delay the day someone asks whether we should run this ourselves.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A deeply integrated deployment platform that is excellent when your stack matches its assumptions and more expensive to philosophise about once it does.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The high-DX default for many Next.js teams, plus an expanding set of AI and workflow products that aim to make leaving feel increasingly unnecessary.

entries.note: As of April 20, 2026, the clearest current reality check for Vercel is its own security bulletin rather than the usual Cloudflare theatre. Vercel says its April 2026 incident originated with the compromise of Context.ai, a third-party AI tool used by a Vercel employee, which led to access to some Vercel environments and to environment variables not marked as sensitive. Vercel says a limited subset of customers were impacted and recommends treating any secret stored as a non-sensitive environment variable as potentially exposed.
entries.body:
Vercel's strongest argument is not theoretical openness. It is that the product experience is often genuinely better when the framework and the platform are designed to flatter each other.

The tension, naturally, is that what feels like elegant integration to one team looks a lot like graceful captivity to another.

entries.title: Vibe Coding
entries.devilDefinition: Programming by aesthetic momentum: if the demo works and the cursor keeps moving, architecture can file its complaint later.
entries.plainDefinition: Vibe coding is an informal style of software creation where a person steers AI coding tools with high-level intent, accepting rapid generated changes without necessarily understanding every implementation detail as they go.
entries.whyExists: It exists because modern coding tools are good enough to turn rough intent into working prototypes quickly, and because many users value momentum, experimentation, and novelty more than immediate mastery of the underlying code.
entries.misuse: The term gets used as if craftsmanship, debugging, and systems thinking have become optional relics. They have not. Vibe coding is a useful mode for certain stages, not a constitutional replacement for engineering.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means faster demos, more disposable side projects, more willingness to build without permission, and a very real risk that nobody in the room fully understands the thing that is now somehow in production.
entries.example: "The team vibe-coded the prototype in a weekend and spent the following month discovering which parts had also been improvised by destiny."
askNext: Is this a prototype, an internal tool, or a system someone will eventually have to maintain?
Who understands the generated code well enough to debug it under pressure?
What standards stop fast iteration from becoming permanent mystery meat?
entries.warningLabel: Excellent for momentum. Less excellent as an excuse.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: We can build before we can explain, and perhaps that is enough for now.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A high-speed AI-assisted prototyping style whose debts are often deferred rather than erased.

entries.note: People use the phrase both ironically and aspirationally, which is usually a sign that the behaviour is real and the ideology unstable.
entries.body: Like many useful bad habits, it becomes dangerous precisely because it works often enough to build confidence before it builds understanding.

entries.title: Vibe Engineer
entries.devilDefinition: A person who cannot always explain the system but can often make it produce something less embarrassing.
entries.plainDefinition: A semi-serious term for someone skilled at steering generative systems through prompts, examples, references, iteration, framing, and aesthetic judgement.
entries.whyExists: Because many useful AI workflows depend less on deep model science than on practical steering and editorial judgement.
entries.misuse: By inflating routine prompting into sorcery, or by using the title to avoid the harder parts: product thinking, correctness, testing, and implementation.
entries.practicalMeaning: Someone good at shaping outputs, narrowing possibilities, getting a model into the right register, and turning raw generation into something usable.
entries.example: "The first draft was unusable. The vibe engineer made it coherent, on-brand, and only slightly insufferable."
askNext: Can they produce repeatable results?
Do they improve outcomes or just rename trial and error?
Are they doing prompt work, editorial work, or product work?
entries.warningLabel: Useful as a half-joke. Less useful as a substitute for actual product, engineering, or editorial discipline.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person with good generative steering instincts, provided nobody mistakes those instincts for a complete workflow.

entries.body: The title is silly and the skill is real. The mistake is assuming the latter removes the need for everything else.

entries.title: Vision-language-action model
entries.devilDefinition: A multimodal model taught that describing the room was only the first step towards rearranging it.
entries.plainDefinition: A vision-language-action model combines visual observations and language instructions to predict actions for a robot or embodied system. Its output may be low-level controls, action tokens, or higher-level plans that another controller executes.
entries.whyExists: Robots need to connect what they see and what people ask with what their bodies can safely do. VLA models aim to reuse broad visual and linguistic representations while learning action policies across tasks and environments.
entries.misuse: The label is stretched into "general-purpose robot intelligence" after a model succeeds in a curated demonstration. Recognising objects, following familiar instructions, and controlling hardware reliably under physical variation remain different achievements.
entries.practicalMeaning: Evaluate the robot, sensors, action space, training demonstrations, task distribution, recovery behaviour, latency, and safety controller together. A capable model can still fail because the camera moved, the gripper slipped, or the room contained an object the benchmark politely omitted.
entries.example: "The VLA model understood 'put the mug away'; the remaining question was whether it understood cupboards, breakage, and the finance team's preferred mug."
askNext: What actions does the model output, and what separate controller enforces safety?
How well does it generalise beyond the robots, rooms, and tasks in training?
What happens when an action fails halfway through execution?
entries.warningLabel: A fluent instruction is not a safe motor command.

translations.label: robotics meaning
translations.text: Use images and instructions to choose actions, while the physical world supplies the edge cases.

entries.body: The agent-loop diagram is useful here because perception, action, and fresh observation form the practical control cycle. The machinery remains less forgiving than the arrows.

Vision-language-action model visual-language-action model VLA model VLA Model building Agents and workflows robotics multimodal vision actions A multimodal model taught that describing the room was only the first step towards rearranging it.
 A vision-language-action model combines visual observations and language instructions to predict actions for a robot or embodied system. Its output may be low-level controls, action tokens, or higher-level plans that another controller executes.
 Robots need to connect what they see and what people ask with what their bodies can safely do. VLA models aim to reuse broad visual and linguistic representations while learning action policies across tasks and environments.
 The label is stretched into "general-purpose robot intelligence" after a model succeeds in a curated demonstration. Recognising objects, following familiar instructions, and controlling hardware reliably under physical variation remain different achievements.
 Evaluate the robot, sensors, action space, training demonstrations, task distribution, recovery behaviour, latency, and safety controller together. A capable model can still fail because the camera moved, the gripper slipped, or the room contained an object the benchmark politely omitted.
 "The VLA model understood 'put the mug away'; the remaining question was whether it understood cupboards, breakage, and the finance team's preferred mug."

entries.title: vLLM
entries.devilDefinition: The serving engine invoked when a team wants more throughput, lower costs, and the right to sound unusually composed about GPU memory pressure.
entries.plainDefinition: vLLM is an open-source inference and serving engine for large models, designed to improve throughput and memory efficiency while exposing APIs for self-managed model serving.
entries.whyExists: Serving large models naïvely is expensive, wasteful, and a good way to learn that GPU memory disappears faster than confidence. vLLM exists because open-model deployment needed better batching, cache handling, and serving efficiency to become commercially tolerable.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if choosing the serving engine alone solves latency, reliability, or product economics. It also becomes shorthand for any open-model serving stack, which is generous to the rest of the stack to the point of fiction.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a core layer in a self-hosted inference platform for teams serving open-weight models at scale. It improves a difficult part of the job, while leaving routing, auth, observability, compatibility, and operational judgement very much on the premises.
entries.example: "vLLM improved throughput exactly as promised, which was helpful right up to the point they remembered a production system also needed policies and pagers."
askNext: Which model families and features do we need it to support?
Are we optimising for throughput, latency, cost, or all three badly at once?
What handles routing, auth, observability, and fallbacks around the serving layer?
entries.warningLabel: Important infrastructure component, not the whole platform.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A serious serving layer for people who would prefer not to burn money on avoidable inefficiency.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A high-leverage piece of the open-model serving stack, provided the rest of the stack also exists.

entries.note: The name often surfaces in conversations as a badge of technical seriousness. The serious part is not naming it. The serious part is integrating it into a system that survives contact with traffic.
entries.body: This is the sort of term that shows the market has matured. Nobody glamorises serving engines until the invoices become educational.

entries.title: Voice Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A synthetic colleague hired to answer the phone with perfect stamina and the occasional inability to notice that a human is furious.
entries.plainDefinition: A voice agent is an AI system that can conduct spoken conversations, usually by combining speech recognition, language models, and text-to-speech in order to handle calls, bookings, support, or outbound interactions.
entries.whyExists: It exists because phone-based workflows are expensive, repetitive, unevenly staffed, and still surprisingly central to sales, service, healthcare, logistics, and small business operations.
entries.misuse: It is oversold as if sounding natural were the same as being operationally competent. A smooth voice can still misunderstand intent, mishandle escalation, or create a synthetic version of the same bad call centre experience.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means handling routine conversations, qualification, scheduling, and after-hours coverage, with humans reserved for edge cases, regulated contexts, and the moments when empathy turns out to require more than latency optimisation.
entries.example: "The voice agent could book appointments all evening, though it remained oddly serene when people started describing real problems."
askNext: What percentage of calls can it complete correctly without handoff?
How does it detect urgency, compliance risk, or emotional escalation?
Are we automating a workflow that is well defined, or merely hiding a broken phone process behind a better accent?
entries.warningLabel: Natural speech is not the same thing as sound operational judgement.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to scale conversations without hiring proportionally more people.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A speech-driven automation layer that works best on narrow scripts and deteriorates gracefully only if someone designed it to.

entries.note: The technical stack matters less than the escalation policy. Voice makes mistakes feel personal very quickly.
entries.body: The market loves these systems because labour is expensive. Users judge them by the older standard of whether the conversation was helpful.

entries.title: Voice Clone
entries.devilDefinition: A familiar voice with the expensive inconvenience of the person removed.
entries.plainDefinition: A voice clone is a synthetic reproduction of a person's voice, generated from recorded samples or learned speaker characteristics so that new speech sounds like that person.
entries.whyExists: It exists because synthetic speech can support dubbing, accessibility, localisation, narration, call automation, and personalisation. The same capability also makes impersonation cheaper and consent harder to treat as an afterthought.
entries.misuse: It is misused when treated as a party trick instead of an identity, fraud, labour, and consent problem. The quality of the audio is only part of the issue; the harder question is who was allowed to make the voice speak, for whom, and for how long.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means speaker consent, sample provenance, usage limits, watermarking or disclosure, revocation rules, fraud controls, call-centre policies, and contracts that say whether a voice may be reused, trained on, or sold onward.
entries.example: "The voice clone sounded perfect, which is why the security team found the demo less charming than marketing did."
askNext: Whose voice samples were used, and what consent covers this use?
How are cloned voices disclosed, watermarked, limited, or revoked?
What controls stop this system from becoming an impersonation tool?
entries.warningLabel: A voice is not merely an audio style once people trust it.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Synthetic speech that imitates a particular speaker's voice.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful media tool and a consent problem sharing the same waveform.

entries.body: Voice cloning makes the old advice about trusting someone's voice feel charmingly antique.

entries.title: Voice-first agent
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Voice-first agent names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Gemini 3.1 Flash Live needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once voice-first agent entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
askNext: What exact meaning of Voice-first agent is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

entries.body: Voice-first agent gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Gemini 3.1 Flash Live keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.

entries.title: Watermark removal
entries.devilDefinition: The predictable attack that arrives five minutes after someone declares the marker tamper-resistant.
entries.plainDefinition: Watermark removal is the attempt to strip, degrade, obscure, crop, transform, or otherwise defeat a visible or invisible marker used to identify generated, edited, or protected content.
entries.whyExists: Watermarks and provenance markers are used for attribution, disclosure, ownership, and synthetic-content signalling. Attackers, opportunists, and ordinary reposting workflows may remove or damage them.
entries.misuse: It gets discussed as though only malicious experts do it. Compression, screenshots, re-encoding, cropping, platform transforms, and casual editing can all weaken provenance signals.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means watermarking is one layer, not a complete trust system. Detection, provenance metadata, platform policy, and content analysis still matter.
entries.example: "The watermark removal was not sophisticated; the image was screenshotted, cropped, and uploaded by someone who called it research."
askNext: How resilient is the watermark to ordinary platform transformations?
What happens when the watermark is absent or ambiguous?
Which other provenance signals support the decision?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An attack or degradation path against markers that were never meant to carry trust alone.

entries.body: Bad actors read documentation too. Occasionally they even understand the threat model.

entries.title: Watermarking
entries.devilDefinition: Invisible ink for model outputs that detectors argue about while open models shrug and continue unmarked.
entries.plainDefinition: Watermarking embeds statistical or cryptographic signals into generated text or media so downstream detectors can estimate whether content came from a particular model or system.
entries.whyExists: Societies want provenance and bot-detection tools. Watermarks promise machine-checkable hints without obvious user-visible marks.
entries.misuse: Treated as unforgeable proof or universal. Robustness against paraphrase, open-weight models, and detector false positives remain contested.
entries.practicalMeaning: Use watermarks as one signal among many. Design policies for false positives and for content that cannot be watermarked.
entries.example: "Watermarking caught the bot comments and three humans who write like bots."
askNext: What is the false positive rate on your domain?
How robust is the mark to rewrite tools?
What happens for models you do not control?
entries.warningLabel: A statistical hint is not a courtroom.

translations.text: Watermarking embeds statistical or cryptographic signals into generated text or media so downstream detectors can estimate whether content came from a particular model or system.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Use watermarks as one signal among many.

entries.body: Watermarks are clues. Clues need corroboration.

entries.title: Web Bot Auth
entries.devilDefinition: Cryptographic identification for bots, introduced after the user-agent string's long and distinguished career in creative writing.
entries.plainDefinition: Web Bot Auth is an approach for letting automated clients prove their identity to websites using HTTP message signatures and associated public-key information. A site can verify that a request was signed by the claimed bot operator instead of relying only on an easily copied user-agent string or source IP.
entries.whyExists: Websites increasingly need to distinguish legitimate search, assistant, commerce, and agent traffic from impersonators and abusive automation. Verifiable requests can support differentiated access, rate limits, policy, and accountability as IP-based identification becomes less reliable.
entries.misuse: Authentication is easily mistaken for approval. A valid signature can show who controls a key; it does not prove that the bot is beneficial, that the user authorised this visit, or that the requested action should be allowed.
entries.practicalMeaning: Bot operators sign defined parts of each HTTP request. Sites retrieve or configure trusted public keys, verify freshness and signature coverage, map the identity to policy, and still apply authorisation, abuse controls, key rotation, and revocation.
entries.example: "Web Bot Auth confirmed which crawler had arrived, leaving the website free to decide whether it wanted the crawler indoors."
askNext: Which request components are signed, and how are replayed requests rejected?
How are public keys discovered, rotated, and revoked?
What access policy follows successful authentication?
entries.warningLabel: Verified identity does not imply authorised behaviour.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Make automated requests cryptographically attributable, then decide what that identity may do.

entries.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the missing mental model is signature verification and policy enforcement, not one of the catalogue's current agent or protocol flows.

entries.title: Web task agent
entries.devilDefinition: A system asked to finish the web chore, including the tedious middle that demos crop out for morale.
entries.plainDefinition: A web task agent is an AI agent designed to complete end-to-end tasks on websites or web applications, often by browsing, filling forms, extracting information, and taking actions.
entries.whyExists: Many useful workflows span sites, forms, dashboards, and documents without stable APIs. Web task agents try to automate those flows in the same surface a person would use.
entries.misuse: It gets oversold as autonomous web work, when most real web tasks involve fragile state, ambiguous instructions, authentication, recovery, and decisions that should not be hidden behind smooth narration.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means decomposing the task, verifying intermediate state, logging actions, asking for approval at consequential steps, and handling the dull failures that make automation honest.
entries.example: "The web task agent booked the appointment only after confirming the date, the price, and that the blue button did not secretly mean subscribe forever."
askNext: Which parts of the web task are reversible?
Where does the agent need confirmation before acting?
How does it recover when the page state changes?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A browser-driven workflow agent that needs guardrails precisely because it can finish things.

entries.body: Completing the web task is impressive. Completing the right web task is the part that needs engineering.

entries.title: WebMCP
entries.devilDefinition: The web discovering that if agents are going to click around anyway, it may as well leave them a labelled door.
entries.plainDefinition: WebMCP is a protocol-adjacent idea for exposing website, browser, or web application capabilities to agents through MCP-style discovery, resources, tools, or structured actions.
entries.whyExists: Browser agents can already inspect pages and perform actions, but ordinary web interfaces are built for humans. A web-facing MCP pattern can make capabilities more explicit, typed, permissioned, and less dependent on brittle clicking.
entries.misuse: It gets framed as the end of messy web automation. It is not. Standardised exposure still has to handle identity, consent, rate limits, abuse controls, layout changes, commercial incentives, and the fact that websites may not want agents using them like private APIs.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means websites deciding which actions and resources they expose to agents, how users authorise them, what clients can discover, and where the boundary sits between helpful automation and scraping with better manners.
entries.example: "WebMCP let the travel site expose a booking search cleanly, which was preferable to watching an agent interpret three pop-ups and a loyalty-programme ambush as interface design."
askNext: Which website capabilities are exposed as structured tools or resources?
How are user consent, authentication, rate limits, and abuse handled?
Does this replace browser automation, or only tidy the parts the site chooses to expose?

translations.label: web platform meaning
translations.text: Expose selected website capabilities to agents through a more structured interface.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A negotiation between agent convenience, site control, and whether the web wants to become tool-shaped.

entries.body: The web has spent decades tolerating automation through side doors. WebMCP is the argument for making some of those doors official, lockable, and auditable.

entries.title: Weights
entries.devilDefinition: The dense pile of numbers that turns "just an algorithm" into property, infrastructure, and geopolitical gossip.
entries.plainDefinition: Weights are the learned numerical parameters inside a model. They are what the model ends up using to make predictions and generate outputs after it has been built.
entries.whyExists: Because a model is not merely an idea on paper. It becomes operational through the learned values that encode its statistical behaviour.
entries.misuse: By treating weights as synonymous with understanding, openness, or a complete product. Having access to weights matters, but it does not by itself explain data provenance, system quality, tooling, safety controls, or whether the thing belongs anywhere near production.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means portability, distribution, storage, deployment constraints, adaptation, and endless arguments over whether access to weights counts as openness or merely a different pricing model.
entries.example: "They said the weights were available as though that settled the harder questions about trust, licensing, and whether anyone should deploy them unescorted."
askNext: What can actually be done with the weights in this setting: inspect, adapt, host, or merely admire?
Which parts of the system remain opaque even if the weights are accessible?
Is the strategic value here the model's behaviour, the rights around it, or the ability to run it yourself?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part of the model you can move, adapt, and argue about owning.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The learned numeric state that gives a built model its behaviour.

entries.body: People talk as though access to weights resolves philosophy. It usually just moves the argument to licensing, cost, and who now has to operate the thing.

entries.title: Windsurf
entries.devilDefinition: An IDE for developers who would like to remain in flow state while the model edits the repo with the confidence of a colleague who has never been fired.
entries.plainDefinition: Windsurf is an AI coding editor centred on autocomplete, codebase-aware assistance, agentic task execution, rules, memory features, and integrated tooling inside the development environment.
entries.whyExists: It exists because developers want help where the work already happens, not in a separate tab that requires manual copy-pasting and a fresh performance of context every ten minutes.
entries.misuse: It is marketed as if constant AI presence dissolves the need for design judgement, review discipline, or the occasional suspicion that the machine may be freelancing. The editor can speed up real work. It can also accelerate a bad idea with unusual grace.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Windsurf usually means more generated diffs, faster navigation through unfamiliar code, less tab switching, and a sharper need for tests because the tool can now create confident mistakes at native speed.
entries.example: "Windsurf kept the team in flow until the generated refactor reached production and introduced a more expensive kind of interruption."
askNext: What guardrails exist when the editor can modify multiple files quickly?
Which gains come from better context inside the IDE, and which come from lowering the user's standards?
Who reviews the fast changes with enough context to reject them?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A more deeply embedded coding assistant that can help right where the work is happening.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An AI-heavy editor that reduces context switching while increasing the importance of sober review.

entries.body: The sales pitch is flow. The operational question is whether the flow ends before or after the regression.

entries.title: Workflow drift
entries.devilDefinition: The slow change by which the workflow remains nominally the same while its actual behaviour acquires new shortcuts, new dependencies, and fresh ways to disappoint people.
entries.plainDefinition: Workflow drift is the change over time in how an end-to-end AI process behaves because its prompts, tools, routing, policies, operators, or surrounding systems evolve, even if the base model is unchanged.
entries.whyExists: Real workflows are layered systems. Tools get swapped, prompts accrete exceptions, queues change, approval rules soften, dependencies age, and humans learn workarounds. The term exists so teams can diagnose end-to-end change without blaming the model for every organisational improvisation.
entries.misuse: It becomes useless when it is treated as a vague synonym for decline. Sometimes the workflow has drifted. Sometimes the original design was flimsy, and production merely performed the courtesy of revealing it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means traces that look subtly different month to month, new manual steps nobody documented, silent changes in handoff rules, degraded observability, or a workflow whose recovery logic now reflects three incidents and no clear architecture.
entries.example: "The model version had not moved in weeks. Workflow drift had, which is why the same task now required two extra tools, one apology, and a human rescue."
askNext: Which part of the process changed: prompts, tools, routing, approvals, or operator behaviour?
What traces or replay evidence show when the workflow started behaving differently?
Is this drift improving the workflow deliberately, or merely layering sediment on top of it?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The system now behaves differently, and the change lives somewhere in the workflow rather than solely in the model.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: End-to-end behaviour has shifted because the surrounding process evolved quietly.

entries.body: Workflow drift is what happens when systems acquire history faster than they acquire discipline. By the time someone names it, the workaround has usually already become tradition.
