entries.title: Working memory
entries.devilDefinition: The small mental desk the agent uses right now, not to be confused with the attic it claims is long-term memory.
entries.plainDefinition: Working memory in AI systems is the short-lived state used during a task—context window contents, scratchpads, tool results—distinct from durable stores like vector memories or databases.
entries.whyExists: Agent designs needed language for ephemeral state versus persistent memory. Capacity and eviction policies differ sharply.
entries.misuse: Overloaded to mean any stored fact. If it vanishes when the session ends, it was working memory whether marketing agrees or not.
entries.practicalMeaning: Size working memory deliberately, summarise or spill to external stores, and avoid stuffing durable secrets into transient context without need.
entries.example: "Working memory held the API response; long-term memory held last month's wrong API response."
askNext: What is discarded at task end?
How do you compress or page working state?
Which facts must be durable instead?
entries.warningLabel: Context windows are not filing cabinets.

entries.title: Workload identity
entries.devilDefinition: Giving software a passport so agents stop borrowing human credentials like a house key under the mat.
entries.plainDefinition: Workload identity is a security pattern where non-human workloads—services, jobs, agents—authenticate with cryptographically verifiable identities rather than long-lived shared secrets or person accounts.
entries.whyExists: Agents and automation multiply. Shared API keys and human logins do not scale safely to machine actors.
entries.misuse: Implemented as another static token renamed 'identity.' Without short life, binding, and policy, it is cosplay.
entries.practicalMeaning: Prefer OIDC/SPIFFE-style identities, least privilege, short credentials, and audit trails that name the workload—not only the team.
entries.example: "Workload identity let the agent call billing without copying a human's cloud key into a prompt."
askNext: Is identity short-lived and environment-bound?
Can you revoke a single workload quickly?
Do logs show which workload acted?
entries.warningLabel: A certificate is only as good as its issuance policy.

entries.title: Worktree
entries.devilDefinition: A separate checkout where the agent can make a mess without immediately decorating the main desk with it.
entries.plainDefinition: A worktree is a linked working tree attached to the same Git repository, allowing different branches or tasks to be checked out in separate directories at the same time.
entries.whyExists: It exists because parallel work is useful and mixing every branch, experiment, dependency state, and agent task into one checkout is how small mistakes become local archaeology.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a sandbox. It is not one. A worktree isolates files and branches, not credentials, networks, databases, or the agent's ability to run a regrettable command.
entries.practicalMeaning: In AI-assisted development, it usually means giving a coding agent or subagent a separate branch and directory so it can edit, test, and report back without colliding with the main checkout or another agent's work.
entries.example: "The worktree let three agents work in parallel, which was helpful until everyone remembered they still shared one confused package cache."
askNext: What is isolated by the worktree, and what shared state still leaks across tasks?
How will changes be reviewed, merged, kept, or discarded?
Do untracked files, environment secrets, and generated artefacts need to be copied or excluded?
entries.warningLabel: A worktree is branch isolation, not a security boundary.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: Another working directory attached to the same repository, useful for parallel branches.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A practical way to let agents work beside you without immediately trampling the main checkout.

entries.body: Worktrees are boring in the way useful tools often are. They do not make agents safe. They make their edits easier to contain, inspect, and throw away.

entries.title: World Model
entries.devilDefinition: The flattering phrase for a system that appears to know enough structure for humans to start projecting philosophy onto it.
entries.plainDefinition: A world model is an internal representation that helps a system predict how states, objects, actions, or events relate to one another over time.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because systems that can anticipate structure, consequence, and continuity often perform better than systems that merely react to the next local cue.
entries.misuse: By treating any evidence of internal structure as proof of deep understanding, common sense, or machine comprehension of life itself. The phrase is often used at exactly the point where caution should become more fashionable.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a model has learned enough regularity to predict useful consequences, not that it has joined the human condition. It is a technical claim that acquires mystical overtones the moment it leaves the lab.
entries.example: "Someone said world model and the meeting immediately upgraded from product review to metaphysical salon."
askNext: What specific predictive behaviour suggests a useful internal model rather than clever pattern matching?
How much of the claim comes from experiments, and how much from the romance of the phrase?
Does the system actually plan better, or do we simply enjoy the story more?

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: A claim that the system has learned structured regularities useful for prediction and planning.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A technical idea that often acquires grander implications once it reaches the public.

entries.body: Some phrases arrive in public life already overpromoted. This one barely waits to leave the paper.

 In practice it usually means a model has learned enough regularity to predict useful consequences, not that it has joined the human condition. It is a technical claim that acquires mystical overtones the moment it leaves the lab.
 "Someone said world model and the meeting immediately upgraded from product review to metaphysical salon."
 What specific predictive behaviour suggests a useful internal model rather than clever pattern matching? How much of the claim comes from experiments, and how much from the romance of the phrase? Does the system actually plan better, or do we simply enjoy the story more? Planning Prediction Internal representation

entries.title: x402
entries.devilDefinition: HTTP 402 with a stablecoin wallet and the confidence of a status code finally invited to a meeting.
entries.plainDefinition: x402 is an open payment protocol built around HTTP `402 Payment Required`. A server can return payment requirements with a response, after which a client pays through a supported digital-asset rail, supplies proof, and retries the request.
entries.whyExists: APIs and autonomous clients often need accounts, subscriptions, API keys, or human checkout before a small purchase. x402 aims to make payment a native part of the request-response exchange so software can buy access to data, compute, or services as needed.
entries.misuse: It is described as frictionless machine commerce, which carefully omits wallet custody, network fees, exchange risk, sanctions controls, refunds, tax, failed settlement, and the person eventually asked to explain the wallet activity.
entries.practicalMeaning: The service declares an amount, asset, network, recipient, and payment scheme. The client decides whether it may pay, obtains or submits settlement proof, and retries. Production systems still need spending policy, identity, receipts, reconciliation, dispute handling, and idempotent delivery.
entries.example: "The agent paid for one API call through x402, converting a missing API key into a finance policy with excellent latency."
askNext: Which assets, networks, facilitators, and settlement guarantees are actually supported?
Who controls the wallet and enforces per-request and aggregate spending limits?
How are duplicate charges, refunds, receipts, and failed delivery handled?
entries.warningLabel: A payment rail is not a purchasing policy.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: Let a service quote a machine-readable price at the HTTP boundary and let authorised software settle it.

entries.body: The agent-loop diagram fits because the client observes a price, checks policy, pays, retries, and receives a result. The wallet is where the loop acquires consequences.

entries.title: xAI
entries.devilDefinition: A company name that sometimes arrives in conversation as though brand velocity were itself a benchmark.
entries.plainDefinition: xAI is a company that develops AI models and products, most notably the Grok family and associated consumer experiences. As with other vendors, the company name is distinct from the specific model or product being discussed.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because public discussion often blends the company, its founder mythology, the Grok product, and any specific model performance claim into one emotionally charged noun.
entries.misuse: People say "xAI" when they mean Grok, or they compare "xAI" to a specific model from another vendor. That makes already noisy comparisons worse.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a vendor option with its own models, product direction, pricing, access paths, and risk profile. Evaluating it requires the same discipline you would apply to any other supplier, not a stronger opinion about the surrounding spectacle.
entries.example: "The investor memo praised xAI. The engineering note underneath had to ask the more useful question: which model, for what workload, at what level of reliability?"
askNext: Do you mean xAI the company, Grok the product, or a specific model?
What evidence supports the performance claim beyond brand momentum?
How much of the attraction is technical fit versus narrative gravity?
entries.warningLabel: Company name. Brand heat is not model evaluation.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Another vendor choice that still needs the same boring questions about model quality, cost, access, and reliability.

entries.body: The faster the public narrative around a company moves, the more useful slow, literal questions become.

entries.title: Yann LeCun
entries.devilDefinition: The prominent researcher most likely to answer AI melodrama with a technical correction and a shrug.
entries.plainDefinition: Yann LeCun is a Turing Award-winning researcher, formerly Meta's chief AI scientist, known for foundational work in deep learning and for public scepticism toward the more theatrical claims surrounding advanced AI.
entries.whyExists: Because names in AI often function as positions in an argument. Invoking Yann usually signals a preference for research realism, engineering confidence, open technical debate, and a lower appetite for civilisational drum rolls.
entries.misuse: By treating his scepticism as proof that major risks are imaginary, or treating his confidence as mere complacency. He is better understood as a durable counterweight to the field's taste for apocalyptic inflation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the conversation is shifting away from mystique and toward questions of architecture, learning, world models, open research, and whether the loudest timeline claims deserve fewer headlines than they get.
entries.example: "Once Yann entered the thread, the tone changed from prophecy to argument, which was healthy if less marketable."
askNext: Is his name being invoked for technical substance or merely as anti-doom theatre?
Does the claim concern research direction, open access, or arguments about capability timelines?
Are we borrowing his scepticism instead of making the case ourselves?

translations.label: debate meaning
translations.text: A shorthand for the camp that finds much AI grandiosity premature, theatrical, or technically loose.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for a more sceptical and engineering-heavy reading of AI progress.

entries.note: LeCun's move out of Meta's chief-scientist role makes the old title useful context rather than a current definition. The cultural shorthand remains: scepticism, world models, and less patience for AI melodrama than the market usually rewards.
entries.body: Prestige can be abused in both directions. Some people borrow it to inflate the future. Others borrow it to sound calmer than their own argument deserves.

entries.title: Zero Data Retention
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that your prompts may pass through the machine without joining its afterlife.
entries.plainDefinition: Zero data retention is a provider policy or product setting indicating that prompts and outputs are not stored for model improvement or long-term retention beyond limited operational handling.
entries.whyExists: Because enterprise buyers, regulated industries, and anyone with a functioning sense of self-preservation want stronger assurances about where sensitive inputs end up after an AI request is made.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a synonym for perfect secrecy. A zero-retention promise usually lives inside a wider reality of transient processing, abuse monitoring, metadata, contracts, and implementation details people discover only after reading the fine print.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a procurement checkbox with real value, provided someone also asks about logs, sub-processors, retention exceptions, and whether the promise applies across every feature being sold under the same cheerful brand.
entries.example: "The security team relaxed at zero data retention, then tensed again when the discussion moved from slogan to specifics."
askNext: Does the promise cover prompts, outputs, logs, attachments, and every relevant product surface?
What operational or abuse-monitoring exceptions still exist?
Is the policy a contractual guarantee, a best-effort setting, or a line in a landing page?
entries.warningLabel: Useful promise. Not a substitute for reading the retention details around logs, attachments, and exceptions.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A line item that can make legal and security conversations somewhat less apocalyptic.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A constrained non-retention policy whose exact safety depends on the surrounding details.

entries.body: Most privacy language sounds best at banner height. The truth usually begins one scroll lower.

entries.title: Zero-click search
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Zero-click search names the nearby AI idea that shows up when AI Overviews needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once zero-click search entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
askNext: What exact meaning of Zero-click search is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

entries.body: Zero-click search gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around AI Overviews keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.

entries.title: Zero-Shot
entries.devilDefinition: Asking the system to improvise on first contact and congratulating yourself when probability looks like generality.
entries.plainDefinition: Zero-shot refers to asking a model to perform a task without giving task-specific examples in the prompt.
entries.whyExists: It is useful to distinguish between tasks a model can handle from instructions alone and tasks that need demonstrations, schemas, retrieval, or tighter scaffolding.
entries.misuse: By treating zero-shot success as proof of broad understanding rather than a description of prompt setup. Many so-called zero-shot wins rely on hidden context, careful wording, or tasks that were never especially demanding.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the cheapest first attempt. If the task is routine and the instructions are clear, zero-shot can be enough. If the task is messy, high-stakes, or structurally ambiguous, it often becomes a polite way to under-specify the work.
entries.example: "They celebrated the zero-shot result right up to the point where the real workflow arrived with edge cases and an invoice."
askNext: Is the task genuinely being done from instructions alone, or is other hidden scaffolding doing the work?
What examples, schemas, or retrieval would reduce failure without much extra cost?
Are we calling this zero-shot because it is elegant, or because we have not designed the workflow yet?

translations.label: demo meaning
translations.text: A neat way to show breadth before anyone asks what the production version needs.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A task attempted without task-specific examples, for better or worse.

entries.body: Zero-shot is attractive because it feels like generality. It is also attractive because it postpones the adult work of specifying the task properly.

categoryStats.title: Core concepts
categoryStats.description: Terms people use as if they were self-explanatory, usually before the definitions start fraying.

categoryStats.title: Cultural terms
categoryStats.description: The social weirdness around AI: belief, taste, cringe, status games, and the internet behaving as it usually does when handed a new machine.

categoryStats.title: Model building
categoryStats.description: How models are trained, tuned, evaluated, and occasionally overpraised.

categoryStats.title: Model usage
categoryStats.description: What it means to actually run a model in production, where invoices and latency live.

categoryStats.title: Agents and workflows
categoryStats.description: Automation patterns, orchestration, and the difference between useful autonomy and stagecraft.

categoryStats.title: Retrieval and memory
categoryStats.description: How systems fetch context, store traces, and pretend they remember you.

categoryStats.title: Product and vendor terms
categoryStats.description: Company names, product labels, and the branding haze around them.

categoryStats.title: Safety and evaluation
categoryStats.description: Guardrails, tests, preferences, and the uncomfortable gap between lab scores and reality.

categoryStats.title: Infrastructure and deployment
categoryStats.description: The machinery underneath the demo: compute, serving, throughput, and operational trade-offs.

categoryStats.title: Economics and operations of AI
categoryStats.description: Cost, labour, incentives, and the very unmagical business of running AI systems.
